1.1: epeideper polloi epecheirésan anatasasthai diegésin — since many have undertaken to compile a narrative — Luke's prologue is the most literary sentence in the NT, using classical Greek historical prologues style (cf. Thucydides, Josephus). Epeideper (since indeed) is formal; anatasasthai diegesin (to draw up a narrative) uses technical historiographical vocabulary. anothen (from the beginning, from above) — Luke claims to have investigated everything carefully (parakolouthekoti, perfect participle: having followed alongside, investigated thoroughly) and writes in order (kathexes) for Theophilus to know the certainty (asphaleia) of the things taught.
1.46: megalynei he psyche mou ton kyrion — my soul magnifies the Lord — the Magnificat is a mosaic of OT texts, especially Hannah's prayer (1 Sam 2:1-10). Megalynei (magnifies, declares great) is the same verb as LXX Ps 34:3 (megazynen ton kyrion). he tapeinosis tes doules autou — the humble estate of his servant — tapeinosis (lowliness, humility) is the covenant-quality of the anawim; Mary identifies herself with the humble poor of Israel who trust YHWH's reversals. The seven reversals that follow (vv.51-53) are the Magnificat's theological core: each pair presents the divine inversion of human expectation.
1.2: hoi ap archēs autoptai kai hyperetai genomenoi tou logou — those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and servants of the word. Two distinct but related categories: autoptai (eyewitnesses, lit. “self-seers”) emphasizes direct visual access; hyperetai tou logou (servants of the word) designates those who transmitted the oral tradition. “The word” (logos) here is not the Johannine divine Word but the oral proclamation of the Jesus tradition — the technical term for the pre-literary gospel.
1.3: parekolouthēkoti anōthen pasin akribōs kathexēs soi grapsai — having carefully investigated everything from the beginning, to write an orderly account for you. Parekolouthēkoti (having followed alongside, traced carefully) combines thoroughness with personal engagement; it is a historiographical term used by Thucydides and Polybius. Kathexēs (in sequence/order) does not necessarily mean strict chronological order but thematic and narrative coherence. Luke signals his literary method: careful investigation, personal research, orderly presentation.
1.4: hina epignōis peri hōn katēchēthēs logōn tēn asphaleian — so that you may know the certainty of the things you have been instructed. Epignōis (full knowledge, thorough recognition) intensifies over simple gnōsis; it suggests a settled certainty rather than mere acquaintance. Katēchēthēs (instructed, catechized) is the source of the English “catechism” — Theophilus has received oral instruction, and Luke writes to ground it in documented history. Asphaleia (certainty, security) is the epistemological goal.
1.5: en tais hēmerais Hērōdou tou basileōs tēs Ioudaias — in the days of Herod king of Judea. Luke’s historical anchor differs from Matthew (who uses “in the days of Herod the king” without specifying Judea); the specificity is characteristic of Luke’s historiographical precision. The ephēmeria (priestly division) of Abijah places Zechariah in one of the 24 rotating priestly courses (1 Chr 24:10) — each serving one week twice per year.
1.6: dikaioi amphoteroi enantión tou theou poreuomenoi en pasais tais entolais kai dikaiōmasin tou kyriou amemptoi — both righteous in the sight of God, walking in all the commands and decrees of the Lord blamelessly. Dikaioi (righteous) is a legal-covenant term; amemptoi (blameless, without blame/fault) is its moral-character complement. The two terms together describe the complete covenant faithfulness of Zechariah and Elizabeth. They represent the best of pre-messianic Israel — faithful Torah observance awaiting the fulfillment.
1.7: kai ouk ēn autois teknon kathoti hē Elisabet ēn steira kai amphoteroi probebēkotes en tais hēmerais autōn ēsan — they had no child because Elizabeth was barren, and they were both advanced in their days. The barrenness-then-miraculous-birth pattern (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Samson’s mother) signals a salvation-history event: the miraculous end of barrenness always precedes a key figure in redemption. Elizabeth’s barrenness is not an accident but a narrative marker.
1.8: egeneto de en tō hierateuein auton en tē taxei tēs ephēmerias autou enanti tou theou — it happened that while he was serving as priest in the order of his division before God. Egeneto de is a Lukan narrative marker (Hebraism: wayehi) that introduces a new scene or event; it signals a Septuagint-influenced style. The “before God” (enanti tou theou) positions the entire scene within the divine presence — what follows happens in sacred space.
1.9: kata to ethos tēs hierateias elagen tou thymiasai eiselthōn eis ton naon tou kyriou — according to the custom of the priestly order, he was chosen by lot to go into the temple of the Lord and burn incense. The incense offering was the most significant individual act of priestly service (only one priest per rotation performed it); selection by lot was the method for this honor. To enter the inner sanctuary for the incense altar was the closest a non-high-priest could come to the divine presence.
1.10: kai pan to plēthos ēn tou laou proseuchomenon exō tō hōra tou thymiamatos — all the assembled multitude of the people were praying outside at the hour of incense. The communal prayer during the incense offering (morning and evening) is well-attested; the people stand outside the sanctuary while the priest enters. The scene establishes a double prayer: the people’s corporate prayer and Zechariah’s private prayer (v.13 — “your prayer has been heard”) whose answer arrives in the same moment.
1.11: ōphthē de autō angelos kyriou hestōs ek dexiōn tou thysiastēriou tou thymiamatos — an angel of the Lord appeared to him, standing at the right side of the altar of incense. Ōphthē (appeared, was seen) is the LXX term for divine appearances and post-resurrection appearances (24:34; Acts 13:31); it emphasizes the objective reality of the vision. The right side of the altar is the position of favor and power (cf. Ps 110:1).
1.12: kai etarachthē Zacharias idōn kai phobos epepesen ep auton — Zechariah was startled when he saw him and fear fell upon him. Etarachthē (was troubled, agitated) and phobos epepesen (fear fell upon him) are the standard responses to theophany in the OT (Dan 10:7-9; Judg 13:22; Isa 6:5). The verb epepesen (fell upon) is an idiom for sudden, overwhelming fear — not psychological anxiety but the appropriate creaturely response to numinous presence.
1.13: eipen de pros auton ho angelos: mē phobou Zacharia dioti eisēkousthē hē deēsis sou — but the angel said to him: Do not be afraid, Zechariah; your prayer has been heard. The standard angel-greeting mē phobou (do not be afraid) appears in every major annunciation (v.30; 2:10; Dan 10:12; Judg 6:23). Deēsis (petition, prayer of request) specifies the type of prayer — Zechariah has been praying for a child; the hearing of the prayer (divine perfect passive eisēkousthē) is announced in the moment of the angelic vision.
1.14: kai estai chara soi kai agalliasis — he will be a joy and delight to you. Chara (joy) and agalliasis (exultation, leaping joy) are paired throughout Luke-Acts at moments of eschatological fulfillment (1:44; 10:21; Acts 2:28). Agalliasis is specifically the joy of the Psalms at divine salvation (Ps 45:7; 51:8); the arrival of John signals the beginning of eschatological joy.
1.15: oinos kai sikera ou mē piē kai pneumatos hagiou plēsthēsetai — he is never to take wine or strong drink, and he will be filled with the Holy Spirit. Oinos (wine) and sikera (strong drink, barley beer) are the Nazirite abstinence pair from Numbers 6:3 and the Samson narrative (Judg 13:4-5). The Nazirite dedication from birth marks John as consecrated to God’s special service. Plēsthēsetai pneumatos hagiou (will be filled with the Holy Spirit) — the filling that was characteristic of OT prophets is promised to John even from the womb (eti ek koilias mētros autou).
1.16: kai pollous tōn huiōn Israēl epistrepsai epi kyrion ton theon autōn — he will bring back many of the people of Israel to the Lord their God. Epistrepsai (turn back, return) is the covenant-renewal term from Malachi 3:7 (“Return to me and I will return to you”) and the great turning prophecies of the prophets. John’s mission is explicitly the turning/returning of Israel — not external reform but covenant renewal of heart (cf. Jer 31:18; Ezek 36:26-27).
1.17: autos proeleusetai enōpion autou en pneumati kai dynamei Ēliou — he will go before the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah. Enōpion autou (before him) — the pronoun refers to “the Lord their God” (v.16); John goes before YHWH, which will mean he goes before Jesus. Pneumati kai dynamei Ēliou (in the spirit and power of Elijah) — not a reincarnation but a functional correspondence: the spirit that worked through Elijah works through John for the same purpose (Mal 4:5-6; Deut 30:6). The mission: to turn the hearts of fathers to children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the righteous.
1.18: kata ti gnōsomai touto? egō gar eimi presbytēs kai hē gynē mou probebēkuia en tais hēmerais autēs — how can I know this? For I am an old man and my wife is well along in years. Zechariah’s question is formally identical to Abraham’s (Gen 15:8 — “How can I know that I will gain possession?”) but receives a harsher answer; Abraham’s doubt was met with a covenant ceremony; Zechariah’s is met with the sign of dumbness. The difference may be the context: Abraham asked before receiving the promise; Zechariah asks after receiving it from an angelic messenger standing in the sanctuary.
1.19: egō eimi Gabriēl ho parestēkōs enōpion tou theou — I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God. Gabriel (“God is my strong one”) appears in Daniel 8:16 and 9:21, where he brings prophetic revelation. His self-identification in terms of his position (“I stand in the presence of God”) invokes the heavenly court imagery of 1 Kgs 22:19 and Isa 6:1-2 — Gabriel is one of the angelic attendants of the divine throne. The commission to “speak good news to you” (euangelisasthai) is the first use of Luke’s key gospel-proclamation verb.
1.20: kai idou esē siōpōn kai mē dynatos lalēsai achri hēs hēmeras genētai tauta — now you will be silent and unable to speak until the day this happens. The dumbness-as-sign (cf. Ezekiel 3:26 — “I will make your tongue stick to the roof of your mouth”) is both a judicial sign of unbelief and a prophetic sign. Zechariah will be mute until John is named (v.64), at which point his mouth opens in praise. The nine-month silence is a gestation of the word: the spoken promise is incubating in his voiceless waiting.
1.21: kai ēn ho laos prossdokōn ton Zacharian kai ethaumazon en tō chronizein auton en tō naō — the people were waiting for Zechariah and wondering why he stayed so long in the temple. Prosdokōn (waiting, expecting) is Luke’s characteristic word for the faithful expectation of Israel (2:25; 2:38; 3:15; 7:19-20). The people’s waiting for Zechariah in the temple mirrors Israel’s larger waiting for the fulfillment of the covenant promises.
1.22: exelthōn de ouk ēdynato lalēsai autois kai epegnōsan hoti optasian heōraken en tō naō — when he came out, he could not speak to them. They realized he had seen a vision in the temple. Optasian (vision) is distinct from horama (dream-vision); it describes an objective visual appearance. The people’s recognition of the vision from Zechariah’s dumbness and gestures anticipates the sign-nature of John’s birth: something in the divine realm has happened that is visible in the ordinary world.
1.23: kai egeneto hōs eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tēs leitourgias autou apēlthen eis ton oikon autou — when his time of service was completed, he returned home. Leitourgia (priestly service, liturgy) is the formal term for the temple service; Luke uses it to maintain the sacral register of the scene. Zechariah returns home after completing the week of temple service — the angelic encounter did not interrupt the service, only transformed its meaning.
1.24: meta de tautas tas hēmeras synelabon Elisabeth hē gynē autou kai periekryben heautēn mēnas pente — after this his wife Elizabeth became pregnant and for five months remained in seclusion. The five months of seclusion have no parallel in the OT; Elizabeth may be waiting until pregnancy is unmistakably established, or the seclusion may reflect the honor-shame dynamics of her social situation (she had been barren — a source of social reproach). The six-month marker (v.26) will bring Gabriel to Mary, connecting the two pregnancies.
1.25: hoti houtōs moi pepoiēken kyrios en hēmerais hais epeiden aphelesin oneidos mou en anthrōpois — the Lord has done this for me. He has shown me this kindness to remove my disgrace among the people. Oneidos (disgrace, reproach) is the word used for Rachel’s shame at barrenness (Gen 30:23) and Hannah’s (1 Sam 1:6); Elizabeth’s statement situates her within the great OT mothers whose barrenness was removed. The removal of disgrace is not just personal relief but a sign of divine covenant faithfulness to those who wait.
1.26: en de tō mēni tō hektō apestaldē ho angelos Gabriēl apo tou theou eis polin tēs Galilaias — in the sixth month God sent the angel Gabriel to Nazareth, a town in Galilee. The six-month link connects John’s and Jesus’s births into a unified divine plan; Gabriel is sent directly by God (apo tou theou), unlike his earlier appearance which was framed as a commission from his standing-presence. Galilee is identified as the geographic location: the Messiah comes from the margins of Jewish religious geography.
1.27: pros parthenon emnēsteumenēn andri hō onoma Iōsēph ex oikou Dauid kai to onoma tēs parthenou Mariam — to a virgin pledged to a man named Joseph, a descendant of David. Parthenos (virgin) is used twice in this verse, bracketing Mary’s identity; the emphasis on virginity anticipates the question of v.34. Ex oikou Dauid (of the house of David) modifies Joseph, establishing the legal Davidic lineage through which Jesus inherits the throne promise (2 Sam 7:12-16; Ps 132:11-12). Mary is not stated to be of David’s line here (though Luke 3 may imply it).
1.28: kecharitoménē — the single word that generated centuries of Mariology. The perfect passive participle of charitóō (to bestow grace/favor upon) means “the one who has been graced/favored” — a completed state of grace-having. The MKT “highly favoured” captures the passive (grace received, not intrinsic) and the intensive perfect (fully, completely graced). The Latin gratia plena (“full of grace,” Jerome’s Vulgate) translates the Greek idiom but has been interpreted as describing Mary’s own holiness rather than the divine action upon her.
1.29: hē de epi tō logō dietarachthē kai dielogizeto potapos eiē ho aspasmos houtos — Mary was greatly troubled at his word and wondered what kind of greeting this might be. Dietarachthē (deeply troubled, thoroughly agitated) is stronger than etarachthē (v.12, Zechariah’s startlement); the intensifying dia- prefix suggests Mary is more deeply moved. Her pondering (dielogizeto) is reflective and theological — she processes the greeting intellectually as well as emotionally. Luke presents Mary as one who reflects on divine revelation (cf. 2:19, 51).
1.30: heure charin para tō theō — you have found favor with God. Charis (grace, favor) here is used as an objective divine disposition toward Mary, not a quality she possesses. The language echoes Genesis 6:8 (Noah found grace/favor in the eyes of the LORD) and Exodus 33:17 (Moses found favor with God). Finding favor is the basis for God’s mission through a person; it is divine election rather than human merit.
1.31: kai idou syllēpsē en gastri kai texē huion kai kaleseis to onoma autou Iēsoun — you will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to call him Jesus. The annunciation formula (conceive-bear-name) follows the pattern of Genesis 16:11 (Hagar), Judges 13:3 (Samson’s mother), and Isaiah 7:14 (Emmanuel). Iēsous (Joshua/Jesus, “YHWH saves”) is the name whose meaning will be explained in Matthew 1:21; Luke presents the naming without the Hebrew etymology but within the annunciation pattern that evokes it.
1.32: houtos estai megas kai huios hypsistou klēthēsetai — he will be great and will be called Son of the Most High. The parallel with v.15 (“he will be great in the sight of the Lord” — John) is deliberate: John’s greatness is before the Lord; Jesus’s greatness is absolute (megas without qualifier). Hypsistos (Most High) is the Semitic divine title El Elyon (Gen 14:18-20; Ps 7:17; Dan 4:34), used here as a circumlocution for God among Jewish audiences who avoided the divine name.
1.33: kai basileusei epi ton oikon Iakōb eis tous aiōnas kai tēs basileias autou ouk estai telos — he will reign over Jacob’s descendants forever; his kingdom will never end. The language is a mosaic of Davidic-covenant texts: 2 Samuel 7:13 (“his kingdom will be established forever”), Isaiah 9:7 (“of the increase of his government and peace there will be no end”), Daniel 7:14 (“his dominion is an everlasting dominion”). The eternal kingdom promised to David is fulfilled in Jesus.
1.34: pōs estai touto epei andra ou ginōskō? — how will this be, since I am a virgin? Mary’s question is formally similar to Zechariah’s (v.18) but different in kind: she asks about the method (how?), not the evidence (how can I know?). Andra ou ginōskō (I do not know a man) — ginōskō is the euphemism for sexual intercourse (Gen 4:1 LXX). Her question is honest inquiry about the mechanics of fulfillment, not disbelief; she receives no rebuke.
1.35: pneuma hagion epeleusetai epi se kai dynamis hypsistou episkiasei soi — the Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you. Epeleusetai (will come upon) is used of the Spirit in Acts 1:8 (the Pentecost promise); it describes an empowering divine action. Episkiasei (will overshadow) is the LXX term for the cloud that covered the tabernacle (Exod 40:35 — the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle); the same verb appears at the Transfiguration (9:34). The conception is framed as a new creation/tabernacle event: the divine presence overshadows and life results.
1.36: kai idou Elisabet hē syngenēs sou kai autē syneilēphen huion en gērei autēs — even Elizabeth your relative has conceived a son in her old age. The connecting of Mary’s annunciation to Elizabeth’s miracle serves as a sign (sēmeion) that confirms the impossible; if the barren-and-aged Elizabeth has conceived, nothing is impossible with God. Syngenēs (relative, kinswoman) establishes the family connection between Jesus and John that will be narratively significant.
1.37: hoti ouk adynamēsei para tō theō pan rhēma — for no word from God will ever fail. The echo of Genesis 18:14 (“Is anything too hard for the LORD?”) is unmistakable. Rhēma here (word/thing/matter) differs from logos (the reasoned word) — it can mean both the spoken word and the thing/matter spoken of; the angel declares both the divine word and the divine action it announces to be beyond failure. The translation “no word from God will ever fail” captures both senses.
1.38: idou hē doulē kyriou genoito moi kata to rhēma sou — I am the Lord’s servant; may your word to me be fulfilled. Doulē (female slave/servant) is Mary’s self-designation; she identifies herself as the lowest-status member of the household, completely subject to the master’s will. The voluntary submission is significant: Mary’s “yes” is the model of covenant response — not merely obedience to an irresistible decree but willing submission to the divine word. Genoito (may it be, optative of wish) expresses active desire, not mere resignation.
1.39: anastaesa de Mariam en tais hēmerais tautais eporeuthē eis tēn oreinēn meta spoudēs — at that time Mary got ready and hurried to a town in the hill country. Anastaesa (rising up) is used for purposeful departure in Luke (cf. 15:18; Acts 1:15); the urgency (meta spoudēs, with haste) reflects both her excitement and practical wisdom — she needs the confirmation of Elizabeth’s pregnancy that the angel has given as a sign. The hill country (oreinēn) of Judea is a natural geographic description connecting to the area around Hebron.
1.40: eisēlthen eis ton oikon Zachariou kai ēspasato tēn Elisabet — she entered Zechariah’s home and greeted Elizabeth. The greeting (aspazesthai) is the formal act of welcome that triggers the Spirit-filling; Elizabeth does not respond to Mary’s words first but to her voice (v.44). The greeting scene is structured as a Spirit-event: Mary arrives, speaks, and the Spirit-prompted response is immediate and prophetic.
1.41: kai egeneto hōs ēkousen ton aspasmon tēs Marias hē Elisabet eskirtēsen to brephos en tē koilia autēs — when Elizabeth heard Mary’s greeting, the baby leaped in her womb. Eskirtēsen (leaped, kicked) is the word for the joyful leaping of the psalms (Ps 114:4,6 LXX — mountains and hills skipping at the Exodus) and of the calf released from the stall (Mal 4:2 LXX). John’s prenatal leap is his first act of prophetic testimony to the one coming after him — the forerunner’s response begins even before birth. Pneumatos hagiou eplēsthē hē Elisabet — and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit.
1.42: eulogēmenē sy en gynaixin kai eulogēmenos ho karpos tēs koilias sou — blessed are you among women, and blessed is the child you will bear. Elizabeth’s blessing is a double blessing: of Mary personally and of the child she carries. The formulation echoes the blessing of Jael (Judg 5:24 LXX — “blessed among women”) and Judith (Jdt 13:18); it is the highest honor-language for a woman in the Jewish tradition. The blessing of the fruit of the womb echoes Deuteronomy 28:4 (the covenant blessings).
1.43: kai pothen moi touto hina elthē hē mētēr tou kyriou mou pros me? — but why am I so favored, that the mother of my Lord should come to me? Hē mētēr tou kyriou mou (the mother of my Lord) — Elizabeth’s Spirit-inspired recognition of Mary’s child as kyrios (Lord) is the first human Christological confession in Luke. Kyrios in the LXX renders the divine name YHWH; Elizabeth’s use of it for the unborn child within Mary names the divine identity. This is the first instance of the “Lord” title applied to Jesus.
1.44: idou gar hōs egeneto hē phōnē tou aspasnou sou eis ta ōta mou eskirtēsen to brephos en agalliasei en tē koilia mou — as soon as the sound of your greeting reached my ears, the baby in my womb leaped for joy. En agalliasei (for joy, in exultation) — Elizabeth provides the interpretation of the leap that the narrator stated but did not explain: John’s prenatal leap is leaping for joy at the arrival of the Lord. The scene connects to the promise of v.14 (John will be a joy and exultation) — the promise begins to be fulfilled before his birth.
1.45: kai makaria hē pisteuasa hoti estai teleiōsis tois lelaleménois autē para kyriou — blessed is she who has believed that the Lord would fulfill his promises to her. The beatitude on Mary’s faith is Elizabeth’s counter to Zechariah’s unbelief (v.20): Zechariah doubted; Mary believed. Pisteuasa (having believed) is the aorist participle of completed faith; Mary’s initial “yes” (v.38) is now recognized and blessed. Teleiōsis (fulfillment, completion) is the accomplishment of the divine word — the same root as the prologue’s “things that have been fulfilled among us” (v.1).
1.47: kai ēgalliasen to pneuma mou epi tō theō tō sōtēri mou — and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior. Ēgalliasen (exulted, rejoiced greatly) is the Lukan joy-word from v.14 (agalliasis); Mary’s spirit leaps with the same joy that John’s body expressed in the womb. Tō sōtēri mou (my Savior) — the first use of sōtēr (Savior) in Luke; it prepares for the angel’s announcement in 2:11 (“a Savior has been born to you”). Mary acknowledges her own need of salvation from the one her child will be.
1.48: hoti epeblepsen epi tēn tapeinōsin tēs doulēs autou idou gar apo tou nyn makariousin me pasai hai geneai — for he has been mindful of the humble state of his servant. From now on all generations will call me blessed. Tapeinōsin (lowliness, humble state) carries both social and theological meaning: Mary’s low social position (an unmarried woman of no social standing in Galilee) and her covenant self-understanding as the humble who receive divine regard. The beatitude “all generations will call me blessed” is fulfilled in the church’s memory of Mary — an eschatological prediction now in the process of fulfillment.
1.49: hoti epoiēsen moi megala ho dynastēs kai hagion to onoma autou — for the Mighty One has done great things for me — holy is his name. Ho dynastēs (the Mighty One, the Powerful One) is a title of divine power found in LXX Zephaniah 3:17 (“The LORD your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save”). The “great things” (megala) echo Deuteronomy 10:21 (God did “great and awesome things” for Israel). “Holy is his name” — a liturgical formula acknowledging the divine identity as source of the salvific act.
1.50: kai to eleos autou eis geneas kai geneas tois phoboumenois auton — his mercy extends to those who fear him from generation to generation. Eleos (mercy, steadfast love) is the LXX rendering of Hebrew hesed (covenant faithfulness, loving-kindness); the Magnificat repeatedly uses covenant-fulfillment language. The qualification “those who fear him” defines the recipients of covenant mercy — not ethnic Israel automatically but those who stand in covenant fidelity before God.
1.51: epoiēsen kratos en brachioni autou dieskorpisen hyperēphanous dianoia kardias autōn — he has performed mighty deeds with his arm; he has scattered those who are proud in their inmost thoughts. The aorist verbs in vv.51-55 are prophetic aorists (past tense expressing certain future fulfillment from God’s perspective). Brachioni autou (with his arm) is the Exodus arm of divine power (Ps 98:1; Exod 6:6). The scattering of the proud (hyperēphanous, arrogant ones) echoes 1 Samuel 2:10 (Hannah’s song) — a direct literary source for the Magnificat.
1.52: kathēilen dynastas apo thronōn kai hypsōsen tapeinous — he has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble. The eschatological reversal (rulers down, humble up) is the central theme of the Magnificat and of Luke’s Gospel broadly (cf. 14:11; 18:14). Dynastas (rulers, powerful ones) anticipates Herod and Caesar; the tapeinoi (humble ones) anticipate the disciples, the poor, the social outcasts who receive the kingdom. The song announces the program of what follows in the Gospel.
1.53: peinōntas eneplēsen agathōn kai ploutountas exapesteilen kenous — he has filled the hungry with good things but has sent the rich away empty. The dietary reversal (hungry filled, rich emptied) is Hannah’s exact language (1 Sam 2:5 LXX). In Luke’s economy of blessing, physical hunger is a sign of spiritual readiness (the Beatitudes: “blessed are you who hunger now”, 6:21) while material satiety may signal spiritual closure (“woe to you who are full now”, 6:25). The Magnificat does not spiritualize poverty but treats it as a concrete reality that the kingdom reverses.
1.54: antelabeto Israēl paidos autou mnēsthēnai eleous — he has helped his servant Israel, remembering to be merciful. Antelabeto (has helped, has taken hold of to support) is used in the LXX for divine assistance (Isa 41:8-9 — God takes Israel’s hand). Paidos autou (his servant) — the servant-Israel of Isaiah 41-53 is gathered up in this phrase; Mary’s child will fulfill what Israel the servant was called to be. The covenant-memory (mnēsthēnai) frames salvation as God’s faithfulness to his own prior commitment.
1.55: kathōs elalēsen pros tous pateras hēmōn tō Abraam kai tō spermati autou eis ton aiōna — just as he promised to our ancestors, to Abraham and his descendants forever. The Abrahamic covenant is the ground of the Magnificat’s eschatological confidence: what God is doing in Mary’s child is the long-delayed fulfillment of what he swore to Abraham (Gen 12:1-3; 17:4-8; 22:15-18). Eis ton aiōna (forever, to the age) — the covenant promises are eternal; their fulfillment in Jesus is the beginning of the eternal kingdom.
1.56: emeinen de Mariam syn autē hōs mēnas treis kai hypestrepsen eis ton oikon autēs — Mary stayed with Elizabeth for about three months and then returned home. The three-month stay brings Mary close to John’s birth (Elizabeth was six months pregnant when Mary arrived); Mary returns before the birth, making John’s naming and circumcision scene a separate unit. Luke’s precision (about three months) is characteristic; he uses hōs (about, approximately) for temporal estimates.
1.57: tē de Elisabet eplēsthē ho chronos tou tekein autēn kai egennēsen huion — when it was time for Elizabeth to have her baby, she gave birth to a son. Eplēsthē ho chronos (the time was fulfilled) is eschatological language (cf. Gal 4:4 — “when the fullness of time came”); every birth in Luke’s infancy narrative is a fulfillment of divine timing. The birth of a son to the barren Elizabeth is the first of the two miraculous births that frame the Gospel’s opening.
1.58: kai ēkousan hoi perioikoi kai hoi syngeneis autēs hoti emegalynen kyrios to eleos autou met autēs kai synechairon autē — her neighbors and relatives heard that the Lord had shown her great mercy, and they shared her joy. Emegalynen to eleos (made great his mercy) — the verb echoes Mary’s Magnificat (megalynei, v.46); the greatness of God’s mercy, celebrated in Mary’s song, is now locally confirmed in Elizabeth’s neighborhood rejoicing. Synechairon (rejoiced with) is the covenant-community response to divine favor.
1.59: kai en tē hēmera tē ogdoē ēlthon peritemein to paidion kai ekaloun auto epi tō onomati tou patros autou Zacharian — on the eighth day they came to circumcise the child, and they were going to name him after his father Zechariah. Circumcision on the eighth day (Gen 17:12; Lev 12:3) marks covenant membership; the occasion is also when the naming formally occurs in Jewish practice. The assumption that the child should be named after his father is a cultural norm; the angel’s specific naming (v.13 — “you shall call him John”) disrupts this convention.
1.60: kai apokritheisa hē mētēr autou eipen: ouchi alla klēthēsetai Iōannēs — his mother spoke up and said: No! He is to be called John. Elizabeth’s insistence on the God-given name, without having been present for the angelic annunciation to Zechariah, suggests her own Spirit-informed knowledge; she has been filled with the Holy Spirit (v.41) and presumably Zechariah communicated the angel’s instruction. The divine naming overrides family and cultural convention.
1.61: kai eipan pros autēn hoti oudeis estin ek tēs syngenias sou hos kaleitai tō onomati toutō — there is no one among your relatives who has that name. The cultural weight of family naming is strong; departing from it requires explanation. The name Iōannēs (“YHWH has been gracious”) is not unknown in the OT tradition but is not in this family’s naming lineage. The choice of a theologically significant name outside family tradition signals a break with inheritance and entrance into a new, divinely-determined identity.
1.62: eneneuon de tō patri autou to ti an theloi kalesthai auton — then they made signs to his father to find out what he would like to name the child. The gesture communication with Zechariah suggests he is deaf as well as mute (or they assume he cannot hear their question). The appeal to the father for the authoritative naming decision is patriarchal convention; Zechariah’s written response will confirm and seal the divine name.
1.63: kai aithēsamenos pinakidion egrapsen legōn: Iōannēs estin onoma autou — he asked for a writing tablet and wrote: His name is John. Pinakidion (writing tablet) — a wax tablet for everyday writing. Zechariah’s writing Iōannēs estin onoma autou (John is his name, present tense) treats the divine naming as already established fact, not a choice being made. The written declaration is the confirmation of the angelic word; his obedient act of naming releases the sign of dumbness imposed by Gabriel.
1.64: aneōchthē de to stoma autou parachrēma kai hē glōssa autou kai elalei eulogōn ton theon — immediately his mouth was opened and his tongue set free, and he began to speak, praising God. The instantaneous restoration (parachrēma, immediately) confirms the angelic word’s authority. Eulogōn ton theon (praising God) — Zechariah’s first words after nine months of silence are praise; the dumbness was a gestation not of bitterness but of wonder. The Benedictus that follows is the fruit of this silenced gestation.
1.65: kai egeneto epi pantas phobos tous perioikountas autous kai en holē tē oreinē tēs Ioudaias dielaleito panta ta rhēmata tauta — all the neighbors were filled with awe, and throughout the hill country of Judea people were talking about all these things. Phobos (awe, fear) is the numinous response to the divine presence (2:9; 5:26; 7:16) — not terror but reverent wonder. The spreading of “all these things” (panta ta rhēmata tauta) throughout the region anticipates the spreading of the gospel; even before Jesus’s birth, the news of his forerunner is proclaimed.
1.66: kai ethento pantes hoi akousantes en tē kardia autōn legontes: ti ara to paidion touto estai? — everyone who heard this wondered, saying: What then is this child going to be? The pondering response (ethento en tē kardia autōn, laid up in their hearts) mirrors Mary’s own reflective response (2:19, 51); it is the appropriate response to a divine event that exceeds immediate comprehension. The question “what then is this child going to be?” is answered by the Benedictus (prophet of the Most High, v.76) and by Jesus’s own testimony (7:28).
1.67: kai Zacharias ho patēr autou eplēsthē pneumatos hagiou kai eprophēteusen legōn — his father Zechariah was filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesied. The triple Spirit-filling in Luke 1 (Elizabeth, v.41; Jesus from the womb, v.15; Zechariah, v.67) establishes the Holy Spirit as the agent of the birth-narrative events. Zechariah’s prophetic utterance is identified as Spirit-speech; the Benedictus is not his personal composition but a Spirit-inspired prophetic oracle.
1.68: eulogētos kyrios ho theos tou Israēl hoti epeskepsato kai epoiēsen lytrōsin tō laō autou — praise be to the Lord, the God of Israel, because he has come to his people and redeemed them. Epeskepsato (visited, came to) is the covenant-visitation term (Exod 4:31 LXX; Ruth 1:6; Ps 106:4); when God “visits” his people, salvation follows. Lytrōsin (redemption, ransom-release) is the Exodus word for the liberation from Egypt; Zechariah’s opening declaration frames the birth of John’s Lord as a new Exodus — a liberation of God’s people from a bondage greater than Egypt.
1.69: kai ēgeiren keras sōtērias hēmin en oikō Dauid paidos autou — he has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David. Keras sōtērias (horn of salvation) is an OT idiom for a powerful deliverer (1 Sam 2:10; Ps 18:2; Ezek 29:21) — the horn being the symbol of power and strength. The horn is raised “in the house of David” — the Davidic covenant is being fulfilled. Ēgeiren (raised up) anticipates the resurrection language; the salvation-horn that is raised will be raised from death.
1.70: kathōs elalēsen dia stomatos tōn hagiōn ap aiōnos prophētōn autou — as he said through his holy prophets of long ago. The parenthetical verse grounds the salvation announcement in prophetic Scripture; what is happening is not new but the fulfillment of “long-standing” (ap aiōnos, from the age of ages) prophetic expectation. The entire birth narrative is framed as prophetic fulfillment.
1.71: sōtērian ex echthrōn hēmōn kai ek cheiros pantōn tōn misountōn hēmas — salvation from our enemies and from the hand of all who hate us. The political-liberation language of Zechariah’s Benedictus (vv.71, 74) reflects the concrete hope of Israel for deliverance from Roman occupation; Jesus’s ministry will redefine “enemies” to include the spiritual powers of sin and death, and “salvation” to encompass the forgiveness (v.77) that addresses the root cause of all human bondage.
1.72: poiēsai eleos meta tōn paterōn hēmōn kai mnēsthēnai diathēkēs hagias autou — to show mercy to our ancestors and to remember his holy covenant. The covenant-memory of God (mnēsthēnai diathēkēs) is the covenant-faithfulness language of Psalms (Ps 105:8; 111:5); what God is doing in the birth of John’s Lord is the fulfillment of the covenantal memory he has been keeping since the patriarchs. Diathēkēs hagias (holy covenant) — the holiness of the covenant is YHWH’s own holiness pledged to its fulfillment.
1.73: horkon hon ōmosen pros Abraam ton patera hēmōn — the oath he swore to our father Abraham. The Abrahamic oath (Gen 22:16-18 — sworn by himself because there was nothing greater, Heb 6:13-17) is the most unbreakable divine commitment in Scripture; salvation through Jesus is the oath’s fulfillment. The oath-structure of the Benedictus (God has sworn / we will serve, vv.73-75) mirrors the covenant renewal structure of Deuteronomy.
1.74: tou dothēnai hēmin aphobōs ek cheiros echthrōn rysthentis latreuein autō — to rescue us from the hand of our enemies, and to enable us to serve him without fear. The purpose of salvation (tou dothēnai, the giving of salvation) is service: rescued from fear-based service to enemies, Israel is freed for fearless (aphobōs) service to God. The Exodus pattern recurs: liberation from Pharaoh is liberation for service to YHWH (Exod 4:23).
1.75: en hosiotēti kai dikaiosynē enōpion autou pasas tas hēmeras hēmōn — in holiness and righteousness before him all our days. Hosiotēti (holiness) and dikaiosynē (righteousness) are the covenant character-pair: holiness is the vertical dimension (consecrated to God) and righteousness is the horizontal (right conduct toward others). “Before him all our days” — the life of covenant service is coram Deo (before God) — not public performance but continuous orientation toward the divine presence.
1.76: kai sy de paidion prophētēs hypsistou klēthēsē proporeusē gar enōpion kyriou hetoimasai hodous autou — and you, my child, will be called a prophet of the Most High; for you will go before the Lord to prepare the way for him. The transition from hymn to address to John marks the Benedictus’s second movement. Prophētēs hypsistou (prophet of the Most High) — John is “more than a prophet” (7:26) but is identified here as one; the “Most High” (hypsistos) whose prophet he is is the same Most High whose Son was just announced (v.32). Going “before the Lord” is the Malachi 3:1 role.
1.77: tou dounai gnōsin sōtērias tō laō autou en aphesei hamartiōn autōn — to give his people the knowledge of salvation through the forgiveness of their sins. Gnōsin sōtērias (knowledge of salvation) — John’s mission is not the full proclamation of salvation (which is Jesus’s) but the giving of knowledge of it: preparing the people to recognize and receive what Jesus brings. Aphesis hamartiōn (forgiveness of sins) is introduced here as the content of salvation; the political-liberation language of vv.71-74 is now interpreted: the true enemy from which Israel needs liberation is sin.
1.78: dia splanchna eleous theou hēmōn en hois episkepsetai hēmas anatolē ex hypsous — because of the tender mercy of our God, by which the rising sun will come to us from heaven. Splanchna eleous (bowels/entrails of mercy) is the most visceral mercy-language in Greek — the gut-feeling of compassion (cf. Col 3:12; Phil 2:1). Anatolē ex hypsous (rising/dawn from on high) — anatolē means both the rising sun and the shoot/branch (Zech 6:12 LXX — the Branch/Shoot prophecy of the Messiah). Both senses are active: the messianic “Branch from on high” who rises like the sun to scatter the darkness.
1.79: epiphanai tois en skotei kai skia thanatou kathēmenois tou kateutheynai tous podas hēmōn eis hodon eirēnēs — to shine on those living in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the path of peace. The dawn imagery fulfills Isaiah 9:2 (“the people walking in darkness have seen a great light”) and Isaiah 42:7 (opening blind eyes, releasing from the dungeon those who sit in darkness). Hodon eirēnēs (the path of peace) — the final word of the Benedictus; the salvation announced in vv.68-77 culminates in eirēnē (shalom) — the comprehensive well-being of restored covenant relationship.
1.80: to de paidion ēuxanen kai ekrataiouto pneumati kai ēn en tais erēmois heōs hēmeras anadeixeōs autou pros ton Israēl — the child grew and became strong in spirit; and he lived in the wilderness until he appeared publicly to Israel. The wilderness-formation narrative of John parallels the Nazareth-formation of Jesus (2:40, 52). Erēmois (desert/wilderness) is the Elijah location (1 Kgs 19:3-4) and the prophetic preparation space; John is being shaped in the desert for his desert proclamation (3:2-4). Anadeixeōs (public appearance, commissioning) is a rare word used for the installation of officials; John’s commissioning moment is the beginning of the Gospel’s main narrative.

2.11: soter hos estin Christos Kyrios — a Savior who is Christ the Lord — the angelic birth announcement stacks three titles on the newborn: soter (savior — the most frequent soteriological title in Luke-Acts, used 4x in Luke, rarely in Matthew/Mark), Christos (the Messiah), and Kyrios (Lord — the LXX name for YHWH). The combination of all three titles in one announcement is Luke's programmatic Christological declaration. en polei David — in the city of David — the Davidic geography grounds the birth in covenant geography.
2.29: nyn apolueis ton doulon sou Despota — now you are releasing your servant, Master, in peace — the Nunc Dimittis of Simeon is structured as a release-prayer: apolueis (you release, let go) uses the legal-release term for a slave being freed or a prisoner being discharged; Simeon describes his death as a release from watchful-keeping-duty, now that he has seen the salvation. Despota (Master, lord with absolute authority) is the prayer address; eirene (peace) is the shalom of complete wellbeing at death's threshold.
2.1: apographesthai pasan ten oikoumenen — to register all the inhabited world — apographē (enrollment, registration) is an administrative census for taxation; oikoumenē (the inhabited world) is Luke's preferred term for the Roman empire. The dating reference to Quirinius (hēgemoneuontos) is the most discussed chronological problem in Luke — Josephus places the Quirinius census in 6-7 CE, not at Jesus's birth. Various solutions include a prior legateship or an earlier enrollment; the historical tension does not affect the theological point Luke is making: the Roman empire's administrative machinery becomes the instrument of the Davidic birthplace prophecy.
2.2: hautē apographē prōtē egeneto — this registration was the first — the phrase prōtē egeneto may be translated "first took place" (superlative) or "was before" (comparative), which some read as "this registration was before the one Quirinius made [as governor]." The syntactical ambiguity has been the source of extensive debate. Luke's purpose is the historical anchoring characteristic of his prologue method (1:1-4): he is demonstrating that the birth of Christ occurred within datable human history.
2.3: eporeuonto pantes apographesthai — all went to be registered — the imperfect eporeuonto (were going, were traveling) depicts the ongoing movement of population. The mass registration is the historical mechanism through which the Davidic birthplace requirement is met; Luke presents the Roman political order as an unwitting instrument of divine providence without labeling it as such.
2.4: anebē de kai Iōsēph apo tēs Galilaias ek poleōs Nazareth — Joseph also went up from Galilee, from Nazareth — anebainō (go up) is the standard verb for travel to Jerusalem/Judea, as higher elevation; the journey is the fulfillment of the Davidic-birthplace requirement. Polin Dauid (city of David) is Bethlehem, not Jerusalem — Luke is precise about which city of David is intended.
2.5: engkuō ousē — who was pregnant — engkuos (pregnant, great with child) is a medical term, consistent with Luke's medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart). The reference to Mary as Joseph's memnesteumenē (promised in marriage, betrothed) at this point of pregnancy maintains the earlier annunciation narrative's implication without requiring an explanation Luke has already provided.
2.6: eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tou tekein autēn — the days for her to give birth were fulfilled — eplēsthēsan (were fulfilled, were completed) is Luke's fulfillment language; the same verb appears throughout Luke-Acts when divine plans reach completion. Tekein (to give birth, infinitive of tiktō) is the verb of the annunciation (1:31); the verb link connects the promise to the fulfillment.
2.7: ton huion autēs ton prōtotokon — her firstborn son — prōtotokos (firstborn) has both legal and theological weight: the firstborn son receives the covenant inheritance and consecration to God (Exod 13:2; Num 18:15-16). The term does not say anything about whether Mary had later children — that is determined by other texts. espargānōsen — she wrapped him in swaddling cloths — sparganoa are strips of cloth for swaddling infants (a standard Greek medical term); Luke's use of the term is both precise and symbolic. phatnē — manger — the manger is the sign in v.12; the word means an animal feeding trough, not necessarily a stable (kataluma = lodging place, not necessarily an inn).
2.8: poimenes — shepherds — the choice of shepherds as the first recipients of the angelic announcement is significant against the background that shepherds in Second Temple Palestine were often considered ritually unreliable (unable to testify in court, frequently in contact with what was considered unclean). Luke presents the announcement going first to those society marginalized. agraulountes — living in the fields — compound of agros (field) + aulē (courtyard, dwelling); they sleep in the open field rather than in shelter.
2.9: angelos kyriou epestē autois — an angel of the Lord appeared to them — ephistēmi (stand upon, appear suddenly) is Luke's preferred verb for angelic appearance (cf. 1:11; Acts 12:7; 23:11); it conveys sudden presence rather than gradual approach. doxa kyriou — the glory of the Lord — doxa here is the Shekinah-light of divine presence; the same luminous manifestation that marked the tabernacle and temple. The shepherds' fear is the standard response to holy divine presence.
2.10: euangelizomai hymin charan megalēn — I bring you good news of great joy — euangelizomai is Luke's distinctive verb (used 10 times in Luke, 15 in Acts, rare elsewhere in the Gospels); it is the proclamation-verb of Isa 61:1 (the Servant announcing good news). The dative hymin (to you — the shepherds, present) followed by panti tō laō (for all the people, future/broader) traces the scope: the announcement begins with the marginalized few and extends to all Israel.
2.12: kai touto hymin to sēmeion — and this will be the sign for you — the sēmeion (sign) is striking: not a miraculous portent but a baby in a manger. The sign is the incarnation itself — the lowliness of the setting confirms, rather than contradicts, that this is the messianic Savior. The phatin (manger) as sign echoes the Isaianic servant-sign of the child: the expected messianic triumph comes in unexpected lowliness.
2.13: stratias ouraniou — a heavenly army/host — stratia is the military term (army, host); the genitive ouraniou (heavenly) qualifies the army's origin. The heavenly host both worships (as the angels of God) and stands ready for battle — the dual meaning of the divine army. The praise they offer (v.14) transforms the military assembly into a liturgical choir, though the military connotation remains: the hosts of heaven declare the peace that Christ's coming brings.
2.14: en anthrōpois eudokias — among people of his good pleasure/favor — the genitive eudokias (of good will, of favor) is the crucial textual and theological crux. The earliest and best manuscripts (p75, B, Sinaiticus, A) read eudokias (genitive), making the phrase "peace to people of his good will/favor" — those whom God favors, the recipients of his gracious election. A later reading, eudokia (nominative), yields "peace on earth, good will to men" — making good will the third coordinate gift. The genitive is almost certainly original (lectio difficilior; reflected in Jerome's "bonae voluntatis") and is theologically richer: the peace announced is not universal but directed to those within the sphere of divine grace. The Qumran texts similarly use "men of his good pleasure" (bnei rezono) as a technical term for the covenant community.
2.15: hōs apēlthon ap autōn — when they had gone from them — the aorist genitive absolute apelthontōn (having departed) is a clean narrative transition. The shepherds' immediate response dielthoumen (let us go through — first person plural aorist subjunctive hortatory) signals communal decision and action. Rēma (word, thing) at the end — "this thing that has happened" — uses the theologically loaded word for divine speech/deed throughout Luke-Acts.
2.16: aneuron te tēn Mariam — they found Mary — aneuriskō (find by searching) is a compound form of heuriskō that emphasizes the searching process; they located what they sought by inquiry. Spoudē (haste) in v.16 describes their movement — not reckless urgency but eager intentionality. The discovery confirms the sign: the baby in the manger is exactly as the angel said.
2.17: egnōrisan peri tou rēmatos — they made known the word — gnōrizō (make known, reveal) is used for the public proclamation of what was privately received; the shepherds become the first evangelists, announcing to others what the angel announced to them. The pattern — receive, go, make known — is the basic missionary structure that Luke will develop throughout Acts.
2.18: pantes hoi akousantes — all who heard — Luke emphasizes the crowd's amazed response (ethaumasan — they were amazed, marveled) as the standard human response to the divine breaking into ordinary life. The imperfect ethaumasan (were marveling) suggests ongoing astonishment, not a momentary reaction. The amazement does not necessarily indicate faith; it is the raw material from which faith can grow.
2.19: hē de Mariam panta sunētērei ta rēmata tauta sunballousa en tē kardia autēs — Mary kept all these things and pondered them in her heart — two participles describe Mary's interior response. Suntēreō (keep together, treasure up) suggests active preservation of memory; it appears in Dan 7:28 LXX for Daniel preserving the vision in his heart. Sunballousa (pondering, throwing together) from sunballō (to throw together, compare) suggests the active work of interpretation — Mary is comparing, correlating, and seeking to understand the meaning of what is happening. This verse establishes Mary as the contemplative witness whose perspective shapes Luke's infancy narrative.
2.20: hupestrepsan hoi poimenes — the shepherds returned — hupostrephō (return) is one of Luke's most frequent verbs (32 times in Luke-Acts vs. 3 in Matthew-Mark-John). The return with praise and glory to God is the standard Lukan conclusion to a healing/salvation event: the recipients of grace return to their lives glorifying God. Doxazontes kai ainountes (glorifying and praising) are the paired verbs of worship throughout Luke-Acts.
2.21: hote eplēsthēsan hēmerai oktō tou peritemein auton — when eight days were fulfilled for circumcising him — eplēsthēsan (were fulfilled) is Luke's covenant-completion language; the eighth day circumcision is the Abrahamic covenant sign (Gen 17:12; Lev 12:3). Peritemein (to circumcise, infinitive of peritemnō) places Jesus within the covenant community from infancy. The name Iēsous is given at circumcision as the angel commanded (1:31) — the name and covenant sign are received together.
2.22: hote eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tou katharismou autōn kata ton nomon Mōuseōs — when the days of their purification according to the law of Moses were completed — the katharismos (purification) follows Lev 12:1-8: 40 days after the birth of a son, the mother undergoes a purification rite at the temple. The pronoun autōn (their) is unusual — purification was the mother's rite, not both parents'; some suggest Luke is assimilating the purification to a presentation. Anagagein (to bring up, present) is the presentation vocabulary.
2.23: kathōs gegraptai en nomō kyriou — as it is written in the law of the Lord — the citation follows Exod 13:2, 12 (every firstborn male is holy to the Lord). Luke uses prōtotokon (firstborn) to connect this verse to v.7; the consecration of the firstborn in the law is the legal context for Jesus's presentation. Hagion tō kyriō klēthēsetai (he will be called holy to the Lord) echoes the annunciation language (1:35, hagion klēthēsetai).
2.24: zeugos trugonōn ē duo nossous peristerōn — a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons — the offering is the poor woman's alternative prescribed in Lev 12:8 (when she cannot afford a lamb). Luke's inclusion of this detail establishes the family's economic status — they are among the anawim, the humble poor of Israel — and foreshadows the Magnificat's theme (the humble lifted up). The use of zeugos (yoke, pair) for the birds is precise.
2.25: paraklēsin tou Israēl — the consolation of Israel — paraklēsis is one of Luke's theologically loaded terms: consolation, comfort, encouragement. It echoes Isa 40:1 (the opening of Deutero-Isaiah: "Comfort, comfort my people") — the messianic expectation that God would return to comfort his exiled, suffering people. Simeon's hope is the hope of Isa 40-66 in a single word. pneuma hagion ēn ep auton — the Holy Spirit was upon him — the threefold Spirit-reference in vv.25-27 (upon him, revealed to him, moved by him) structures the Nunc Dimittis scene as a pneumatic event.
2.26: kechrēmatismenon hypo tou pneumatos tou hagiou — divinely revealed/warned by the Holy Spirit — chrēmatizō is the technical term for receiving a divine oracle or divine instruction (used for the Magi's warning in Matt 2:12; for Noah in Heb 11:7); it conveys authoritative divine communication. The content of the revelation — mē idein thanaton prin an idē ton Christon kyriou (he would not see death before seeing the Lord's Christ) — ties Simeon's hope to the specific messianic moment.
2.27: en tō pneumati — in/by the Spirit — the preposition en with pneumati can be instrumental (by the Spirit) or locative (in the sphere of the Spirit); the ambiguity is typically Lukan. Simeon's Spirit-directed movement to the temple and the parents' bringing of Jesus at the same moment is presented as providential coordination — both parties are following separate promptings that converge in the temple.
2.28: edexato auto eis tas agklas autou — he took him up in his arms — agkale (arm, elbow) is the word for the bent arm that cradles a child; the physical gesture of reception is the enacted reception of the Messiah by old Israel. Simeon holding the infant Jesus is the visual image that the Nunc Dimittis makes verbal: he has received the salvation his nation waited for.
2.30: hoti eidon hoi ophthalmoi mou to sōtērion sou — for my eyes have seen your salvation — sōtērion (salvation as a noun/neuter) is Luke's preferred form (also 3:6; Acts 28:28); the phrase echoes Ps 98:2-3 LXX (eidon panta ta perata tēs gēs to sōtērion tou theou hēmōn). The salvation that is seen by the eyes is the infant — salvation is personified in Christ, not merely a concept.
2.31: ho hetoimasas kata prosōpon pantōn tōn laōn — which you have prepared in the presence of all peoples — hetoimazō (prepare) echoes Mal 3:1 and the Baptist's preparation language; YHWH has prepared the salvation in advance. Kata prosōpon pantōn tōn laōn (in the face/presence of all peoples) — the salvation is public, not private or esoteric.
2.32: phōs eis apokalupsin ethnōn — a light for revelation to the Gentiles — apokalupsis (unveiling, revelation) combined with phōs (light) draws on Isa 42:6 and 49:6 (the Servant as light to the nations). Ethnōn (Gentiles/nations) is the first explicit mention of Gentile inclusion in Luke's birth narrative — the scope of salvation transcends Israel. doxan laou sou Israēl — the glory of your people Israel — the salvation is for Israel's glory as well; the Gentile inclusion does not displace Israel's priority but extends the benefit.
2.33: kai ēn ho patēr autou kai hē mētēr thaumazontes — his father and mother were amazed — Luke uses ho patēr autou (his father) for Joseph without theological embarrassment, consistent with the legal/social reality that Joseph is Jesus's father in every sense except biological generation. The amazement (thaumazō) at Simeon's words about Jesus is the same response as the shepherds' audience (v.18) — the appropriate human response to the unexpected scope of the divine plan.
2.34: keimai eis ptōsin kai anastasin pollōn en tō Israēl — set for the fall and rising of many in Israel — ptōsis (fall) and anastasis (rising) are the two responses Jesus will produce in Israel; the same Greek word anastasis is used for both the resurrection and the "rising" here. sēmeion antilegomenon — a sign that is spoken against/contradicted — antilegō (speak against, contradict) anticipates the opposition that will define Jesus's ministry and lead to the cross. The sign that was the silent baby in the manger will become the controversial rabbi and the executed Messiah.
2.35: rhomphaia — a sword — the rhomphaia is the large, curved Thracian sword (as opposed to the shorter machaira); used in LXX for the sword that drove Adam from Eden (Gen 3:24), and the sword of divine judgment throughout the prophets. The sword that will pierce Mary's soul is the sword of grief at the cross — but also, perhaps, the sword of the word itself (Heb 4:12). hopōs an apokalupphthōsin ek pollōn kardiōn dialogismoi — so that thoughts from many hearts will be revealed — dialogismos (reasoning, internal debate) is consistently negative in Luke; the revealing of hearts is the effect of encountering Christ.
2.36: prophetis Hanna — a prophetess, Anna — prophētis (prophetess, feminine form) is the technical term for one who speaks under divine inspiration; Anna is one of the few NT women explicitly named prophets. The genealogical specificity (thugatēr Phanouel ek phylēs Asēr — daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher) is unusual for a woman, suggesting her public significance. The note about her age is textually complex: she had lived with her husband seven years, then was a widow of/for 84 years — placing her age at approximately 105 if widowed at 14, or the 84 years may describe the period of her widowhood as the total.
2.37: nēsteiais kai deēsesin latreuousa nykta kai hēmeran — worshiping with fasting and prayer night and day — latreuō (worship, serve with religious devotion) is the liturgical service term; Anna's fasting and prayer is continuous, structured worship, not occasional piety. The night-and-day formula (nykta kai hēmeran) emphasizes the comprehensive, uninterrupted nature of her devotion — the posture of one wholly consecrated to anticipating the messianic redemption.
2.38: lytrōsin Ierousalēm — the redemption of Jerusalem — lytrōsis (redemption, ransom-release) echoes lytron (ransom) and the LXX release terminology; it is the technical term for the covenant redemption of God's people (Ps 111:9 LXX). The phrase "redemption of Jerusalem" situates Anna's hope in the nationalprophetic expectation (Isa 52:9 — "for the LORD has redeemed Jerusalem") that the city's exile condition would end with the Messiah's coming.
2.39: hōs etelesan panta ta kata ton nomon kyriou — when they had performed all things according to the law of the Lord — Luke's telos-language (teleoō = complete, fulfill) here describes legal compliance rather than prophetic fulfillment, but the vocabulary overlap is deliberate. The family's complete Torah observance at the birth establishes Jesus's continuity with the covenant community even as his identity transcends it.
2.40: to de paidion ēuxanen kai ekrataiouto plēroumenon sophia — the child grew and became strong, filled with wisdom — auxanō (grow) and krataioomai (become strong) are Luke's standard developmental verbs (also 1:80 for John); both are imperfects, indicating gradual progress over time. plēroumenon sophia — filled with wisdom — plēroō (fill, fulfill) with sophia (wisdom) describes the progressive increase of wisdom in the growing child; the divine wisdom fills him as he grows into his human capacities. Charis theou ēn ep auton — the grace of God was upon him — the same formula as Elizabeth's womb-grace for John (1:66); divine favor rests on the child continuously.
2.41: kai eporeuonto hoi goneis autou kat etos eis Ierousalem — his parents went to Jerusalem every year — kata etos (year by year, annually) describes the established practice of Passover pilgrimage. The Deuteronomic law required three annual festivals (Deut 16:16) but Passover was the primary requirement observed by devout families. Luke's specification of the parents' annual practice establishes the piety context for the temple episode and shows that Jesus grew up within regular Passover observance.
2.42: hote egeneto etōn dōdeka — when he was twelve years old — the age of 12 is significant in Jewish practice as approaching bar mitzvah responsibility (technically 13 in rabbinic tradition, but the approach to legal adulthood began earlier). Luke specifies this age because it marks the threshold of religious majority at which a boy begins to take personal responsibility for Torah observance. This is Jesus's first appearance as a speaking actor in the narrative.
2.43: hupestreyen ho pais Iēsous en Ierousalēm — the boy Jesus remained in Jerusalem — hypomenō (remain behind, stay) rather than hupostrephō (return) signals that Jesus did not return with the caravan; he stayed deliberately. Hoi goneis autou ouk egnōsan (his parents did not know) — the unknown is their not knowing that he stayed, not a mistake on Jesus's part. They assumed he was in the synōdia (traveling company, caravan) — the large group of Galilean pilgrims traveling together.
2.44: nomisantes de auton einai en tē synōdia — supposing him to be in the group — synōdia (company, caravan, traveling group) is a hapax in the NT; the compound (syn = with + hodos = road, journey) describes the pilgrimage group. The assumption that Jesus was somewhere in the group is reasonable — in large caravan travel, extended family and acquaintances would spread across the group. A day's journey would have carried them well away from Jerusalem before the absence was noticed.
2.45: anastraphentes de anezētoun auton — when they did not find him, they returned, searching — anazēteō (search for, seek up and down) is an intensive compound of zēteō (seek); the ana- prefix suggests thorough searching in every direction. The three days of searching (one day traveling, one returning, one day in Jerusalem) before finding him in the temple is Luke's only narrative that places Jesus in a human learning environment.
2.46: en tō hierō kathezomenon en mesō tōn didaskalōn — sitting in the temple among the teachers — didaskālos (teacher, doctor of the law) in the plural indicates the professional scribal teachers who taught in the temple courts. Kathezomenon en mesō (sitting in the middle) is the posture of a student participating in instruction — sitting on the floor among the teachers (who sat on chairs or benches, the elevated teaching position). The teachers are asking questions and he is answering; but the direction of inquiry suggests that both parties are engaged in the Socratic dialogue of Jewish learning.
2.47: existanto de pantes hoi akouontes autou epi tē sunēsei kai tais apokrisesin autou — all who heard him were astonished at his understanding and his answers — existēmi (stand outside, be astonished — from which we get ekstasis) describes the teachers' reaction to his answers. Sunesis (understanding, comprehension) is the cognitive intelligence; apokrisis (answer, response) is the verbal expression. The astonishment of the professional teachers is not patronizing (smart child!) but genuine recognition of exceptional insight.
2.48: odunōmenoi — anguished — the word is one of Luke's strongest emotional terms (used for the rich man's anguish in Hades at 16:24-25; for Paul's associates's grief at Acts 20:38). Mary's report uses the parental first-person dual — ho patēr sou kagō (your father and I) — with Joseph named as father; Luke does not soften the parental perspective to exclude Joseph.
2.49: dei me einai en tois tou patros mou — I must be in my Father's things/house — the crucial phrase is tois tou patros mou (the things/house of my Father). The Greek phrase is deliberately ambiguous: ta tou patros can mean "my Father's house" (oikos being understood), "my Father's affairs/business," or "the things that belong to my Father." Early translations diverged: the Vulgate has in his quae Patris mei sunt (in the things that are my Father's); English versions split between "house" and "business/affairs." The ambiguity is productive — in the temple he is in his Father's house AND doing his Father's business. dei — must, it is necessary — the divine necessity (dei) that governs Jesus's mission throughout Luke (4:43; 9:22; 13:33; 17:25; 24:7) appears here at its first speaking: his entire life is shaped by this necessity.
2.50: kai autoi ou sunēkan to rēma — and they did not understand the word — their failure to understand (ou sunēkan, from suniēmi — to understand, put together) establishes a pattern that runs throughout Luke: those closest to Jesus repeatedly fail to grasp the meaning of his words, especially those concerning the dei of his mission. Understanding comes only after the resurrection (24:45).
2.51: kai katebē met autōn kai ēlthen eis Nazareth kai ēn hypotassomenos autois — he went down with them and came to Nazareth and was subject/submitting to them — hypotassō (subject, subordinate, arrange under) in the present participle (hypotassomenos) describes an ongoing, voluntary self-subordination of the twelve-year-old who has just spoken of divine necessity. The one who must be in his Father's affairs voluntarily places himself under parental authority — the same paradox of the incarnation that Phil 2:6-8 articulates theologically. Hē de mētēr autou dietērei panta ta rēmata tauta en tē kardia autēs — his mother treasured all these things in her heart — the second Mary-treasuring notice (cf. 2:19), bookending the temple episode.
2.52: Iēsous proekopten en tē sophia kai hēlikia kai chariti para theō kai anthrōpois — Jesus advanced in wisdom and stature and favor with God and men — prokoptō (advance, make progress, cut a path forward) is an imperfect indicating gradual development over time. The fourfold description — wisdom (sophia), age/stature (hēlikia), grace with God (charis para theō), grace with people (charis para anthrōpois) — is modeled on 1 Sam 2:26 (Samuel grew in favor with both God and men). Luke presents Jesus's development as a genuine human growth process, consistent with the incarnation; the one who had divine wisdom grew in its human expression.

3.22: sy ei ho huios mou ho agapetos, en soi eudokesa — you are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased — Luke's baptism declaration is identical to Mark's; a variant reading (D manuscript) gives you are my Son, today I have begotten you, quoting Ps 2:7 directly. The standard text combines Ps 2:7 (my Son) with Gen 22:2 (agapetos = only/beloved son) and Isa 42:1 (with you I am well pleased = my chosen one in whom my soul delights). The triplicity of the title signals the depth of the Christological identification.
3.1: en etei de pentekaidekaton tēs hēgemonian Tiberiou Kaisaros — in the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar — Luke's synchronistic dating, using six concurrent rulers, is the most elaborate historical synchronism in the NT and follows Thucydidean historiographical method. The fifteenth year of Tiberius (who began sole reign in 14 CE) places John's ministry at approximately 28-29 CE. The list: Tiberius (emperor), Pilate (prefect of Judea), Herod Antipas (tetrarch of Galilee), Philip (tetrarch of Ituraea-Trachonitis), Lysanias (tetrarch of Abilene), Annas and Caiaphas (high priests). The two high priests reflect the political reality that Rome had replaced the Hasmonean-era single high priest with appointees.
3.2: egeneto rēma theou epi Iōannēn — the word of God came upon John — ginomai rēma theou epi is the standard LXX formula for prophetic commissioning (cf. Jer 1:1-2; Ezek 1:3; Hos 1:1; Joel 1:1; Mic 1:1); Luke presents John's calling as formally equivalent to the classical OT prophets. En tē erēmō (in the wilderness) is the prophetic staging location — John is commissioned in the wilderness, the same space as Moses at the burning bush and Elijah at Horeb.
3.3: baptisma metanoias eis aphesin hamartiōn — a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins — the phrase is dense: baptisma (a dipping, submersion — not merely sprinkling); metanoia (change of mind/orientation, turning — from meta = after/change + nous = mind; in LXX it translates shuv, the covenantal turning-back to God); eis aphesin hamartiōn (for/unto the release/forgiveness of sins — aphesis from aphiēmi = to release, let go, forgive). The preposition eis is hotly debated: it can indicate purpose (for the purpose of forgiveness) or result (resulting in forgiveness), affecting whether the baptism is a sign of a prior repentance or an instrument of forgiveness.
3.4: phōnē boōntos en tē erēmō — a voice of one crying in the wilderness — the LXX text of Isa 40:3 that Luke quotes positions the comma differently from the MT: "a voice of one crying: in the wilderness prepare the way" vs. "a voice of one crying in the wilderness: prepare the way." Luke follows the LXX, connecting the voice to the wilderness (John is in the wilderness, v.2) rather than the way. Ktl: Luke's Isaiah citation (40:3-5) is the longest of the Synoptic quotations of this passage, extending to "all flesh will see the salvation of God" — the Gentile-inclusive conclusion that Matthew and Mark omit.
3.5: pasa pharagx plērōthēsetai kai pan oros kai bounos tapeinōthēsetai — every valley will be filled, every mountain and hill brought low — the quotation continues Isa 40:4-5 in LXX form; Luke extends the citation to include the universalist conclusion. The road-building imagery (eutheias = straight, tracheias = rough) is Isaiah's metaphor for divine preparation of the way for the returning glory of YHWH. The straightening and leveling is both literal (road construction for royal processions) and metaphorical (the removal of obstacles to divine visitation).
3.6: kai opsetai pasa sarx to sōtērion tou theou — and all flesh will see the salvation of God — pasa sarx (all flesh) is the universalist formula for humanity without ethnic distinction; sōtērion (salvation, noun form) rather than sōtēria is Luke's preferred form (also 2:30; Acts 28:28). Luke's extension of the Isaiah citation to this verse is programmatic: his Gospel and Acts will trace the fulfillment of the "all flesh" scope of salvation. The salvation is to be seen (opsetai) — Luke's emphasis on witnesses who see is characteristic (1:2; Acts 1:8).
3.7: genēmata echidnōn — offspring/brood of vipers — the noun genēmata (things born, brood) from ginomai rather than gennēmata from gennaō (to beget); the distinction is between "what has been born" and "what has been begotten." Some manuscripts have gennēmata (cf. Matt 3:7). Echidna (viper) is one of the most venomous snakes in the Levant; the address "offspring of vipers" carries the resonance of the serpent of Gen 3 — John is accusing the crowd of being offspring of the ancient adversary. Tis hypedeicen hymin phugein — who warned you to flee — hypodeiknymi (show under, warn) is used of prophetic or divine warning (cf. Acts 9:16; 20:35).
3.8: poiēsate oun karpous axious tēs metanoias — therefore produce fruits worthy of repentance — the agricultural metaphor of fruit as evidence of genuine inner change is standard in prophetic rhetoric (Isa 5:1-7; Jer 17:8). The command is plural (poiēsate — all of you, collectively) addressed to the crowd. mē arxēsthe legein en heautois — do not begin to say to yourselves — archomai with the infinitive is a Lukan idiom for beginning an action; the warning not to begin a thought implies that this rationalizing has already started. ek tōn lithōn toutōn — from these stones — the possible Aramaic wordplay (avanim/stones = banim/sons) works in Aramaic but not Greek, suggesting an underlying Aramaic tradition.
3.9: ēdē de kai hē axinē pros tēn rhizan tōn dendrōn keitai — even now the axe is laid to the root of the trees — ēdē (even now, already) creates urgency; the axe is not yet swinging but is positioned. Axinē (axe) is the chopping implement for felling trees, used as a judgment metaphor throughout the prophets (Isa 10:15; Jer 46:22). The fruit/root/axe metaphor is coherent: trees that produce no fruit have their root-existence threatened. Pan de dendron mē poioun karpon kalon — every tree not bearing good fruit — the singular universal (pan... mē) addresses each hearer individually.
3.10: kai epērōtōn auton hoi ochloi — the crowds were asking him — epērōtaō (ask, question) combined with the imperfect (epērōtōn) suggests repeated or ongoing questioning — the crowd continues to ask. The three-group structure (crowd, tax collectors, soldiers) gives John's ethical instruction a social breadth that covers the economic (sharing), the systemic (tax justice), and the coercive (military/police power) dimensions of the community's life.
3.11: ho echōn duo chitōnas metadotō tō mē echonti — the one who has two tunics, let him share with the one who has none — chitōn (inner garment, tunic) is the basic garment worn under the outer himation; to have two was modest prosperity, to have none was poverty. Metadidōmi (share, give a portion from one's own) is Luke's verb for practical generosity (Rom 1:11; Eph 4:28). The food instruction (brōmata = food) follows the same pattern: share from what you have. John's ethical teaching is not revolutionary redistribution but basic covenant solidarity.
3.12: elthon de kai telōnai baptisthēnai — tax collectors also came to be baptized — telōnēs (tax collector) describes those who bid for the right to collect imperial taxes in a region and were despised as collaborators with Rome and as systemically dishonest. Their coming for baptism signals that John's call includes those outside the social-religious mainstream. Didaskalos, ti poiēsōmen — teacher, what shall we do — the honorific didaskalos (teacher) from the tax collectors signals their genuine inquiry.
3.13: mēden pleon para to diatetagmenon hymin prassete — collect nothing more than what you have been authorized — diatetagmenon (ordered, authorized — perfect passive participle of diatassō) refers to the officially sanctioned collection rate. Tax collectors regularly charged above the official rate and pocketed the difference; John commands ethical compliance with the legal framework. He does not condemn the profession but its abuse — a notable moderation compared to the revolutionary expectations he might have encouraged.
3.14: mēdena diaseiēte mēde sykophantēsēte — do not intimidate anyone or extort falsely — diaseiō (shake thoroughly, intimidate) is specifically the extortion by force or threat; sykophantēsēte (from sykophantēs) denotes false accusation for personal gain, extortion by false charges. The soldiers' ethics (strateuomenoi = serving as soldiers, possibly including police/enforcement roles) are addressed with the same concrete specificity as the tax collectors. Arkeisthe tois opsōniois hymin — be content with your wages — opsōnion (soldier's pay, rations) is military pay vocabulary.
3.15: prosdokōntos de tou laou kai dialogizomenōn pantōn en tais kardiais autōn peri tou Iōannou — while the people were in expectation, all reasoning in their hearts about John — prosdokaō (expect, await) describes the messianic anticipation that John's ministry had awakened; the people were wondering whether he himself was the Christ. Dialogizomai en kardiais (reason in hearts) is Luke's phrase for the interior deliberation that is not yet spoken aloud (cf. 1:29; 5:22; 12:17).
3.16: baptizō hymas hydati, erchetai de ho ischuroteros mou — I baptize you with water, but one who is mightier than I is coming — the contrast is water/pneumati hagiō kai puri (Holy Spirit and fire). The single preposition en governing both pneumati hagiō and puri (Holy Spirit and fire) suggests these are one unified baptism rather than two separate ones — the Spirit-fire baptism is the single eschatological event. Ou ouk eimi hikanos lysai ton himanta tōn hypodēmatōn autou — whose sandal strap I am not worthy to untie — the untying of sandals was the task of the lowest slave; John places himself below even slave rank relative to the Coming One.
3.17: hou to ptuon en tē cheiri autou — whose winnowing fork is in his hand — ptuon (winnowing fork, shovel) is the flat wooden implement used to toss threshed grain into the wind so the lighter chaff is blown away and the heavier grain falls back. The threshing floor (alōn) metaphor is a standard prophetic judgment image (Isa 41:16; Jer 15:7; Mic 4:12). Achuron (chaff) vs. siton (wheat/grain) — the separation of chaff and grain is the judgment that the Coming One will execute. Puri asbstō — unquenchable fire — the eschatological fire of judgment.
3.18: polla men oun kai hetera parakalōn euēngelizeto ton laon — with many other exhortations he was preaching good news to the people — parakalōn (exhorting, encouraging — present participle of parakaleō) is one of Luke's favorite verbs for the proclamation that calls to action. Euēngelizeto (imperfect of euangelizō) — Luke's distinctive proclamation-verb characterizes even John's preaching as "good news" proclamation, though the content is primarily repentance and warning rather than kingdom arrival.
3.19: ho de Hērōdēs ho tetrarchēs — Herod the tetrarch — Luke introduces John's imprisonment prolepticly (before the baptism narrative is complete) to clear the Herodian complication from the narrative. Elenkhomenos hyp autou (being reproved by him) — the participle of elenchō (reprove, expose) describes John's confrontation of Herod over Herodias (his brother Philip's wife, Lev 18:16) and his other crimes (ponēria).
3.20: prosēthēken kai touto epi pasin — he added this also to all of them — the phrase frames Herod's imprisonment of John as the culmination of a series of ponērias (evil deeds) that Luke summarizes without detailing. The proleptic imprisonment notice removes Herod from the baptism scene — Luke will not have Herod present at the baptism as Matthew and Mark do not either.
3.21: en tō baptisthēnai hapanta ton laon kai Iēsou baptisthentos kai proseuchomenou — when all the people were being baptized and when Jesus was baptized and was praying — three notable Lukan features: (1) Jesus's baptism is subordinated grammatically (Iēsou baptisthentos — aorist passive participle, genitive absolute, not the main verb); (2) the main verb is proseuchomenou (while praying — present participle); (3) the heavens open specifically in response to prayer.
