Luke's prayer emphasis places the baptism-theophany within Jesus's prayer life — the revelation comes while he prays.
3.23: hōsei etōn triakonta archomenos — about thirty years old when he began — hōsei (about, approximately) is Luke's qualification that this is not a precise number; thirty was the traditional age of priestly service (Num 4:3) and of Davidic kingship (2 Sam 5:4). Archomenos (beginning — present participle, perhaps nominative absolute) signals the inception of the public ministry. Hōs enomizeto (as was supposed/thought) — the passive of nomizō (think customary, suppose) signals that what follows is the legal genealogy, not the biological one.
3.24: Luke's genealogy runs from Jesus back to Adam in ascending order (unlike Matthew's descending from Abraham), which serves Luke's universal theological purpose: he is tracing humanity's origin, not merely Israel's Davidic line. The names between Joseph and Zerubbabel differ significantly from Matthew's list, which has generated extensive discussion; the most likely explanation is that Luke records Mary's biological line (through Heli = Mary's father) while Matthew records Joseph's legal Davidic line — the genealogy is Joseph's in both cases legally, but Luke's biological connections may trace Mary's ancestry through different branches.
3.25: Luke's genealogy from Mattathias through Cosam and Addi covers the post-exilic period with names not found in the OT or Matthew, suggesting access to tribal/family records preserved into the Second Temple period. The genealogical precision — with 77 names from Adam to Jesus (symbolic of completeness, 7×11) — indicates that Luke is presenting a carefully structured theological genealogy, not merely a historical list.
3.26: The name sequence through Maath, Mattathias, and Semein in Luke's genealogy is part of the post-exilic segment that diverges from Matthew. Luke's genealogy through Nathan (son of David, v.31) rather than Solomon (Matthew's line) may reflect the use of 2 Sam 5:14 / 1 Chr 3:5; Zechariah's prophecy that the house of Nathan would mourn (Zech 12:12) is sometimes cited as relevant to the messianic line.
3.27: Luke's genealogy passes through Zerubbabel (the Davidic governor who led the return from exile) and Shealtiel — names also in Matthew's genealogy at this point — before diverging again. The convergence at Zerubbabel/Shealtiel is the one consistent point where the two genealogies agree after David; from here both traditions required connection to the Davidic line through the post-exilic community.
3.28: Luke's genealogy continues its careful enumeration through the Davidic line; the names in this portion (Cosam, Addi, Melchi, etc.) are not cross-referrable to OT records, which is consistent with post-exilic family records that were maintained within priestly and royal families for purposes of covenant inheritance claims.
3.29: The continuation through Elmadam, Er, and Joshua in Luke's genealogy maintains the careful tracing of the Davidic line. Luke's genealogy contains 77 names from Adam to Jesus — a number that is likely deliberate (seven sets of eleven, or seven being the number of completeness), reflecting Luke's theological shaping of the historical material he received.
3.30: Luke's genealogy through Simeon, Levi, and Matthat covers the pre-exilic portion with additional Davidic line names. The presence of Levi and Simeon as ancestors of Jesus (not the patriarchs but later figures) is consistent with the common practice of naming children after famous ancestors; multiple generations might share the same names, creating the appearance of conflation with earlier figures.
3.31: tou Nathan tou Dauid — son of Nathan, son of David — Luke's genealogy traces the Davidic descent through Nathan (2 Sam 5:14; 1 Chr 3:5) rather than Solomon (Matthew's line). Both Nathan and Solomon were sons of David by Bathsheba; the divergence at this point explains the different name lists in the two genealogies. Zechariah 12:12 mentions the "house of Nathan" separately, and this Nathan-branch was a recognized alternative Davidic line.
3.32: tou Iessai tou Iōbēd tou Boos — son of Jesse, son of Obed, son of Boaz — this section of Luke's genealogy converges with Matthew's (and with Ruth 4:17-22): Jesse-Obed-Boaz-Salmon. Both genealogies must pass through the same pre-Davidic figures; the differences between Matthew and Luke are concentrated in the post-Davidic and post-exilic periods. Boaz and Ruth's union (the Moabitess) is the Gentile inclusion precedent in the Davidic ancestry.
3.33: tou Aminadab tou Admin tou Arni — Luke's genealogy includes Arni (not in Matthew) between Hezron and Aminadab; Matthew has Ram (= Arni in Greek transliteration of the same Hebrew name) directly. The slight variations in transliteration of Hebrew names between Matthew and Luke reflect independent Greek translations of the same Aramaic/Hebrew sources.
3.34: tou Iakōb tou Isaak tou Abraam — son of Jacob, son of Isaac, son of Abraham — Luke reaches the patriarchs at this point; from here to Adam the genealogy follows the LXX Septuagint genealogies (Gen 5; 11). Matthew stops at Abraham; Luke continues to Adam and God, serving his universal theological purpose. The three patriarchs are the covenant foundation — the promises to Abraham, confirmed through Isaac and Jacob, now reach their fulfillment in Jesus.
3.35: Luke's genealogy continues through the pre-Abrahamic patriarchs in descending order: Peleg, Reu, Serug, Nahor, Terah — the Table of Nations and primeval genealogy of Gen 11:10-26. Luke follows the LXX enumeration, which adds Cainan (son of Arphaxad) in v.36 — a name present in the LXX of Gen 11:12-13 but absent from the MT.
3.36: tou Kainam tou Arphaxad — son of Cainan, son of Arphaxad — the Cainan between Shem and Arphaxad is present in the LXX of Gen 11:12-13 and Luke's genealogy follows this reading. The MT lacks Cainan here; the LXX addition (possibly from Gen 10:24) creates a textual difference between the Hebrew and Greek OT. Luke's use of the LXX Cainan is consistent with his broader dependence on the LXX for OT citations throughout Luke-Acts.
3.37: Luke's genealogy continues through the ante-diluvian patriarchs: Methuselah, Enoch, Jared, Mahalaleel, Cainan — following Gen 5 in LXX order. Enoch is particularly significant: Gen 5:24 notes that "Enoch walked with God and was no more, because God took him away" — a cryptic reference to translation without death that generated extensive Second Temple literature (1 Enoch, 2 Enoch). The presence of Enoch in the lineage of Jesus connects the eschatological traditions of Enoch's translation to the ultimate eschatological figure.
3.38: tou Sēth tou Adam tou theou — son of Seth, son of Adam, son of God — Luke's genealogy ends where it began, with the divine: the son of Adam, son of God that closes the genealogy frames Jesus as the completion of what Adam began. Adam was the son of God by creation; Jesus is the Son of God by eternal generation and incarnation. Where Adam failed the sonship (the temptation narrative immediately follows in 4:1-13), Jesus will succeed. The genealogy that began with "son of God" (in the baptism voice, 3:22) ends with "son of God" (the first man's relationship to his Creator) — the inclusio structures Luke's understanding of what the incarnation accomplishes: the restoration of humanity's original relationship to God, realized in the Son who is both the Father's eternal Son and Adam's descendant.

4.18: pneuma kyriou ep eme hou heneken echrisen me — the Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me — the Nazareth synagogue reading is the programmatic Christological text for Luke's Gospel. Echrisen (anointed) shares the root with Christos; Jesus identifies himself as the Spirit-anointed one of Isa 61. The four infinitives that follow specify the anointing's purpose: euangelisasthai ptochois (proclaim good news to the poor), keruxai aichmalotos aphesin (announce release to captives), aposteilai tethrausmenous en aphesei (send away the broken in release), keruxai eniauton kyriou dekton (announce the year of the Lord's favor = the Jubilee).
4.21: semeron peplerothe he graphe haute en tois osin hymon — today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing — the adverb semeron (today) is Luke's characteristic eschatological-present marker (also 19:9 salvation came today; 23:43 today you will be with me in paradise). The fulfillment is not a future event Jesus predicts but a present reality he declares happening as he speaks; the scripture's fulfillment is coterminous with his proclamation.
4.1: pleres pneumatos hagiou — full of the Holy Spirit — Luke adds pleres (full) to Mark's simpler account; the same adjective describes Stephen (Acts 6:5) and Barnabas (Acts 11:24), making Spirit-fullness a mark of God's commissioned agents. ēgeto en tō pneumati — was led by the Spirit — the passive ēgeto (imperfect, ongoing action) stresses that the wilderness journey is Spirit-driven, not a retreat. The eremos (wilderness/desert) echoes Israel's forty-year desert testing (Num 14:33; Deut 8:2).
4.2: hemeras tesserakonta — forty days — the number recapitulates Israel's forty-year wilderness test (Deut 8:2) and Moses's forty-day fast on Sinai (Exod 34:28). peirazomenos hypo tou diabolou — being tempted by the devil — diabolos (slanderer, accuser) is Luke's preferred term (vs. satanas in Mk 1:13); it carries the forensic overtone of an adversarial prosecutor. The imperfect peirazomenos indicates ongoing pressure throughout the forty days, not just the three climactic exchanges that follow.
4.3: ei huios ei tou theou — if you are the Son of God — the conditional is first class in Greek, assuming the reality of the premise; the devil does not doubt the title but uses it to direct what the Son of God should do. eipe tō lithō toutō hina genētai artos — command this stone to become bread — the test targets trust in God's provision; Israel complained about lack of bread in the wilderness (Exod 16:3; Num 11:4-6). The singular stone contrasts with Matthew's plural "these stones" (Mt 4:3), focusing the personal challenge.
4.4: gegraptai hoti ouk ep artō monō zesetai ho anthrōpos — it is written: man shall not live on bread alone — Jesus cites Deut 8:3 from memory, the passage about YHWH's provision of manna and the purpose of the forty-year desert: to teach Israel to trust God's word over physical appetite. Luke's version omits Matthew's continuation ("but by every word that comes from the mouth of God"), keeping the focus on the sufficiency of the written word as a counter to the devil's premise. Gegraptai (it is written, perfect passive) frames scripture as settled, authoritative, and present-tense binding.
4.5: anagagōn auton edeixen autō pasas tas basileias tēs oikoumenēs — leading him up, he showed him all the kingdoms of the inhabited world — oikoumenē (the inhabited world, the Roman empire's self-description) implies political totality; Luke's version omits the specific "very high mountain" of Matthew. The display is instantaneous (en stigmē chronou = in a moment of time), suggesting visionary or supernatural perception rather than geographic vantage. Luke places this temptation second (Matthew places it third), centering the political-power temptation before the temple-leap.
4.6: soi dōsō tēn exousian tautēn hapasan kai tēn doxan autōn, hoti emoi paradedotai — I will give you all this authority and their glory, because it has been handed over to me — the devil claims delegated authority over the kingdoms: paradedotai (perfect passive, has been handed over) implies YHWH's permission, not self-seizure. This theological datum appears only in Luke; it frames earthly political power as operating within a sphere the devil administers — a sobering commentary on the nature of worldly authority. Exousia (authority) and doxa (glory) are the two components of what Gentile rulers pursued; the devil offers both.
4.7: sy oun ean proskunēsēs enōpion emou, estai sou pasa — if therefore you worship before me, it will all be yours — proskuneō (worship, do obeisance, prostrate) is the term used for proper worship of YHWH and of Jesus in Luke-Acts; the devil demands the same posture. The offer short-circuits the cross: all authority without suffering. The irony Luke's audience perceives: Jesus will receive all authority (Mt 28:18; Acts 1:8) — but through death and resurrection, not demonic bargain.
4.8: gegraptai, Kyrion ton theon sou proskunēseis kai autō monō latreuseis — it is written: you will worship the Lord your God, and him only will you serve — Deut 6:13 (LXX), from the Shema context (Deut 6:4-15). Latreuseis (you will serve, do cultic service) is the term for Levitical temple service; exclusive liturgical devotion to YHWH is the counterpart to exclusive political authority offered by the devil. The citation repudiates the offer by grounding authority in worship rather than negotiation.
4.9: hēgagen de auton eis Ierousalem kai estēsen epi to pterygion tou hierou — he led him to Jerusalem and set him on the pinnacle of the temple — Luke's final temptation is in Jerusalem, not in Galilee (reversing Matthew's order), which serves Luke's theological geography: Jerusalem is the climax of every movement in his narrative (the Gospel ends with the disciples remaining in Jerusalem; Acts begins there). Pterygion tou hierou (pinnacle of the temple) likely refers to the southeast corner of Herod's temple complex where the Royal Stoa met the Kidron Valley far below.
4.10: tois angelois autou enteleistai peri sou tou diaphylaxai se — he will command his angels concerning you to guard you — Ps 91:11-12 quoted by the devil as the basis for a trust-test: if Jesus is the Son of God, God's own promise guarantees protection from fall. The irony is that Ps 91 is a wisdom psalm about the one who trusts YHWH in genuine danger; the devil weaponizes it to encourage manufactured danger. The text of the citation is verbatim LXX except for the omission of "in all your ways" (Ps 91:11b).
4.11: kai hoti epi cheirōn arousin se, mēpote proskopsēs pros lithon ton poda sou — and they will lift you up on their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone — Ps 91:12, completing the citation. The imagery is angelic protection: divine messengers sustaining the trusting one from stumbling. The devil's inversion: what the psalm celebrates as God's care for the endangered believer is here reframed as justification for self-induced catastrophe. Deliberate misreading of scripture is the third temptation's method.
4.12: kai apokritheis eipen autō ho Iēsous hoti eirētai, Ouk ekpeiraseis Kyrion ton theon sou — Jesus answered and said to him: it is said, you shall not put the Lord your God to the test — Deut 6:16 (LXX), citing Israel's testing of YHWH at Massah/Meribah (Exod 17:1-7) where they demanded miraculous water as proof of God's presence. Ekpeiraseis (you will test, put to the test) is the compound form of peirazō, intensified by the prefix. Jesus refuses to weaponize God's faithfulness as a safety-net for manufactured crises.
4.13: kai syntelesas panta peirasmon ho diabolos apestē ap autou achri kairou — having completed every temptation, the devil departed from him until an opportune time — syntelesas panta peirasmon (having finished all the temptation) — Luke's phrase implies these three exchanges were representative of a comprehensive testing, not exhaustive in themselves. Achri kairou (until an opportune time, an appointed moment) is Lukan foreshadowing: in 22:3 Satan enters Judas and the Passion begins. The wilderness conflict is temporarily suspended, not resolved; the cross will be the decisive engagement.
4.14: kai hypestrepsen ho Iēsous en tē dynamei tou pneumatos eis tēn Galilaian — Jesus returned to Galilee in the power of the Spirit — dynamis (power) is Luke's key word for the Spirit's operative force (cf. 1:35; Acts 1:8; 10:38). The Spirit who led Jesus into the wilderness now empowers his Galilean ministry; the forty-day testing produced not defeat but intensified spiritual authority. phēmē exēlthen kath holēs tēs perichōrou — news went out through the whole surrounding countryside — phēmē (reputation, fame) spreads from first-hand witness before Jesus himself explains anything.
4.15: kai autos edidasken en tais synagōgais autōn doxazomenos hypo pantōn — and he was teaching in their synagogues, being glorified by all — the imperfect edidasken (was teaching) indicates repeated synagogue teaching across multiple towns; doxazomenos (being glorified, praised) reflects the audience's positive initial reception before the Nazareth controversy. The synagogue circuit (cf. Mt 4:23; Mk 1:39) is Jesus's primary early platform: public reading and exposition of Torah within the covenant community.
4.16: kai ēlthen eis Nazara, hou ēn tethrammenos — he came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up — tethrammenos (perfect passive participle of trephō, to nurture, raise) emphasizes Nazareth as the site of Jesus's formation, not just his birthplace. Luke places this synagogue scene at the opening of the Galilean ministry (vs. Mark 6:1-6, which places it after many miracles); it functions as the programmatic statement of Jesus's mission before it plays out. kata to eiōthos autō — as was his custom — Jesus's synagogue attendance is habitual, not occasional; he is a Torah-observant Jew.
4.17: kai epedothē autō biblion tou prophētou Ēsaiou — the scroll of the prophet Isaiah was handed to him — the biblion (scroll, book) was the Isaiah scroll of the synagogue. The attendant (hyperetēs) would hand the scroll to the reader; the reader would stand to read and sit to expound. Luke notes that Jesus unrolled it (anaptuxas, unrolled/opened) and found (heuren) the place — a deliberate search, not random selection. The passage is Isa 61:1-2 with an insertion from Isa 58:6 ("to set the oppressed free").
4.19: kēruxai eniauton Kyriou dekton — to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor — the Jubilee year (Lev 25:8-55) is the economic-social institution in which debts are cancelled, slaves freed, and alienated land restored. Jesus does not announce a calendar Jubilee but the eschatological reality the Jubilee pointed toward: the age of God's acceptance and restorative mercy. Dekton (acceptable, favored, welcomed) is the quality of the era; Luke's theology of time presents this as semeron (today, v.21) — the present moment of proclamation is the era of favor.
4.20: ptuxas to biblion apodous tō hyperetē ekathisen — rolling up the scroll and giving it back to the attendant, he sat down — the seated posture was the authoritative teaching position for rabbis and synagogue teachers (cf. Mt 5:1; 23:2; Lk 5:3); standing was for public reading. The transition from standing reader to seated teacher signals that exposition is about to begin. kai pantōn hoi ophthalmoi en tē synagōgē ēsan atenizovtes autō — the eyes of all in the synagogue were fixed on him — atenizō (to fix one's eyes on, gaze intently) is a Lukan word for concentrated attention (Acts 1:10; 3:4; 6:15).
4.22: kai pantes emartyroyn autō kai ethaumazon epi tois logois tēs charitos — all bore witness to him and were amazed at the gracious words coming from his mouth — hoi logoi tēs charitos (the words of grace/gift) is unusual; it may mean words of gracious content (announcing favor) or words spoken with charis (rhetorical grace). The initial reception is positive — amazement — before the challenge. ouchi huios estin Iōsēph houtos — is this not Joseph's son? — the rhetorical question marks the audience's frame for Jesus: a hometown craftsman's son whose social standing is known. The question is incredulous, not hostile yet.
4.23: pantōs ereite moi tēn parabolēn tautēn, Iatre, therapeusen seauton — surely you will say to me this proverb: Physician, heal yourself — Jesus anticipates the Nazareth crowd's demand with a proverb known from Greek and Semitic sources (cf. b. Sanhedrin 17a). The implicit challenge: demonstrate at home what you have done elsewhere. Hosa ēkousamen genomena eis tēn Kapharnaoum, poiēson kai hōde — whatever we heard happened in Capernaum, do also here — the Nazareth audience has heard about Capernaum miracles (though Luke has not yet narrated them); the hometown crowd expects privileged access to divine power.
4.24: oudeis prophētēs dektos estin en tē patridi autou — no prophet is accepted in his hometown — dektos (acceptable, welcome) echoes v.19 ("the year of the Lord's favor/acceptance"); the Jubilee-favor pronounced on the poor is withheld from the prophet by his own town. This is not a proverb Jesus merely cites but a pattern he identifies as programmatic for his own ministry; prophets from Moses to Elijah faced rejection from Israel. Luke's amēn legō hymin (truly I tell you) marks the saying as solemn declaration.
4.25: pollai chērai ēsan en tais hēmerais Ēliou — there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah — Jesus cites 1 Kgs 17-18 to make the scandalous point that divine mercy bypassed the covenant people and went to a Gentile. The three-and-a-half-year drought (cf. Jas 5:17) created mass famine. Chērai (widows) are the paradigmatic vulnerable poor of the OT; their plight during the drought was Israel's judgment situation, yet Elijah was sent to none of them.
4.26: all epi Sarepta tēs Sidōnias pros gynaika chēran — but to Zarephath of Sidon, to a widow woman — Sarepta is in Phoenician territory, outside Israel's borders; the widow there is a Gentile. This is not a minor detail: YHWH deliberately bypassed the many needy Israelite widows and directed Elijah to a foreign woman. Luke uses this OT precedent to legitimize Jesus's ministry reaching beyond ethnic Israel — a theme that will climax in Acts. The pattern offends Nazareth precisely because it decentralizes covenant privilege.
4.27: kai polloi leproi ēsan epi Elisaiou tou prophētou en tō Israēl — and there were many lepers in Israel in the time of the prophet Elisha — 2 Kgs 5 is the parallel case: Naaman the Syrian (Aramean) general, not an Israelite, was the one cleansed. Oudeois autōn ekatharisthē ei mē Naiman ho Syros — none of them was cleansed except Naaman the Syrian. The point is double: divine healing power was available during Elisha's era, but it went to a Gentile military officer. The Nazareth crowd's fury (v.28) is the direct consequence of this implication for Jesus's ministry.
4.28: eplesthesan pantes thymon en tē synagōgē — all in the synagogue were filled with rage — thymos (wrath, rage, passionate anger) contrasts with the initial thaumazein (amazement) of v.22; the congregation moved from wonder to murderous fury in moments. The trigger was not an insult to their religion but the implication that God's eschatological favor might pass over Israel in favor of Gentiles — the scandal of divine election working beyond ethnic boundaries. Their fury is the first enactment of the rejection-of-the-prophet pattern Jesus just announced.
4.29: kai anastantes exebalon auton exō tēs poleōs kai ēgagon auton heōs ophryos tou orous — rising up, they drove him out of the city and led him to the brow of the hill on which the city was built — mob action: the congregation becomes a mob and drives Jesus from the synagogue, out of town, and to a cliff. Ophrys (brow, ridge, eyebrow) refers to the hill's edge above a precipice. Katakremnisai auton (to throw him down) was the method of summary execution by cliff-throwing used in Israel's antiquity (cf. 2 Chr 25:12); they intend to kill him.
4.30: autos de dielthōn dia mesou autōn eporeueto — but he passed through the middle of them and went on his way — the escape is narrated without explanation of method: no angelic intervention, no miraculous blindness is mentioned. The point is simply that Jesus departed; Luke's reticence is more impressive than Matthew's or John's (cf. Jn 8:59; 10:39). His hour has not come (cf. Jn 7:30); the first attempt to kill him fails because the appointed time has not arrived. He "went on his way" (eporeueto imperfect = continued traveling) — the mission continues undeterred.
4.31: kai katēlthen eis Kapharnaoum polin tēs Galilaias — he went down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee — katēlthen (went down) reflects geography: Nazareth is at 370m elevation; Capernaum is on the north shore of the Sea of Galilee at -210m. Capernaum becomes Jesus's Galilean base (cf. 4:23, where the Nazarenes anticipated this). Luke adds "a city of Galilee" — a Gentile reader's orientation note. kai ēn didaskōn autous en tois sabbasin — and he was teaching them on the Sabbaths — the imperfect indicates regular weekly synagogue teaching.
4.32: kai exeplēssonto epi tē didachē autou, hoti en exousia ēn ho logos autou — they were astonished at his teaching, because his word was with authority — exeplēssonto (were astonished, struck out of their senses) is a strong reaction verb. Exousia (authority) is the theological key: rabbis taught by citation of predecessors ("Rabbi X says..."); Jesus taught with direct pronouncement. His word carries its own authorization without appeal to tradition. Luke pairs exousia (authority) with dynamis (power) in v.36 — the conceptual twins of his ministry.
4.33: kai en tē synagōgē ēn anthrōpos echōn pneuma daimoniou akathartou — in the synagogue there was a man having a spirit of an unclean demon — the demon's presence in the synagogue is deliberate: the first confrontation occurs in the house of worship, not in the streets. Pneuma daimoniou akathartou is Luke's compound phrase combining daimonion (demon, divine being) and akathartos (unclean, ritually impure). The man's possession disrupts the covenant assembly. kai anekraxen phōnē megalē — and he cried out with a loud voice — the demon's involuntary confrontation of Jesus.
4.34: Ea, ti hēmin kai soi, Iēsou Nazarēne — Let us alone! What have you to do with us, Jesus of Nazareth? — Ea is an interjection of protest or alarm. Ti hēmin kai soi (what to us and to you? = what do we have to do with each other?) is a Semitic idiom of non-engagement (cf. Judg 11:12; 2 Sam 16:10). The demon identifies Jesus geographically (Nazarēne) and with full theological precision: ho hagios tou theou — the Holy One of God — a unique Christological title combining holiness (consecrated, set apart) with divine origin. Demonic knowledge precedes human confession.
4.35: kai epitimēsen autō ho Iēsous legōn, Phimōthēti kai exelthe ap autou — Jesus rebuked him saying: Be muzzled and come out of him — epitimēsen (rebuked, charged sternly) is Luke's standard verb for Jesus's authoritative command over demons and natural forces (cf. 8:24 wind and waves; 4:39 fever). Phimōthēti (be silenced, be muzzled) suppresses both the demon's speech and its further activity. mēden blapsas auton — having harmed him in nothing — Luke's medical observation (cf. Mk 1:26 where convulsion and a loud cry are noted); Luke emphasizes the clean, harmless exit as evidence of complete divine control.
4.36: kai egeneto thambos epi pantas — astonishment came upon all — thambos (astonishment, wonder mixed with fear) is the reaction to the unprecedented. They interpret what they witnessed: hoti en exousia kai dynamei epitassei tois akathartois pneumasin — because with authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out. Exousia kai dynamis — authority and power — are Luke's paired theological concepts: exousia is the right to command; dynamis is the effective force executing the command. Both characterize Jesus's ministry throughout Luke-Acts.
4.37: kai exeporeueto ēchos peri autou eis panta topon tēs perichōrou — and a report about him went out to every place in the surrounding region — ēchos (echo, sound, report — Luke's medical vocabulary extended metaphorically) marks the spread of reputation without formal announcement. Perichōros (surrounding region) implies the countryside around Capernaum, including the Jordan Valley settlements. News of exorcisms by word alone was extraordinary; Jewish exorcism practices of the period involved elaborate rituals and incantations (cf. Josephus, Ant. 8.46-48).
4.38: anastas de apo tēs synagōgēs eisēlthen eis tēn oikian Simōnos — rising from the synagogue he entered Simon's house — the sequence is direct: synagogue → Simon's house in Capernaum. This is the first mention of Simon Peter's home in Luke (though Mk 1:29-31 introduces Simon earlier). penthera de tou Simōnos ēn synechomenē pyretō megalō — Simon's mother-in-law was being held by a great fever — synekhomenē (was being held, constrained, pressed) is a medical term; Luke adds megalō (great/high) to describe the fever's severity, consistent with Luke's medical vocabulary (pyretō megalō corresponds to what ancient physicians called a dangerous systemic fever).
4.39: kai epistas epanō autēs epetimēsen tō pyretō — standing over her, he rebuked the fever — the same verb epitimēsen used against the demon (v.35) is here applied to a fever — an unprecedented usage that treats disease as something subject to command and rebuke, not merely to medical treatment. Jesus does not administer medicine or pray; he commands the fever directly. kai aphēken autēn — and it left her — the fever's departure is described with the same verb (aphiēmi) used for the departure of demons; Luke treats the healing as a liberation. parachrēma de anastasa diēkonei autois — immediately she rose and was serving them — instant and complete restoration.
4.40: dynontos de tou hēliou hapantes hosoi eichon asthenountas nosois poikilais ēgagon autous pros auton — as the sun was setting, all who had sick people with various diseases brought them to him — the Sabbath ended at sunset; carrying the sick was prohibited on the Sabbath (m. Shabb. 10:5), so the crowd waited until sunset before bringing the ill. The mass healing session is Lukan: ho de heni hekastō autōn tas cheiras epititheis etherapeuev autous — laying his hands on each one of them, he healed them — Luke's individual attention: not a collective healing wave but personal laying on of hands (cheiras epititheis) on each person, the apostolic gesture of conferring blessing and healing.
4.41: exērcheto de kai daimonia apo pollōn krazonta kai legonta hoti sy ei ho huios tou theou — demons were coming out of many, crying and saying: you are the Son of God — the collective demonic confession of Jesus's divine sonship (cf. v.34 "the Holy One of God") is the involuntary testimony of the spirit world to what the human world has not yet recognized. kai epitimōn ouk eia auta lalein, hoti ēdeisan ton Christon auton einai — rebuking them, he would not allow them to speak, because they knew him to be the Christ — the messianic secret: Jesus silences demonic identification of his identity. The reason Luke gives is theological: their knowledge of his Messianic role (not just his divine sonship) is what must not be premature announced.
4.42: genomenēs de hēmeras exelthōn eporeuthe eis erēmon topon — when day came, he went out and went to a solitary place — Luke's characteristic emphasis on Jesus at prayer: this verse in Luke becomes an explicit prayer scene in the parallel account's pattern (cf. 5:16; 6:12; 9:18; 11:1; 22:41). The withdrawal to erēmos topos (deserted/solitary place) after a full day of healing and exorcism follows the pattern of Moses and Elijah in wilderness retreat. hoi de ochloi epezētoun auton — the crowds were seeking him — epizēteō (seek eagerly, search for) indicates pressing need; they tried to prevent him from departing.
4.43: ho de eipen pros autous hoti kai tais hetrais polesin euangelisasthai me dei tēn basileian tou theou — he said to them: I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns also — dei (it is necessary, I must) is Luke's divine-compulsion word, marking what God has appointed and what Jesus cannot deviate from (cf. 2:49; 9:22; 13:33; 24:26,44). Euangelisasthai (to proclaim good news) is Luke's preferred verb for Jesus's mission; it occurs more in Luke-Acts than in all other NT books combined. hoti epi touto apestalmēn — because for this I was sent — the mission statement in brief: apostolic sending by the Father to proclaim the kingdom.
4.44: kai ēn kēryssōn eis tas synagōgas tēs Ioudaias — and he was preaching in the synagogues of Judea — Ioudaias here likely means the broader Jewish region (cf. 1:5 where "Judea" encompasses the whole land), not the southern province exclusively, since the preceding narrative is in Galilee. Luke uses kēryssōn (preaching, proclaiming as a herald) as a summary-verb for the Galilean circuit, distinguishing it from the more content-specific euangelizesthai (v.43). The imperfect tense continues the panoramic summary of the mission's scope.

6.20: makarioi hoi ptochoi, hoti hymetera estin he basileia tou theou — blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God — Luke's beatitudes are in the second person (you, not they as in Matthew) and unqualified (poor without spirit; hungry without for righteousness). Luke's social realism presents poverty and hunger as the literal conditions of Jesus's audience; the kingdom reversal is specifically material as well as spiritual. The four beatitudes are balanced by four corresponding woes (6:24-26), creating the sharpest contrast structure in the Synoptic tradition.
6.1: en sabbatō deuteroproto — on a Sabbath called “second-first.” The textual problem is acknowledged: deuteroprotos (literally “second-first”) is a hapax legomenon absent from many manuscripts; Origen and Chrysostom knew it; the UBS text brackets it with a C rating. Several interpretations have been proposed: the first Sabbath after Passover, the second Sabbath of a new year counting from Nisan, or the second day of unleavened bread. No solution is fully satisfying; the reading may be a scribal gloss that entered the text. The translation “One Sabbath” wisely avoids specifying.
6.2: ti poieite ho ouk exestin tois sabbasin — why are you doing what is unlawful on the Sabbath? The Pharisees’ question cites the rabbinic ruling that plucking grain in a field = reaping (m. Shabbat 7:2) and rubbing ears = threshing. The disciples are not stealing; gleaning rights were legally guaranteed to the poor (Lev 19:9-10; Deut 23:25). The dispute is about the category of Sabbath labor, not property. Jesus does not dispute the principle but disputes the application by citing a Torah precedent.
6.3: oude touto anegnote ho epoiesen Dauid — have you never read what David did? The appeal to the David-precedent (1 Sam 21:1-6) is a rabbinic-style argument from the greater to the lesser: if David’s need justified eating the bread of the Presence (restricted to priests by Lev 24:9), then the disciples’ need justifies gleaning grain on the Sabbath. The argument also implicitly identifies Jesus with David — he who has the authority to define what is lawful.
6.4: tous artous tēs protheseōs — the bread of the Presence. The shewbread (Exod 25:30; Lev 24:5-9) was the consecrated loaves set before YHWH on the table in the holy place, replaced each Sabbath and eaten only by the priests. David’s eating of them with his companions was technically an irregular priestly action — it was justified by necessity and the priest’s judgment. Luke’s version (unlike Mark 2:26) does not name Abiathar (who is chronologically problematic), quietly avoiding an historical difficulty in the tradition.
6.5: kyrios estin tou sabbatou ho huios tou anthropou — the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath. Kyrios tou sabbatou (Lord of the Sabbath) — the conclusion is a Christological claim, not merely a principle about human need. The Son of Man title (Aram. bar enasha / Dan 7:13-14) carries the “given authority, glory and sovereign power” of the Danielic figure; to say this figure is Lord of the Sabbath is to claim that he has the authority to define Sabbath observance — the authority of the institution’s Creator.
6.6: ēn ēi anēr ekei kai hē cheir autou hē dexia ēn xēra — a man was there whose right hand was withered. The specification of the right hand (Luke only; Mark and Matthew say simply “withered hand”) may be Luke’s medical precision (xēra = dry, atrophied, as in medical texts), or it may emphasize the functional disability: the right hand is the working hand; its withering renders the man unable to labor. The healing restores not just physical function but social capacity and livelihood.
6.7: paretērounto de autou hoi grammateis kai hoi pharisaioi ei en tō sabbatō therapeuei — the scribes and Pharisees were watching him closely to see if he would heal on the Sabbath. Paretērounto (were watching alongside, closely observing) is the same verb used for watching a city gate for an enemy (Acts 9:24); the surveillance is hostile. The rabbinic ruling permitted healing on the Sabbath only in life-threatening cases (m. Yoma 8:6); a withered hand was not life-threatening. Their question is not whether he will heal but whether he will do it on the Sabbath.
6.8: autos de ēidei tous dialogismous autōn kai eipen tō andri tō xēran echonti tēn cheira egeire kai stēthi eis to meson — he knew their thoughts and said to the man with the withered hand: Get up and stand in front of everyone. Jesus’s knowledge of unspoken thoughts (dialogismous) recurs in Luke (5:22; 9:47; 11:17) as a marker of divine perceptiveness. The public staging of the healing — “stand in the middle” — transforms a potential private healing into a deliberate confrontation with the objectors. He creates the conflict rather than avoiding it.
6.9: exestin tō sabbatō agathon poiēsai ē kakopoiēsai, psychēn sōsai ē apolysai — which is lawful on the Sabbath: to do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy it? The question restructures the debate from what is prohibited to what is required: the Sabbath is not merely a day for abstaining from labor but a day that belongs to YHWH, who is the God who saves. The antithesis “save life or destroy it” is sharper in Luke than in Mark (where “kill” is the verb). The implication: those who refuse to heal when they can are choosing to destroy life by inaction.
6.10: eksteinon tēn cheira sou kai epoiēsen — stretch out your hand, and he did so. The healing is accomplished by word alone — no physical touch, no anointing, no clay. Apokatestathē hē cheir autou (his hand was completely restored) — the prefix apo- intensifies the restoration: not merely healed but restored to its former whole state. The instant and complete restoration challenges any claim that healing-work violates the Sabbath; there is no manual labor here, only divine command and its fulfillment.
6.11: autoi de eplēsthēsan anoias — they were filled with madness. Anoia (madness, senselessness, lack of understanding) is a strong word — the scribes and Pharisees’ response to the undeniable healing is not reflection but rage. Dielaloyn pros allēlous ti an poiēsaien tō Iēsou (they discussed together what they might do to Jesus) — the subjunctive poiēsaien expresses deliberation; the conspiracy against Jesus begins here in Luke. The Sabbath healing has exposed the opposition of those who should have welcomed the divine mercy.
6.12: exēlthen eis to oros proseuxasthai kai ēn dianyktereuōn en tē proseuchē tou theou — he went out to a mountainside to pray, and he spent the night in prayer to God. Dianyktereuōn (spending the night, passing the night) is a NT hapax — only Luke records this detail. Luke’s Jesus prays at all the decisive moments of the Gospel: before the choosing of the Twelve (here), at his baptism (3:21), before Peter’s confession (9:18), at the Transfiguration (9:28-29), in Gethsemane (22:41-44). The appointment of the apostles is preceded by a night of prayer: human discernment submits to divine guidance.
6.13: hous kai apostolous onomasen — whom he also named apostles. The title apostolos (sent one, commissioned representative) distinguishes these twelve from the larger group of disciples who also followed Jesus. Luke’s use of apostle is more technically defined than Mark’s: the Twelve are the authoritative witnesses who will testify to the resurrection and lead the early community (Acts 1:15-26; the requirement of having witnessed from the beginning). The naming is an act of commissioning, not merely labeling.
6.14: Simōna hon kai onomasen Petron — Simon, whom he also named Peter. The naming of Simon as Peter (Petros = rock) is noted by Luke without explanation; the reader is assumed to know the significance. The list of the Twelve follows Markan order with slight variations; Andreas (Andrew) is Simon’s brother; Iacōbus and Iōannēs (James and John) are the Zebedee brothers; Philippos (Philip) and Bartholomaios (Bartholomew, possibly = Nathanael in John) complete the first six.
6.15: Matthaion kai Thoman, Iakōbon ton tou Alphaiou kai Simōna ton kaloumenon Zēlōtēn — Matthew, Thomas, James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot. Zēlōtēs (Zealot) may denote membership in the revolutionary group that advocated violent resistance to Rome, or it may simply mean “the zealous one” in a general sense. Luke also records Matthew in this list, the same figure as Levi of 5:27-29. The Twelve include tax collectors and a Zealot — representatives of opposite ends of Jewish political loyalty — united by their calling to follow Jesus.
6.16: Ioudan Iakōbou kai Ioudan Iscariōth, hos egeneto prodotēs — Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor. The final entry in the list is marked by prodotēs (traitor, betrayer — a strong word; the same term used for those who hand over fellow believers in Acts 7:52). Egeneto (became) rather than “was” suggests Judas’s treachery is not his original state but a tragic development. Iscariot is most likely “man from Kerioth” (a Judean town), making Judas possibly the only non-Galilean among the Twelve.
6.17: kai stēnai epi topou pedinou kai ochlos polys mathētōn autou kai plēthos poly tou laou — he came down with them and stood on a level place, with a large crowd of his disciples and a great multitude of people. The “level place” (topou pedinou, plain, flat place) has generated a harmonization question with Matthew’s “mountain” (Matt 5:1); these may be different occasions, or Luke may intend a level area on a mountainside. The audience is explicitly double: mathētōn (disciples, the larger following) and laou (the people, including the geographical range from Judea, Jerusalem, and the Phoenician coastal cities).
6.18: hoi enochloumenoi hypo pneumatōn akathartōn etherapeuonto — those troubled by unclean spirits were cured. The summary verse covers the two healings: those with diseases (nosōn) and those with unclean spirits (pneumata akatharta). Etherapeunto (were being healed, imperfect = continuous action) alongside the explicit exorcisms indicates that the kingdom’s arrival manifests as liberation from both physical illness and spiritual oppression — Luke’s consistent double-healing motif.
6.19: pas ho ochlos ezētoun haptesthai autou, hoti dynamis par autou exērchetō kai iato pantas — all the crowd were trying to touch him, because power was coming out from him and healing everyone. Dynamis par autou exērchetō (power was going out from him) — dynamis (power, divine energy) is Luke’s characteristic term for the operative force in Jesus’s healings (5:17; 8:46; Acts 10:38). The imperfect exērchetō (kept going out) portrays a continuous flow of healing power; Jesus is not merely performing isolated miracles but radiating the kingdom’s restorative power to all who seek it.
6.21: makarioi hoi peinōntes nyn, hoti chortasthēsesthe — blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Peinōntes (hungering, literally starving) has no qualifier “for righteousness” as in Matthew (Matt 5:6); the literal hunger of the poor is blessed. Chortasthēsesthe (you will be filled, satisfied — the word used for animals fed to contentment, Ezek 16:49 LXX) is the divine passive: God will satisfy the hungry with good things (cf. Magnificat 1:53). The second beatitude (klaiō weep/mourn) and its reversal (gelasete you will laugh) are also second-person and literal.
6.22: makarioi este hotan misēsōsin hymas hoi anthrōpoi — blessed are you when people hate you. The fourth beatitude is the longest and most social: it names four escalating actions — hate (misēsōsin), exclude (aphorizōsin, excommunicate), insult (oneidisōsin, reproach), reject as evil (ekbalōsin to onoma hymon hōs ponēron, cast out your name as evil). The cause is “on account of the Son of Man” — the connection between the disciples’ suffering and Jesus’s own rejection is made explicit. Identification with Jesus brings the treatment Jesus receives.
6.23: chairete en ekeinē tē hēmera kai skirtēsate — rejoice in that day and leap for joy. Skirtēsate (leap, jump for joy) is the word for John’s prenatal leap (1:41, 44) and for the calf released from the stall in Mal 4:2 LXX — it is the extravagant, uninhibited joy of liberation. Hoti gar ho misthos hymon polys en tō ouranō (for your reward is great in heaven) — the eschatological framing: present suffering has a corresponding heavenly weight of glory. The prophets-as-precedent clause (kata tauta gar epoioun tois prophētais) identifies the disciples with the OT prophetic succession.
6.24: plēn ouai hymin tois plousiois, hoti apechete tēn paraklēsin hymōn — but woe to you who are rich, for you have already received your comfort. The four woes (vv.24-26) are Luke’s unique contribution; Matthew has no parallels. Ouai (woe) is the prophetic word of judgment and grief (as in Isa 5:8-23; Amos 5:18). Apechete (you have received in full, a commercial term for receipts — “paid in full”) — the rich have already cashed in their comfort; no future comfort awaits them. The economic reversal of the Magnificat (1:52-53) is here applied as direct address.
6.25: ouai hymin hoi empeplēsmenoi nyn, hoti peinas ete — woe to you who are well fed now, for you will go hungry. The future tense of the woes (peinas ete you will hunger; penthēsete kai klausete you will mourn and weep) is eschatological: the reversal is not immediate but certain. The structural parallelism with the beatitudes is exact: the well-fed now will hunger; those who hunger now will be filled. The pairing condemns not wealth or satiety in themselves but the false security they create — the assumption that present comfort is permanent.
6.26: ouai hotan hymas kalōs eipōsin pantes hoi anthrōpoi — woe to you when everyone speaks well of you. The fourth woe closes the chiasm with the fourth beatitude: the blessed are socially rejected; the condemned are socially praised. Kata ta auta gar epoioun tois pseudoprophētais (for that is how their ancestors treated the false prophets) — universal social approval was the mark of the false prophet (Jer 5:31; Mic 2:11), not the true. Popularity is a warning sign, not a validation.
6.27: agapate tous echthroys hymōn, kalōs poieite tois misousin hymas — love your enemies, do good to those who hate you. Agapate (love, the command form of agapaō — not eros or philia but the willed, active disposition of seeking the other’s good regardless of their response) directed at enemies is the most radical element of the Sermon. The enemies are defined in relational terms: those who hate (misousin), those who curse (katarōmenois), those who mistreat (epēreazontōn). The love commanded is not an emotion but a practice: do good, bless, pray.
6.28: eulogeite tous katarōmenous hymas, proseuchesthe peri tōn epēreazontōn hymas — bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you. The four pairs form a chiastic pattern of increasing intimacy of response: love (general disposition), do good (practical action), bless (spoken contrary to curse), pray (internalized self-offering for the other). Eulogeite (bless) is the spoken counterpart to the received curse; proseuchesthe (pray) is the deepest: interceding for those who actively mistreat is the completion of love.
6.29: tō typtonti se epi tēn siagona parechete kai tēn allēn — if someone strikes you on the cheek, turn to them the other also. The cheek-slap in the honor-shame culture was a blow of contempt rather than assault; to offer the other cheek is not passive submission but active refusal to be defined by the attacker’s framework. To himation (outer garment, the more valuable) and to chiton (inner tunic) — the coat/shirt reversal: if someone takes the more valuable outer cloak, give also the inner. The legal background (Exod 22:26) makes this a commentary on debt-recovery law: give beyond what can legally be taken.
6.30: panti tō aitounti se didou kai apo tou ainontos ta sa mē apaiteite — give to everyone who asks you, and if anyone takes what belongs to you, do not demand it back. The principle is unlimited generosity rather than calculated reciprocity. Apaiteite (demand back, exact return) — the word for legally demanding repayment; the disciples are called to release claims rather than enforce them. The verse operates against the honor economy that requires debts to be balanced and insults to be avenged.
6.31: kai kathōs thelete hina poiōsin hymin hoi anthrōpoi, poieite autois homoiōs — do to others as you would have them do to you. The Golden Rule appears across many traditions (Rabbi Hillel’s negative form: “What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbor”, Shabbat 31a; Tobit 4:15). Jesus’s formulation is positive and proactive rather than negative and restraining. Luke places it after the enemy-love commands, giving it a universalizing function: the standard for treatment of enemies is the same as the standard for all human relations.
6.32: kai ei agapate tous agapōntas hymas, poia hymin charis estin — if you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? Charis here means “credit, merit, deserving” (not grace in the theological sense) — what distinguishes-to-your-advantage does this give you? The three examples (love-for-love, v.32; good-for-good, v.33; lend-to-repay, v.34) are all practices of calculated reciprocity that even “sinners” (hamartōloi) perform. The disciples are called to something qualitatively beyond the social norm.
6.33: kai gar hoi hamartōloi tous agapōntas autous agapōsin — even sinners love those who love them. The repeated appeal to “sinners” as the baseline of ordinary human reciprocity sets a rhetorical contrast: the disciples are not called to outperform the Torah-observant but to exceed the practices of those outside the covenant entirely. Hamartōloi (sinners) in the Synoptic context typically means those outside the social/religious norms of Jewish piety — tax collectors, Gentiles, the disreputable.
6.34: kai ean danizēte par hōn elpizete labein, poia hymin charis estin — and if you lend to those from whom you expect repayment, what credit is that to you? The lending-with-expectation example moves into the economic sphere: ancient lending was often social capital exchange rather than commercial transaction. Lending to those who will repay preserves the honor network; lending to those who cannot repay is economic gift. Kai hamartōloi hamartōlois danizousin hina apolabōsin ta isa (even sinners lend to sinners expecting to receive back the same amount) — pure reciprocity is the sinners’ economic logic.
6.35: plēn agapate tous echthroys hymon kai agathopoieite kai danizete mēden apelpizontes — love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them without expecting to get anything back. The climactic command repeats and intensifies v.27; the three verbs (love, do good, lend without expectation) are the practical content of the love commanded. Hoti autos chrēstos estin epi tous acharistous kai ponērous — because God is chrēstos (kind, good) to the ungrateful and wicked. The divine character is the ground of the ethical demand: imitate God’s indiscriminate kindness. Chrēstos is a Greek wordplay on Christos (Christ), possibly intentional in Hellenistic Christian ears.
6.36: ginesthe oiktirmones kathōs kai ho patēr hymon oiktirmōn estin — be merciful, just as your Father is merciful. Oiktirmōn (merciful, compassionate) is the LXX rendering of Hebrew rachum (the womb-compassion of God, Ps 103:8; Exod 34:6 — “the LORD, the LORD, the compassionate and gracious God”). Matthew’s parallel reads “be perfect as your Father is perfect” (Matt 5:48); Luke’s “merciful” is both a translation variant and a thematic emphasis: Luke’s Gospel is organized around divine mercy (eleos) and human compassion (oiktirmos) as the central kingdom qualities.
6.37: kai mē krinete kai ou mē krithēte; kai mē katadikasete kai ou mē katadikasthēte; apolyete kai apolythēsesthe — do not judge and you will not be judged; do not condemn and you will not be condemned; forgive and you will be forgiven. Three paired commands with divine-passive responses. The sequence escalates: not judging (withholding negative evaluation) → not condemning (withholding legal-moral verdict) → releasing (active release of claims/offenses). Apolyete (release, forgive) is the same root as aphesis (forgiveness/release), Luke’s programmatic gospel term. The passive responses are divine: God judges, condemns, and releases in corresponding proportion.
6.38: metron kalon pepiesmenon sesaleumenon hyperekchynnomenon dōsousin eis ton kolpon hymōn — a good measure, pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be poured into your lap. The four participial clauses describe the excess of the divine measure given in return for generous giving: pepiesmenon (pressed down, compacted to fit more), sesaleumenon (shaken together to settle), hyperekchynnomenon (overflowing, running over the edge). Eis ton kolpon (into the lap/fold) — the fold of a robe used to carry grain; the image is of market abundance. Hō gar metrō metreite antimethrēthēsetai hymin — the measure principle applies both to human generosity and to divine judgment.
6.39: mēti dynatai typhlos typhlon hodēgein; ouchi amphoteroi eis bothynon pesountai — can the blind lead the blind? Will they not both fall into a pit? The parable of the blind guide is applied contextually to the scribes and Pharisees as blind guides (cf. Matt 23:16, 24) who cannot lead others in the way of the kingdom they cannot see. Luke’s context immediately links this to the teacher-student principle (v.40): the disciples cannot exceed their teacher’s sight. The warning applies to the disciples too: if they follow blind guides rather than Jesus, they will share the guide’s fate.
6.40: ouk estin mathētēs hyper ton didaskalon; katērtismenos de pas estai hōs ho didaskalos autou — the student is not above the teacher; but everyone who is fully trained will be like their teacher. Katērtismenos (fully trained, completely equipped — the verb used for mending fishing nets, Matt 4:21; for the body assembled by God, Heb 10:5) — the goal of discipleship is not to exceed the teacher but to become like him. The implication: only a disciple of Jesus, who is not blind, can follow a guide who sees. The teacher defines the ceiling of the student’s achievement.
6.41: ti de blepeis to karphos to en tō ophthalmō tou adelphou sou — why do you look at the speck of sawdust in your brother’s eye? Karphos (speck, small piece of straw or wood chip) and dokos (plank, beam) form an absurdist contrast: the one criticizing perceives a tiny defect in the other while being oblivious to a structural beam in their own eye. The humor is deliberately exaggerated; the point is the selective blindness of self-righteousness. The address adelphou (brother) situates the critique within the covenant community — those who should know better are most prone to this blindness.
6.42: hypokritē, ekbale prōton tēn dokon ek tou ophthalmou sou — you hypocrite, first take the plank out of your own eye. Hypokritēs (hypocrite, actor — one who performs a role different from their reality) is the only use of this term in Luke; Matthew uses it far more frequently. The command sequence is important: first (prōton) self-examination and correction, then (tote) correction of the other. The verse does not forbid correction; it requires the corrector to be in a state of self-examination first. The cleared eye is the condition of seeing clearly enough to help another.
6.43: ou gar estin dendron kalon poioun karpon sapron, oude palin dendron sapron poioun karpon kalon — a good tree does not bear bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit. The tree-fruit principle is a creation-order consistency argument: the nature of the tree determines the nature of the fruit; there is no arbitrary disconnection between internal character and external action. The principle is applied to human moral character (vv.44-45): what a person produces consistently reveals what they are, not merely what they choose to do in isolated moments.
6.44: hekastos gar dendron ek tou idiou karpou ginōsketai — each tree is recognized by its own fruit. Ginōsketai (is known, recognized, identified) — the fruit is the epistemological key to the tree’s identity. The examples (figs from thornbushes, grapes from briers) invoke Isa 5:2,4 (the vineyard that produced wild grapes — the symbol of Israel’s unfaithfulness) and the Synoptic tradition of demanding fruits of repentance (3:8-9). The conclusion is diagnostic: you recognize the tree by what it consistently produces.
6.45: ho agathos anthrōpos ek tou agathou thēsaurou tēs kardias propherei to agathon — a good man brings good things out of the good stored up in his heart. Thēsauros kardias (treasure of the heart) — the heart is the storage vault of moral character; what is stored there (agathos good or ponēros evil) is what comes out in speech and action. Ek gar perisseumaros kardias lalei to stoma autou (from the overflow of the heart the mouth speaks) — the heart is not merely the emotions but the deep moral center of the person (OT usage: Jer 17:9; Ps 51:10). Speech is involuntary testimony to the heart’s actual condition.
6.46: ti de me kaleite: kyrie kyrie, kai ou poieite ha legō — why do you call me Lord, Lord and do not do what I say? The doubled kyrie kyrie (Lord, Lord) is liturgical address — a formulaic reverence in worship. Jesus’s question challenges the gap between verbal acknowledgment and practical obedience. The title Kyrios (Lord) is the LXX rendering of YHWH; to call Jesus Lord without obeying him is to use the divine name as an incantation while ignoring the divine command. The parable that follows defines what hearing-and-obeying looks like concretely.
6.47: pas ho erchomenos pros me kai akouōn mou tōn logōn kai poiōn autous — everyone who comes to me and hears my words and puts them into practice. The three participial phrases define the obedient disciple: coming (erchomenos — direction of life, orientation toward Jesus), hearing (akouōn — active reception of his teaching), and doing (poiōn — practical embodiment). Luke’s version of the parable (vv.47-49) differs from Matthew’s (Matt 7:24-27) in the construction details: Luke specifies digging deep (eskapse kai ebathynen), while Matthew mentions rock. Luke’s emphasis is on the effort of deep foundation-laying.
6.48: homoios estin anthrōpō oikodomounti oikian hos eskapse kai ebathynen kai ethēken themelion epi tēn petran — they are like a man building a house who dug down deep and laid the foundation on rock. Eskapse kai ebathynen (dug and deepened) — Luke’s unique detail: the obedient disciple labors to reach bedrock; the process is effortful, not obvious. The Palestinian setting: houses were sometimes built on alluvial soil; a flood (plēmyrēs, a rising of water, an inundation) could undermine the foundations. The flood-house-foundation triad invokes Noah’s ark and the testing of structures by crisis. Dia to kalōs oikodomēsthai (because it was well built) — Luke’s explanation emphasizes craftsmanship; Matthew emphasizes rock as the foundation material.
6.49: ho de akousas kai mē poiēsas homoios estin anthrōpō oikodomēsanti oikian epi tēn gēn chōris themelion — but the one who hears and does not put it into practice is like a man who built a house on the ground without a foundation. Chōris themelion (without a foundation, lacking a foundation) — the structural deficiency is not poor materials but the complete absence of foundational support. Hē rhēxis tēs oikias ekeinēs egeneto megalē (the ruin of that house was great) — Luke’s intensified conclusion: the collapse is complete (cf. Matthew’s “great was its fall”). The stakes of hearing-without-doing are not minor adjustment but total catastrophic failure in the testing crisis.

7.34: elelythen ho huios tou anthropou esthion kai pinon — the Son of Man has come eating and drinking — the Son of Man self-reference in the social context of table-fellowship is Luke's Christological signature: the one with Danielic universal authority is present at dinner with sinners and tax collectors. The Pharisees' critique (phagos kai oinopotes, philos telonon kai hamartolon = a glutton and a drunkard, friend of tax collectors and sinners) is the social observation; Jesus turns it into the theological point: Wisdom is justified by all her children (v.35).
7.1: epeidē eplērōsen panta ta rhēmata autou eis tas akoas tou laou — when he had completed all his sayings in the hearing of the people. Luke’s transitional verse uses plērōsen (fulfilled, completed) — the same root as plēroō (to fulfill scriptural promises); the Sermon is “completed” as a formal discourse. Eis tas akoas tou laou (into the hearing of the people) — Luke emphasizes the public nature of the teaching; the word that has gone out to the crowd now leads to an action that demonstrates the same word’s power. The transition to Capernaum (Kapharnaum) grounds the following miracle in the teaching’s context.
7.2: hekatontarchou de tinos doulos kakōs echōn ēmellen teleutagn — a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was ill and about to die. Hekatontarchos (centurion, commander of one hundred) — a Roman military officer stationed in Capernaum, possibly attached to Herod Antipas’s forces. Doulos (slave/servant) — the word for a slave; contrast pais (v.7, child/boy) used by the centurion himself. The use of doulos by the narrator and pais by the centurion may indicate affection (pais as “my boy”) or a different tradition. Entimos (valued, precious, honored) indicates the centurion’s unusual care for a slave.
7.3: akousas peri tou Iēsou apesteilen pros auton presbyterous tōn Ioudaiōn — having heard about Jesus he sent elders of the Jews to him. The centurion’s intermediary approach through Jewish elders is socially sophisticated: a Gentile military officer sends the community’s own leaders as advocates, acknowledging Jewish religious authority. Presbyterous tōn Ioudaiōn (elders of the Jews) are the local synagogue leadership, not the Jerusalem Sanhedrin. The request (erōtōn auton hopōs elthōn diasōsē — asking him to come and heal) is a formal petition through proper social channels.
7.4: hoi de paragenomenoi pros ton Iēsoun parekaloun auton spoudaiōs — when they came to Jesus they pleaded earnestly with him. Spoudaiōs (earnestly, urgently) — the adverb intensifies the advocacy; the elders are genuinely persuaded of the centurion’s deserving. The word axios (deserving, worthy — v.4) begins the remarkable sequence where the centurion and Jesus each contest who is worthy: the elders say he is worthy; the centurion says he is not worthy (hikanon, v.6, and axios, v.7). The contest of unworthiness is the narrative heart of the pericope.
7.5: agapai gar to ethnos hēmōn kai tēn synagōgēn autos ōkodomēsen hēmin — he loves our nation and has built our synagogue for us. The elders’ advocacy argues from the centurion’s deeds: agapai to ethnos (he loves our nation) and tēn synagōgēn autos ōkodomēsen (he built our synagogue). This Gentile officer has funded the local synagogue — an act of extraordinary religious patronage and social good will. The argument is pragmatic: he has done good to Israel and deserves reciprocal good from Israel’s God. But Jesus will honor him for something else entirely.
7.6: ho de Iēsous eporeueto syn autois. ēdē de autou ou makran apechontos apo tēs oikias epempsas philous ho hekatontarchos — Jesus went with them. He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say: Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. The mid-journey interruption by a second message is a narrative heightening device. Mē skyllou (don’t trouble yourself, stop being bothered — skylloō is “to flay, then to trouble, harass”) is a polite urgency. Ou gar hikanos eimi hina hypo tēn stegēn mou eiselthēs — the centurion’s self-assessment of unworthiness (hikanos = sufficient, adequate) is the opposite of the elders’ advocacy.
7.7: dio oude eme axion hegēsamēn elthein pros se: alla eipe logō kai iathētō ho pais mou — that is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word and my servant will be healed. Axion hegēsamēn (I judged myself worthy) — the centurion’s self-judgment contradicts the elders’ judgment (v.4 axios estin); he alone knows his own inadequacy. Alla eipe logō (but say a word, say it with a word) — the centurion reduces the request to its absolute minimum: a single spoken word from Jesus is sufficient. He has understood something about the nature of Jesus’s authority that the disciples have not yet articulated.
7.8: kai gar egō anthrōpos eimi hypo exousian tassomenos echōn hyp emauton stratiōtas — for I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. The military analogy is the centurion’s theological argument: he understands command authority by experience. A soldier under authority (hypo exousian) can exercise delegated power (exousia) over those under him; his word is effective not because of his personal power but because of the authority behind him. The centurion infers that Jesus has a corresponding relationship to a higher authority — one whose command is effective at any distance, through word alone.
7.9: akousas de tauta ho Iēsous ethaumasen auton — when Jesus heard this, he marveled at him. Ethaumasen (marveled, was amazed) — one of only two places in the Gospels where Jesus is said to marvel (the other: Mark 6:6, at Nazareth’s unbelief). Here he marvels at the centurion’s faith; there, at his own townspeople’s unbelief. Oude en tō Israēl tosautēn pistin heuron (I have not found such great faith even in Israel) — the comparison is remarkable: a Gentile officer who has never been taught the Torah has grasped the nature of Jesus’s authority more clearly than those within the covenant. Faith, for Luke, is not ethnic privilege.
7.10: kai hypostrepsantes eis ton oikon hoi pemphthentes heuron ton doulon hygiainonta — those who had been sent returned to the house and found the servant well. The healing is confirmed by the returned messengers, not witnessed by Jesus or any disciple. Hygiainonta (healthy, well, sound — the root of hygiene) is the complete recovery. The narrative structure mirrors the healing: a word spoken at a distance produces a change at a distance, confirmed by witnesses at the destination. Luke omits any explicit mention of the moment of healing, focusing instead on the discovery of the result — the word’s effectiveness proven by outcome.
7.11: egeneto en tē hexēs eporeuēto eis polin kaloumenēn Naïn — soon afterwards Jesus went to a town called Nain. En tē hexēs (on the next day, afterward — the transitional phrase) connects the centurion’s story to the widow’s son. Nain (Naïn, “pleasant” in Hebrew) is a town in southern Galilee, near Shunem — where Elisha raised the widow’s son (2 Kgs 4:32-37). The geographical proximity to the Elisha miracle is likely deliberate; Luke will invite comparison. Hoi mathētai autou hikanoi kai ochlos polys (his disciples and a large crowd) — both a following and witnesses to what follows.
7.12: hōs de ēngisen tē pylē tēs poleōs, kai idou exekomizeto tethnēkōs — as he approached the town gate, a dead person was being carried out. The two processions — Jesus’s coming-in and the funeral procession going out — meet at the city gate (pylē), the threshold space between life-city and death-space. Tethnēkōs (dead person) and the three details of social tragedy: monogenēs (only son — the unique son, same word as John 3:16’s only-begotten), tē mētri (his mother), and chēra (widow). The triple tragedy — only son, bereaved mother, without a husband — exhausts the social support network. The crowd (ochlos hikanos) is the public mourning community.
7.13: kai idōn autēn ho kyrios esplanchnisthē ep autē kai eipen autē: mē klaie — when the Lord saw her, his heart went out to her and he said: Don’t cry. Esplanchnisthē (had compassion, was moved in the bowels) is Luke’s compassion verb — the visceral, gut-level response to human suffering (also 10:33 for the Samaritan; 15:20 for the father of the prodigal). Luke uses ho kyrios (the Lord) as a narrator’s title for Jesus (7:13; 10:1; 11:39; 13:15 etc.) — the LXX divine title used by the narrator as a Christological identifier. Mē klaie (stop weeping) is a command, not comfort; he intends to give her the reason to stop.
7.14: kai proselthōn hēpsato tēs sorou, hoi de bastaszontes estēsan — he went up and touched the bier, and those carrying it stood still. Hēpsato tēs sorou (touched the bier, the open stretcher for carrying a body) — contact with a corpse or bier renders a person ritually unclean (Num 19:11-13); Jesus initiates the contact deliberately, reversing the direction of contagion. Hoi de bastasontes estēsan (those carrying it stopped) — the bearers freeze at the touch, creating the arrested moment in which Jesus speaks. Neaniske, soi legō, egerthēti (young man, I say to you, rise) — the address is personal, intimate, authoritative; “I say to you” marks the word as direct divine command.
7.15: kai anekathisen ho nekros kai ērxato lalein — the dead man sat up and began to speak. Anekathisen (sat up, raised himself to sitting position) — the physical description is precise: the movement of one who was lying now rises from the bier. Ērxato lalein (began to speak) — the first action of the restored life is speech; the dead speak. Kai edōken auton tē mētri autou (and he gave him back to his mother) — the deliberate echo of 1 Kgs 17:23 (Elijah: “gave him to his mother”) and 2 Kgs 4:36 (Elisha: “gave him to his mother”). Jesus is aligned with but exceeds the Elijah-Elisha prophetic tradition.
7.16: elaben de phobos pantas kai edoxazon ton theon legontes hoti prophētēs megas ēgerthē en hēmin kai hoti epeskepsato ho theos ton laon autou — they were all filled with awe and praised God, saying: A great prophet has appeared among us; God has come to help his people. The double acclamation is the crowd’s theological interpretation: prophētēs megas ēgerthē (a great prophet has arisen) — the prophetic category places Jesus in the Elijah-Elisha succession; ēgerthē (has arisen, passive) is the same verb as the resurrection. Epeskepsato ho theos ton laon autou (God has visited his people) — the covenant-visitation language of Zechariah’s Benedictus (1:68); what Zechariah announced is now being enacted.
7.17: kai exēlthen ho logos houtos en holē tē Ioudaia peri autou kai pasē tē perichōrō — this news about Jesus spread throughout Judea and the surrounding country. Ho logos houtos (this word/report) — the same term (logos) used for the gospel proclamation; the report of a miraculous deed becomes the medium of wider proclamation. The spread from Galilee (Nain) throughout Judea and the surrounding regions prepares for John’s question (v.18): the news of Jesus has reached the prison in which John is held (3:20).
7.18: kai apēngeilan Iōannē hoi mathētai autou peri pantōn toutōn — John’s disciples told him about all these things. The connection between the Nain miracle and John’s question is explicit: John has received reports of Jesus’s activity and now asks the decisive messianic question. Proskalesamenos duo tinas tōn mathētōn autou ho Iōannēs (calling two of his disciples) — the formal dispatch of two messengers follows Deuteronomic legal procedure for testimony (Deut 19:15); John’s question is framed as a legal inquiry requiring a witnessed answer.
7.19: su ei ho erchomenos ē allon prosdokōmen — are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else? Ho erchomenos (the Coming One) is the messianic title from Psalm 118:26 (“Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD”) and Malachi 3:1 (“the Lord, whom you seek, will suddenly come to his temple”). John used the Coming One language in his own proclamation (3:16 — “one more powerful than I is coming”); he now asks whether Jesus is that figure or whether he should recalibrate his expectation. The question is not unbelief but eschatological discernment.
7.20: Iōannēs ho baptistēs apesteilen hēmas pros se legōn: su ei ho erchomenos ē allon prosdokōmen — John the Baptist sent us to you to ask: Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else? The repetition of John’s question by the messengers is a Lukan narrative technique of double confirmation (the messengers report the exact words). The formal identification of the sender — “John the Baptist” rather than simply “John” — signals the official character of the inquiry: this is John’s prophetic question, not a casual personal doubt.
7.21: en ekeinē tē hōra etherapeusen pollous apo nosōn kai mastegōn kai pneumatōn ponērōn kai typhlos pollois echarisato blepein — at that very time Jesus cured many who had diseases, sicknesses and evil spirits, and gave sight to many who were blind. Luke’s unique staging: Jesus performs miracles as John’s messengers watch. The answer to John’s question is not a verbal proposition but a demonstration. Mastegōn (scourges, plagues — literally whips; used metaphorically for chronic afflictions, also 8:29) indicates the severity of the conditions. The giving of sight to the blind is foregrounded because it is the first item in Jesus’s answer (v.22).
7.22: poreuphentes apangeilate Iōannē ha eidete kai ēkousate: typhloi anablepousin, chōloi peripatousin, leproi katharizontai kai kōphoi akouousin, nekroi egeirontai, ptōchoi euangelizontai — go back and report to John what you have seen and heard. The six miracle-categories are an Isaiah-mosaic: blind see (Isa 29:18; 35:5), lame walk (Isa 35:6), lepers cleansed (Isa 61?), deaf hear (Isa 35:5), dead raised (Isa 26:19), poor receive good news (Isa 61:1). The accumulation of Isaiah’s new-exodus signs in one answer confirms that Jesus is not merely a coming one but the eschatological deliverer of the servant songs. The dead raised and the poor evangelized are the climax — the first is the greatest miracle, the second is the most counter-cultural claim.
7.23: kai makarios estin hos ean mē skandalisthē en emoi — blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me. Skandalisthē (stumble, be offended — the skandalon is the trigger-stick of a trap, the stone of stumbling) — the blessing is for those who do not trip over the unexpected character of Jesus’s messiahship. The implicit warning is for John: the imprisoned prophet, who expected a fiery-axe Messiah (3:9, 17), must not be offended that the Coming One heals and proclaims rather than judges and burns. The beatitude is a gentle test.
7.24: kakamos tōn angelōn Iōannou ērxato legein pros tous ochlous peri Iōannou — after John’s messengers left, Jesus began to speak to the crowd about John. The timing is deliberate: Jesus defends John in John’s absence to the crowds who had questioned John’s integrity. Three rhetorical questions (ti exēlthate eis tēn erēmon theasasthai; — what did you go out to see?) use anaphora to build toward the climactic answer. The rhetorical questions are not mere oratory but a diagnostic of the crowds’ own inconsistency in rejecting John after flocking to him.
7.25: alla ti exēlthate idein; anthrōpon en malakoisa himatiois ēmphiesmenon — but what did you go out to see? A man dressed in fine clothes? Malakoisa himatiois (soft, luxurious garments) — contrasted with John’s camel-hair clothing (Mark 1:6). Hoi en himatismō endoxō kai tryphe hyparchontes en tois basileiois eisin (those in splendid clothing and luxury are in royal palaces) — the implicit contrast: John is the opposite of the courtier; his presence in the desert is by choice, his message not shaped by the need for royal favor. The crowd went out to a desert prophet, not a palace advisor.
7.26: alla ti exēlthate idein; prophētēn; nai, legō hymin, kai perissoteron prophētou — but what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet. The climactic answer: nai (yes) — John is indeed a prophet; kai perissoteron prophētou (and more than a prophet) — the “more than” (perissoteron, excess, surplus) signals a qualitative difference. All the OT prophets announced the coming day; John announces the coming person. He does not merely predict the Messiah — he is the Messiah’s immediate forerunner, stationed at the hinge between preparation and fulfillment.
7.27: houtos estin peri hou gegraptai: idou apostello ton angelon mou pro prosopou sou, hos kataskeuasei tēn hodon sou emprosthen sou — this is the one about whom it is written: I will send my messenger ahead of you who will prepare your way before you. The composite citation blends Malachi 3:1 (“I will send my messenger, who will prepare the way before me”) with Exodus 23:20 (“I will send an angel before you”). The pronoun shift in Malachi is significant: “before me” (in Malachi) becomes “before you” (the quotation addressed to Jesus) — identifying Jesus as the “me” of Malachi: YHWH coming to his temple.
7.28: legō hymin, meizōn en gennētois gynaikōn Iōannou oudeis estin: ho de mikroteros en tē basileia tou theou meizōn autou estin — I tell you, among those born of women no one is greater than John; yet the one who is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he. The superlative praise followed by the paradox: the greatest of the old order is surpassed by the least in the new. Mikroteros (least, smaller) — the comparative of mikros; the paradox is not about rank within the kingdom but about the eschatological privilege of kingdom membership. John stands at the threshold; those inside, however least, participate in the fulfilled reality he could only herald. This verse is a key statement of Lukan inaugurated eschatology.
7.29: kai pas ho laos akousas kai hoi telōnai edikaiōsan ton theon baptisthentes to baptisma Iōannou — all the people, even the tax collectors, acknowledged that God’s way was right, because they had been baptized by John. Luke’s parenthetical narrator-comment (vv.29-30) interprets the two groups’ responses to John. Edikaiōsan ton theon (declared God righteous, justified God) — the reverse of the judicial language: the people who were baptized pronounced that God was in the right in sending John and requiring repentance. The tax collectors are the paradigm case of the unexpected respondents to John’s message.
7.30: hoi de pharisaioi kai hoi nomikoi tēn boulēn tou theou ēthētēsan eis heautous mē baptisthentes hyp autou — but the Pharisees and the experts in the law rejected God’s purpose for themselves, because they had not been baptized by John. Tēn boulēn tou theou ēthētēsan (nullified/rejected God’s purpose/plan) — boulē (purpose, plan, counsel) is Luke’s term for the divine redemptive plan (Acts 2:23; 4:28; 20:27); to reject baptism is not merely personal religious choice but opposition to God’s overarching plan. Eis heautous (for themselves, against their own interest) — the rejection harms them, not the plan.
7.31: tini oun homoiōsō tous anthrōpous tēs geneas tautēs kai tini eisin homoioi — to what, then, can I compare the people of this generation? What are they like?
