Hē genea hautē (this generation) is a consistent term in Luke for the contemporary generation that has witnessed Jesus and John and refused both (11:29, 30, 31, 32, 50, 51; 17:25; 21:32). The comparison is a parabolic pronouncement of judgment shaped as a diagnostic: what is the character of a people who reject both John’s austerity and Jesus’s feasting?
7.32: homoioi eisin paidois tois en agorā kathēmenois kai prosfonousin allēlois ha legei: ēulēsamen hymin kai ouk ōrchēsasthe: ethrēnēsamen kai ouk eklausate — they are like children sitting in the marketplace calling out to each other: We played the flute and you did not dance; we sang a dirge and you did not cry. The children’s game-parable depicts sulky children who refuse to play either wedding or funeral. Ēulēsamen (we played the flute — the wedding music) and ethrēnēsamen (we sang a dirge — funeral lament) — the two basic communal responses to life’s transitions. The generation refuses both: no joy at the wedding of God’s kingdom, no sorrow at the funeral of their own spiritual condition.
7.33: elēlythen gar Iōannēs ho baptistēs mē esthiōn arton mēte pinōn oinon kai legete: daimonion echei — for John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say: He has a demon. The two rejections are diagnostic of the generation’s condition: John came with the ascetic intensity of a Nazirite and was accused of demonization. The refusal of food and wine (mē esthiōn arton mēte pinōn oinon) marks John’s prophetic separation; his critics explain this not as consecration but as disorder — daimonion echei (he has a demon).
7.35: kai edikaiōthē hē sophia apo pantōn tōn teknōn autēs — but wisdom is proved right by all her children. Hē sophia (wisdom, the divine plan embodied in both John and Jesus) is vindicated not by argument but by her children — those who have responded to John’s baptism and Jesus’s ministry. Luke’s version reads “all her children” (Matthew: “her deeds”); the children are the tax collectors and sinners who have responded, justifying God (v.29). The conclusion is ironic: the religious experts who should be wisdom’s advocates have rejected her; the disreputable have welcomed her.
7.36: ērōta de tis auton tōn pharisaiōn hina phagē met autou: kai eiselthōn eis ton oikon tou pharisaiou kateklithē — when one of the Pharisees invited Jesus to have dinner with him, he went to the Pharisee’s house and reclined at the table. Jesus accepts the dinner invitation of a Pharisee named Simon (v.40) — demonstrating that the love-your-enemies principle extends to eating with those who will challenge him. The dinner setting is a semi-public event; invited guests recline, but others could enter the dining room. The table is the arena of confrontation, hospitality, and forgiveness in this episode.
7.37: kai idou gynē hētis ēn en tē polei hamartōlos kai epignousa hoti katakeitai en tē oikia tou pharisaiou — a woman in that town who lived a sinful life learned that Jesus was eating at the Pharisee’s house. Gynē hamartōlos (a sinful woman) — the term designates social reputation rather than a specific sin; the town’s judgment of her is what matters for the narrative’s social dynamics. Epignousa (having learned, recognized — the prefix epi- intensifying the knowing) — her coming is deliberate and informed: she knows where Jesus is and goes. The alabaster jar of perfume (alabastron myrou) is an expensive luxury, her act an extravagant gift.
7.38: kai stasa opisō para tous podas autou klaiōusa, tois dakrysin ērxato brechein tous podas autou kai tais thrixin tēs kephalēs autēs exemassen — as she stood behind him at his feet weeping, she began to wet his feet with her tears, then wiped them with her hair, kissed them and poured perfume on them. The fourfold action (wet with tears, wipe with hair, kiss, pour perfume) is an escalating sequence of loving service. Using her hair to wipe his feet is a culturally transgressive act: a woman’s unbound hair in public was socially scandalous, but she uses her greatest social asset (her hair) as a towel for the teacher’s feet. The excess of her devotion corresponds to the excess of her forgiven debt.
7.39: idōn de ho pharisaios ho kalestas auton eipen en heautō legōn — when the Pharisee who had invited him saw this, he said to himself. Eipen en heautō (said within himself) — the Pharisee’s internal monologue is accessible to the reader and to Jesus, though not spoken aloud. Ei hē prophētēs ēn (if this man were a prophet) — the conditional is contrary-to-fact in the Pharisee’s mind: he has concluded Jesus is not a prophet because a prophet would know the woman’s sinful character and avoid her touch. The Pharisee’s logic assumes ritual purity requires physical distance from the impure.
7.40: kai apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen pros auton: Simōn, echō soi ti eipein — Jesus answered him: Simon, I have something to tell you. Jesus’s response to the unspoken thought (apokritheis — answered, though no question has been spoken aloud) is itself the answer to the Pharisee’s doubt: a true prophet does know — he reads the unspoken thought and addresses it. Didaskale, eipe (Teacher, speak) — Simon’s respectful form of address acknowledges Jesus as teacher even while questioning his prophetic status. The parable that follows is designed to lead Simon to judge rightly — and then to recognize that he has judged himself.
7.41: dyo chreopheiletat ēsan daneistē tini: ho heis ophile dēnaria pentakosia ho de heteros pentēkonta — two people owed money to a certain moneylender. One owed five hundred denarii, the other fifty. The parable’s debt-amounts are carefully calibrated: 500 denarii was roughly 500 days’ wages — almost a year and a half’s income; 50 denarii was a month and a half. Both are substantial debts impossible to repay quickly; the quantitative difference (10:1) is large enough to make the love-response proportional. The moneylender (daneistē) is not criticized; the parable is about gratitude, not usury.
7.42: mē echontōn de autōn apodounai amphotherois echarisato: tis oun autōn pleon agapēsei auton — neither of them had the money to pay him back, so he forgave the debts of both. Now which of them will love him more? Echarisato (he forgave, freely gave — charizomai shares the root of charis, grace) — the forgiveness is an act of grace, not a legal accommodation. The question Jesus asks Simon is designed to elicit self-condemnation: Simon will rightly identify the greater love as belonging to the greater debtor — and then discover that he himself has been playing the role of the smaller debtor who shows less love.
7.43: apokritheis Simōn eipen: hypolambano hoti hō to pleion echarisato — Simon replied: I suppose the one who had the bigger debt forgiven. Hypolambano (I suppose, I assume — with some reluctance in the formulation) — Simon senses the trap but cannot avoid answering honestly; the parable’s logic is inescapable. Orthōs ekrinas (you have judged rightly) — Jesus affirms the judgment; the word krinas (judged) will now be turned back on Simon: he has just judged the case, and the case is his own.
7.44: kai strapheis pros tēn gynaika tō Simōni ephē: blepeis tautēn tēn gynaika — turning toward the woman he said to Simon: Do you see this woman? The physical turn toward the woman while speaking to Simon is a deliberate rhetorical gesture: Jesus places the woman in Simon’s line of sight for a new kind of seeing. Blepeis tautēn (do you see this woman?) — Simon has seen the woman as a hamarotlos (sinner); Jesus asks him to see a person with a story of love and forgiveness. The three comparisons (no water / tears, no kiss / kissing, no oil / perfume) systematically expose Simon’s failure of hospitality.
7.45: philēma moi ouk edōkas: hautē de aph hēs eisēlthon ou dieleipen kataphilousa mou tous podas — you did not give me a kiss, but this woman, from the time I entered, has not stopped kissing my feet. The contrast is between the minimal courtesy (philēma, greeting kiss) that Simon withheld and the continuous devotion (ou dieleipen kataphilousa, she has not stopped kissing) that the woman has given from the moment of entry. Kataphilousa (kissing fervently, with intensity — the prefix kata- intensifying) — the woman’s devotion is not a single act but a sustained expression of love. The contrast exposes the relative magnitude of each one’s sense of their own debt.
7.46: elaiō tēn kephalēn mou ouk ēleipsas: hautē de myrou tous podas mou ēleipsen — you did not put oil on my head, but she has poured perfume on my feet. The third comparison is the most emphatic: anointing the head with common oil was a simple act of hospitality; anointing the feet with costly perfume is an extravagant reversal. The woman has given the more precious gift to the less valued body part. The oil/perfume contrast and the head/feet contrast together demonstrate the inversion of values that forgiveness creates: the forgiven one pours out her most precious resource at the lowest point.
7.47: hou charin legō soi: apheōntai hai hamartiai autēs hai pollai, hoti ēgapēsen poly — therefore I tell you, her many sins have been forgiven — as her great love has shown. The causal direction is debated: does her love cause the forgiveness, or does the forgiveness cause the love? The parable settles it: the debtor loves because the debt has been forgiven — not the reverse. Apheōntai (have been forgiven, divine perfect passive — already accomplished) — the forgiveness precedes the actions; her love is the evidence of prior forgiveness, not its cause. Hō de oligon aphietai, oligon agapāi (but whoever has been forgiven little loves little) — the contrast implies Simon has a large debt but a small sense of it.
7.48: eipen de autē: apheōntai sou hai hamartiai — then Jesus said to her: Your sins are forgiven. The public declaration of forgiveness (apheōntai sou hai hamartiai) is the gospel in miniature: Jesus speaks the divine word of release. The perfect passive apheōntai (have been forgiven, are forgiven — both completed and present) places the forgiveness in both God’s past act and the woman’s present status. The declaration is public, before the table of people who know her reputation — the social rehabilitation of a forgiven person is as important as the spiritual reality.
7.49: kai ērxanto hoi synanakeimenoi legein en heautois: tis houtos estin hos kai hamartias aphiēsin — the other guests began to say among themselves: Who is this who even forgives sins? The table-guests’ reaction echoes the scribes’ response to the paralytic’s healing (5:21): “Who can forgive sins but God alone?” The question tis houtos estin (who is this?) is the reader’s question throughout Luke — and the answer is precisely what the question implies: only God forgives sins; therefore Jesus is doing what only God does.
7.50: eipen de pros tēn gynaika: hē pistis sou sesōken se; poreuou eis eirēnēn — Jesus said to the woman: Your faith has saved you; go in peace. Hē pistis sou sesōken se (your faith has saved you) — the same formula Jesus uses for other healing-salvation events (8:48; 17:19; 18:42). Sōzō (save) encompasses both physical healing and spiritual salvation in Luke; the woman’s “saving” is the comprehensive restoration of her person before God and in the community. Poreuou eis eirēnēn (go into peace, go in peace) — the Hebrew shalom farewell; the peace is not merely the absence of conflict but the positive wholeness of restored relationship with God.

9.51: en de to sympleroysthai tas hemeras tes analepseos autou — when the days for his assumption were being fulfilled, he set his face to go to Jerusalem — analempsis (assumption, taking up) is a rare word used for Elijah's being taken up (2 Kgs 2:11 in Josephus) and for Jesus's ascension (Acts 1:11). Luke frames the Jerusalem journey (9:51-19:44) as a journey toward the assumption-through-death-resurrection-ascension; Jerusalem is the city where prophets die (13:33) and where the mission is fulfilled. Estersen to prosopon autou (set his face) echoes Isa 50:7 (the Servant who set his face like flint).
9.1: dunamin kai exousian — power and authority — Luke uses both terms for what the Twelve receive: dunamis (inherent power, capability) and exousia (delegated authority, the right to exercise power). The distinction matters: dunamis is operative force (used for miracles throughout Luke-Acts), while exousia is the authorization that legitimizes its use. Together they signal that the disciples' mission is a genuine extension of Jesus's own ministry, not a lesser imitation. Epi panta ta daimonia — over all the demons — the panta (all) is emphatic: no category of demonic opposition is exempt from the authority granted.
9.2: kēryssein tēn basileian tou theou kai iāsthai — to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal — the mission pairing is proclamation (kēryssō = herald, proclaim) and healing (iāomai = heal, cure). Luke's version of the commissioning omits Mark's explicit exorcism mandate but subsumes it in the authority over all demons (v.1). Kēryssein is the proclamation-verb of the herald who announces an authoritative message; it is not teaching (didaskō) or persuading (peithō) but announcing with the authority of the one who sent the herald.
9.3: mēden airein eis tēn hodon — take nothing for the road — the equipment list is the most restrictive in Luke: no staff (rhabdos), no bag (pēra), no bread, no money, no extra tunic. Matthew and Mark permit a staff; Luke does not — reflecting either a different source tradition or a stricter symbolic point about radical dependence on hospitality. The prohibition on taking equipment creates the conditions for the hosts' ministry: the disciples' need is what occasions the reciprocal relationship of proclamation and provision.
9.4: ekei menete kai ekeithen exerchesthe — remain there and from there depart — the single-house strategy prevents the disciples from "house-shopping" for better lodging, which would be an honor-competitive behavior inconsistent with their mission posture. Menō (remain, stay) is not inertia but committed presence — the proclamation happens through sustained relationship with the hosts, not drive-by announcement.
9.5: ton koniorton apo tōn podōn hymōn apotinassete — shake off the dust from your feet — the dust-shaking was a Jewish practice performed when returning from Gentile territory, as a sign of separation from Gentile impurity. Applied to a Jewish city that rejects the gospel, it signals that the city has placed itself outside the covenant. Eis marturion ep autous — as a testimony against them — marturion (testimony, witness) makes the gesture legal: it is a formal witness-act, not an emotional gesture of frustration.
9.6: euangelizomenoi kai therapenontes pantachou — proclaiming the good news and healing everywhere — the imperfect diērchonto (were going through) describes ongoing movement through the villages; pantachou (everywhere) is the geographic scope. The paired participles euangelizomenoi (proclaiming good news) and therapenontes (healing) are the practice-form of the commissioning (v.2); the disciples do what they were sent to do.
9.7: dietōrei — was perplexed, at a loss — diaporeō (be thoroughly at a loss, be perplexed) is one of Luke's psychological-state verbs (also Acts 2:12; 10:17); the dia- prefix intensifies the confusion. Herod's perplexity about Jesus's identity introduces the question that will be answered at Caesarea Philippi (v.20). The options he considers — John raised, Elijah, an ancient prophet — map the current popular categories that Peter's confession will transcend.
9.8: hōti Ēlias ephanē — that Elijah appeared — ephanē (aorist passive of phainō, appear, be manifested) is Luke's verb for supernatural appearance. The Elijah identification is grounded in Mal 4:5-6; prophētēs tis tōn archaiōn anestē (one of the ancient prophets rose) uses anistēmi (rise, stand up) — the same verb used for resurrection. The popular speculation that one of the ancient prophets had risen is an implicit resurrection belief that Jesus will claim for himself.
9.9: Iōannēn egō apekephalisa — John I beheaded — Herod's first-person past tense admission is psychologically vivid: he is the one who knows he executed John and therefore knows John is not the one doing miracles. Tis de estin houtos (but who is this?) is Herod's genuine question — the same question the disciples will answer (v.20) and that Luke's Gospel is structured to answer. Herod's curiosity (ezētei idein auton = he sought to see him) will be satisfied mockingly at the trial (23:8-11).
9.10: hupechorēsen kat idian eis polin kaloumenēn Bēthsaïda — withdrew privately to a city called Bethsaida — hupochōreō (withdraw, retire) is Luke's word for strategic retirement from public activity; it occurs at 5:16 for Jesus's wilderness prayer withdrawal. Bethsaida (Peter and Andrew's hometown, John 1:44) is on the northeastern shore of the lake; the withdrawal is both physical (crossing to the other side) and intentional (private time with the Twelve).
9.11: edexato autous kai elalei autois peri tēs basileias tou theou — he received them and spoke to them about the kingdom of God — dechomai (receive, welcome) is Luke's hospitality verb; Jesus receives the crowd that followed despite the withdrawal. Tous echontas chreian therapeias iāto — those who had need of healing he healed — the imperfect iāto describes ongoing healing activity throughout the day; the healings continue even in the context of kingdom teaching.
9.12: hē de hēmera ērxato klinein — the day began to decline — the poetic phrase hēmera klinein (day declining, literally "bending/inclining") is Lukan literary style at its most classical; it evokes the end of the day with a participial image rather than a simple time-reference. The disciples' concern for food and lodging is practical; their suggestion to dismiss the crowd uses apolys (release/dismiss) — the same word later used for Pilate releasing Jesus.
9.13: ei mēti poreuthentes hēmeis agorasōmen brōmata — unless we ourselves go and buy food — the disciples' suggestion involves personal effort and market purchase (agorazō = buy in the market); Jesus's response redirects from market dependence to miraculous provision. Plēn ē artoi pente kai ichthyes duo — five loaves and two fish — the contrast between the crowd's size (five thousand men, v.14) and the provision (five loaves, two fish) is the setup for the miracle's demonstration of divine superabundance.
9.14: kataklinate autous klisiās hōsei ana pentēkonta — make them recline in groups of about fifty — kataklinate (make lie down, make recline) is the reclining-at-table position for a formal meal; the feeding miracle is structured as a meal, not a distribution of emergency rations. Klisias (groups, literally "leaning-positions" from klinō) — the same root as the day-declining verb (v.12). The organization into groups of fifty reflects the Exodus wilderness camp structure (Exod 18:21; Num 31:14).
9.15: kai epoiēsan houtōs — and they did so — the disciples' compliance without protest or explanation is the narrative signal of faith in action: they organize five thousand people for a meal with five loaves and two fish, trusting the one who gave the command. Luke's terse narration of the compliance focuses the reader's attention on the action Jesus will take rather than the disciples' inner state.
9.16: labōn de tous pente artous kai tous duo ichthyas — taking the five loaves and the two fish — the fourfold eucharistic action: labōn (taking), anablepas eis ton ouranon (looking up to heaven), eulogēsen autous (blessed them), kateklasen (broke), edidou (was giving — imperfect, indicating repeated distribution). The fourfold pattern is identical to the Last Supper (22:19) and the post-resurrection meal at Emmaus (24:30). Luke presents every feeding from Jesus as eucharistically patterned.
9.17: ephagon kai echortasthēsan pantes — all ate and were satisfied — chortazō (satisfy, fill to capacity — from chortos = fodder, grass, the satisfaction of animals fully fed) signals complete fulfillment of hunger, not partial provision. Artō klasmatōn dōdeka kophinoi — twelve baskets of fragments — the twelve baskets exceed the original supply by any measure; the abundance that remains after the messianic feeding exceeds what was present before. Twelve echoes the twelve tribes and the twelve apostles.
9.18: en tō einai auton proseuchomenon kata monas — while he was praying alone — Luke introduces the Petrine confession scene with the prayer-notice that is his distinctive signature for major revelatory moments (3:21 — baptism; 6:12 — choosing the Twelve; 9:28-29 — Transfiguration; 22:41 — Gethsemane). The revelation of Jesus's identity to the disciples comes while he prays; the disclosure is embedded in the filial communion of the Son with the Father. Sunēsan autō hoi mathētai — his disciples were with him — the juxtaposition of his private prayer (kata monas) and the disciples' presence is characteristic Lukan paradox.
9.19: Iōannēn ton baptistēn, alloi de Ēlian, alloi de hoti prophētēs tis tōn archaiōn anestē — John the Baptist, others Elijah, others that one of the ancient prophets rose — the three popular identifications are the same as Herod's (vv.7-8), showing that the question is widespread. The use of anistēmi (rise up, resurrect) for the ancient prophet — the same verb for resurrection — shows that popular belief in prophetic return already had a resurrection dimension before the Petrine confession redefines the category.
9.20: Christon tou theou — the Christ of God — Luke's form of Peter's confession is unique: ton Christon tou theou (the Christ of God / the Messiah of God) rather than Mark's ho Christos (the Christ) or Matthew's ho Christos ho huios tou theou tou zōntos (the Christ, the Son of the living God). The genitive tou theou (of God) in Luke's version places the messianic title explicitly within the divine relationship — he is not merely the Messiah of Israel's expectation but the one specifically designated by God.
9.21: parangeilas autois mēdeni legein touto — commanding them to tell no one — parangelō (command with authority) is stronger than prostassō or keleuō; it is the military command-verb. The Messianic Secret command in Luke is characteristically terse — one verse — before moving immediately to the passion prediction and the cross-bearing call. The confession of messiahship must be interpreted through the cross before it can be proclaimed; the secret protects the confession from premature or misunderstood proclamation.
9.22: dei ton huion tou anthrōpou polla pathein — the Son of Man must suffer many things — the dei (must, it is necessary) governs the entire passion prediction; it is the divine necessity that runs through Luke's Gospel (2:49; 4:43; 13:33; 17:25; 24:7, 26, 44). apodokimasthēnai — be rejected — apodokimazō is the technical term for official rejection after examination (testing a coin and declaring it counterfeit); the elders, chief priests, and scribes will examine and formally declare Jesus unacceptable. apoktanthēnai kai tē tritē hēmera egerthēnai — killed and on the third day raised — both verbs are aorist passive infinitives governed by dei; the resurrection is as necessary as the death.
9.23: arato ton stauron autou kath hēmeran — take up his cross daily — Luke's unique addition of kath hēmeran (daily, day by day) transforms the cross-bearing from a one-time eschatological act to an ongoing daily practice. Mark and Matthew say simply "take up his cross"; Luke's kath hēmeran addresses the ongoing life of discipleship rather than only a final crisis. The daily cross is not a metaphor for general difficulty but the daily pattern of self-denial shaped by the master's self-giving. Arnēsasthō heauton — deny himself — arneomai (deny, renounce, disown) is the same verb used for Peter's denial of Jesus (22:34, 57); self-denial is the opposite movement to Peter's defensive self-assertion.
9.24: hos gar an thelē tēn psychēn autou sōsai apolesei autēn — whoever wishes to save his life will lose it — the psychē (life, soul, self) is the subject of the saving/losing paradox. The sōzō/apollymi (save/lose) antithesis uses the salvation and destruction vocabulary throughout Luke-Acts; the word sōzō will be used for healing, rescue, and ultimate salvation interchangeably. Heneken emou — for my sake — Luke lacks the additional "and the gospel's sake" of Mark 8:35; the Lukan focus is on personal attachment to Jesus rather than a program.
9.25: kerdēsas ton kosmon holon heauton de apolesas ē zēmiōtheis — having gained the whole world but losing or forfeiting himself — the kerdainō/zēmioō (gain/forfeit) pair uses commercial profit-loss vocabulary. Zēmioō (forfeit, suffer loss — a financial term for fining or penalizing) is the word Paul uses for the losses he counted as skubalon (refuse) compared to knowing Christ (Phil 3:8). The whole world (ton kosmon holon) as the maximum gain is set against the self (heauton) as the thing forfeited — the comparison exposes the asymmetry: the self is worth more than everything else combined.
9.26: hos gar an epaischunthē me kai tous emous logous — whoever is ashamed of me and my words — epaischunomai (be ashamed of, be disgraced by) is the honor-shame vocabulary of Mediterranean culture; shame is the social emotion of fearing others' judgment. Touto ho huios tou anthrōpou epaischunthēsetai — the Son of Man will be ashamed of him — the future reversal: the social shame of confessing Jesus now is repaid in cosmic shame before the divine tribunal then. Hotan elthē en tē doxē autou — when he comes in his glory — Luke adds the Father's glory and the holy angels alongside the Son's own glory.
9.27: eōs an idōsin tēn basileian tou theou — until they see the kingdom of God — the saying that some standing there will not taste death until they see the kingdom is Lukan without the parallel Markan qualifier "coming with power." Luke's version may point to Pentecost (Acts 2) as the kingdom's inaugural arrival in power, visible to those present at the conversation. Alēthōs legō hymin — truly I say to you — the amēn legō formula replaced by alēthōs legō is characteristic of Luke's translation of Semitic idiom into Greek idiom.
9.28: meta tous logous toutous hōsei hēmerai oktō — about eight days after these sayings — Luke's hōsei hēmerai oktō (about eight days) differs from Mark and Matthew's "six days." Various harmonization proposals exist; the most likely is that Luke counts inclusively (including the day of the saying and the day of the Transfiguration). The "about" (hōsei) softens the number. Anebē eis to oros proseuxasthai — he went up on the mountain to pray — the mountain is Luke's prayer setting for major revelations; the Transfiguration occurs in the context of the Son's prayer.
9.29: to eidos tou prosōpou autou heteron kai ho himatismos autou leukos exastraptōn — the appearance of his face became different and his clothing white, flashing like lightning — Luke's Transfiguration description uses eidos (form, appearance — not morphē) and heteron (different, other — not allos = another of the same kind); the face's eidos became heteron (a qualitatively different thing). The clothing is exastraptōn (flashing, shining like lightning — from exastraptō, a uniquely Lukan hapax), vivid meteorological light imagery. Mark uses stilbō (glitter, gleam); Matthew lampō (shine); Luke's exastraptōn is the most dramatic.
9.30: Mōysēs kai Ēlias — Moses and Elijah — the two figures who appear represent the Law (Mōysēs = Torah) and the Prophets (Ēlias = the prophetic corpus); their presence confirms that what Jesus is about to do in Jerusalem is the fulfillment of the entire OT witness. Both figures also experienced theophanies on Sinai/Horeb (Exod 33-34; 1 Kgs 19) and both departed from life in unusual ways (Deut 34:6; 2 Kgs 2:11). En doxē — in glory — Luke notes that Moses and Elijah appear in glory (doxa) — they share the transformed state that Jesus is manifesting.
9.31: tēn exodon autou hēn emellen plēroun en Ierousalēm — his departure which he was about to fulfill in Jerusalem — exodos is Luke's unique term for the subject of the Transfiguration conversation (absent from Mark and Matthew). The word carries enormous theological weight: (1) literal sense = departure, going out (the death-and-resurrection complex as a going out from this life); (2) Exodus allusion = Jesus's death as the new Exodus that fulfills Moses's foundational event; (3) plēroō (fulfill) confirms that the exodos is the completion of what was promised. The Transfiguration is a conversation about the cross framed as the fulfillment of the Exodus.
9.32: hoi de Petros kai hoi sun autō ēsan bebarēmenoi hypnō — Peter and those with him were weighed down with sleep — bebarēmenoi hypnō (weighed down with sleep — perfect passive participle of bareō = weigh down) foreshadows the Gethsemane scene (22:45) where they again fall asleep from grief (apo tēs lupēs). Diagrēgorēsantes de — remaining fully awake — diagrēgoreō (remain awake, be fully awake — intensive compound with dia-) describes the state of watchful wakefulness they achieve before the vision ends.
9.33: epistata epistata — Master, Master — Luke uses epistata (master, overseer — the one standing over) rather than Mark's Rabbi or Matthew's kyrie for Peter's address; epistata occurs six times in Luke and nowhere else in the NT. The doubled address (epistata epistata) signals agitation and urgency. Skenās treis — three tents/booths — skēnē (tent, booth, tabernacle) connects to the Feast of Booths (Sukkot, the tabernacle festival) and to the tabernacle of the divine presence — Peter's instinct to build booths may reflect his sense that the eschatological Sukkot has arrived.
9.34: nephelē kai epeskiazen autous — a cloud came and was overshadowing them — episkiazō (overshadow, cast a shadow over) is the LXX word for the Shekinah cloud covering the tabernacle (Exod 40:35). Luke uses the same word for the Spirit's overshadowing at the annunciation (1:35). The cloud is the divine presence-vehicle throughout the wilderness narrative; its appearance at the Transfiguration signals that what is happening is a new Sinai theophany. Ephobeθēsan de en tō eiselthein autous eis tēn nephelēn — they were afraid as they entered the cloud — entering the cloud = entering the divine presence.
9.35: ho huios mou ho eklelegmenos — my Son, the Chosen/Elect One — Luke's Transfiguration voice is unique: eklelegmenos (perfect passive participle of eklegō = choose, select, elect) rather than the agapētos (beloved) of Mark and Matthew. The eklelegmenos variant is supported by the Bodmer papyrus (p75), Sinaiticus, and Vaticanus. The "Chosen One" resonates with the Servant of Isa 42:1 (eklektos in LXX) and with Luke's elective theology throughout Luke-Acts. autou akouete — listen to him — the imperative akouete echoes Deut 18:15 (the prophet-like-Moses whom Israel must hear); the command identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of the Mosaic prophet-promise.
9.36: esigēsan kai oudeni apēngeilan en ekeinais tais hēmerais ouden — they were silent and told no one in those days anything — Luke's concluding secrecy notice uses sigaō (be silent, keep silent — the same word for Elizabeth's husband Zechariah struck silent, 1:20; and for the disciples at the triumphal entry, 19:40). The double negative (oudeni... ouden) and the time limitation (en ekeinais tais hēmerais) are both emphatic — total silence, for now. The resurrection will be the moment when silence ends.
9.37: katelthen apo tou orous — came down from the mountain — the descent from the mountain after a theophany mirrors Moses's descent from Sinai (Exod 32:15; 34:29). The scene that immediately follows — the helpless disciples unable to cast out a demon from the boy — creates the Transfiguration's ironic frame: the glory on the mountain meets the failure in the valley. The contrast is the narrative argument for the cross: divine glory does not prevent the Son of Man's suffering but leads through it.
9.38: edeēthēn tōn mathētōn sou hina ekbalōsin auto kai ouk ēdunēthēsan — I begged your disciples to cast it out and they could not — deomai (beg, plead, pray) is the petition verb used for urgent supplication; the father has begged (edeēθēn = aorist of earnest request) and the disciples have failed. Ouk ēdunēθēsan (they were not able — aorist passive of dunamai) echoes the dunamis (power) given to them in v.1; the power that was given has not been effectively deployed here.
9.39: pneuma lambanei auton kai exaiphnēs krazei kai sparassei auton — a spirit seizes him and suddenly he cries out and convulses him — lambanō (seize, take hold of) for demonic possession is vivid — the spirit is possessing agent rather than passive presence. Sparassō (convulse, tear) describes violent physical agitation. The symptom description (crying, convulsing, foaming, barely departing) in Luke's account is among the most detailed in the Synoptics, possibly reflecting Luke's medical interest in symptomatology.
9.40: kai edeeθēn tōn mathētōn sou hina ekbalōsin — I begged your disciples to cast it out — the repetition of the begging and the failure creates the father's lament: he turned to the disciples as the authorized representatives of Jesus's power (v.1) and found them unable. The failure sets up Jesus's lament over the faithless generation (v.41) and then the successful exorcism (v.42), demonstrating that the disciples' failure was not due to any limit in Jesus's authority but in their own application of it.
9.41: ō genea apistos kai diestrammenē — O faithless and twisted generation — apistos (faithless, unbelieving) and diestrammenō (twisted, perverted — perfect passive participle of diastrephō = twist, distort) echo Deut 32:5 (LXX: genea skolia kai diestrammenē = a crooked and twisted generation). Jesus's lament applies the Mosaic wilderness-generation indictment to his contemporaries. Heōs pote esomai pros hymas kai anexomai hymōn — how long shall I be with you and bear with you — the language of divine patience exhausted: anechomai (bear with, endure, tolerate) describes the sustained forbearance of the Son of God in the presence of persistent unbelief.
9.42: ērrēxen auton to daimonion kai esparagmato — the demon convulsed him and threw him down violently — rhēgnymi (tear, dash, convulse) and sparassō (convulse, tear) describe the demon's violent final resistance before departure. Epetimēsen de Iēsous tō pneumati tō akathartō kai iāsato ton paida — Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit and healed the boy — the exorcism is accomplished with the rebuke alone (epetimēsen = rebuke authoritatively); no extended ritual, no prayer, no struggle. Apedōken auton tō patri autou — he gave him back to his father — the restoration language: the boy is returned to his father, as a healing gift.
9.43: eplasson de pantes epi tē megaleiōtēti tou theou — all were astonished at the majesty of God — ekplēssō (strike out of one's senses, astonish) with the imperfect describes the crowd's ongoing astonishment. Megaleiōtēs (majesty, magnificence — from megas = great) is a rare word used for divine grandeur (also Acts 19:27 for Artemis's greatness). The crowd's attribution of the exorcism to the majesty of God rather than to Jesus directly is the Lukan pattern: healings and miracles lead to glorifying God.
9.44: thesthe hymeis eis ta ōta hymōn tous logous toutous — let these words sink into your ears — the idiom tithēmi eis ta ōta (place into the ears) is a Semitic expression for focused, attentive hearing (cf. Exod 17:14; Job 37:2 — "let my ears take in the vastness"). The command emphasizes the disciples' need to truly receive the passion prediction they are about to hear — not just process it cognitively. ho huios tou anthrōpou mellei paradidosthai eis cheiras anthrōpōn — the Son of Man is about to be handed over into human hands — mellō (be about to, imminently going to) + paradidōmi (hand over, betray) is the second passion prediction in Luke.
9.45: ēgnooun to rēma touto kai ēn parakekalummenon ap autōn — they did not understand this word and it was hidden from them — the double explanation of non-understanding is unique to Luke: (1) they did not understand (ēgnooun, imperfect of agnoeō = not know); (2) ēn parakekalummenon ap autōn (it was hidden/covered from them — perfect passive participle suggesting a deliberate divine concealment). The passive indicates that the hiddenness is not merely cognitive failure but divine withholding — the disciples cannot understand the passion until the resurrection opens their minds (24:45). Kai ephobounto erōtēsai auton peri tou rēmatos — they were afraid to ask him about this word — deos (fear/awe) prevents inquiry; the subject is too weighty.
9.46: eisēlthen de dialogismos en autois — a dispute entered into them — dialogismos (reasoning, debate, dispute) is Luke's word for the internal deliberation that is usually negative — the reasoning of unbelief (1:29; 2:35; 5:22; 12:17). Here the dispute (to tis an eiē meizōn autōn = who might be the greatest among them) comes immediately after the second passion prediction — the disciples respond to the announcement of the Son of Man's humiliation by debating their own greatness. The irony is sharp and Lukan.
9.47: ho Iēsous eidōs ton dialogismon tēs kardias autōn — Jesus, knowing the reasoning of their hearts — eidōs (knowing — perfect participle of oida) with ton dialogismon tēs kardias is the omniscience-of-hearts theme that Luke develops throughout (2:35; 5:22; 6:8; 7:39-40; 9:47). Jesus does not ask; he knows. Epilabomenos paidiou — taking a child — epilambanō (take hold of, grasp) suggests an intentional, deliberate act of drawing the child forward; the child is the object lesson placed in the center of the greatness debate.
9.48: hos ean dexētai touto to paidion epi tō onomati mou — whoever receives this little child in my name — dechomai (receive, welcome) is Luke's hospitality verb; the child is to be received as a guest, with honor. The chain of receiving: child → Christ → the One who sent Christ → the Father. ho gar mikroteros en pasin hymin hyparchōn houtos estin megas — for the one who is least among all of you, that one is great — mikroteros (comparative of mikros = small, little) as superlative in Koine Greek: "the smallest/least" — the one who occupies the lowest social position in the group is the one who is truly great. The reversal redefines greatness by the criterion of humble service rather than social rank.
9.49: kōlyomen auton hoti ouk akolouthei meth hēmōn — we forbade him because he does not follow with us — John's report reveals the disciples' proprietary attitude toward exorcistic authority: only those who follow with us (the inner circle) are legitimate. The meth hēmōn (with us) is the exclusive criterion John applies. The debate about greatness (v.46) and the attempt to control who exercises Jesus's power are two sides of the same status-anxiety.
9.50: mē kōlyete, hos gar ouk estin kath hymōn hyper hymōn estin — do not forbid him, for the one who is not against you is for you — the criterion is reversed from the disciples' criterion: instead of requiring explicit membership in the following (meth hēmōn = with us), Jesus applies the negative test: whoever is not actively opposing is effectively supporting. The principle is the opposite of the Matthaean version ("whoever is not with me is against me," Matt 12:30) — both logions express the eschatological urgency of the kingdom, but from different angles.
9.52: apesteilen aggelous pro prosōpou autou — he sent messengers before his face — apostelloō (send as apostles/messengers) with pro prosōpou autou (before his face) echoes Exod 23:20 LXX (apostello ton angelon mou pro prosōpou sou — I will send my angel before your face) and Mal 3:1; the messianic journey is prepared by advance agents just as Israel's wilderness journey was prepared by the divine messenger. The Samaritan mission anticipates the Acts mission (Acts 8).
9.53: hoti to prosōpon autou ēn poreuomenon eis Ierousalēm — because his face was going toward Jerusalem — Luke's explanation of the Samaritans' refusal: the direction of travel (toward Jerusalem) is the offense. The historic Samaritan-Jewish enmity (cf. John 4:9, 20) made Samaritans unwilling to assist pilgrims headed for the Jerusalem temple, which they regarded as the wrong worship site. The phrase to prosōpon... poreuomenon (his face traveling) personifies the resolve established in v.51.
9.54: kyrie, theleis eipōmen pyr katabibnai ap ouranou kai analōsai autous — Lord, do you want us to call down fire from heaven to consume them — the disciples' suggestion cites the Elijah precedent (2 Kgs 1:10-12, where Elijah called fire on the captain and fifty soldiers). Hōs kai Ēlias epoiēsen — as Elijah also did (some manuscripts add this phrase). The comparison is theologically revealing: the disciples are thinking in terms of the old prophetic paradigm of judgment; Jesus's response will reframe the mission.
9.55: strapheis de epetimēsen autois — turning, he rebuked them — strephō (turn) + epitimaō (rebuke) is the same sequence Jesus uses in the exorcisms (v.42) and in rebuking the fever (4:39). The disciples' fire-calling suggestion receives the same authoritative rebuke as the unclean spirit — their retaliatory impulse is a spiritual disorder equivalent to demonic opposition. Some manuscripts add: "You do not know what kind of spirit you are of; for the Son of Man did not come to destroy men's lives but to save them" — a textually disputed but thematically accurate expansion.
9.56: kai eporeuthēsan eis heteran kōmēn — and they went to another village — the terse narrative resolution: no argument, no explanation, simply departure to another village. The pragmatic realism of Jesus's ministry strategy — when rejected, move on — matches the commissioning instruction (v.5). The Samaritan rejection does not halt the journey; it is absorbed into the Travel Narrative's forward momentum toward Jerusalem.
9.57: akolouthēsō soi hopou ean apierchē — I will follow you wherever you go — the unconditional offer of discipleship (hopou ean apierchē = wherever you depart) receives the Son of Man saying: foxes have holes and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head. The cost of following is not theoretical piety but actual homelessness and instability. Poreumenōn autōn en tē hodō — as they were going along the road — Luke's Travel Narrative is the frame; these encounters happen on the road to Jerusalem.
9.58: ho de huios tou anthrōpou ouk echei pou tēn kephalēn klinē — but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head — pou klinē tēn kephalēn (where to lay the head) is idiomatic for having a settled dwelling place. The saying's Christological weight is the title: huios tou anthrōpou (Son of Man — the Danielic figure who receives universal dominion) has no earthly home, no property, no settled shelter. The contrast between the animal world's natural provision and the Son of Man's deliberate homelessness is stark: the one who rules all creation accepts voluntary displacement for the sake of his mission.
9.59: epitrepson moi prōton apelthonti thapsai ton patera mou — allow me first to go and bury my father — prōton (first) is the problem word: burying the father is the most urgent filial duty in Jewish culture (superseding Torah study for the Rabbis); the candidate is asking for temporary priority of family obligation. Jesus's response ("Let the dead bury their own dead") is one of his most shocking sayings and demands interpretation: either the father has not yet died (the candidate is deferring discipleship until after his natural inheritance) or Jesus is using hyperbole to signal the absolute priority of kingdom over family obligation.
9.60: aphes tous nekrous thapsai tous heautōn nekrous — let the dead bury their own dead — the paradox deploys nekros (dead) in two senses: the physically dead can be buried by the spiritually dead (those outside the kingdom); those who are alive to the kingdom have an urgent mission that supersedes the burial duty. Su de apelthōn diaggelle tēn basileian tou theou — you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God — diaggellō (proclaim throughout, announce widely) is an intensive compound of angellō; the second candidate is explicitly commanded to the proclamation mission. The call to discipleship supersedes even the most sacred of family obligations.
9.61: epitrepson moi prōton apotaxasthai tois eis ton oikon mou — allow me first to say farewell to those at home — the third candidate's request echoes Elisha's request to Elijah (1 Kgs 19:20: "Let me kiss my father and mother goodbye") — and Elijah granted that request. Jesus denies the equivalent here: the eschatological urgency of the kingdom exceeds even the Elijah-Elisha precedent. Oudeis epibas cheira ep arotron — no one putting a hand to the plow — the plow idiom appears in v.62's response.
9.62: oudeis epibas cheira ep arotron kai blepōn eis ta opisō euthetos estin tē basileia tou theou — no one who puts a hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God — arotron (plow) requires eyes forward to plow a straight furrow; looking back (eis ta opisō) produces crooked furrows and wasted effort. Euthetos (fit, suitable, useful) is the practical-effectiveness term — the backward-looking disciple is simply not serviceable for the kingdom task. The image echoes Lot's wife (Gen 19:26) as the classic OT backward-looking figure; plowing straight furrows requires the same forward commitment as following the eschatological Jesus toward Jerusalem.

10.18: etheoroun ton Satanan hos astrapen ek tou ouranou pesonta — I was watching Satan fall like lightning from heaven — the aorist pesonta (having fallen) suggests a definitive past event rather than a process; the 70's mission success is the visible sign of an already-accomplished heavenly event. Astrapen (lightning) evokes the sudden, complete, and irresistible nature of the fall. The statement is Jesus's interpretation of the exorcism-mission: what the disciples experience as individual deliverances is the symptom of a decisive cosmic defeat of the enemy already accomplished through Jesus's authority.
10.21: exomologoumai soi pater kyrie tou ouranou kai tes ges, hoti apekrupsas tauta apo sophon kai synetōn kai apekalypsas auta nepiois — I thank you Father Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little ones — the prayer is Luke's unique Trinitarian pericope: Jesus addresses the Father, the Spirit's enabling is implied by the eschatological joy (egalliasthato en to pneumati to hagio, v.21), and Jesus speaks as the Son who alone knows the Father (v.22). Nepiois (little children, infants, the simple) are the recipients of revelation because divine grace rather than human capacity is its source.
10.1: meta de tauta anedeixen ho kyrios heterous hebdomekontadyo — after this the Lord appointed seventy-two others. The textual variant between hebdomekonta (70, Alexandrian tradition) and hebdomekontadyo (72, Western tradition) is significant: 70 echoes the 70 elders of Num 11:16-17 and the Table of Nations in Gen 10 (70 in Hebrew, 72 in LXX); 72 = 6 x 12 (extending the Twelve’s mission). Both readings have ancient support. The larger mission (this group unique to Luke) extends the Twelve’s commission into the universal scope: every town and place where Jesus was about to go.
10.2: ho men therismos polys, hoi de ergatai oligoi — the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few. The harvest metaphor is eschatological: in the OT, harvest-day is a metaphor for the judgment/ingathering of nations (Isa 27:12; Joel 3:13; Jer 51:33). Deēthēte oun tou kyriou tou therismou (ask the Lord of the harvest) — the Lord of the harvest is YHWH (Amos 9:13); the mission workers are sent by God, not recruited by human organization. The command to pray for more workers precedes the sending, establishing prayer as the engine of mission.
10.3: hypagete; idou apostellō hymas hōs arnas en mesō lykōn — go! I am sending you out like lambs among wolves. Apostellō (I am sending) is the commissioning verb that gives the apostoloi their name; the authority behind the sending is Jesus himself. Arnas en mesō lykōn (lambs among wolves) — the vulnerability is real and intentional; the mission is not accomplished through strength or cunning but through the willingness to be lamb-like. Isaiah 11:6 (the wolf and the lamb lying together) provides the eschatological background: the wolf-and-lamb relationship will be reversed in the kingdom, but not yet.
10.4: mē bastasete ballantion, mē pēran, mē hypodēmata — do not take a purse, bag, or sandals. The mission equipment list is deliberately reduced to nothing: no money-pouch (ballantion), no traveler’s bag (pēra), no extra sandals (hypodēmata). The restriction signals complete dependence on the hospitality of those who receive the message. Mēdena kata tēn hodon aspasēsthe (greet no one on the road) — the urgency of the mission precludes the extended social rituals of ancient greeting; the peace-greeting is reserved for the households that will receive it.
10.5: eis hēn d an eiselthēte oikian, prōton legete: eirēnē tō oikō toutō — when you enter a house, first say: Peace to this house. Eirēnē (peace, shalom) as the mission greeting is not a casual salutation but a performative pronouncement: the peace of the kingdom is being spoken over the household. The greeting is the mission in miniature: what the kingdom brings is shalom — the comprehensive wellbeing of restored covenant relationship. Whether the greeting remains or returns depends on what follows in the household’s response.
10.6: kai ean ekei ē huios eirēnēs — if a son of peace is there. Huios eirēnēs (son of peace) is a Semitic idiom for a person whose character is defined by peace — one prepared to receive the peace-word. The idiom is unique to Luke’s version of the mission discourse and may reflect Aramaic source material. Epanapaēsetai ep auton hē eirēnē hymōn (your peace will rest upon him) — the peace is personified as a presence that settles or departs; it has a locative quality, finding its place in a receptive person.
10.7: en autē de tē oikia menete, esthiontes kai pinontes ta par autōn — stay there, eating and drinking whatever they give you. The instructions against moving house-to-house prevent both the exploitation of multiple hospitalities and the social awkwardness of being a demanding guest. Axios gar ho ergatēs tou misthou autou estin (the worker deserves his wages) — the principle cited here (also 1 Tim 5:18; 1 Cor 9:14) grounds the support of mission workers in a principle of just compensation: the harvest-work has a legitimate wage. The saying may derive from an existing proverb that Jesus quotes as principle.
10.8: kai eis hēn an polin eiserchēsthe kai dechontai hymas, esthiete ta paratithemena hymin — when you enter a town and are welcomed, eat what is set before you. The prohibition on dietary selectivity (ta paratithemena, whatever is put before you) has early-church implications: eating whatever is served without inquiry into its ritual status (cf. 1 Cor 10:27) anticipates the Gentile mission, where Mosaic food laws would create barriers to table fellowship. Luke’s version of the mission instruction may be shaped by post-resurrection missionary experience.
10.9: kai therapeuete tous en autē astheneis kai legete autois: ēngiken eph hymas hē basileia tou theou — heal the sick there and tell them: The kingdom of God has come near to you. The mission action and the mission proclamation are a unity: healing is the visible sign of the kingdom’s nearness, and the verbal announcement interprets the sign. Ēngiken eph hymas (has come near upon/to you — the eph intensifying the arrival) — the kingdom’s nearness is not a vague temporal proximity but an arrival that touches the specific persons being addressed. The second-person hymas makes the announcement personal and local.
10.10: eis hēn d an polin eiselthēte kai mē dechontai hymas — but when you enter a town and are not welcomed. The asymmetry between welcome and rejection is a mission reality; the instructions for rejection are as important as those for welcome. The dust-wiping action (ton koniorton ton kollēthenta hēmin ek tēs poleōs hymōn apomassometha hymin) that follows is a prophetic symbolic act: Jews shook dust from their sandals when leaving Gentile territory to keep the holy land clean; the mission workers perform the reverse, treating the rejecting Jewish town as if it were pagan territory.
10.11: plēn touto ginōskete hoti ēngiken hē basileia tou theou — yet be sure of this: the kingdom of God has come near. The kingdom’s nearness is proclaimed even to those who reject it: the announcement is not contingent on reception. The combination of the dust-wiping sign of judgment and the proclamation of the kingdom’s nearness is deliberately paradoxical: the kingdom comes near even to those who refuse it, which is precisely what makes their rejection so serious. The nearness increases both the opportunity and the culpability.
10.12: legō hymin hoti Sodumois en tē hēmera ekeinē anektoteron estai ē tē polei ekeinē — it will be more bearable for Sodom on that day than for that town. Hē hēmera ekeinē (that day) is the eschatological day of judgment (Amos 5:18; Joel 1:15; Isa 2:12). Sodom’s sin was hospitality-failure (Gen 19) — the rejecting towns replicate Sodom’s sin in refusing the messengers who bring the kingdom. The comparison (Sodom vs. the towns that rejected Jesus’s mission) is the most severe judgment-saying in the tradition: greater light means greater accountability.
10.13: ouai soi, Chorazin! ouai soi, Bēthsaïda! — woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! The woe-oracle (ouai) against Galilean towns is unique in its retrospective naming of miracles not recorded in the Gospels (hoti ei en Tyrō kai Sidōni egenēthēsan hai dynameis hai genomenai en hymin). Dynameis (miracles, acts of power) performed in Chorazin and Bethsaida are not narrated in Luke; Jesus’s statement implies a much larger body of activity than the Gospel records. Tyre and Sidon, historically condemned by the prophets (Ezek 28-29; Isa 23), are now presented as more responsive than the privileged Galilean towns.
10.14: Tyrō kai Sidōni anektoteron estai en tē krisei ē hymin — it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment than for you. The comparative judgment principle (anektoteron, more bearable) assumes graduated accountability: judgment is proportional to the revelation received. The Gentile cities of the Phoenician coast, without Israel’s covenant history, will fare better than Israelite towns that witnessed the kingdom’s signs and rejected them. The principle challenges ethnic covenant presumption: proximity to Jesus increases responsibility.
10.15: kai sy, Kapharnaoum, mē heōs ouranou hypsōthēsē; heōs tou hadou katabibasthēsē — and you, Capernaum, will you be lifted to the heavens? No, you will go down to Hades. The language is a deliberate echo of Isaiah 14:13-15 (the taunt against the king of Babylon: “You said in your heart, ‘I will ascend to the heavens’... but you are brought down to the realm of the dead”). Capernaum is Jesus’s base of operations (4:31) — the most privileged town in Galilee in terms of exposure to his ministry. The Babel-reversal pattern (exaltation presumed, descent decreed) is the judgment on privilege misused.
10.16: ho akouōn hymōn emou akouei kai ho atheton hymas eme athetei, ho de eme atheton athetei ton aposteilanta me — whoever listens to you listens to me; whoever rejects you rejects me; but whoever rejects me rejects him who sent me. The chain of representation (disciple → Jesus → the Father) is the apostolic authority principle: the apostolos (sent one) represents the sender’s person and authority. Athetei (rejects, sets aside as invalid — the verb used for nullifying a legal agreement) — to reject the mission workers is not a personal slight but a rejection of the divine sending-authority behind them.
10.17: hypestrepsan de hoi hebdomekontadyo meta charas legontes: kyrie, kai ta daimonia hypotassetai hēmin en tō onomati sou — the seventy-two returned with joy saying: Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name! The exorcistic authority of the mission workers (hypotassetai, present tense — is being subjected) is exercised en tō onomati sou (in your name) — the name as the medium of delegated authority. Jesus’s response will redirect the joy from power-over-demons to the greater gift: names written in heaven (v.20).
10.19: idou dedōka hymin tēn exousian tou patein epanō opheōn kai skorpiōn kai epi pasan tēn dynamin tou echthrou — I have given you authority to tread on snakes and scorpions and over all the power of the enemy. Exousia (authority, right to act) is distinguished from dynamis (power); the disciples have been given the authority that underlies the power. The serpent-and-scorpion imagery invokes Psalm 91:13 (trampling the lion and the serpent) and the Genesis 3:15 enmity between the seed and the serpent. Ouden hymas ou mē adikēsē (nothing will harm you) — the promise is mission-specific, not a general immunity guarantee.
10.20: plēn en toutō mē chairete hoti ta pneumata hymin hypotassetai — however, do not rejoice that the spirits submit to you. The redirected joy is a priority statement: exorcistic success is real but penultimate. Chairete de hoti ta onomata hymōn eggegraptai en tois ouranois (rejoice that your names are written in heaven) — the heavenly enrollment recalls the Book of Life (Exod 32:32; Ps 69:28; Dan 12:1; Rev 3:5). The ultimate ground of joy is not what the disciples accomplish but what God has already done: inscribing their names in the divine ledger. Power over demons is a gift; heavenly registration is an identity.
10.22: panta moi paredothē hypo tou patros mou — all things have been committed to me by my Father. The Johannine-sounding logion (also Matt 11:27) in the Synoptic tradition: paredothē (has been handed over, entrusted — the same root as paradidōmi used for betrayal and for the Lord’s Supper tradition) emphasizes the totality and the relational dynamic of the transfer. Oudeis ginōskei tis estin ho huios ei mē ho patēr kai tis estin ho patēr ei mē ho huios — the mutual exclusivity of Sonship-knowing (only the Father knows the Son fully; only the Son knows the Father fully) is the ground of revelatory dependence: encountering the Father requires going through the Son.
10.23: makarioi hoi ophthalmoi hoi blepontes ha blepete — blessed are the eyes that see what you see. The beatitude on the disciples’ sight (privately, kat idian, said to the Twelve) is the positive counterpart to v.15’s woe on Capernaum. Ophthalmoi blepontes (eyes that see) — the eyes of the disciples who see the kingdom arriving are blessed with a privilege that the greatest OT figures could only anticipate. The sight is not merely physical presence but kingdom-recognition: they see the signs and understand what they signify.
10.24: polloi prophētai kai basileis ēthelēsan idein ha hymeis blepete kai ouk eidan — many prophets and kings wanted to see what you see but did not see it. The prophets and kings of Israel desired the messianic era with anticipatory longing: Abraham desired to see Jesus’s day and saw it and was glad (John 8:56); Daniel longed for the interpretation of his visions (Dan 12:8). The disciples’ blessing is not their own achievement but their historical position: they are the generation at the hinge of the ages when what was promised has arrived.
10.25: kai idou nomikos tis anestē ekpeirazōn auton legōn: didaskale, ti poiēsas zōēn aiōnion klēronomiō — an expert in the law stood up to test him: Teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Ekpeirazōn (testing, putting to the test — with hostile intent, cf. the Deuteronomy 6:16 prohibition on testing God) frames the encounter as an adversarial one. Zōēn aiōnion klēronomiō (inherit eternal life) — the question is correctly framed as inheritance (received from a superior, the Father) rather than achievement (earned by works). Jesus turns the question back on the questioner: what does the Law itself say?
10.26: ho de eipen pros auton: en tō nomō ti gegraptai; pōs anaginōskeis — he replied: What is written in the Law? How do you read it? Jesus’s counter-question is a rabbinical technique: direct the student to the source and ask for their interpretation. Pōs anaginōskeis (how do you read it? — literally, how do you recognize/read it) — the emphasis is on the hermeneutical method: the letter of the text and its interpretive application. Jesus grants that the answer to the life-question lies within the Torah itself; his role is to question the mode of application.
10.27: ho de apokritheis eipen: agapēseis kyrion ton theon sou ex holēs tēs kardias sou — he answered: Love the Lord your God with all your heart. The combination of Deuteronomy 6:5 (the Shema love-command) and Leviticus 19:18 (neighbor-love) as the Torah’s double summary is known from rabbinic tradition; Jesus affirms the lawyer’s answer as correct. The four faculties (kardia, psychē, ischus, dianoia — heart, soul, strength, mind) are Luke’s expansion of the Deuteronomy text’s three (heart, soul, strength), emphasizing total engagement of all human capacities in the love of God.
10.28: orthos apekrithēs; touto poiei kai zēsē — you have answered correctly; do this and you will live. Jesus’s validation of the double love-command is without qualification. Touto poiei (do this — present imperative, continuous action) and zēsē (you will live) — the connection between doing and living is the Leviticus 18:5 formula (“keep my decrees and ordinances; the person who obeys them will live”). The simplicity of the answer is precisely what the lawyer finds uncomfortable: the question “who is my neighbor?” follows because the answer is too simple, too demanding.
10.29: ho de thelōn dikaiōsai heauton eipen pros ton Iēsoun: kai tis estin mou ho plesion — but he wanted to justify himself, so he asked Jesus: And who is my neighbour? Thelōn dikaiōsai heauton (wanting to justify himself) — the self-justification motive is Luke’s psychological insight into the question’s origin. The question “who is my neighbor?” is a definitional question seeking to limit the obligation: if neighbor can be defined narrowly (fellow Jew, covenant-community member), the love-command has manageable scope. Jesus’s parable will flip the question from “who is my neighbor?” to “who acted as neighbor?” — from definition to practice.
10.30: hypolabōn ho Iēsous eipen: anthrōpos tis katebainen apo Ierousalēm eis Ierichō — Jesus replied: A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho. The Jerusalem-to-Jericho road descends roughly 3,300 feet in 17 miles through rocky, robber-prone terrain — the detail is geographically precise. The man is unidentified (anthrōpos tis, a certain man) — no ethnic marker, no religious status; the story’s universalizing strategy begins with the nameless victim. Lēstais periepesan (fell into the hands of robbers) — the aorist signals the sudden, violent nature of the attack; the robbers strip, beat, and leave him hēmithanē (half-dead).
10.31: kata synkyrion de hiereus tis katebainen en tē hodō ekeinē, kai idōn auton antiparēlthen — a priest happened to be going down the same road, and when he saw the man, he passed by on the other side. Kata synkyrion (by coincidence, by chance — a hapax in the NT) indicates an unplanned encounter; the priest did not seek this situation. The priest’s reason for passing is not stated; interpreters have suggested concern about ritual impurity from contact with a potentially dead body (Lev 21:1-3). But Jesus does not supply the reason — the parable focuses on the action: seeing and passing by, rather than seeing and responding.
10.32: homoiōs de kai Leuitēs kata ton topon elthōn kai idōn antiparēlthen — so too, a Levite, when he came to the place and saw him, passed by on the other side. The Levite’s response mirrors the priest’s; the repetition reinforces the expected pattern. The audience would anticipate a third Israelite — the priest-Levite-Israelite triplet is a known Jewish storytelling pattern. The shock of the parable’s structure is the substitution of a Samaritan for the expected Israelite in the third position: the religiously and ethnically despised figure becomes the model of neighbor-love.
10.33: Samaritēs de tis hodeuōn ēlthen kat auton kai idōn esplanchnisthē — but a Samaritan, as he traveled, came where the man was; and when he saw him, he had compassion. Samaritēs — the Samaritan is the expected villain of the story from a Jewish audience’s perspective (John 4:9; 8:48). Esplanchnisthē (had compassion, was moved in the bowels — Luke’s characteristic compassion verb; see 7:13; 15:20) — the Samaritan’s response is visceral, immediate, and identical to the language used for Jesus himself and the father in the prodigal. The most despised figure in the story has the most God-like response.
10.34: kai proselthōn katedēsen ta traumata autou epicheōn elaion kai oinon — he went to him and bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. The medical treatment is detailed: oil (for soothing and antiseptic properties) and wine (for cleansing, as an astringent) are the first-century field medicine. The Samaritan uses his own traveling supplies for the stranger. Epibibasas de auton epi to idion ktēnos ēgagen auton eis pandocheion kai epemeleuthē autou — placing him on his own animal, taking him to an inn, and caring for him: three further acts of practical self-expenditure for the other. Each action costs him something.
10.35: kai epi tēn aurion ekbalōn duo dēnaria edōken tō pandochei kai eipen: epimeleuthēti autou — the next day he took out two denarii and gave them to the innkeeper. Two denarii represented two days’ wages — sufficient for several weeks of basic board at the time. Ho ti an prosdapanēsēs egō en tō epanercesthai mou apodōsō soi (whatever extra expense you incur, I will repay you when I return) — the open-ended financial commitment goes beyond the immediate payment; the Samaritan takes personal financial responsibility for the stranger’s ongoing care. The parable ends not with the resolution of the victim’s condition but with the open commitment of the helper.
10.36: tis toutōn tōn triōn plēsion dokei soi gegenēsthai tou empesontos eis tous lēstas — which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers? The question shifts the frame entirely: the lawyer asked “who is my neighbor?” (a question about the object of obligation); Jesus asks “who became neighbor?” (a question about the subject of action). The neighbor is not defined by proximity, ethnicity, or religion but by the practice of mercy. The question forces the lawyer to answer from the logic of the story rather than from his own prejudice.
10.37: ho de eipen: ho poiēsas to eleos met autou; eipen de autō ho Iēsous: poreuou kai sy poiei homoiōs — the expert replied: The one who had mercy on him. Jesus told him: Go and do likewise. The lawyer cannot bring himself to say “the Samaritan” — he uses the functional description ho poiēsas to eleos (the one who showed mercy). Eleos (mercy, steadfast love — the LXX rendering of hesed) is the covenant-quality that the Samaritan exemplifies. Jesus’s conclusion poreuou kai sy poiei homoiōs (go and you do likewise) applies the parable directly: the answer to “what must I do?” is “be the Samaritan.”
10.38: en de tō poreuesthai autous autos eisēlthen eis kōmēn tina; gynē de tis onomati Martha hypedexato auton — as they were on their way, Jesus came to a village where a woman named Martha welcomed him. The Martha-Mary episode follows the Good Samaritan without transition, creating a diptych: active service (the Samaritan) and contemplative reception (Mary). Hypedexato (welcomed, received as a guest — a formal hospitality term) — Martha is the householder who initiates the welcome; her name (Martha, Aramaic for “mistress/lady of the house”) identifies her social role.
10.39: kai tēde ēn adelphē kaloumenē Mariam, hē kai parakathisasa pros tous podas tou kyriou ēkouen ton logon autou — she had a sister called Mary, who sat at the Lord’s feet listening to what he said. Parakathisasa pros tous podas (sat alongside at the feet) — the posture of a student receiving instruction from a teacher; “at the feet” is the technical term for the disciple’s position (Acts 22:3 — Paul “educated at the feet of Gamaliel”). Mary’s posture is that of a disciple, not a servant; she has taken the role of a learner, which in the cultural context was typically reserved for men. Ton logon autou (his word) — the singular logos as the content of the teaching session.
10.40: hē de Martha periespa peri pollēn diakonion — Martha was distracted by all the preparations. Periespa (was distracted, pulled away in different directions — a compound intensifying the pulling-apart image) conveys the scattered, anxious state of someone managing many details at once. Diakonios (service, preparation — the same root as diakonos, deacon) is the domestic service Martha is providing, which is itself a genuine act of hospitality. Her complaint to Jesus (kyrion, ou melei soi — don’t you care, Lord?) reveals the pressure she is under and the resentment accumulating toward her sister.
10.41: apokritheis de eipen autē ho kyrios: Martha Martha, merimnās kai thorybazē peri polla — the Lord answered: Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things. The double address (Martha Martha) is tender and searching — the same pattern as Jesus’s address to Saul (Saul Saul, Acts 9:4) and to Jerusalem (Ierousalēm Ierousalēm, 13:34). Merimnās (you are anxious/worried — the worry-word that appears in the cares-and-riches parable, 8:14; and in 12:22,25,26 about not worrying about life’s necessities) and thorybazē (you are troubled, disturbed) — the two verbs describe the internal state of Martha’s distraction.
10.42: henos de estin chreia; Mariam gar tēn agathēn merida exelexato — but few things are needed — or indeed only one. Mary has chosen what is better, and it will not be taken away from her. The textual variants here are significant: some manuscripts read “few things are needed” (oligōn de estin chreia), others “only one thing is needed” (henos), and some combine both. The “one thing” reading (preferred) identifies the single necessary thing as the hearing of Jesus’s word — the logos that Mary is receiving. Hē agathē meris (the good portion/share) is wisdom language (Ps 16:5-6); Mary has chosen the better portion — the one that will not be taken away even when the meal-service ends.

15.20: heti de autou makran apechontos eiden auton ho pater autou kai esplanchnisthe — while he was still a long way off his father saw him and was moved with compassion — esplanchnisthe is the gut-compassion verb (cf. Luke 7:13 for the widow's son; 10:33 for the Good Samaritan); it is the visceral response that precedes and motivates the action. The sequence in v.20 is rapid and movement-intensive: ran (edrame), fell on his neck (epepesen epi ton tracheton autou), kissed him (katefilesen auton) — three verbs of urgent, excessive welcome that exceed what Mediterranean honor-culture would expect of a father toward a disgraced son. The parable's God-image is defined by this excess of welcoming love.
15.1: ēsan de autō engizoontes pantes hoi telōnai kai hoi hamartōloi akouein autou — all the tax collectors and sinners were drawing near to him to hear him — telōnai (tax collectors — from telos = tax/toll + oneō = buy; they purchased the right to collect taxes and profited by overcharging) are regularly paired with hamartōloi (sinners) throughout Luke (5:30; 7:34; 15:1-2; 18:11); the pairing is a fixed social category in the Pharisaic worldview. Engizō (draw near — Luke's approach verb) is the movement of those coming to Jesus throughout the Gospel; it is the opposite of the Pharisees' watching distance (14:1).
15.2: kai diegongyzon hoi Pharisaioi kai hoi grammateis legontes — and the Pharisees and scribes were grumbling — diagongyzō (grumble, mutter — intensive compound of gongyzō = grumble/murmur, the LXX word for Israel's wilderness murmuring; dia- prefix intensifies) links the Pharisees' protest directly to the wilderness generation's murmuring against Moses (Exod 16:7-9; Num 11:1). houtos hamartōlous prosdechetai kai synesthiei autois — this man receives sinners and eats with them — prosdechomai (receive, welcome — from pros- + dechomai = receive toward oneself; to actively welcome) + synesthiō (eat with, share table — compound of sun- + esthiō): table-fellowship with sinners implies acceptance and solidarity, which is the Pharisaic complaint.
15.3: eipen de pros autous tēn parabolēn tautēn legōn — he told them this parable — tautēn (this — singular) before the three parables: Luke treats the lost sheep, lost coin, and prodigal son as a single parable-sequence (tēn parabolēn tautēn in the singular) responding to the single complaint of v.2. The three parables are a triptych, each intensifying the theme: a man loses a sheep (external), a woman loses a coin (domestic), a father loses a son (relational/existential). Each recovery triggers communal celebration; each celebration is the narrative icon of heaven's rejoicing.
15.4: tis anthrōpos ex hymōn echōn hekaton probata — what man among you having a hundred sheep — the rhetorical address tis ex hymōn (who among you) draws the listener into the scenario as the owner; the question assumes the obvious answer. Hekaton probata (a hundred sheep) represents a mid-sized flock in ancient Palestine; not a wealthy shepherd but a working-class owner for whom the loss of one is significant. katalipōn ta enenēkonta ennea — leaving the ninety-nine — katalipōn (aorist participle of kataleipō = leave behind, abandon temporarily) — the shepherd's paradox: protecting ninety-nine by leaving them to find one; the logic of disproportionate pursuit.
15.5: kai heurōn epitithēsin epi tous ōmous autou chairōn — and finding it he puts it on his shoulders rejoicing — epitithēsin epi tous ōmous (puts on the shoulders — ōmos = shoulder; the shepherd carries the sheep rather than driving it) is the image of complete rescue, not just location; the lost sheep is carried back because it cannot find its own way. Chairōn (rejoicing — present participle, indicating continuous joy during the return journey) — the joy begins at the moment of finding, before the communal celebration. Luke's word choice emphasizes the joy as intrinsic to the recovery itself.
15.6: kai elthōn eis ton oikon synkalei tous philous kai tous geitonas — and coming home he calls together friends and neighbors — the community celebration (synkaleō = call together) is the social expression of the private joy that began at finding. sugchārēte moi — rejoice with me — sunchairō (rejoice together with — compound of sun- + chairō); the invitation to shared joy reflects the honor-shame social fabric: significant events (recovery, rescue, restoration) are communal events. Hoti heuron to probaton mou to apolōlos — because I have found my lost sheep — apolōlos (perfect participle of apollymi = lost, destroyed; "the having-been-lost"); the perfect tense marks the permanent state of lostness that has now been reversed.
15.7: legō hymin hoti houtōs chara en tō ouranō estai — I say to you that in this way there will be joy in heaven — houtōs (in this way, similarly) draws the comparison: as the shepherd celebrated, so heaven celebrates. Chara en tō ouranō (joy in heaven — a divine-euphemism: "heaven" = God rejoicing) is the theological claim behind the parable. ē epi enenēkonta ennea dikaiois hoi ouk echousin chreian metanoias — more than over ninety-nine righteous persons who have no need of repentance — the ninety-nine "righteous" is ironic in context: the Pharisees are the self-identified righteous who consider themselves beyond needing repentance, and the parable implies their status may not be what they think.
15.8: ē tis gynē drachmas echousa deka ean apolesē drachmen mian — or what woman having ten silver coins if she loses one — the second parable shifts from male-outdoor (shepherd, fields) to female-domestic (woman, house). Drachma (a Greek silver coin ≈ one day's wage; the Roman denarius equivalent) — the ten-coin collection may represent a household savings or a woman's headdress ornament (coins sewn on a wedding headdress were a dowry symbol). The domestic setting (oikia = house) requires lamp and broom; the search is interior and careful rather than outdoor and ranging.
15.9: kai heurasa synkalei tas philas kai geitonas — and finding it she calls together the friends and neighbors — the exact communal-celebration structure mirrors the shepherd's (v.6): synkaleō (call together) + philas kai geitonas (friends and neighbors — feminine plural, the woman's social network). The three-parable sequence uses the same celebration formula for all three: shepherd (v.6), woman (v.9), father (v.23-24), building from small group to large party. Each celebration is both the parable's resolution and the icon of heaven's joy.
15.10: houtōs legō hymin, ginetai chara enōpion tōn angelōn tou theou — in this way I say to you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God — Luke varies the divine-euphemism: v.7 says "joy in heaven"; v.10 says "joy in the presence of the angels of God" (enōpion tōn angelōn tou theou). Enōpion (in the presence of, before) is Luke's characteristic positional word (1:15, 17; 4:7; 5:25; 16:15); "the angels of God" is a circumlocution for God's presence (the heavenly court), parallel to "heaven" in v.7. Epi heni hamartōlō metanoounti — over one sinner who repents — the singular heni (one) emphasizes that individual restoration, not mass movement, provokes divine joy.
15.11: anthrōpos tis eichen duo huious — a certain man had two sons — the barest narrative introduction; Luke uses anthrōpos tis (a certain man) for parables featuring a representative human figure (14:16; 16:1; 16:19). The two sons immediately set up the contrast structure: the younger who departs and returns vs the older who remains and resents. The father is the parable's central figure but is introduced only through his relation to the sons. The parable has no received title in the ancient sources; "Prodigal Son" is a Reformation-era label.
15.12: dos moi to epiballon meros tēs ousias — give me the share of the estate that falls to me — epiballon (falling to, belonging to — present participle of epiballō = throw upon, belong to by right; here the inheritance share that "falls upon" the younger son). Ousia (estate, property, substance — from eimi = be; "being-ness," what constitutes one's material existence) is the word for the full property being divided. In Jewish law (Deut 21:17) the younger son's share is one-third; the request is extraordinary but not without legal basis. Dieilen autois ton bion — he divided the livelihood between them — bios (life, livelihood — material sustenance, not spiritual life; contrast zoē = life) is what is divided: the living that sustains existence.
15.13: diaskorpisas tēn ousian autou zōn asōtōs — squandering his property living recklessly/prodigally — diaskorpizō (scatter, squander — from dia- + skorpizō = scatter; to throw in all directions, disperse completely; also used for the shepherd scattering the sheep, Matt 26:31) is the waste verb.
