Asōtōs (prodigally, recklessly, dissolutely — adverb from asōtos = one who cannot be saved/is past saving; from a- + sōzō = not-saving; the prodigal title derives from this word) is the English "prodigal" itself: prodigus (Latin) ≈ asōtos (Greek) = one who throws everything away, past saving by ordinary means.
15.14: limos ischuros kata tēn chōran ekeinēn — a severe famine came upon that region — limos ischuros (powerful/severe famine — ischuros = strong, mighty; the same word for the stronger man at 11:22) intensifies the son's destitution: his self-caused poverty is compounded by external disaster. Hyperistato (he began to fall short, to be in need — imperfect of hypereomai); the imperfect describes the onset of progressive need. Luke presents the famine not as punishment but as context that reveals the depth of the son's situation.
15.15: ekollēthē heni tōn politōn tēs chōras ekeinēs — he attached himself to one of the citizens of that region — kollaō (cling to, attach to, join — from kolla = glue; to glue oneself to someone, join urgently; also used for cleaving to a spouse and for the prodigal's clinging to the citizen). The citizen sends him to feed pigs (boskein choirous) — the supreme Jewish degradation: pigs are unclean animals (Lev 11:7) and pig-herding in a foreign land signals total defilement and social humiliation. The son who had an inheritance is now a pig-herder in Gentile territory.
15.16: epethymei chortasthēnai ek tōn keratōn hōn ēsthion hoi choiroi — he longed to fill himself from the pods that the pigs were eating — epithumeō (desire earnestly, long for — the strong desire verb, also used for the Passover longing at 22:15) + chortasthēnai (to be filled/satisfied — from chortos = fodder, grass; animal satisfaction; also the feeding miracle verb). Kerata (pods — the carob pods of the carob tree, Ceratonia siliqua; cheap pig fodder and poor-person food in antiquity) = the English "prodigal" itself; no one gave him even this. Kai oudeis edidou autō — and no one gave him anything — the social isolation is total; he cannot even receive the level of charity given to strangers.
15.17: eis heauton de elthōn ephē — coming to himself he said — eis heauton de elthōn (coming to himself — literally "coming into himself") is a Greek idiom for recovering one's senses, regaining awareness; it is used in Greek literature for returning to right judgment after madness or delirium. The son's repentance begins not with guilt about God but with practical calculation: how many of my father's hired servants have surplus food while I am perishing here? The movement from self-deception to self-knowledge is the beginning of return.
15.18: anastas poreusomai pros ton patera mou — rising I will go to my father — anastas (rising — aorist participle of anistēmi; the resurrection verb; used for arising from sleep, from a seat, and from the dead) + poreusomai (I will go — future, a firm intention). The rise-and-go combination (anastas + poreuomai) occurs at significant turning points in Luke (1:39; 10:35; 15:20). Hēmarton eis ton ouranon kai enōpion sou — I have sinned against heaven and before you — the double object: heaven (ouranon = God-euphemism) and before you (the father); the sin is both vertical and horizontal.
15.19: ouketi eimi axios klēthēnai huios sou — I am no longer worthy to be called your son — axios (worthy, deserving — the worth/merit word) in the negative: the son has forfeited the relational standing. His proposed remedy: poiēson me hōs hena tōn misthiōn sou (make me as one of your hired servants — misthios = hired worker, day-laborer; contrast doulos = slave born into the household; a hired worker is the lowest free status). The son calculates that servant-status with his father's household is better than freedom in the far country.
15.21: eipen de ho huios autō, Pater, hēmarton eis ton ouranon kai enōpion sou — the son said to him: Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you — the son delivers his prepared speech (vv.18-19) but the father interrupts before the servant-request can be delivered. Compare v.19 and v.21: the son's scripted speech planned to say "make me a servant," but the father's action (v.22) preempts the negotiated-status request with full restoration. The incomplete speech captures the gap between the son's self-assessment and the father's response.
15.22: exenegkate stolin tēn prōtēn — bring out the best robe — stolē (robe, long garment — from stellō = put in order, equip; a formal or festive garment; used for the rich man's clothing at 20:46; the resurrection angels at Mark 16:5) with the superlative prōtēn (first, finest) — the best robe in the household, not a servant's garment. dote daktylion eis tēn cheira autou kai hypodēmata eis tous podas — put a ring on his hand and sandals on his feet — ring: authority and family membership (a signet ring, daktylios); sandals: free man's mark (slaves went barefoot). The three items (robe, ring, sandals) together constitute full restoration of sonship status.
15.23: kai pherete ton moschon ton siteuton thysate — bring the fattened calf and slaughter it — moschos (calf) + siteuton (fattened — from siteuō = fatten by grain-feeding; the most prized meat animal, kept for major celebrations). The fattened calf (reserved for special occasions, not daily meals) signals that the celebration is the highest-register festive event the household can produce. Phagontes euphrainthōmen — let us eat and be merry — euphrainō (rejoice, celebrate — the festivity verb; also vv.24, 29, 32; Luke 12:19; 16:19); the eating and rejoicing are the celebratory response to the restoration.
15.24: hoti houtos ho huios mou nekros ēn kai anezēsen — for this son of mine was dead and has come back to life — nekros ēn (was dead — the complete lostness is named death) + anezēsen (came back to life — aorist of anazaō = live again, revive; re-animation verb). The dead/alive + lost/found couplet (apolōlos ēn kai heurethē) is the parable's thematic core and is repeated at v.32. Kai ērxanto euphrainesathai — and they began to celebrate — the imperfect ērxanto (began) + the festivity verb: the celebration starts immediately and continues.
15.25: ēn de ho huios autou ho presbuteros en agrō — his older son was in the field — presbuteros (older, elder) son as the parable's second major character introduced only after the younger son's restoration is underway; the delay in introducing him makes the contrast sharp. Agros (field) is the place of faithful labor; the older son has been working while the younger was wasting his inheritance in the far country. Hōs de erchomenos ēggisen tē oikia ēkousen symphōnias kai chorōn — as he came near the house he heard music and dancing — symphōnia (music, harmony — musical performance; borrowed from Greek symphōneō = sound together) + choros (dance, dancing group).
15.26: proskalesmenos hena tōn paidōn epynthaneto ti an eiē tauta — calling one of the servants he asked what these things might be — proskaleomai (call to oneself, summon — Luke's call-to-oneself verb) + pyntanomai (inquire, ask — the formal inquiry verb; also Acts 23:20; Matt 2:4). The optative ti an eiē tauta (what these things might be — oblique optative in indirect question) is classical Greek syntax that Luke preserves; the older son does not know yet what has happened because no one has told him.
15.27: ho adelphos sou hēkei kai ethusen ho patēr sou ton moschon ton siteuton — your brother has come and your father has slaughtered the fattened calf — the servant reports two things: the brother's return (hēkei — perfect with present sense: "he is here") and the father's action (slaughter of the fattened calf). Hygiainonta auton apelabon — he received him back in good health — hygiainō (be in good health, be sound — from which hygienic; the "healthy" verb; contrast the physical and spiritual sickness implicit in the far-country life). The father's joy is expressed through the highest celebration available.
15.28: ōrgisthē kai ouk ēthelen eiselthein — he became angry and was not willing to go in — orgizō (become angry — the full-anger word, also 14:21). The older son's refusal to enter (ouk ēthelen eiselthein — imperfect of thelō = was not willing; sustained unwillingness, not a momentary refusal) is its own honor-challenge: refusing to participate in the father's celebration is a public dishonoring of the father. Ho de patēr autou exelthōn parekalei auton — his father came out and was pleading with him — parakaleō (plead, urge, encourage — the same word for the Holy Spirit as Paraclete) in the imperfect: sustained pleading.
15.29: idou tosauta etē douleuō soi kai oudepote entolēn sou parēlthon — look, so many years I have been serving you like a slave and I never transgressed your command — douleuō (serve as a slave — from doulos; the older son describes his faithful service in slave-vocabulary; he has been a faithful doulos while calling himself a son). Oudepote entolēn sou parēlthon (I never transgressed your command — parerchomai = pass by, transgress; also 11:42 for tithing while neglecting justice). Emoigde eriphon oudepote edōkas — you never gave me even a young goat — eriphos (young goat — the lowest-value festive animal; far less than the fattened calf given for his brother).
15.30: ho de huios sou houtos ho kataphagōn sou ton bion meta pornōn — but this son of yours who has devoured your livelihood with prostitutes — the older son does not say "my brother" but "this son of yours" (ho huios sou houtos) — the deliberate distance is the emotional core of his resentment. Kataphagōn (devoured — intensive compound of kata- + phagō = eat down, consume completely) + ton bion (the livelihood; the same bios word as v.12). Meta pornōn (with prostitutes) — the older son's accusation; the parable text at v.13 said only "reckless living" (asōtōs); "prostitutes" is the older son's interpretation, possibly projecting, possibly inferring.
15.31: teknon, su pantote met emou ei kai panta ta ema sa estin — child, you are always with me and everything I have is yours — the father's address teknon (child, dear child — the affectionate diminutive of family intimacy; not huie = son, but teknon = child, the intimate family word; also used in 2:48; 16:25) signals warmth rather than rebuke. Panta ta ema sa estin — everything I have is yours — the father's statement of the older son's standing: the inheritance is secure, the relationship is unbroken, the proximity (always with me) is the gift the younger son abandoned. The contrast reveals the older son's failure to receive what he already had.
15.32: euphranthenai de kai charēnai edei — it was necessary to celebrate and rejoice — edei (it was necessary, it had to be — the divine necessity word, also 4:43; 9:22; 13:33; 22:37; 24:7; 24:26) applied to the celebration: the father's party is not optional sentiment but necessary response to the dead-alive reversal. The closing couplet (nekros ēn kai ezēsen, apolōlos kai heurethē — was dead and lived, was lost and was found) exactly repeats the father's exclamation of v.24: both halves of the parable end with the same formula. The parable ends without resolving the older son's response — his choice remains open, as does the Pharisees'.

17.21: he basileia tou theou entos hymon estin — the kingdom of God is in your midst / within you — entos is a rare preposition meaning either within (internal, in your hearts) or in your midst (among you, present in your community). The context (the Pharisees asking when the kingdom comes) favors in your midst: the kingdom is not a future observable event coming at a detectable moment but a present reality already among the Pharisees in the person of Jesus and his ministry. Luke's eschatology is simultaneously present (the kingdom is here) and future (the Son of Man's coming, vv.22-37).
17.1: anendekton estin tou ta skandala mē elthein — it is impossible for stumbling blocks not to come. Skandalon (stumbling block, trigger of a trap — the bait-stick that springs a snare) is the occasion of another’s spiritual fall; its source can be the teacher, the community, or the world. Anendekton (impossible, inadmissible — a NT hapax) is absolute: the world in its fallen condition guarantees that stumbling blocks will appear. Plēn ouai (but woe) — the inevitability of offenses does not diminish the culpability of the offender; the agent of the stumbling block bears full responsibility despite the world’s structural sinfulness.
17.2: lysitelei autō ei lithos mylikos perikeitai peri ton trachēlon autou kai erriptai eis tēn thalassan ē hina skandalisē tōn mikrōn hena — it would be better for them to have a millstone hung around their neck and be thrown into the sea than to cause one of these little ones to stumble. Lithos mylikos (millstone — specifically the upper millstone turned by a donkey) is massive; to drown with one around the neck is the extreme death by water. The comparison is not a literal punishment but a hyperbole of severity: any fate is better than being the instrument of a little one’s spiritual destruction. Tōn mikrōn (the little ones) refers to disciples, the vulnerable members of the community.
17.3: prosechete heautois. ean hamartē ho adelphos sou, epitimēson autō, kai ean metanoesei, aphes autō — watch yourselves. If a brother or sister sins against you, rebuke them; and if they repent, forgive them. The self-watching command (prosechete heautois) precedes the community-ethics instruction: the community member must first examine their own standing before administering discipline or forgiveness. Epitimēson (rebuke, reprove — the same word used for Jesus’s exorcistic rebukes) — the rebuke is not punitive but corrective; it aims at metanoia (repentance, change of direction). Repentance is the condition for forgiveness; the rebuke creates the opportunity for both.
17.4: kai ean heptakis tēs hēmeras hamart��sē eis se kai heptakis epistrepsē pros se legōn: metanow, aphēseis autō — even if they sin against you seven times in a day and seven times come back saying, I repent, you must forgive them. Seven times (heptakis) — the number of completeness; the forgiveness demand is unlimited in principle, not merely generous. Matthew’s version (77 times or 70 x 7, Matt 18:21-22) makes the same point with larger numbers. Aphēseis (you will forgive — future indicative, the strongest imperative form in Greek) — the forgiveness is required, not optional. The community that can forgive without limit is the embodiment of the limitless forgiveness of God.
17.5: kai eipan hoi apostoloi tō kyriō: prosthes hēmin pistin — the apostles said to the Lord: Increase our faith! The apostles’ request is understandable: the demand for unlimited forgiveness has exceeded their sense of personal resource. Prosthes hēmin pistin (add to us faith) — the request treats faith as a quantifiable resource that can be augmented. Jesus’s response will challenge this framing: it is not the quantity of faith that matters but its object; even the smallest genuine faith has power beyond their expectations.
17.6: ei echete pistin hōs kokkon sinapeōs, elegete an tē sykaminō tautē: ekrizōthēti kai phyteuth��ti en tē thalassē — if you have faith as small as a mustard seed, you can say to this mulberry tree: be uprooted and planted in the sea. The mustard-seed comparison (also Matt 17:20 with a mountain; Mark 11:23 with a mountain) here is applied to a sykaminos (mulberry tree, not fig or sycamore — a tree with deep roots and long lifespan). Kai hypēkousen an hymin (and it would obey you) — the divine passive behind the obedience: nature obeys the faith-word because nature obeys God, and faith aligned with God participates in that obedience. The disciples’ problem is not the quantity of their faith but its direction.
17.7: tis de ex hymōn doulon echōn arotriōnta ē poimainonta — suppose one of you has a servant plowing or looking after the sheep. The Unworthy Servant parable (unique to Luke) addresses the disciples’ attitude toward their own obedience after the unlimited-forgiveness command. The parable depicts a culturally normal master-slave relationship: the slave works in the field all day, then comes in to serve dinner before eating himself. No extraordinary demand is being made — this is simply the normal expectation of slavery.
17.8: all ouchi erei autō: hetoimason ti deipnēsō kai perizōsamenos diakonei moi heōs phagō kai piō — won’t the master say: prepare my supper, get ready and wait on me while I eat and drink; and after that you may eat and drink? The master’s expectation is sequential: serve first, eat after. The slave’s schedule is subordinated to the master’s needs without the master feeling any obligation to reverse the order or express gratitude. The parable is describing a normal social reality, not an ideal one; Jesus will use it to make a point about the attitude appropriate to disciples, not to endorse slavery.
17.9: mē echei charin tō doulō hoti epoiēsen ta diatachthenta; ou dokō — does he thank the servant because he did what he was told to do? I think not. Charin (grace, gratitude, thanks — the same word as “grace” in theological usage) — the master owes no extraordinary gratitude for a slave doing what slaves are supposed to do. The point is not the master’s virtue but the slave’s role: gratitude is appropriate when someone goes beyond obligation, not when they simply meet it.
17.10: houtōs kai hymeis, hotan poiēsēte panta ta diatachthenta hymin, legete hoti douloi achreioi esmen — so you also, when you have done everything you were told to do, should say: we are unworthy servants; we have only done our duty. Achreioi (unworthy, useless, unprofitable — the word for something that has produced no surplus beyond what was required) — the disciples who have obeyed fully have only met the baseline requirement; they have created no surplus of merit. The parable dismantles any foundation for merit-theology: covenant obedience is the minimum expected of a covenant member, not the basis for reward claims beyond grace.
17.11: kai egeneto en tō poreuesthai eis Ierousalēm kai autos diērcheto dia meson Samareias kai Galilaias — now on his way to Jerusalem, Jesus traveled along the border between Samaria and Galilee. The geographical notation (between Samaria and Galilee) sets the scene for the Samaritan leper’s appearance; the border region is where social mixing occurred between Jews and Samaritans. Poreuesthai eis Ierousalēm (traveling to Jerusalem) — Luke’s journey-narrative motif (9:51 through 19:44) frames all the travel-narrative teaching within the deliberate movement toward the passion.
17.12: kai eiserchomenou autou eis tina kōmēn ēntēsan autō deka leproi andres — as he was going into a village, ten men who had leprosy met him. Leproi (those with leprosy — the OT term tsara’at covered a range of skin conditions rendering one ritually unclean and socially excluded, Lev 13-14). Hoi estēsan porrōthen (they stood at a distance) — the legal requirement of distance (m. Negaim 13:12) meant lepers could not approach uninfected people; they called out from a prescribed separation. The ten are a mixed group as v.16 will reveal: nine Jews and one Samaritan.
17.13: kai autoi ēran phōnēn legontes: Iēsou epistata, eleēson hēmas — they called out: Jesus, Master, have pity on us! Epistata (master, one who stands over, superintendent — a Lukan title for Jesus, appearing 6 times in Luke and nowhere else in the NT) replaces the didaskalos (teacher) or kyrios (Lord) of other traditions. The plural eleēson hēmas (have pity on us) is a single petition from a group whose shared condition has overridden their social-ethnic distinctions: Jew and Samaritan together cry for mercy.
17.14: kai idōn eipen autois: poreutheientes epideixate heautous tois hiereusin — when he saw them, he said: go, show yourselves to the priests. The instruction follows Leviticus 14:1-32 (the ritual for the cleansed leper to present to the priest who certifies the cure and performs the purification rite). Kai egeneto en tō hypagein autous ekathristhēsan (and it happened that as they went they were cleansed) — the healing is not instantaneous at the command but progressive during obedient departure: faith expressed as action is met by healing during the action. The obedience precedes the sign.
17.15: heis de ex autōn idōn hoti iathē hypostrepsen meta phōnēs megalēs doxazōn ton theon — one of them, when he saw he was healed, turned back, praising God in a loud voice. The single turning-back (hypostrepsen) is emphasized: nine continue their journey; one reverses course. Meta phōnēs megalēs doxazōn ton theon (with a loud voice glorifying God) — the loud praise is public, the attribution (ton theon) is correct: the glory goes to God, not to the human healer. The gratitude-response is precisely what the other nine’s absence marks as absent.
17.16: kai epesen epi prosōpon para tous podas autou eucharistōn autō, kai autos ēn Samarit��s — he threw himself at Jesus’ feet and thanked him — and he was a Samaritan. The prostration (epesen epi prosōpon, fell on his face) is the posture of worship; the giving of thanks (eucharistōn — the source of the word “Eucharist”) is directed to Jesus personally. Luke’s climactic identification “and he was a Samaritan” (autos ēn Samaritēs) is the narrative shock: the grateful worshiper is the one the audience would have expected to be least responsive to a Jewish healer.
17.17: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen: ouchi hoi deka ekathristhēsan; hoi de ennea pou — Jesus asked: Were not all ten cleansed? Where are the other nine? The rhetorical question is not a complaint but a diagnostic: the nine have received healing and gone their way; the one has received healing and returned with worship. The question “where are the other nine?” hangs unanswered, pointed at both the nine and at the reader: what does receiving divine mercy without gratitude-return look like?
17.18: ouch heurethēsan hypostrepsantes dounai doxan tō theō ei mē ho allogenēs houtos — has no one returned to give praise to God except this foreigner? Allogenēs (of another race, foreigner — a term from inscriptions on the Jerusalem temple warning Gentiles not to enter the inner courts) is the strongest term for outsider status. Jesus uses the temple-exclusion word for the one person who has returned to give glory to God — the irony is complete: the temple-outsider performs the temple-worship (giving glory to God) that the nine with temple-access have failed to perform.
17.19: kai eipen autō: anastas poreuou; hē pistis sou sesōken se — then he said to him: Rise and go; your faith has saved you. Sesōken se (has saved you) — the same formula as 7:50 (sinful woman), 8:48 (hemorrhaging woman), and 18:42 (blind Bartimaeus). The other nine were cleansed (ekathristhēsan, ritual purity restored); this one is saved (sesōken, the comprehensive term covering physical, spiritual, and social restoration). The distinction is Luke’s Christological point: healing is available to all ten; saving is available to those who return in faith and worship.
17.20: eperoētheis de hypo tōn Pharisaiōn pote erchetai hē basileia tou theou, apekrithē autois kai eipen — once, on being asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God would come, Jesus replied. The Pharisees’ question (pote erchetai hē basileia tou theou, when is the kingdom of God coming?) operates within the framework of Jewish apocalyptic expectation: the kingdom as a future observable political event. Meta paratērēseōs (with observation, with visible signs to be watched for) — paratērēsis is a medical/astronomical term for the careful observation of symptoms or heavenly bodies; the Pharisees want a timetable that can be calculated from visible indicators.
17.22: eipen de pros tous mathētas: eleusontai hēmerai hote epithymēsete mian tōn hēmerōn tou huiou tou anthrōpou idein kai ouk opsesthe — he said to his disciples: The time is coming when you will long to see one of the days of the Son of Man, but you will not see it. The warning to disciples (private, unlike the public response to the Pharisees) addresses the temptation toward false messianic speculation during persecution. Epithymēsete (you will long for, desire intensely) — in times of suffering, the disciples will crave the immediate return and vindication of the Son of Man; Jesus warns that this desire will leave them vulnerable to false claimants.
17.23: kai erousin hymin: idou ekei; ē idou hōde; mē apelthēte mēde diōxēte — people will tell you, There he is! or Here he is! Do not go running off after them. The warning against following false localized claims about the Son of Man’s presence echoes the sign-refusal of the Pharisees (v.20): the genuine coming of the Son of Man will not be observed in a specific location — it will be universal and unmistakable. Mē apelthēte mēde diōxēte (do not go, do not pursue) — the imperative negative commands: the disciples are to resist the geographic magnetism of false messianic claims.
17.24: hōsper gar hē astrapē astraptousa ek tēs hypo ton ouranon eis tēn hyp ouranon lampei, houtōs estai ho huios tou anthrōpou en tē hēmera autou — for the Son of Man in his day will be like the lightning, which flashes and lights up the sky from one end to the other. Astrapē (lightning) — instantaneous, simultaneous across the entire sky, impossible to miss or localize. The image answers the false claimants of v.23: the real coming of the Son of Man will require no announcement, no specific location, and no pursuit. It will be self-evidently universal. The comparison establishes the eschatological character of the Parousia: it will have the quality of a lightning flash — total, immediate, cosmic.
17.25: prōton de dei auton polla pathein kai apodokimasthēnai apo tēs geneas tautēs — but first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation. The insertion of the passion prediction into the eschatological discourse is Luke’s characteristic linkage of suffering and glory: before the lightning-flash of the Parousia comes the darkness of the passion. Dei (it is necessary — divine necessity) frames the suffering as the required path, not an accident or setback. Apodokimasthēnai (to be rejected — the word for a stone failing the builder’s inspection, Ps 118:22 LXX) — the rejection is as specific as the suffering is general.
17.26: kai kathōs egeneto en tais hēmerais Nōe, houtōs estai kai en tais hēmerais tou huiou tou anthrōpou — just as it was in the days of Noah, so also will it be in the days of the Son of Man. The Noah-comparison and the Lot-comparison (vv.26-30) are not about wickedness primarily but about normality: the people of Noah’s and Lot’s day were going about ordinary life — eating, drinking, marrying, buying, selling, planting, building. The judgment did not arrive during a moral crisis obvious to observers; it arrived during business as usual.
17.27: ēsthion, epinon, egamoun, egamizonto, achri hēs hēmeras eisēlthen Nōe eis tēn kibōton — people were eating, drinking, marrying and being given in marriage up to the day Noah entered the ark. The six activities (eating, drinking, marrying, being given in marriage, building, selling) are all neutral — lawful human activities. The judgment that interrupted them was not a response to a specific escalation of sin but the arrival of the predetermined moment. The lesson is not that normal life is sinful but that normal life’s continuation should not be taken as evidence that judgment is not imminent.
17.28: homoiōs kathōs egeneto en tais hēmerais Lōt: ēsthion, epinon, ēgorazon, epōloun, ephyteuon, ��kodomoun — it was the same in the days of Lot. People were eating and drinking, buying and selling, planting and building. The Lot-comparison adds four more activities (buying, selling, planting, building) to the Noah-list, extending the pattern: ordinary economic and agricultural life was uninterrupted until the moment of destruction. Sodom’s sin is not specifically named here (the context in Genesis is sexual violation of the divine messengers, but also pride and inhospitality, Ezek 16:49); the focus is on the abrupt interruption of normal life.
17.29: hē de hēmera exēlthen Lōt apo Sodōmōn, ebreksen pyr kai theion ap ouranou kai apōlesen pantas — but the day Lot left Sodom, fire and sulfur rained down from heaven and destroyed them all. Pyr kai theion (fire and sulfur — the Gen 19:24 judgment language) — the sudden totality of the destruction mirrors the lightning-flash suddenness of the Son of Man’s day. Apōlesen pantas (destroyed them all) — the universality of the destruction within the condemned city; no one remaining in Sodom survived. The escape of Lot (and his daughters) is the paradigm of rescue by departure before judgment falls.
17.30: kata ta auta estai hē hēmera ho huios tou anthrōpou apokalyptetai — it will be just like this on the day the Son of Man is revealed. Apokalyptetai (is revealed, is uncovered — the same root as apokalypsis, revelation) — the Parousia is a revelation/uncovering, not merely a return: what was hidden (the Son of Man’s glory) will be made manifest. The Noah-Lot comparisons frame it as the arrival of a predetermined moment during which ordinary life will be interrupted. The warning is to live with readiness rather than absorption in the ordinary.
17.31: en ekeinē tē hēmera hos estai epi tou dōmatos kai ta skeuē autou en tē oikia, mē katabatō arai auta — on that day no one on the housetop with possessions inside should go down to get them. The urgency of departure without retrieval echoes the Exodus instructions (no time to let bread rise, Exod 12:11) and the Lot-escape (don’t look back, Gen 19:17). The housetop-to-inside movement would cost precious seconds in a moment requiring instant flight. The possessions are dispensable; the moment of the Son of Man’s day is not.
17.32: mnēmoneuete tēs gynaikos Lōt — remember Lot’s wife! The single-sentence command is the most economical warning in the Synoptics. Lot’s wife looked back (Gen 19:26) and became a pillar of salt — a monument to the deadly attraction of the condemned life she was leaving. The look was more than curiosity: it was the expression of attachment to what was being left behind. In the eschatological context, looking back is the failure to commit fully to the departure that the Son of Man’s day requires.
17.33: hos ean zētēsē tēn psychēn autou peripoiēsasthai apolesei autēn, hos d an apolesē zōsai autēn — whoever tries to keep their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life will preserve it. The life-losing-life paradox (also 9:24; Mark 8:35; Matt 16:25; John 12:25) is placed in the eschatological context: on the day of the Son of Man, those who prioritize self-preservation will lose everything; those who have released their grip on life will find it preserved. Psychēn here is both physical life and the whole person/self; the paradox operates at both levels.
17.34: legō hymin, tautē tē nykti esontai dyo epi klinēs mias, ho heis paralēmphthēsetai kai ho heteros aphethēsetai — I tell you, on that night two people will be in one bed; one will be taken and the other left. The three examples of division (bed-partners, grain-grinders, field-workers) illustrate the selective nature of the eschatological separation: proximity — sharing a bed, a millstone, a field — does not create identical destiny. The divine selection is not visible in advance from external circumstances. Paralēmphthēsetai (will be taken) and aphethēsetai (will be left/released) — which is better is not specified in Luke; the context of the Son of Man’s coming may imply the taken are gathered to him.
17.35: esontai dyo alēthouaai epi to auto, hē mia paralēmphthēsetai hē de hetera aphethēsetai — two women will be grinding grain together; one will be taken and the other left. The grinding-together image is of two women working the same millstone in close cooperation; their shared labor does not predict shared destiny in the eschatological separation. The pattern reinforces v.34’s point: physical proximity, shared work, and common circumstances are not determinative of eschatological outcome. The hidden differentiation of readiness/faith is what matters.
17.36: duo en tō agrō, ho heis paralēmphthēsetai kai ho heteros aphethēsetai — two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left. This verse is absent from several important manuscripts (including P75, B, and the Sahidic Coptic) and was likely added from the parallel in Matthew 24:40; Luke’s authentic text may end the series with v.35. If original, it completes a triad (bed/house, mill/house, field) covering rest, domestic work, and agricultural labor — all spheres of ordinary life in which the eschatological division will occur.
17.37: kai apokrithentes legousin autō: pou, kyrie; ho de eipen autois: hopou to sōma, ekei kai hoi aetoi episynachthēsontai — where, Lord? he replied: Where there is a dead body, there the eagles will gather. The disciples’ locational question (“where?”) receives a proverbial answer that resists pinning down a geographic location. Aetoi (eagles or vultures — the Greek aetos can mean either; vultures are the more likely image since they gather around corpses) — the carrion-birds find the corpse without needing directions; the Son of Man’s coming will be self-evidently obvious in the same way. The parable-proverb also carries a darker connotation: the judgment of the Son of Man’s day will find its object as surely as vultures find a carcass.

18.13: ho de telones makrothen hestōs ouk ethelen oude tous ophthalmous eis ton ouranon eparai, alla etypton to stethos autou legon ho theos hilastheti moi to hamarolio — the tax collector standing far off would not even lift his eyes to heaven but beat his breast saying God be merciful to me the sinner — hilastheti (be propitious/merciful to me, the aorist passive of hilaskomai) is the LXX atonement verb (used for the priest making atonement on Yom Kippur, Lev 16 LXX). The tax collector prays the Day of Atonement prayer in his own person: God, let atonement be made for me. The article (the sinner, not a sinner) suggests the man sees himself as the paradigm case of sinfulness.
18.1: elegen de parabolēn autois pros to dein pantote proseuchesthai autous kai mē enkakein — then Jesus told his disciples a parable to show them that they should always pray and not give up. Pros to dein (showing the necessity of — Luke’s dei construction used for divine necessity, applied here to persistent prayer) and mē enkakein (not to give up, not to grow weary and slack — enkakō means to behave badly from weariness, to collapse in the middle of an effort). The parable’s structure is an a fortiori argument: if even an unjust human judge eventually responds to persistent petition, how much more will God respond to the persistent prayer of his people?
18.2: kritēs tis ēn en tini polei ton theon mē phoboumenos kai anthrōpon mē entrepomenos — there was a judge who neither feared God nor cared what people thought. The double negative characterization (ton theon mē phoboumenos, not fearing God; anthrōpon mē entrepomenos, not respecting people) identifies the judge as outside both the vertical covenant structure (no accountability to God) and the horizontal honor-shame structure (no accountability to social pressure). He is the maximally unresponsive figure: if even he eventually responds, no possible petitioner could be harder to move than God.
18.3: chēra de ēn en tē polei ekeinē kai ērcheto pros auton legousa: ekdikēson me apo tou antidikos mou — there was a widow who kept coming to him with the plea: grant me justice against my opponent. Chēra (widow) is in Jewish law the paradigmatic vulnerable person who deserves special protection (Exod 22:22-23; Deut 27:19; Ps 68:5). Ekdikēson me (grant me justice, avenge me — the verb from dike, justice/right) — the widow is not asking for revenge but for the legal recognition of her legitimate claim. Antidikos (opponent in a legal case, legal adversary) — she has a specific legal dispute, not a personal grievance.
18.4: kai epi chronon ouk ēthelen; meta de tauta eipen en heautō — for some time he refused. But finally he said to himself. The judge’s refusal is extended (epi chronon, for a period of time) — the delay is part of the parable’s structure. Eipen en heautō (said to himself, an internal monologue) — the judge’s reasoning is made accessible to the reader; his eventual capitulation is purely pragmatic: not because of any ethical principle but because of personal inconvenience avoidance.
18.5: dia ge to parechein moi kopon tēn chēran tautēn ekdikēsō autēn, hina mē eis telos erchomenē hypōpiazē me — yet because this widow keeps bothering me, I will see that she gets justice so she won’t wear me out by her endless coming. Hypōpiazē (wear me down, exhaust me — literally “to strike under the eye, to give a black eye”; metaphorically to wear out by persistent battering) is a boxing term; the widow’s persistence is described as relentless physical battering. Eis telos (in the end, to the end, finally) — the phrase combines temporal and telic senses: the widow will keep coming until she prevails.
18.6: eipen de ho kyrios: akousate ti ho kritēs tēs adikias legei — and the Lord said: Listen to what the unjust judge says. Ho kritēs tēs adikias (the judge of unrighteousness — a Semitic genitive meaning the unrighteous judge) — the judge is defined by his moral character. Luke uses ho kyrios (the Lord) as the narrator’s title for Jesus (cf. 7:13; 10:1) signaling this as an authoritative interpretive comment on the parable. The instruction to “listen to” the unjust judge is ironic: the unrighteous man’s reasoning reveals something true about the nature of persistent petition.
18.7: ho de theos ou mē poiēsē tēn ekdikēsin tōn eklektōn autou tōn boōntōn autō hēmeras kai nyktos — and will not God bring about justice for his chosen ones, who cry out to him day and night? The a fortiori argument is made explicit: the unjust judge who cares nothing for the widow eventually responds; will not God — who loves his chosen ones (hoi eklektoi, the elect, those chosen in covenant relationship) — respond to their persistent crying? Hēmeras kai nyktos (day and night) — the continual nature of the prayer, corresponding to the widow’s continual coming. Kai makrothymei ep autois (and is he patient/long-suffering toward them?) — textual difficulty: does God delay or is he patient with his people while they wait?
18.8: legō hymin hoti poiēsei tēn ekdikēsin autōn en tachei — I tell you, he will see that they get justice, and quickly. En tachei (quickly, soon — either “without delay once he acts” or “soon in eschatological time”) — the assurance of swift divine action stands in tension with the apparent delay of the parable; the “quickly” is eschatological, not chronological. Plēn ho huios tou anthrōpou elthōn ara heurēsei tēn pistin epi tēs gēs (however, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?) — the eschatological question with which the parable-application ends: persistent faith-prayer is what the Son of Man will look for at his coming; will it still be present?
18.9: eipen de kai pros tinas tous pepoithotas eph heautois hoti eisin dikaioi kai exouthenountas tous loipous tēn parabolēn tautēn — to some who were confident of their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else, Jesus told this parable. The audience identification is unusually specific: Luke names the attitude (pepoithotas eph heautois hoti eisin dikaioi — those who trust in themselves that they are righteous) and the behavior (exouthenountas tous loipous — treating others with contempt). The parable of the Pharisee and Tax Collector diagnoses the spiritual condition of self-righteous contempt, which is the opposite of the widow’s persistent, dependent prayer.
18.10: anthrōpoi dyo anebēsan eis to hieron proseuxasthai, ho heis pharisaios kai ho heteros telōnēs — two men went up to the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector. The two figures represent opposite ends of Jewish religious respectability: the Pharisee is the model of Torah-observance and religious discipline; the tax collector is a collaborator with Rome and a ritual outcast. Both go to the same temple, both to pray — the parable is about two kinds of prayer, not two moral categories of human being.
18.11: ho pharisaios statheis pros heauton tauta proseucheto: ho theos, eucharistō soi hoti ouk eimi hōsper hoi loipoi tōn anthrōpōn — the Pharisee stood and prayed about himself: God, I thank you that I am not like other people. Pros heauton (about/to himself) — a textual and interpretive crux: does he pray “about himself” (the prayer’s content is self-referential) or “to himself” (the prayer never leaves the self, never reaches God)? Both senses may be intended. The prayer is formally a thanksgiving (eucharistō) but its content is comparative self-praise: his virtue is defined by contrast with others. The negative list (not like robbers, evildoers, adulterers, or this tax collector) is his spiritual identity.
18.12: nēsteuō dis tou sabbatou, apodekatō panta hosa ktōmai — I fast twice a week and give a tenth of all I get. The Pharisee’s positive credentials: fasting twice weekly (dis tou sabbatou) exceeded the Torah requirement (Yom Kippur) and the common practice (Mondays and Thursdays were voluntary fast days for the pious); tithing all possessions (panta hosa ktōmai, all that I acquire) extended the Torah tithe to include untithed produce bought at market. Both are genuine acts of religious discipline — the problem is not the practices themselves but their function as the basis of self-comparison and divine claim.
18.14: legō hymin, katebē houtos dedikaiōmenos eis ton oikon autou par ekeinon — I tell you that this man, rather than the other, went home justified before God. Dedikaiōmenos (having been justified, declared righteous — the perfect passive participle of dikaioō) — the same term that Paul uses for forensic justification in Romans and Galatians. The tax collector who prayed the atonement prayer (v.13 — “God, be propitious to me the sinner”) is declared righteous; the Pharisee who catalogued his virtues is not. The parable enacts the reversal-principle that closes it: pas ho hypsōn heauton tapeinōthēsetai, ho de tapeinōn heauton hypsōthēsetai (everyone who exalts himself will be humbled; the one who humbles himself will be exalted).
18.15: prosepheron de autō kai ta brephē hina autōn haptētai; idontes de hoi mathētai epetimōn autois — people were also bringing babies to Jesus for him to place his hands on them. When the disciples saw this, they rebuked them. Brephē (infants, newborns — the earliest stage of life) distinguishes Luke’s version from Matthew’s and Mark’s paidia (children); the adults bringing infants to Jesus is a more extreme case of the kingdom’s reception. The disciples’ rebuke (epetimōn) reflects their role-protective instinct: the teacher’s time and attention should not be consumed by infants. Jesus’s reversal will make their rebuke the occasion for a kingdom lesson.
18.16: ho de Iēsous proskalesamenos auta eipen: aphete ta paidia erchesthai pros me kai mē kōluete auta — but Jesus called the children to him and said: Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them. Aphete (let, release — the same root as aphesis, forgiveness/release) and mē kōluete (do not hinder) — Jesus removes both the disciples’ barrier and the prohibition. Tōn gar toioutōn estin hē basileia tou theou (for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these) — the genitive tōn toioutōn (of such as these) is the ground of the welcome: the kingdom is constituted by those who have this quality of receptive dependence.
18.17: amēn legō hymin, hos an mē dexētai tēn basileian tou theou hōs paidion, ou mē eiselthē eis autēn — truly I tell you, anyone who will not receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it. Hōs paidion (like a child, as a child) — the child is not a model of innocence or purity but of receptivity: a child receives a gift without any contribution of merit or any transaction of exchange. The kingdom is not earned, negotiated, or deserved — it is received. Ou mē eiselthē (will certainly not enter — the double negative ou mē is the strongest negative in Greek) — the double-strength negative emphasizes that no alternative path of entry exists.
18.18: kai epērōtēsen tis auton archōn legōn: didaskale agathe, ti poiēsas zōēn aiōnion klēronomiō — a certain ruler asked him: Good teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Archōn (ruler — Luke specifies he is a ruler, giving him social authority; Matthew and Mark say “someone” or “a young man”). Didaskale agathe (good teacher) — the address to which Jesus responds with a counterquestion about goodness. Ti poiēsas (what having done, what must I do?) — the question assumes the active merit-earning model of eternal life; the ruler is looking for an achievable task. Jesus will expose the assumption.
18.19: eipen de autō ho Iēsous: ti me legeis agathon? oudeis agathos ei mē heis ho theos — why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone. The counterquestion is not a denial of Jesus’s own goodness but a redirection: if “good” is a meaningful category in the absolute sense, it belongs to God alone. The ruler’s complimentary address has used “good” as a social honorific; Jesus insists on its theological weight. The implicit question is: if God alone is good, and if the ruler recognizes Jesus as good, what does that say about who Jesus is? The Christological implication is not explicit but hangs in the air.
18.20: tas entolas oidas: mē moicheusēs, mē phoneusēs, mē klepsēs, mē pseudomarturēsēs, tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera — you know the commandments: do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not give false testimony, honor your father and mother. The subset of commandments Jesus cites is the second table of the Decalogue — the social/horizontal commandments (duties toward other humans). The first table (duties toward God) is conspicuously absent; Jesus will expose the ruler’s failure precisely in the area of the first commandment: his god is his wealth.
18.21: ho de eipen: tauta panta ephylaxamēn ek neotētos — all these I have kept since I was a boy. The ruler’s claim of lifelong commandment-keeping is not challenged by Jesus; the statement is accepted as his self-assessment. The MKT “since I was a boy” (ek neotētos, from youth) may indicate teenage years when a Jewish boy came under full Torah obligation. The claim is not necessarily false — a man of wealth and social position could maintain the observable social commandments. What he lacks is beyond observable compliance.
18.22: akousas de ho Iēsous eipen autō: eti hen soi leipei; panta hosa echeis pōlēson kai diaskorpison ptōchois — when Jesus heard this, he said to him: You still lack one thing. Sell everything you have and give to the poor. Hen leipei (one thing is lacking) — the singular lack contrasts with the plural commandments kept; the one missing thing is more significant than all the kept ones combined. Pōlēson kai diaskorpison ptōchois (sell and distribute to the poor) — the command is total: panta hosa echeis (everything you have). Kai deuro akolouthei moi (and come, follow me) — the follow-me invitation is the completion; the selling is the prerequisite for discipleship, not the merit-act that earns eternal life.
18.23: ho de akousas tauta perilypos egeneto, ēn gar plousios sphodra — when he heard this, he became very sad, because he was very wealthy. Perilypos (very sad, deeply grieved — peri- intensifying lypos/grief; the same word used for Jesus in Gethsemane, Mark 14:34) — the ruler’s grief is genuine; he genuinely desired eternal life and genuinely cannot bring himself to relinquish the wealth to which he is deeply attached. Ēn plousios sphodra (he was very wealthy) — the “very” intensifies the attachment; his wealth is not marginal but central to his identity and social power.
18.24: idōn de auton ho Iēsous perilypon genomenon eipen: pōs dyskōlōs hoi ta chrēmata echontes eis tēn basileian tou theou eisporeuontai — Jesus looked at him and said: How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God! Dyskōlōs (with difficulty, hard — from dys- bad + koilon the stomach; one who is hard to please even with food, hence finicky, difficult). The observation is compassionate and diagnostic: Jesus looks at the departing ruler and reads in him the universal problem of wealth as attachment. The difficulty is not moral condemnation but structural: wealth creates an alternative trust-system that competes with trust in God.
18.25: eukolōteron gar estin kamēlon dia trēmatos belonēs eiselthein ē plousion eis tēn basileian tou theou eiselthein — indeed, it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God. The hyperbole of the camel through a needle’s eye (kamēlos... trēmatos belonēs) is the largest animal in Palestine passing through the smallest aperture — the most vivid impossibility available in the cultural context. The attempts to domesticate this (a “camel” rope, a “Needle’s Gate” at Jerusalem) lack historical support; the image is deliberately, humorously extreme to make the point about the difficulty of wealth-detachment.
18.26: eipan de hoi akousantes: kai tis dynatai sōthēnai — those who heard this asked: Who then can be saved? The crowd’s conclusion is logical: if the wealthy are paradigmatically unable to enter the kingdom (and wealth was widely taken as a sign of divine blessing), then no one can qualify. The question exposes the assumption that the wealthy are the most likely to be saved; Jesus’s response will dismantle both the wealth-blessing assumption and the human-effort model of salvation.
18.27: ho de eipen: ta adynata para anthrōpois dynata para tō theō estin — Jesus replied: What is impossible with man is possible with God. Adynata para anthrōpois (impossible alongside/with humans, humanly impossible) and dynata para tō theō (possible alongside/with God, divinely possible) — the framing shifts the locus of possibility from human effort to divine action. The salvation of the wealthy is not impossible because of wealth itself but because human attachment to wealth makes self-rescue impossible; what God can do is not limited by what wealth-attachment has blocked.
18.28: eipen de ho Petros: idou hēmeis aphentes ta idia ēkolouthēsamen soi — Peter said to him: We have left all we had to follow you. Peter’s statement is factually accurate (they left their fishing nets, tax booth, family) and contextually pointed: the disciples have done what the ruler could not do. Aphentes ta idia (having left our own things) — the aorist participle emphasizes the completed act of leaving; Peter is identifying the disciples as those who have already crossed the threshold the ruler failed to cross.
18.29: ho de eipen autois: amēn legō hymin hoti oudeis estin hos aphēken oikian ē gynaika ē adelphous ē goneis ē tekna — truly I tell you, no one who has left home or wife or brothers or parents or children. Luke’s list includes gynaika (wife) which the parallel in Mark 10:29 lacks in some manuscripts; the Lukan expansion of the list to include wife reflects the more radical demands of Luke’s travel-narrative discipleship. The “for the sake of the kingdom of God” (heneken tēs basileias tou theou) replaces Mark’s “for me and the gospel.”
18.30: hos ouchi mē apolampsē pollaplasiona en tō kairō toutō kai en tō aiōni tō erchomenō zōēn aiōnion — will fail to receive many times as much in this age, and in the age to come eternal life. Pollaplasiona (many times over, manifold) — the promise is extravagant: the leaving of family and property for the kingdom is met with a manifold return already in “this age” (en tō kairō toutō — the present eschatological age inaugurated by Jesus). En tō aiōni tō erchomenō zōēn aiōnion (in the coming age eternal life) — the double promise (manifold now, eternal life then) encompasses both the present and the future dimensions of kingdom participation.
18.31: paralab̄ōn de tous dōdeka eipen pros autous: idou anabainomen eis Ierousalēm kai telesthēsetai panta ta gegrammena dia tōn prophētōn tō huiō tou anthrōpou — Jesus took the Twelve aside and told them: We are going up to Jerusalem, and everything written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled. This is the third formal passion prediction in Luke (cf. 9:22; 9:43-45) and the most detailed. Telesthēsetai (will be completed, fulfilled — from teleō, to complete, bring to the appointed end) — the passion is a teleological completion: the Son of Man’s prophetically scripted destiny is being accomplished. Ta gegrammena dia tōn prophētōn (what has been written through the prophets) makes the passion a scriptural necessity.
18.32: paradothēsetai gar tois ethnesin kai empaichthēsetai kai hybristhēsetai kai emptysthēsetai — he will be handed over to the Gentiles. They will mock him, insult him and spit on him. Paradothēsetai tois ethnesin (will be handed over to the Gentiles) — the divine passive of the handing-over (paradidōmi) is the passion’s central verb; the handing-over to Rome is the mechanism of the scriptural fulfillment. The four acts of humiliation (mock, insult, spit, flog — a four-verb passion inventory) correspond to the Servant songs and Psalm 22: the Son of Man’s passion is the Servant’s suffering enacted.
18.33: kai mastigōsantes apoktennousin auton kai tē hēmera tē tritē anastēsetai — they will flog him and kill him. On the third day he will rise again. Mastigōsantes (having flogged) anticipates Pilate’s chastisement before the crowd (23:16,22). Apoktennousin (they will kill) — the divine passive is abandoned here for the active voice, placing human agency explicitly in the passion’s killing. Tē hēmera tē tritē anastēsetai (on the third day he will rise again) — the resurrection promise is embedded in the passion prediction; the announcement is not merely of death but of death-and-resurrection as a single event with two phases.
18.34: kai autoi ouden toutōn synēkan — the disciples did not understand any of this. The triple incomprehension formula (ouden toutōn synēkan, they understood nothing of these things; kekrymmenon ap autōn to rhēma touto, this word was hidden from them; ouk eginōskon ta legomena, they did not know what was being said) is uniquely intensive in Luke. The hiddenness is providential (kekrymmenon, passive — hidden by divine action): they cannot understand the passion-resurrection until the resurrection itself opens their understanding (24:45). The three-fold incomprehension formula protects the resurrection as the hermeneutical key to the passion.
18.35: egeneto de en tō engizein auton eis Iericho typhlos tis ekathēto para tēn hodon epaitōn — as Jesus approached Jericho, a blind man was sitting by the roadside begging. The blind beggar episode immediately follows the third passion prediction and the disciples’ incomprehension, creating a contrast: the disciples cannot see what Jesus has just told them; the blind man will receive sight by faith. Para tēn hodon epaitōn (by the road begging) — the roadside is the natural location for beggars who depend on the generosity of passing travelers; the Jericho road, the route from the Jordan valley to Jerusalem, was well-traveled.
18.36: akousas de ochlou diaporeuomenou epynthaneto ti eiē touto — when he heard the crowd going by, he asked what was happening. Epynthaneto (he was asking, inquiring — imperfect, continuous asking) — the blind man depends entirely on auditory information; hearing the crowd’s movement, he asks for a visual report. Ti eiē touto (what this might be — optative of indirect question) — the polite form of an eager inquiry. His dependence on others for information about the visible world is the physical expression of his general condition; his healing will be both literal and symbolic.
18.37: apēngeilan de autō hoti Iēsous ho Nazōraios parerchestai — they told him: Jesus of Nazareth is passing by. Iēsous ho Nazōraios (Jesus the Nazarene — the geographic identification that became a title; Acts 2:22; 3:6; 4:10 all use this form as a proper identification of the risen Jesus in early preaching). Parerchestai (is passing by) — the present tense conveys urgency: the moment of passage is happening now. The blind man’s response will be immediate and desperate.
18.38: kai eboēsen legōn: Iēsou huie David, eleēson me — he called out: Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me! Huie David (Son of David) is the messianic title from the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:14; Ps 89:3-4; Isa 11:1-3); in the Synoptic tradition, it is the title used by those who recognize Jesus’s Messianic identity from outside the inner circle (also Matt 9:27; 15:22; 20:30). The blind man’s cry is a Christological confession that the disciples have struggled to articulate; his blindness has not obscured his recognition of who Jesus is.
18.39: kai hoi proagontes epetimōn autō hina sigēsē; autos de pollō mallon ekrazen: huie David, eleēson me — those who led the way rebuked him and told him to be quiet, but he shouted all the more: Son of David, have mercy on me! The crowd’s rebuke (epetimōn) creates a social pressure to silence — the same verb used for the disciples’ rebuke of those bringing children (v.15). The blind man’s response (pollō mallon ekrazen — he shouted all the more) is the opposite of compliance: the resistance increases the volume of his petition. His persistence parallels the widow’s (18:3) and is the parable’s enacted embodiment.
18.40: statheis de ho Iēsous ekeleusen auton achthēnai pros auton — Jesus stopped and ordered the man to be brought to him. Statheis (having stopped, standing still) — the entire procession toward Jerusalem pauses; Jesus halts the journey for the blind beggar. The stopping is a narrative gesture of compassion: the Son of David stops for the one who called on him by that title. Ekeleusen achthēnai (commanded him to be brought) — the summons reverses the crowd’s exclusion; the one they tried to silence is now summoned to the center.
18.41: eggisantos de autou epērōtēsen auton: ti soi theleis poiēsō? ho de eipen: kyrie, hina anablepō — what do you want me to do for you? Lord, I want to see. The open question (ti soi theleis poiēsō, what do you want me to do for you?) — also asked by Jesus to James and John (Mark 10:36), but there the request is for thrones. Here the blind man’s desire is simply and completely expressed: hina anablepō (that I might see again — the ana- prefix suggests restored sight, implying he was not born blind). His request is clear, self-aware, and entirely dependent: he wants what only Jesus can give.
18.42: kai ho Iēsous eipen autō: anablepson; hē pistis sou sesōken se — Jesus said to him: Receive your sight; your faith has saved you. Anablepson (look up, see again — the same root as the petition) — the command is the fulfillment of the request, word-for-word. Hē pistis sou sesōken se (your faith has saved you) — the formula of salvation (also 7:50; 8:48; 17:19) confirms this as more than physical healing: the blind man’s pistis (faith, trust — expressed in calling on the Son of David and persisting despite opposition) is the receptive medium through which saving power flows.
18.43: kai parachrēma aneblepsen kai ēkolouthei autō doxazōn ton theon — immediately he received his sight and followed Jesus, praising God. Parachrēma (immediately) — Luke’s favorite term for miraculous instantaneity (also 1:64; 4:39; 5:25; 8:44; 13:13; 19:11). Ēkolouthei autō (he was following him, imperfect — continuous following) — the healed man does not go home; he joins the procession to Jerusalem. His following is the embodiment of discipleship: the one who was sitting by the road is now walking the road with Jesus. Doxazōn ton theon (glorifying God) is the proper response to the saving act; the crowd who saw it also praised God.

19.10: elthen gar ho huios tou anthropou zetesai kai sosai to apololes — for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost — Luke's mission-statement (parallel to Matt 20:28 / Mark 10:45 but using sozo and apollymi instead of the ransom-language). Zetesai (to seek) echoes the seeking-shepherd of Ezek 34:11 and the seeking-woman of Luke 15:8; sosai (to save) is Luke's characteristic salvation-vocabulary; to apololes (the lost thing, the perishing one) echoes all three parables of Luke 15 (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son). Zacchaeus is the concrete instance of the Son of Man's seeking-and-saving mission.
19.1: Kai eiselthōn diērcheto tēn Iericho — entering, he was passing through Jericho — Iericho (Jericho) was the final major stop on the ascent road from the Jordan valley to Jerusalem; ~27km separated it from Jerusalem, with a 1,000m altitude gain. Eiselthōn diērcheto (entering, was passing through — two verbs indicating momentary entry into a through-passage) sets up the dramatic interruption: Jesus was not stopping in Jericho but is detained by two encounters (blind Bartimaeus in 18:35-43; Zacchaeus here). The Jericho scenes are Luke's final station before the Jerusalem entry.
19.2: kai idou anēr onomati kaloumenos Zakchaios, kai autos ēn architelōnēs kai autos plousios — and there was a man called by name Zacchaeus, and he was a chief tax collector and wealthy — Zakchaios is the Greek form of the Hebrew Zakkay (pure, innocent — a name whose irony Luke's audience would appreciate). Architelōnēs (chief tax collector — a compound unique to Luke in the NT; archi- = head, chief + telōnēs = tax collector) implies a supervisory role over a revenue district, not merely a single customs booth. Jericho was a wealthy customs location on the balsam-trade route; plousios (wealthy) is the third descriptor, completing his social profile: named, powerful, rich.
19.3: kai ezētei idein ton Iēsoun tis estin kai ouk ēdynato apo tou ochlou, hoti tē hēlikia mikros ēn — he was seeking to see who Jesus was and could not because of the crowd, because he was small in stature — ezētei idein (was seeking to see — imperfect of persistent effort) before the encounter begins. Tis estin (who he is) — the same Christological question the storm disciples asked (8:25); Zacchaeus is asking the right question. Tē hēlikia mikros (small in stature — hēlikia can mean age or stature; here physical height, confirmed by v.4). The crowd that surrounds Jesus is the immediate obstacle; Zacchaeus's social marginality (tax collectors were despised) may have meant no one would let him through.
19.4: kai prodramōn eis to emprosthen anebē epi sykomorean — running ahead to the front, he climbed a sycamore-fig tree — prodramōn (running ahead — aorist participle of proactive initiative; an undignified act for a wealthy official in honor-based Mediterranean culture). Sykomorea (sycamore-fig — Ficus sycomorus, different from the sycamore of temperate climates; a spreading tree with low branches accessible for climbing, common in the Jericho valley). The wealthy tax official climbing a tree to see Jesus inverts the expected social hierarchy: the powerful man humbles himself to a childlike position. hina idē auton, hoti ekeinēs emellen diercheisthai — to see him because he was about to pass through that way.
19.5: kai hōs ēlthen epi ton topon, anablepsas ho Iēsous eipen pros auton, Zakchaie, speusas kataba — when he came to the place, looking up Jesus said to him: Zacchaeus, hurry and come down — Jesus looks up (anablepsas — the verb for restored sight in 18:41-43; the Bartimaeus healing is still fresh in the narrative) and addresses Zacchaeus by name: divine initiative from below. sēmeron gar en tō oikō sou dei me meinai — for today it is necessary for me to remain in your house — dei (divine necessity — Luke's compulsion word) — the stay in Zacchaeus's house is not social preference but divine appointment. Sēmeron (today — Luke's eschatological present; cf. 4:21; 23:43) marks the moment of salvation's arrival.
19.6: kai speusas katebē kai hypedexato auton chairōn — hurrying he came down and welcomed him rejoicing — the speed and joy of the response: speusas (hurrying — the same root as the angels' urgency in 2:16; the shepherds who "went with haste") and chairōn (rejoicing — the characteristic Lukan response to grace). Hypedechomai (to welcome, receive as a guest — a compound of dechomai: to accept, receive; the posture of hospitality and humble reception) — Zacchaeus is not a passive recipient but an active welcomer. The reception mirrors the prodigal's father (15:20) and the host who runs to meet the returning son.
19.7: kai idontes pantes diegoggyzon legontes hoti Para hamartōlō andri eisēlthen katalysai — all seeing it were murmuring, saying: he has gone in to stay with a sinful man — diegoggyzon (were murmuring, grumbling thoroughly — the dia-prefix intensifies; the LXX term for Israel's wilderness complaints, cf. 5:30) from the entire crowd. Hamartōlō andri (a sinful man) — the social verdict on the tax collector; he is categorically excluded from table-fellowship for those maintaining purity. The crowd's grumble is the same as the Pharisees' in 5:30 ("Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?") — the question the next three verses will answer definitively.
19.8: statheis de Zakchaios eipen pros ton Kyrion, Idou ta hēmisē mou tōn hyparchontōn, Kyrie, tois ptōchois didōmi — Zacchaeus stood and said to the Lord: behold, half of my possessions, Lord, I give to the poor — statheis (standing — taking a public, formal stance) marks a public declaration. Ta hēmisē (half — not all, which would be poverty, but the substantial half, exceeding the legal restitution requirement). The present tense didōmi (I give — not "I will give") indicates a current practice or an immediate decision, not a future promise. kai ei tinos ti esykophantēsa, apodidōmi tetraploun — and if I defrauded anyone of anything, I repay fourfold — sykophantō (to defraud, extort — cf. 3:14) followed by fourfold restitution exceeds the Torah's requirement (Exod 22:1 four-fold for stolen sheep; Lev 6:5 requires 100% + 20%).
19.9: eipen de pros auton ho Iēsous, Sēmeron sōtēria tō oikō toutō egeneto — Jesus said to him: today salvation has come to this house — Sēmeron sōtēria egeneto (today salvation happened/came into existence — aorist of accomplished fact) — sōtēria (salvation) has arrived in the person of Jesus; it came with him when he entered. Tō oikō toutō (to this house) — household-inclusive salvation: Zacchaeus's whole household is encompassed (cf. Acts 16:31; 18:8 where household salvation becomes normative in Acts). kathoti kai autos huios Abraam estin — because he also is a son of Abraham — the "son of Abraham" declaration (cf. 13:16 "daughter of Abraham") restores Zacchaeus's covenant status that his profession had forfeited in popular estimation; the declaration is divine, not social.
19.11: Akouontōn de autōn tauta prostheis eipen parabolēn — as they were hearing these things he added a parable — the parable of the Pounds is told because of a mistaken expectation: dia to engys einai Ierousalēm auton kai dokein autous hoti parachrēma mellei hē basileia tou Theou anaphainesthai — because he was near Jerusalem and they thought the kingdom of God was going to appear immediately — parachrēma (immediately, at once — Luke's healing-stamp word applied here to eschatological expectation) captures the disciples' imminent-kingdom excitement. The parable corrects this: there is an intervening period (the nobleman going to a distant country) before the kingdom's full arrival.
19.12: eipen oun, Anthrōpos tis eugenēs eporeuthē eis chōran makran labein heautō basileian kai hypostrephai — a man of noble birth went to a distant country to receive for himself a kingdom and to return — eugenēs (well-born, of noble lineage — the aristocratic qualifier) and the journey to receive a kingdom in a distant land evokes the historical precedent of Archelaus, who in 4 BCE traveled to Rome to receive confirmation of his inheritance of Judea from Caesar Augustus (Josephus, Ant. 17.299-303). A delegation of Jews had petitioned against him (Josephus, Ant. 17.300) — precisely the pattern of v.14. The allegory maps the absent nobleman onto Jesus's ascension and return.
19.13: kalesas de deka doulous heautou edōken autois deka mnas kai eipen pros autous, Pragmateusasthe en hō erchomai — calling ten of his servants he gave them ten minas and said to them: do business until I come — mna (mina — a Greek monetary unit = 100 drachmas = ~3.3 months' wages for a laborer; vs. Matthew's talanta which were 60 minas each). Ten servants receive one mina each — a modest but real commission. Pragmateuomai (to do business, trade, engage in commerce) is a commercial term for active investment. The instruction "until I come" (en hō erchomai) marks the period of the absent king as the time for faithful stewardship — an ecclesiological comment on the church's mandate in the interim.
19.14: hoi de politai autou emisoun auton kai apesteilan presbeiav opisō autou legontes, Ou thelomen touton basileusai eph hēmas — but his citizens hated him and sent a delegation after him saying: we do not want this man to reign over us — emisoun (were hating — imperfect of continuous hatred), presbeia (a delegation, embassy — the formal ambassadorial term for an official delegation sent to a higher authority). The historical parallel with the anti-Archelaus delegation is exact (Josephus, Ant. 17.300: a delegation of fifty Jews sent to Augustus). In the parable's allegory, the rejection of the nobleman is the rejection of Jesus by the Jerusalem establishment, climaxing in the Passion.
19.15: kai egeneto en tō epanelthein auton labonta tēn basileian kai eipen phōnēthēnai autō tous doulous toutous — when he returned having received the kingdom, he commanded that these servants be called — the return completes the allegory of the ascension-and-parousia: the nobleman (Jesus) has received the kingdom (exaltation to God's right hand) and returns to reckon with his servants. hina gnoi ti diepragmateusanto — in order to know what business they had conducted — diapragmateuomai (to conduct business thoroughly, trade diligently — compound of dia + pragmateuomai) is the accounting moment: what the servants did with the commission during the absent period.
19.16: parGeneto de ho prōtos legōn, Kyrie, hē mna sou deka mnas prosērgasato — the first came saying: Lord, your mina has earned ten minas more — prosērgasato (earned in addition, produced as a surplus — from prosergazomai: to work toward in addition; a commercial production term). Ten-fold return on a single mina is exceptional; the servant's use of "your mina" (hē mna sou) is the key theological detail — the servant acknowledges the capital as belonging to the master, not himself. The faithful steward returns the master's investment with return, not the investment alone.
19.17: kai eipen autō, Euge, agathe doule, hoti en elachistō pistos egenou, isthi exousian echōn epanō deka poleōn — well done, good servant! because you were faithful in a very small matter, be having authority over ten cities — Euge (well done! — a Greek exclamation of approval; Matthew's parallel has "well done" differently). Elachistō (the least, smallest thing — a mina relative to a kingdom is elachiston) — the faithful management of a small commission earns authority over ten cities. The scale of reward exceeds the scale of the commission: faithful stewardship in the interim period earns eschatological authority in the kingdom. The reward is increased responsibility, not rest.
19.18: kai ēlthen ho deuteros legōn, Hē mna sou, Kyrie, epoiēsen pente mnas — the second came saying: your mina, Lord, has made five minas — the second servant's return is proportionally strong (5x) though less than the first (10x). Like the first, he uses "your mina" — acknowledging the master's ownership. Luke does not record the same verbal commendation as Matthew's parallel (Mt 25:23 "well done") but the reward follows the same proportional logic.
19.19: eipen de kai toutō, Kai sy epanō ginou pente poleōn — he said also to this one: you also take charge of five cities — five cities for five-mina profit: the reward is proportional to return. The pattern of proportional reward (10 cities for 10 minas, 5 cities for 5 minas) establishes the kingdom's economy of faithfulness: the master does not flatten outcomes but calibrates recognition to the servant's results. The political-administrative character of the reward (governing cities) points to the disciples' eschatological role in the renewed creation (cf. Mt 19:28; Rev 20:4-6).
19.20: kai ho heteros ēlthen legōn, Kyrie, idou hē mna sou hēn eichon apokeimenēn en soudarion — the other came saying: Lord, behold your mina which I had stored in a cloth — apokeimenēn (stored away, laid up — perfect passive participle: kept in a fixed, unchanged state) — the mina returned is precisely the mina given; nothing gained, nothing lost. Soudariov (cloth, napkin — a Latin loanword from sudarium, a sweat cloth) was not a secure storage vessel; it suggests informal, low-security preservation rather than banking. The contrast with the first servants' active trading is stark: this servant did nothing with the commission.
19.21: ephoboumēn gar se, hoti anthrōpos austēros ei, aireis ho ouk ethēkas kai therizeis ho ouk espeiras — for I was afraid of you, because you are an exacting man; you take up what you did not set down and reap what you did not sow — austēros (austere, strict, exacting — from austēros meaning dry, harsh, stringent; used of a demanding master) is the servant's characterization of the master. The reaping-what-one-didn't-sow idiom describes appropriating others' labor — the servant's stated reason for non-investment is fear of the master's expectations, which ironically makes his inaction even more culpable: if the master is demanding, more reason to produce.
19.22: legei autō, Ek tou stomatos sou krinō se, ponēre doule — he says to him: from your own mouth I judge you, wicked servant — Ek tou stomatos sou krinō se (from your own mouth I judge you) — the servant's own stated beliefs about the master are turned into the basis of judgment. ēdeis hoti egō anthrōpos austēros eimi, airōn ho ouk ethēka kai therizōn ho ouk espeira — you knew that I am an exacting man, taking what I did not set down and reaping what I did not sow — if the servant's assessment of the master was correct, his failure to invest was the more inexcusable: the most exacting possible master would demand returns, not merely preservation of capital.
19.23: kai dia ti ouk edōkas mou to argyrion epi trapezan — and why therefore did you not give my money to the bank? — trapeza (table, banker's table — the money-changer's or banker's counter; the term for banking institutions in the Hellenistic world, cf. the temple money-changers' trapezai in Mt 21:12). The minimum acceptable action was deposit for interest: even without active trading, the servant could have made something. kagō elthōn syn tokō an auto epraxa — and I, coming, would have collected it with interest — tokos (interest on a loan — literally "offspring," money that grows; cf. Exod 22:25; Deut 23:19-20 which restricted interest among Israelites but not in commercial Gentile contexts) — even modest interest would have been better than no return.
19.24: kai tois parestōsin eipen, Arate ap autou tēn mnan kai dote tō tas deka mnas echonti — and to those standing by he said: take the mina from him and give it to the one who has ten minas — the redistribution is the application of the principle stated in v.26. Parestōsin (those standing by) — the bystanders at the judgment scene who carry out the king's orders. The mina taken from the unproductive servant goes to the most productive — not to the second servant with five but to the first with ten. The logic of kingdom economy: resources flow toward demonstrated fruitfulness, not toward potential or need.
19.25: kai eipan autō, Kyrie, echei deka mnas — they said to him: Lord, he already has ten minas — the objection of the bystanders is the expected human reaction: fairness would suggest giving to one with less, not more. The objection receives no rebuke but prompts the economic principle of v.26. Luke includes this exchange (absent from Matthew) to create space for the explicit principle-statement; the objection voices what the audience is thinking.
19.26: legō hymin hoti panti tō echonti dothēsetai, apo de tou mē echontos kai ho echei arthēsetai — I tell you that to everyone who has, more will be given; but from the one who does not have, even what he has will be taken away — the principle of kingdom economy (cf. 8:18; Mk 4:25; Mt 13:12; 25:29): productive use of what is given generates increase; non-use leads to loss. The principle is not about initial endowment but about faithfulness with what one has been given. In the parable's context: those who stewarded the commission faithfully accumulate; those who did not lose even the original. The principle operates in the eschatological accounting, not in material economy.
19.27: plēn tous echthrous mou toutous tous mē thelēsantas me basileusai ep autous agagete hōde kai katasphaxate autous emprosthen mou — but as for these enemies of mine who did not want me to reign over them, bring them here and slaughter them before me — the king's command regarding the rebellious citizens (v.14) applies the political allegory to its historical conclusion: the anti-Archelaus delegation failed and Archelaus was confirmed; in the parable's deeper logic, this anticipates Jerusalem's destruction in 70 CE as the consequence of rejecting the Messiah-King. The violence of the image is the parable's realism about political consequences, not a pastoral instruction.
19.28: kai tauta eipōn eporeueto emprosthen anabainōn eis Ierousalēm — having said these things he was going ahead, going up to Jerusalem — emprosthen (ahead, in front) — Jesus leads the ascent; the disciples follow. Anabainōn eis Ierousalēm (going up to Jerusalem) — the geographical-theological ascent: from Jericho (-250m below sea level) to Jerusalem (+750m above sea level) is a 1,000m climb, always described as "going up" (anabainō). The journey narrative (9:51) that has been building since the Galilee chapters now reaches its destination: the next step after the parable is the entry into the city.
19.29: kai egeneto hōs ēggisen eis Bēthphagē kai Bēthanian pros to oros to kaloumenon Elaiōn — when he came near to Bethphage and Bethany at the mount called the Mount of Olives — Bēthphagē (Bethphage — "house of unripe figs," a village on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives) and Bēthanian (Bethany — the home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus; ~2km from Jerusalem). Oros ... Elaiōn (Mount of Olives) is the ridge east of Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley; Zech 14:4 places YHWH's feet there at the eschaton, giving the mountain eschatological significance. apesteilen dyo tōn mathētōn — he sent two of the disciples — the dual-witness commission (Deut 19:15).
19.30: Hypagete eis tēn katenanti kōmēn, en hē eisporeuomenoi heurēsete pōlon dedemenon, eph hon oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen — go into the village opposite, in which entering you will find a colt tied, on which no one has ever sat — pōlos (a young animal — in this context a young donkey; the LXX uses pōlos for the donkey's foal of Zech 9:9). Eph hon oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen (on which no one has ever sat) — an unridden animal was considered sacred and appropriate for holy purposes (cf.
