Num 19:2 the unworked red heifer; 1 Sam 6:7 the unworked cows). The unused animal is fitting for a king's ride. kai lysantes auton agagete — and untying him, bring him.
19.31: kai ean tis hymas erōtā, Dia ti lyete, houtōs ereite, Hoti ho Kyrios autou chreian echei — and if anyone asks you: why are you untying it? say thus: because the Lord needs it — ho Kyrios (the Lord) is used absolutely without further identification — the title the post-resurrection community used for the risen Jesus (cf. Acts 2:36). Chreian echei (has need, needs it) — the Lord's need is the authorization that overrides the owners' ordinary property rights. The pre-arranged answer (or a miraculous provision of the right words) demonstrates Jesus's sovereign knowledge of what will happen and his authority to commandeer what he needs for the messianic entry.
19.32: apelthontes de hoi apestalmenoi heuron kathōs eipen autois — going, those sent found it exactly as he had told them — kathōs eipen autois (just as he told them) is the verification of prophetic knowledge: Jesus's instruction was not a guess but foreknowledge. Luke uses this verification pattern in 22:13 (the Passover room) to frame both the triumphal entry and the Last Supper as divinely arranged events. The disciples' experience of finding things "just as he said" is itself a discipleship formation: training them to trust Jesus's foreknowledge in preparation for the Passion's apparent chaos.
19.33: lyontōn de autōn ton pōlon eipan hoi kyrioi autou pros autous, Ti lyete ton pōlon — as they were untying the colt, its owners said to them: why are you untying the colt? — kyrioi autou (its owners — plural) — the colt has multiple owners (perhaps a family). The challenge is natural and expected; the disciples have been prepared for it (v.31). Luke's inclusion of the encounter makes the scene concrete: the disciples face an actual challenge and respond with the prepared answer. The owners' question is not hostile but naturally curious; what follows (v.34) is their simple acceptance of the answer.
19.34: hoi de eipan, Ho Kyrios autou chreian echei — they said: the Lord needs it — the identical phrase from v.31 is repeated verbatim: the prepared answer is given, word for word. Luke's repetition is narrative confirmation: what was predicted was enacted exactly. The owners' acceptance of this explanation without further question may indicate: (a) they knew Jesus, (b) they recognized a divine commission, or (c) Luke abbreviates their response. In any case, the colt is released; the messianic entry can proceed.
19.35: kai ēgagon auton pros ton Iēsoun kai epiriptes autōn ta himatia epi ton pōlon epebibasan ton Iēsoun — they brought it to Jesus and throwing their garments on the colt they set Jesus on it — epiriptes (throwing over, casting on) their garments (himatia) on the colt creates the impromptu saddle; the gesture is simultaneously practical and honorific. The placing of garments under a new king echoes 2 Kgs 9:13 where the generals spread their garments under Jehu when proclaiming him king. Epebibasan (set him on it) — the disciples act as royal attendants placing the king on his mount.
19.36: poreuomenou de autou hypestrōnnyon ta himatia autōn en tē hodō — as he was going, they were spreading their garments on the road — hypestrōnnyon (were spreading under, strewing — imperfect of continuous action throughout the procession) the garments on the road — again the royal-installation gesture (2 Kgs 9:13). Matthew (21:8) and Mark (11:8) add branches cut from trees; Luke mentions only garments, perhaps for literary simplicity or because branches were less common in the Jericho/Olivet corridor. The road-spreading creates a royal carpet for the Messiah-King's entrance.
19.37: eggizovtos de autou ēdē pros tē katabasei tou orous tōn Elaiōn ērxanto hapan to plēthos tōn mathētōn chairovtes ainein ton Theon phōnē megalē — as he was already approaching the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of disciples began rejoicing to praise God with a great voice — katabasis (descent, the downward slope) — on the descent from the Mount of Olives, Jerusalem comes into view across the Kidron; this is the moment of the crowd's eruption. Hapan to plēthos tōn mathētōn (the whole crowd of disciples — not just the Twelve but the wider disciple community) begins the acclamation. peri pasōn hōn eidon dynameōn — for all the works of power they had seen — the praise is grounded in witnessed miracles.
19.38: legontes, Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos ho basileus en onomati Kyriou — saying: blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord — Ps 118:26 (LXX) is the citation (the final verse of the Hallel, sung at Passover). Luke uniquely inserts ho basileus (the King) into the Psalm citation, making the messianic application explicit. en ouranō eirēnē kai doxa en hypsistois — peace in heaven and glory in the highest — Luke's version replaces the crowd's "Hosanna" (Matthew, Mark, John) with a heavenly doxology that mirrors the birth announcement (2:14: "glory to God in the highest and on earth peace"). The triumphal entry announces the convergence of heaven and earth: peace that was "on earth" at the birth is now "in heaven" as the King approaches his throne through the cross.
19.39: kai tines tōn Pharisaiōn apo tou ochlou eipan pros auton, Didaskale, epitimēson tois mathētais sou — some of the Pharisees from the crowd said to him: Teacher, rebuke your disciples — the Pharisaic objection is to the messianic title "king" and the Hallel-citation; the messianic acclamation is publicly dangerous (Roman authorities would consider it seditious) and, in Pharisaic judgment, theologically inappropriate. Epitimēson tois mathētais sou (rebuke your disciples) — the same verb used for Jesus's rebukes of demons and fever; the Pharisees demand that Jesus silence the messianic proclamation.
19.40: kai apokritheis eipen, Legō hymin, ean houtoi siōpēsousin, hoi lithoi kraxousin — answering he said: I tell you, if these keep silent, the stones will cry out — the declaration is categorical: the messianic acclamation is so intrinsic to the moment that its suppression is cosmologically impossible. Hoi lithoi kraxousin (the stones will cry out) — an echo of Hab 2:11 (the stones of unjust walls cry out) and the broader prophetic tradition of creation testifying to divine truth. The stones of Jerusalem — which in 40 years will be hurled down (19:44) — are here presented as potential proclaimers of the King.
19.41: kai hōs ēggisen, idōn tēn polin eklausen ep autēn — when he came near, seeing the city he wept over it — eklausen (he wept — aorist: a specific act of weeping, not merely watering eyes; the strong word for grief-weeping, used also for the widow of Nain 7:13 and Peter 22:62). Luke is the only Gospel that records Jesus weeping over Jerusalem (Jn 11:35 records weeping over Lazarus; Mk/Mt have no parallel here). The weping occurs on the descent of the Mount of Olives as Jerusalem comes fully into view — the city of the temple, the city that kills prophets (13:34), the city he has been moving toward since 9:51.
19.42: legōn hoti Ei egnōs en tē hēmera tautē kai sy ta pros eirēnēn — saying: if you had known, even you, in this day the things for peace — Ei egnōs ... ta pros eirēnēn (if you had known the things for peace — a second-class conditional expressing a contrary-to-fact situation: you did not know) — the lament over what might have been. Ta pros eirēnēn (the things for peace = what peace requires, the conditions of peace) — the shalom that the Messiah-King brings but Jerusalem cannot recognize. nyn de ekrybē apo ophthalmōn sou — but now it has been hidden from your eyes — ekrybē (was hidden — divine passive: God has hidden; the hardening of 8:10 applied nationally) — the opportunity has passed; the time of visitation (v.44) is closing.
19.43: hoti hēxousin hēmerai epi se kai parembalousin hoi echthroi sou charaka soi — because days will come upon you when your enemies will throw up a rampart against you — Jesus's prediction of Jerusalem's siege follows the prophetic pattern of Isa 29:3 (YHWH laying siege to Ariel), Jer 6:6 (cutting down trees for siege-works), and especially Ezek 4:1-3 (the symbolic siege of Jerusalem). Charax (rampart, palisade of sharpened stakes — a Roman military term for circumvallation) describes precisely the siege techniques Titus used in 70 CE (Josephus, War 5.491-511): the circumvallation wall preventing escape. Perikyklōsousin se (will encircle you) is Josephus's exact language for Titus's siege.
19.44: kai edaphiousin se kai ta tekna sou en soi — they will dash you to the ground, you and your children within you — edaphizō (to raze to the ground, level — from edaphos, the floor or ground; a rare verb for total demolition) — Josephus describes the complete leveling of Jerusalem in 70 CE (War 7.1-3). kai ouk aphēsousin en soi lithon epi lithon — and they will not leave stone upon stone in you — the precise prediction of the temple's destruction confirmed by Josephus (War 7.1: "the Romans led by Titus demolished the city and the sanctuary to its foundations"). anth hōn ouk egnōs ton kairon tēs episkopēs sou — because you did not recognize the time of your visitation — kairos tēs episkopēs (the time of visitation — episkopē: inspection, oversight, the official visit of a dignitary or divine messenger; cf. Isa 10:3 LXX).
19.45: kai eiselthōn eis to hieron ērxato ekballein tous pōlountas — entering the temple he began to drive out those who were selling — hieron (the temple complex — the whole precinct, not the inner sanctuary naos). The sellers were in the Court of the Gentiles, selling ritually approved animals for sacrifice and exchanging foreign coins for the temple shekel required for the temple tax. Luke's version is the briefest of the four Gospel accounts: no mention of money-changers, doves, or buyers; simply the sellers driven out. The concision focuses on the prophetic act of cleansing rather than its commercial details.
19.46: legōn autois, Gegraptai, Kai estai ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs, hymeis de auton epoiēsate spēlaion lēstōn — saying to them: it is written: My house shall be a house of prayer; but you have made it a den of robbers — Isa 56:7 (LXX: ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs klēthēsetai pasi tois ethnesin — my house will be called a house of prayer for all nations; Luke omits "for all nations," Matthew and Mark retain it) + Jer 7:11 (spēlaion lēstōn = den of robbers/bandits). The Jeremiah citation is from the Temple Sermon where YHWH condemned those who treated the temple as a refuge for their crimes. The "den of robbers" (spēlaion lēstōn) is not a comment on commercial exploitation alone but on using the temple as a hiding place — lēstēs means armed rebel/bandit, not petty thief.
19.47: kai ēn didaskōn to kath hēmeran en tō hierō — and he was teaching daily in the temple — the imperfect ēn didaskōn (was teaching, continued to teach) throughout the Jerusalem period; the temple becomes Jesus's regular teaching platform after the entry. hoi de archiereis kai hoi grammateis ezētoun auton apoplesai kai hoi prōtoi tou laou — but the chief priests and the scribes and the principal men of the people were seeking to destroy him — apōlesai (to destroy, kill) — the triple coalition: archiereis (chief priests — the Sadducean high-priestly families), grammateis (scribes — the Torah experts), and prōtoi tou laou (the principal men of the people — the lay aristocracy) form the Jerusalem establishment.
19.48: kai ouch heuriskon to ti poiēsōsin, ho laos gar hapas exekremato autou akouōn — and they could not find what they might do, for all the people were hanging on him listening — exekremato autou akouōn (were hanging on him listening — exkremamai: to hang on, be suspended from; perfect picture of rapt attention; the crowd is figuratively "hung" on Jesus's words, unable to be detached from him). This crowd-protection prevents immediate arrest; the authorities cannot act openly without provoking a popular uprising. The people's attentive hearing is the buffer between Jesus and his opponents through the Jerusalem teaching days.

22.37: kai gar to peri emou telos echei — for what is written about me has its fulfillment — Luke's unique passion-eve citation of Isa 53:12 (he was numbered with the transgressors) is the only place in the Synoptics where Jesus explicitly quotes a Servant Song as being about himself and being fulfilled in the impending events. Telos echei (has its end/completion) uses the teleological vocabulary of purpose reaching its goal; the Servant vocation that Jesus has lived throughout the Gospel is now reaching its culminating moment in the passion.
22.1: hē heortē tōn azymōn hē legomenē pascha — the Festival of Unleavened Bread called Passover — Luke identifies the two festivals by their relationship: heortē tōn azymōn (Feast of Unleavened Bread — azymos = unleavened, without yeast; the seven-day festival following Passover, Lev 23:6) and pascha (Passover — from Aramaic paskha, Hebrew pesaḥ = pass over, the Exodus-commemoration sacrifice). By the first century the two festivals were conflated in popular usage as "Passover week." Ēngizen (was drawing near — imperfect of engizō; Luke's approach verb) signals the imminence of the passion timeline.
22.2: ezētoun hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis to pōs anelōsin auton — the chief priests and teachers of the law were looking for some way to put him to death — zēteō (seek, look for — the ongoing search verb; imperfect indicating continuous plotting) + to pōs (how — the indirect question articular infinitive = "the how of killing him") + anelōsin (destroy, put to death — aorist subjunctive of anaireō = take up/away; the killing verb used throughout Acts for executions). Ephobounto gar ton laon — for they feared the people — phobeomai (fear) + ton laon (the people — laos = the covenant people; the crowd's protection of Jesus is the political constraint on the authorities).
22.3: eisēlthen de Satanas eis Ioudan ton kaloumenon Iskariōtēn — Satan entered into Judas called Iscariot — Luke's explicit statement that Satan entered Judas is unique among the Synoptics (only John 13:27 has a parallel, during the Last Supper); Luke places it before the conspiracy. Eiserchomai eis (enter into) is the same construction used for demonic possession throughout Luke (8:30, 33; 11:26); the use for Satan's entry into Judas places the betrayal in the category of demonic agency. Iskariōtēs (Iscariot — possibly from Hebrew ʾîš qerîyôt = man of Kerioth, a town in Judea; alternatively from Aramaic šeqarya = falseness). Ontos ek tou arithmou tōn dōdeka — being from the number of the Twelve — the insider status makes the betrayal worse.
22.4: apelthōn synelalésen tois archiereusin kai stratēgois — going away, he conferred with the chief priests and officers — synlaleō (speak with, confer with — from syn- + laleō = talk together; used at 9:30 for Moses and Elijah speaking with Jesus at the Transfiguration; now used for Judas's treacherous conference). Stratēgois (officers — from stratēgos = military commander, general; here the officers of the temple guard, the temple police; also Acts 4:1; 5:24-26) are the temple security force under the chief priests. The collusion includes both religious leadership and their enforcement arm.
22.5: echarēsan kai synethento autō argyrion dounai — they were delighted and agreed to give him silver — chairō (rejoice, be delighted — the joy verb; the authorities' joy contrasts sharply with heaven's joy over one repenting sinner in ch 15) in the aorist: the moment of relief at finding a solution. Syntithēmi (agree, covenant together — from syn- + tithēmi = put together; to make a joint arrangement) + argyrion dounai (to give silver — argyrion = silver coin, money; the betrayal price is not specified here, unlike Matt 27:3's thirty pieces of silver).
22.6: kai exōmologēsen kai ezētei eukairian tou paradounai auton — he consented and was looking for an opportunity to betray him — exomologeō (consent, agree — from ex- + omologeō = confess, acknowledge; here in the sense of agreeing/committing to the arrangement). Ezētei eukairian (was looking for a favorable moment — imperfect, ongoing search; eukairos = good-time, favorable moment = the window of opportunity). Ater ochlou (without a crowd — ater is a rare preposition meaning "without, apart from"; the crowd's presence throughout the Jerusalem days protects Jesus from arrest; Judas's task is finding the uncrowded moment).
22.7: ēlthen de hē hēmera tōn azymōn, hē edei thyesthai to pascha — the day of Unleavened Bread came, on which the Passover lamb had to be sacrificed — edei thyesthai (it was necessary to sacrifice — the divine necessity dei governing the Passover sacrifice; the same necessity-verb that governs the passion, 9:22; 24:7). Thyō (sacrifice, slaughter — the technical sacrificial term; the Passover lamb is to pascha, the sacrifice itself). The necessity of the Passover sacrifice is placed in the same divine-necessity frame as Jesus's coming death, creating the theological resonance that Luke develops through the passion narrative.
22.8: apesteilen Petron kai Iōannēn — he sent Peter and John — the leadership pair of the early church (Acts 3:1; 4:13; 8:14) are commissioned together for the Passover preparation; this is Luke-distinctive (Mark and Matthew say "two disciples"). Poreutheutes hetoimasate hēmin to pascha hina phagōmen — go and prepare the Passover for us so that we may eat — hetoimazō (prepare — the Passover-preparation verb; the disciples fulfill the same role as the disciples sent ahead for the triumphal entry, 19:29-30); the purpose clause hina phagōmen (so that we may eat) sets the goal of the entire preparation activity.
22.9: hoi de eipan autō, pou theleis hetoimasōmen — they said to him, where do you want us to prepare — the disciples' question is practical and presupposes Jesus's knowledge of a prepared location. The verb theleis (you want/will — from thelō) places the choice entirely with Jesus; the disciples are implementing his intention, not making independent arrangements. The implied background is that Jesus has already arranged the upper room in advance, and the sign (man with water jar) is a prearranged signal.
22.10: anthrōpos keramion hydatos bastazōn apantēsei hymin — a man carrying a jar of water will meet you — keramion (clay jar — from keramos = clay, pottery) + hydatos (of water — genitive) + bastazōn (carrying — the carrying verb). In antiquity, carrying water was typically women's work; a man carrying a water jar was unusual and therefore a recognizable identifying sign. Akolouthēsate autō eis tēn oikian — follow him into the house — the sequence (follow → enter → ask the owner) describes a prearranged contact system, suggesting Jesus made arrangements in advance through an unnamed disciple.
22.11: kai ereit ho oikodespotē tēs oikias — and say to the owner of the house — oikodespotēs (owner/master of the house — from oikos + despotēs = house-master; also used in the parables at 12:39; 13:25; 14:21; 22:11). Ho didaskalos legei soi, pou estin to katalyma — the Teacher says to you, where is the guest room — didaskalos (Teacher) is the title the disciples use for Jesus in formal address (7:40; 9:38; 10:25; 11:45; 12:13; 18:18; 19:39; 21:7; 22:11). Katalyma (guest room — from katalyō = loose/unyoke, the stopping place where animals are unharnessed; also used at 2:7 for the "inn" where there was no room).
22.12: anōgeon mega estrōmenon — a large upper room furnished — anōgeon (upper room — from anō = above + gē = earth; an upper-story room; only here and Mark 14:15 in the NT; possibly the same room as the hyperōon = upper room of Acts 1:13 where the disciples were gathered). Mega (large) + estrōmenon (furnished, spread out — perfect passive participle of strōnnymi = spread, furnish; the room is already equipped with couches and table-covering). The room is ready; the disciples need only supply the Passover elements.
22.13: apelthontes de heuron kathōs eirēken autois — going away, they found things just as he had told them — the fulfillment formula (kathōs eirēken = just as he had said) is Luke's pattern for confirming Jesus's prophetic foreknowledge (cf. 19:32 for the colt; 24:24 for the women's report). The disciples' finding confirms that the prediction was accurate in every detail; the narrative invites the reader to trust Jesus's subsequent predictions (Peter's denial, the passion, the resurrection) with the same confidence. Kai hētoimasan to pascha — and they prepared the Passover — the preparation was completed without incident.
22.14: kai hote egeneto hē hōra — and when the hour came — hōra (hour) in the Johannine theological sense appears in Luke at the passion narrative: the "hour" (hōra) in ancient timekeeping is the appointed moment, but theologically it carries the weight of the divinely determined moment of crisis (cf. John 2:4; 7:30; 8:20; 12:23, 27; 13:1; 17:1). Anepesen kai hoi apostoloi syn autō — he reclined and the apostles with him — anapiptō (recline at table — the formal dining posture); Luke uses apostoloi (apostles — the commissioned ones, from apostellō = send) rather than "disciples" for the Twelve at this meal, emphasizing their official status.
22.15: epithymia epethymēsa touto to pascha phagein meth hymōn pro tou me pathein — with desire I have desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer — the construction epithymia epethymēsa (desire I have desired — a figure of speech known as figura etymologica: noun + cognate verb for emphasis) is a Semitic intensification idiom (cf. Gen 2:17 LXX: thanatō apothaneisthe = you will surely die; also Luke 8:5 LXX). Epithymia (desire, longing — also used for the prodigal's longing, 15:16; Lazarus's longing, 16:21) with the force of "I have earnestly/urgently desired." Pro tou me pathein (before I suffer — paschō = suffer; the passion-verb) frames this Passover as the final one before the passion event.
22.16: ou mē phagō auto heōs hotou plērōthē en tē basileia tou theou — I will not eat it again until it finds fulfillment in the kingdom of God — the strong negation ou mē (definitely not, certainly not) + aorist subjunctive phagō (eat) is the Greek emphatic negative. Plērōthē (be fulfilled — aorist passive subjunctive of plēroō; fulfilled/completed) + en tē basileia tou theou (in the kingdom of God): the Passover meal will have its ultimate fulfillment in the eschatological kingdom-feast. The implication is that Jesus will next eat the Passover in its fulfilled form — the messianic banquet (Isa 25:6; Luke 13:29; 14:15-24; Rev 19:9).
22.17: dexamenos potērion eucharistēsas eipen — taking a cup, giving thanks he said — the cup-with-thanksgiving before the bread is unusual; if this is the order recorded by Luke, it may be the cup of the Passover Seder (the first or second cup) before the eucharistic cup (v.20), or Luke may present a compressed version. Eucharistēsas (having given thanks — aorist participle of eucharistō = give thanks; from eu- + charis = well-grace; the origin of "Eucharist"). Labete touto kai diameristate eis heautous — take this and divide it among yourselves — the cup is shared among all the disciples; the sharing is communal.
22.18: legō gar hymin, ou mē piō apo tou nyn apo tou genēmatos tēs ampelou — for I say to you, I will certainly not drink from now on from the fruit of the vine — the strong negation ou mē piō (I will definitely not drink) + apo tou nyn (from now on — Luke's distinctive temporal phrase; also 1:48; 5:10; 12:52; 22:18, 69; Acts 18:6). Genēmatos tēs ampelou (fruit of the vine — genēma = produce, product; ampelos = vine; the rabbinic Passover formula for wine: peri gennēmatos hagapelou) is the Passover Seder's formulaic reference to wine. Heōs hou hē basileia tou theou elthē — until the kingdom of God comes — the eschatological horizon governs all Jesus's final-meal statements.
22.19: labōn arton eucharistēsas eklasen kai edōken autois legōn — taking bread, giving thanks, he broke it and gave it to them saying — the fourfold eucharistic action: labōn (taking), eucharistēsas (giving thanks), eklasen (broke — aorist of klaō; the breaking is the distribution-preparation), edōken (gave). The fourfold sequence is identical at 9:16 (feeding of five thousand) and 24:30 (Emmaus meal); Luke frames every meal of Jesus in this pattern. touto estin to sōma mou to hyper hymōn didomenon — this is my body given for you — to sōma mou (my body — sōma = body, the whole person in bodily existence); eis tēn emēn anamnēsin — in remembrance of me — anamnēsis (remembrance, memorial re-presentation — from ana- + mimnēskō = remember again; in the LXX cultic usage, anamnēsis is the memorial sacrifice that re-presents before God the original redemptive event; Exod 12:14; Lev 24:7; Num 10:10; not mere mental recollection but a liturgical re-enactment that participates in the original event).
22.20: to potērion hōsautōs meta to deipnēsai, legōn, touto to potērion hē kainē diathēkē en tō aimati mou — the cup likewise after supper, saying: this cup is the new covenant in my blood — Textual note: This verse is absent from some Western manuscripts (notably Codex Bezae/D), leading to debate between the "shorter text" (vv.17-18 only, without a second cup) and the "longer text" (vv.17-20, with two cups). The majority of manuscripts and the Nestle-Aland text include vv.19b-20. Kainē diathēkē (new covenant — kainē = new in quality/character, not merely new in time; diathēkē = covenant, but also the Greek word for "testament/will/disposition"; the LXX uses diathēkē for Hebrew berît = covenant) — Jer 31:31-34 is the background for the new covenant promise; Jesus identifies his blood as the covenant-ratifying sacrifice (Exod 24:8: "the blood of the covenant"). Ekchunnomenon hyper hymōn (poured out for you — present participle indicating the self-giving is already in motion).
22.21: plēn idou hē cheir tou paradidrontos me met emou epi tēs trapezēs — but behold, the hand of the one betraying me is with mine on the table — plēn (but, however, nevertheless — Luke's adversative conjunction; a term of contrast) + idou (behold — the attention-focusing particle). Ho paradidous me (the one betraying me — present participle of paradidōmi = hand over, betray; the passion-verb that runs through all four Gospels for the betrayal). The hand (cheir) on the table during the meal is the intimacy betrayal: table fellowship creates loyalty obligations in Mediterranean culture; the betrayer violates the most sacred form of social trust.
22.22: hoti ho huios tou anthrōpou kata to hōrismenon poreuetai — for the Son of Man goes as it has been determined — hōrismenon (determined, decreed — perfect passive participle of horizō = set a boundary, determine, designate; from horos = boundary; the word from which "horizon"; also Acts 2:23: tē hōrismenē boulē = by the determined will; Acts 10:42; 17:26, 31). The divine determination is the theological frame for the passion: what is happening is not an accident or a triumph of evil but the execution of the divine plan. plēn ouai tō anthrōpō ekeinō di hou paradidotai — but woe to that man through whom he is betrayed — moral responsibility coexists with divine determination; the betrayer is not excused by the divine plan.
22.23: kai autoi ērxanto suzētein pros heautous to tis ara eiē ex autōn ho touto mellōn prassein — they began to question among themselves which of them it might be who would do this — suzēteō (investigate, question among themselves — from sun- + zēteō; to search/investigate together). The optative tis ara eiē (who might it be — present optative in indirect question) reflects the uncertainty and deliberative mood. Ara (then, indeed, in that case — a particle of inference); the question is not rhetorical but genuinely investigative: the disciples do not know who the betrayer is. Luke's placement of the betrayal announcement at the table (unlike Mark/Matthew where it precedes the words of institution) creates greater dramatic tension.
22.24: egeneto de kai philoneikia en autois — a dispute also arose among them — philoneikia (love of strife, quarreling spirit, competitive disputing — from phileō = love + neikos = strife/quarrel; the love-of-quarreling attitude; hapax legomenon in the NT, occurring only here) is Luke's unique word for the greatness-dispute that Matthew and Mark place earlier (Matt 18:1; Mark 9:34). Luke's placement of this dispute at the Last Supper — immediately following the words of institution — is the sharpest possible irony: the disciples quarrel about greatness at the moment Jesus institutes the memorial of his self-giving death.
22.25: hoi basileis tōn ethnōn kyrieuousin autōn kai hoi exousiazōntes autōn euergetai kalountai — the kings of the Gentiles lord it over them and those who exercise authority are called Benefactors — kyrieuō (lord it over, dominate — from kyrios = lord; to exercise lordship over; used negatively for domination, positively for Christ's lordship in Rom 6:9; 14:9) + exousiazō (exercise authority — from exousia = authority). Euergetai (Benefactors — from eu- + ergon = well-doer; the Hellenistic royal title Euergetes was claimed by Ptolemy III, Ptolemy VIII, Antiochus VII, and others; rulers who extracted wealth from subjects while calling themselves benefactors; Jesus names the hypocrisy of the title).
22.26: hymeis de ouch houtōs — but you are not to be like that — the adversative de + ouch houtōs (not in this way) establishes the kingdom alternative: inversion of the Gentile power model. All ho meizōn en hymin ginesthō hōs ho neōteros — instead, the greatest among you must become like the youngest — ho neōteros (the younger/newest — comparative of neos; in Mediterranean society the youngest had the lowest status and served the older); ho hēgoumenos (the one who leads — the leadership word, from hēgeomai = lead, consider; also used for community leaders in Heb 13:7, 17, 24) contrasted with ho diakonōn (the one who serves — the servant/deacon word).
22.27: tis gar meizōn, ho anakeimenos ē ho diakonōn — for who is greater, the one who reclines or the one who serves — the rhetorical question assumes the obvious answer: the table-guest who reclines (anakeimenos = reclining at table, the honored dining position) is greater than the servant (diakonōn = one who serves/ministers). egō de en mesō hymōn eimi hōs ho diakonōn — but I am among you as the one who serves — Jesus places himself in the category of the servant (ho diakonōn), not the master. This saying interprets the footwashing (John 13) that Luke does not record; the verbal claim and the physical act are two expressions of the same truth. The diakoneō word-group (service/ministry) becomes the defining term for leadership in the early church (Acts 6:1-4; 1 Tim 3:8-13).
22.28: hymeis de este hoi diamenēkotes met emou en tois peirasmois mou — you are the ones who have remained with me in my trials — diamenō (remain through, continue with — intensive compound of dia- + menō = remain through; persistent staying; also Gal 2:5 for maintaining the truth) in the perfect participle diamenēkotes (have remained, have stayed alongside): the disciples' loyal presence through the trials (peirasmois = trials, testing — the same word as "temptation" in 4:13 and the Lord's Prayer; here the trials of Jesus's ministry under opposition) is acknowledged before the betrayal and denial are announced.
22.29: kagō diatithmai hymin kathōs dietheto moi ho patēr mou basileian — and I appoint/covenant to you, as my Father appointed to me, a kingdom — diatithēmi (appoint, covenant — from dia- + tithēmi = set/place through; the covenant-making verb; the diathēkē = covenant is the noun form; here Jesus makes a covenant-appointment with the disciples). The Father has made a covenant-appointment of a kingdom to Jesus; Jesus now makes a covenant-appointment of the same kingdom to the disciples. The chain of covenanted kingdom-authority runs: Father → Son → Twelve. Basileian (a kingdom — the object of the covenant-appointment) is what is being transmitted.
22.30: hina esthiēte kai pinēte epi tēs trapezēs mou en tē basileia mou kai kathēsesthe epi thronōn — so that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom and sit on thrones — the kingdom-appointment includes both table-fellowship (esthiēte kai pinēte = eat and drink) and judicial authority (kathēsesthe epi thronōn = sit on thrones — the same authority-symbol as Matt 19:28). Krinontes tas dōdeka phulas tou Israēl — judging the twelve tribes of Israel — krinō (judge — the judicial authority verb); the twelve apostles' eschatological authority over the twelve tribes is both a governance and a prophetic witness role; the promise anticipates the reconstitution of Israel under the messianic king.
22.31: Simōn Simōn, idou ho Satanas exēitēsato hymas tou sinisai hōs ton siton — Simon, Simon, behold Satan has demanded you to sift you like wheat — the doubled name (Simōn Simōn) signals urgency and solemnity (cf. Abraham, Abraham — Gen 22:11; Moses, Moses — Exod 3:4; Martha, Martha — Luke 10:41; Saul, Saul — Acts 9:4). Exaiteō (demand, ask for from — from ex- + aiteō = ask out from; to request/demand from someone for a specific purpose; only here in the NT) — Satan has formally requested (from God) permission to test the disciples, as at Job 1:6-12. Siniasai hōs ton siton (sift like wheat — siniazo = to pass through a sieve; the wheat is tossed in the sieve to separate grain from chaff; the disciples are the wheat; the sifting will test their quality). Hymas (plural: all of you — the whole group is under threat).
22.32: egō de edeēthēn peri sou hina mē eklipē hē pistis sou — but I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail — edeēthēn (I prayed, I besought — aorist of deomai = ask, beseech; the earnest-petition verb; the singular sou [you/your] shifts from the plural of v.31 to the singular: Jesus prays specifically for Simon). Hina mē eklipē hē pistis sou — that your faith may not fail — ekleipō (fail, give out, cease — also used for the sun failing at 23:45; "eclipse" is from this root). kai su pote epistrepsas stērison tous adelphous sou — and when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers — epistrepho (turn back, return — the conversion/repentance verb; also 17:4 for repeated turning; Acts 3:19; 26:20; the word for conversion throughout Luke-Acts). Stērizō (strengthen, establish — the same root as the fixed chasm at 16:26; here the positive use = stabilizing the community).
22.33: ho de eipen autō, Kyrie, meta sou hetoimos eimi kai eis phylakēn kai eis thanaton poreuesthai — he said to him, Lord, I am ready to go with you both to prison and to death — hetoimos eimi (I am ready — hetoimos = prepared, equipped for; the readiness claim is sincere but will be tested). Eis phylakēn kai eis thanaton (to prison and to death — the extreme commitment claim covers both incarceration and execution). Peter's readiness-claim is the setup for Jesus's denial-prediction; the more emphatic the claim, the sharper the irony of the triple denial.
22.34: ho de eipen, Legō soi, Petros, ou phōnēsei sēmeron alektōr — he said, I tell you, Peter, the rooster will not crow today — Petros (Peter — the Aramaic Kēphas/Rock name; Jesus addresses him by name, not "Simon" as in v.31) in the prediction. Heōs tris aparnēsē me eidenai — before you have denied three times that you know me — aparneomai (deny, disown — intensive compound of apo- + arneomai = deny completely away; the total-denial word; the same verb as 9:23 for "deny oneself" and the eschatological denial of 12:9). Eidenai (to know — perfect infinitive of oida; "to know" as relationship-knowledge, not merely information). The triple denial structure (tris) mirrors Peter's threefold denial in vv.56-60.
22.35: hote apesteila hymas ater ballontiou kai pēras kai hypodēmatōn, mē tinos hysterēsate — when I sent you without purse, bag, or sandals, did you lack anything — the question references the original commissioning (9:3; 10:4) when Jesus sent the disciples without supplies and they were provided for through hospitality. Hystereomai (lack, fall short — from hystereō = be behind, fall short; the "shortage" verb; also 15:14 for the prodigal falling into need) in the aorist: at any time during the mission, did you lack something? The implied answer is no — the provision model worked.
22.36: alla nyn ho echōn ballantion aratō, homoiōs kai pēran — but now the one who has a purse, let him take it, and likewise a bag — the reversal from the mission-without-provision (v.35) to the provision-required stance is dramatic. Kai ho mē echōn, pōlēsatō to himation autou kai agorasatō machairan — and the one who does not have, let him sell his cloak and buy a sword — machaira (sword — a short sword, fighting knife, or dagger; distinct from rhomphaia = the large broadsword). The sword-instruction has been interpreted: (1) literally (Jesus permits defensive weapons); (2) figuratively (symbolic readiness for conflict); (3) ironically (provoking the "two swords are enough" response that he dismisses). Context (the Isa 53:12 fulfillment in v.37) suggests the instruction is about fulfilling the "with transgressors" prophecy — appearing armed makes Jesus and the disciples look like rebels.
22.38: hoi de eipan, Kyrie, idou machaira hōde duo — they said, Lord, see here are two swords — the disciples produce two swords, showing they already had weapons. ho de eipen autois, hikanon estin — he said to them, it is enough — hikanon (enough, sufficient — from hikanós = sufficient, able; also 7:6 for "I am not worthy"; 22:38). Jesus's response hikanon estin is deliberately ambiguous: either "two swords suffice for the literal purpose" (but two swords are obviously insufficient for a military force) or "enough of this topic" (cutting off the conversation). The second reading fits better: Jesus is ending the sword-discussion, not approving a two-sword militia. The Gethsemane incident (v.51) where Jesus stops the sword-use confirms this reading.
22.39: kai exelthōn eporeuthē kata to ethos eis to oros tōn elaiōn — going out, he went according to his custom to the Mount of Olives — kata to ethos (according to the custom — ethos = custom, habit; Luke's note that this was habitual: Jesus regularly went to the Mount of Olives during his Jerusalem days; cf. 21:37: "each day he taught in the temple and each evening went out to the Mount of Olives"). The Mount of Olives is east of Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley; the Gethsemane garden is on its western slope. Ēkolouthēsan de autō kai hoi mathētai — his disciples followed him — the disciples follow as always; they do not know yet what this particular evening holds.
22.40: genomenos de epi tou topou eipen autois, proseuchesthe mē eiselthein eis peirasmon — arriving at the place, he said to them: pray that you will not enter into temptation — genomenos de epi tou topou (arriving at the place — ginomai epi = come to, arrive at; the place is identified as Gethsemane in Mark 14:32 and Matt 26:36) — Luke simply says "the place" (ton topon), suggesting a known location. Proseuchesthe mē eiselthein eis peirasmon — pray that you will not enter into temptation — the prayer-against-temptation command frames both the beginning (v.40) and the end (v.46) of the Gethsemane scene in Luke, creating an inclusio; the disciples' failure to pray explains their failure to stand in the trial.
22.41: kai autos apespasthē ap autōn hōsei lithou bolēn — he withdrew from them about a stone's throw — apespaō (draw away, separate — from apo- + spaō = draw/pull; to withdraw deliberately; also Acts 20:30; 21:1 for the pull of departure) + hōsei lithou bolēn (about a stone's throw — a distance measurement; bolē = throw; approximately 30-50 meters). theis ta gonata prosēucheto — kneeling, he prayed — theis ta gonata (having placed the knees = kneeling — gony = knee; the kneeling posture is Luke's unique Gethsemane detail; Mark and Matthew say "falling on his face"; kneeling is the prayer posture throughout Luke-Acts: Acts 7:60; 9:40; 20:36; 21:5).
22.42: Pater, ei boulei, parenengke touto to potērion ap emou — Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me — boulei (you are willing — present indicative of boulomai = will, desire; the will-of-God verb; Luke uses boulei rather than Matthew's dynaton estin = if it is possible; Luke's conditional is about divine willingness, Matthew's about divine possibility — subtly different theological framings). Potērion (cup — the cup of divine wrath and suffering; Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17, 22; Jer 25:15-17; the metaphor runs through the passion narrative). plēn mē to thelēma mou alla to son ginesthō — yet not my will, but yours be done — thelēma (will — from thelō) in the contrast: Jesus's thelēma (will, desire) vs the Father's thelēma. The prayer is the Son's full submission of his own will to the Father's will — the inverse of Adam's assertion of human will against God.
22.43: ōphthē de autō angelos ap ouranou enischyōn auton — an angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him — vv.43-44 are textually uncertain; they are absent from important manuscripts (p75, Codex Alexandrinus B, Codex Vaticanus, some Syriac versions) and present in others (Codex Sinaiticus, the Byzantine tradition). The UBS/NA text brackets them as secondary. If original: ōphthē (appeared — aorist passive of horaō; the appearance verb for angelic and divine manifestations throughout Luke-Acts; 1:11; 9:31; Acts 2:3; 7:26, 30, 35). Enischyōn (strengthening — present participle of enischuō = give strength in/through; from en- + ischuō = strengthen within; only here and Acts 9:19 in the NT). The angel's strengthening echoes the angels' service after the wilderness temptation (Matt 4:11; Mark 1:13).
22.44: kai genomenos en agōnia ekteneteron prosēucheto — being in agony he prayed more earnestly — also textually disputed (see v.43). Agōnia (agony, intense struggle — from agōn = athletic contest, struggle; the word for the intense physical/mental strain of an athletic agonist; only here in the NT) describes the psychological-physical intensity of the prayer. Ekteneteron (more earnestly — comparative adverb of ektenōs = earnestly, with outstretched effort; from ekteinō = stretch out). egeneto de ho hidrōs autou hōsei thromboi haimatos katabainontes epi tēn gēn — his sweat became like drops/clots of blood falling to the ground — thromboi haimatos (drops/clots of blood — thrombos = clot, lump; in medical writers the clotted form of blood; Luke the physician's choice of the precise medical term for blood that has thickened; the sweat was not blood but resembled blood-clots in its appearance).
22.45: kai anastas apo tēs proseuchēs, elthōn pros tous mathētas, heuren koimōmenous autous apo tēs lypēs — rising from prayer and coming to the disciples, he found them sleeping from grief — anastas (rising — the resurrection verb in its mundane use) + apo tēs proseuchēs (from the prayer — a notable prepositional phrase: Jesus rises from the prayer encounter as from a meeting). Apo tēs lypēs (from grief — lypē = grief, sorrow; Luke's unique explanation for the disciples' sleep; Matthew says simply they were sleeping; Luke adds the cause: the disciples are not lazy but overwhelmed by sorrow). The disciples feel the weight of the approaching passion even if they do not understand it.
22.46: ti katheudete, anastatontes proseuchesthe, hina mē eiselthēte eis peirasmon — why are you sleeping? Rise and pray that you may not enter into temptation — the inclusio-closing command (matching v.40) frames the entire Gethsemane scene between two identical prayer-against-temptation commands. The disciples have failed to follow the instruction of v.40; their failure to pray will result in their failure to stand when tested: Peter will deny (vv.54-62) and the others will scatter (implied). The prayer against temptation (hina mē eiselthēte eis peirasmon) is the practical means of perseverance that the disciples have neglected.
22.47: eti autou lalountos idou ochlos — while he was still speaking, behold a crowd — eti autou lalountos (while he was still speaking — genitive absolute construction: the ongoing speech is interrupted) + idou (behold — the attention-particle signaling a sudden arrival). Ho legomenos Ioudas heis tōn dōdeka proeporeueto autōn — the one called Judas, one of the Twelve, was leading them — proporeuomai (lead ahead of, go before — compound of pro- + poreuomai; Judas is at the front of the arresting party; he knows the location from his own time with Jesus there). Ēngisen tō Iēsou philēsai auton — he drew near to Jesus to kiss him — philesai (to kiss — infinitive of phileō = love, kiss; the friendship-kiss of greeting, not agapaō).
22.48: Ioudas, philēmati ton huion tou anthrōpou paradidōs — Judas, are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss — the rhetorical question rather than a command; Jesus identifies Judas's action without stopping it. Philēmati (with a kiss — the instrumental dative; the instrument of betrayal is the greeting that signals friendship and affection). The contrast between philēma (kiss of affection) and paradidōmi (betray, hand over) is the moral grotesque of the scene. Jesus names Judas by name and identifies the action, perhaps giving him one final moment to reconsider, but the arrest proceeds.
22.49: idontes de hoi peri auton to esomenon eipan — seeing what was going to happen, those around him said — hoi peri auton (those around him — the disciples as a group; the "around him" phrase for close associates). Kyrie, ei pataxomen en machairē — Lord, should we strike with a sword — patassō (strike, hit — the blow-word; also used for the angel striking Herod, Acts 12:23) + en machairē (with a sword — the short sword from v.36-38). The disciples ask permission before acting; the question implies they are not sure whether to use the weapons. Luke's version makes the disciples' action a question; the blow (v.50) happens immediately after, perhaps without waiting for an answer.
22.50: kai epataxen heis tis ex autōn tou archierēos ton doulon kai apheilen to ous autou to dexion — one of them struck the servant of the high priest and cut off his right ear — heis tis ex autōn (one of them — unnamed in Luke; John 18:10 names him as Peter) + ton doulon (the servant — doulos = slave, servant; John 18:10 names him Malchus) + to ous to dexion (the right ear — the specificity of "right" is preserved across all four Gospels). The detail of the right ear is consistent across the tradition; in ancient combat, reaching the opponent's right ear requires swinging a right-handed stroke that the opponent has partially blocked (reducing it from a lethal head blow to an ear).
22.51: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen, Eate heōs toutou — Jesus replied: Stop, no more of this — eate heōs toutou (let be as far as this — eaō = let, allow; heōs toutou = as far as this/up to this point; the command to stop). kai hapsamenos tou ōtiou iāsato auton — and touching the ear, he healed him — hapsamenos (touching — aorist participle of haptō = touch; the healing-touch verb throughout Luke: 5:13; 6:19; 7:14; 8:44-47). Iāsato (healed — aorist of iaomai = heal thoroughly) + auton (him = the high priest's servant). This detail is unique to Luke; only Luke records that Jesus healed the wounded ear of his own arrestor at the moment of arrest — the final miracle of Jesus's ministry is an act of mercy on an enemy.
22.52: eipen de Iēsous pros tous paragenomenous ep auton archiereis kai stratēgous tou hierou kai presbyterous — Jesus said to the chief priests, officers of the temple, and elders who had come for him — the three-element arrest party (chief priests + temple officers + elders) represents the full Sanhedrin structure: the priestly aristocracy, the temple police, and the lay elders. Hōs epi lēstēn exēlthate meta machairōn kai xylōn — as against a robber you came out with swords and clubs — lēstēs (robber, brigand — from lēizomai = plunder; the term for an armed criminal; also Barabbas is a lēstēs at 23:19; the same word Jesus used in 10:30 and 19:46). The comparison to a robbery-arrest is an ironic protest: Jesus has been teaching openly in the temple daily; the nighttime armed arrest treats him as a dangerous criminal.
22.53: kath hēmeran ontos mou meth hymōn en tō hierō ouk exeteinate tas cheiras ep eme — every day I was with you in the temple and you did not stretch out your hands against me — kath hēmeran (daily, every day — Luke's characteristic temporal phrase, also 9:23; 11:3; 16:19; 19:47) + ontos mou meth hymōn (while I was with you — genitive absolute) + en tō hierō (in the temple — the public space where arrest was impossible due to the crowd). all autē estin hymōn hē hōra kai hē exousia tou skotous — but this is your hour and the power of darkness — hē exousia tou skotous (the authority of darkness — exousia = authority, power; skotos = darkness; the "authority of darkness" is a uniquely Lukan phrase, naming the cosmic dimension of the arrest; also Col 1:13 which contrasts it with the kingdom of the Son).
22.54: syllabanōtes de auton ēgagon kai eisēgagon eis tēn oikian tou archierōs — seizing him, they led him away and brought him into the house of the high priest — syllambanō (seize, arrest — the arrest verb; also 1:24, 31, 36 for the supernatural conceptions; here the physical arrest) + eisagō (bring into — the compound entry verb). The high priest's house (oikia tou archiereōs) is the location for the preliminary examination before formal Sanhedrin proceedings. Ho de Petros ēkolouthei makrothen — Peter followed at a distance — makrothen (from a distance — the spatial adverb of safe following distance); the courageous follower who swore to go to prison and death (v.33) now follows at a safe remove.
22.55: periaapsantōn de pyr en mesō tēs aulēs kai synkathisantōn hēlthen ho Petros eis to meson autōn — kindling a fire in the middle of the courtyard and sitting down together, Peter sat down in the middle of them — periapsantōn pyr (kindling a fire — periaptō = kindle around, light around; from peri- + haptō; a fire lit in the center of the courtyard for warmth on a cold spring night). Peter integrates himself into the group around the fire (eis to meson autōn = into the middle of them): the strategy of camouflage, of blending in with the crowd near the fire that will lead to his identification and denial.
22.56: idousa de auton paidiskē tis kathēmenon pros to phōs kai atenisasa autō eipen — a servant girl saw him sitting at the firelight and staring at him she said — paidiskē (servant girl — diminutive of pais = child, servant; a young female slave) + atenisasa (staring, gazing intently — aorist participle of atenizō = gaze fixedly; from atenēs = stretched, strained; Luke's characteristic intensive-gaze verb: 4:20; Acts 1:10; 3:4, 12; 6:15; 7:55; 10:4; 11:6; 13:9; 14:9; 23:1). The firelight (pros to phōs) is what enables the servant girl to see Peter's face clearly — the light that betrays him.
22.57: ho de ērnēsato auton legōn, Ouk oida auton, gynai — he denied him, saying, Woman, I do not know him — arneomai (deny, disown — the simple form; the intensive aparneomai was used in Jesus's prediction at v.34; here the simpler form but the same act). Ouk oida auton (I do not know him — oida = know, be acquainted with; relationship-knowledge, not mere information) directly contradicts the relational knowledge that discipleship is. The address gynai (woman — the formal/neutral address, not rude but also not warm; cf. John 2:4; 4:21; 19:26; 20:13) to the servant girl is the first step of Peter's withdrawal from relationship.
22.58: kai meta brachy heteros idōn auton ephē, kai sy ex autōn ei — a little later another one saw him and said, you also are one of them — meta brachy (a little later — brachus = short; the brief interval allows Peter to think the danger has passed). Kai sy ex autōn ei (you also are one of them — the "also" = kai implicates Peter in the group of Jesus's followers). Ho de Petros ephē, Anthrōpe, ouk eimi — Peter said, Man, I am not — anthrōpe (man — the address to a male addressee, neutral but slightly defensive); the denial is flat and simple: ouk eimi (I am not) — the direct contradiction of the truth, and an ironic contrast to Jesus's "I am" (egō eimi) before the Sanhedrin (v.70).
22.59: kai diastasēs hōsei hōras mias allos tis diischurizeto legōn, Ep alētheias kai houtos met autou ēn — about an hour later someone else insisted emphatically, truly this man also was with him — diastasēs hōras mias (about an hour having elapsed — diistēmi = stand apart, elapse; the time-interval verb). Diischyrizomai (insist, maintain strongly — from dia- + ischyrizō = strengthen through; to insist with force; also Acts 12:15) — the third accuser is emphatic, not tentative. Ep alētheias (truly, in truth — the truth-assertion formula) + Galillaios gar estin (for he is a Galilean — the dialect identification; Galilean Greek had a distinctive accent that identified speakers to Judeans).
22.60: eipen ho Petros, Anthrōpe, ouk oida ho legeis — Peter said, Man, I do not know what you are saying — the third denial escalates to total ignorance: not just "I don't know him" (v.57) or "I am not one of them" (v.58), but "I don't even understand what you're talking about." Kai parachrēma eti lalountos autou ephōnēsen ho alektōr — and immediately while he was still speaking the rooster crowed — parachrēma (immediately — Luke's characteristic instantaneous-fulfillment adverb; also 1:64; 4:39; 5:25; 8:44, 47; 13:13; 18:43; 19:11; Acts 3:7; 5:10; 12:23; 13:11; 16:26, 33). The rooster's crow (ephōnēsen ho alektōr) arrives at the moment of the third denial, fulfilling Jesus's prediction exactly.
22.61: kai strapheis ho kyrios eneblepsēn tō Petrō — and the Lord turned and looked at Peter — strapheis ho kyrios (the Lord turned — aorist passive of strephō; "the Lord" is Luke's post-resurrection title used here proleptically and throughout the passion narrative) is unique to Luke: no other Gospel records Jesus turning to look at Peter during the denial. Eneblepsen (looked at, gazed at — aorist of emblepō = look into/at; to look directly, intently at someone). The wordless gaze of the Lord on Peter at the moment of the crowing rooster is the narrative heart of the denial account: no rebuke, no words — only the silent look that recalls the prediction and breaks Peter's heart.
22.62: kai exelthōn exō eklausen pikrōs — going outside he wept bitterly — exelthōn exō (going outside — the redundant directional compounds: going-out outside, the emphatic exit from the scene of his shame). Eklausen (wept — aorist of klaiō = weep, cry; not just tears in the eyes but active weeping) + pikrōs (bitterly — from pikros = bitter; the sharp, cutting quality of bitter weeping, grief that burns). Peter's bitter weeping is the authentic response to the recognition of his failure; it is the beginning of the turning (epistrepsas) that Jesus predicted in v.32.
22.63: kai hoi andres hoi synechontes auton enepaizon autō dernontes — the men who held him began mocking and beating him — synechontes (holding, guarding — from sunechō = hold together, restrain; the temple-guard holding him in custody) + empaizoō (mock, ridicule — from en- + paizō = play at someone's expense; the mockery verb; also 14:29; 18:32; 23:11, 36) + derontes (beating — from derō = flay, beat; used in 12:47-48 for the servant beaten with blows; a physical striking). Luke's night-session mockery by the guards contrasts with the morning session before the Sanhedrin (v.66-71); the mockery is by the soldiers while the official proceedings are by the council.
22.64: kai perikalypsantes auton epērōtōn legontes, Prophēteuson, tis estin ho paisas se — blindfolding him they questioned him saying, Prophesy! Who hit you — perikalyptō (blindfold, cover around — from peri- + kalyptō = cover completely around; to blindfold by covering the face). The mockery-game is ironic: they ask the Prophet to prophesy who struck him while blindfolded. The irony is that he has already prophesied accurately (Peter's denial, Judas's betrayal, the passion) and will prophesy accurately (the temple's destruction, the Son of Man's coming). The blindfold hides his accusers from him while revealing their mockery to Luke's readers.
22.65: kai hetera polla blasphēmountes elegon eis auton — and many other things insulting him they said against him — blasphēmeō (insult, revile, blaspheme — from blaptō = harm + phēmē = word/speech; to harm with words, to speak injuriously; in NT usage, blasphemy against God is the most serious form but the word covers human insult as well). The summary hetera polla (many other things) compresses the full extent of the abuse into an open-ended notation; Luke does not specify every insult but signals that the mockery was extensive and verbal as well as physical.
22.66: kai hōs egeneto hēmera, sunēchthē to presbyterion tou laou, archiereis te kai grammateis — and when day came, the council of the elders of the people was assembled, both chief priests and scribes — hōs egeneto hēmera (when day came — Luke's daybreak marker; the night's beatings give way to the formal morning session) + presbyterion (council of elders — the Senate of the elders; presbyterion = body of elders, the presbutery; also Acts 22:5; 1 Tim 4:14 for the council of elders that laid hands on Timothy). Synēgagon auton eis to synedrion autōn — they led him into their council — synedrion (Sanhedrin — from syn- + edra = seat together; the council of 70 elders + high priest; the supreme Jewish judicial body).
22.67: ei su ei ho Christos, eipon hēmin — if you are the Christ, tell us — the Sanhedrin's question places the messianic claim as the primary issue. Iēsous de eipen autois, Ean hymin eipō, ou mē pisteusēte — Jesus said to them: If I tell you, you will not believe — ou mē pisteusēte (you will certainly not believe — double negative with aorist subjunctive; the emphatic Greek negative). Jesus's response identifies the questioners' disposition as already determined: they are not inquiring to believe but to find accusation material. The conditional (ean = if) + ou mē (certainly not) = the impossibility of genuine response from this audience.
22.68: ean de erōtēsō, ou mē apokrithēte — and if I ask you, you will not answer — the second conditional extends the no-answer dynamic in both directions: Jesus cannot get a genuine response by telling them directly, and cannot elicit genuine engagement by asking them. The symmetry of non-response (they won't believe; they won't answer) identifies the trial as performative rather than genuine inquiry; the verdict is predetermined. The statement makes the formal appearance of a trial even more hollow.
22.69: apo tou nyn de estai ho huios tou anthrōpou kathēmenos ek dexiōn tēs dynameōs tou theou — from now on the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God — apo tou nyn (from now on — Luke's distinctive temporal phrase: 1:48; 5:10; 12:52; 22:18, 69; Acts 18:6; only here at the trial among the Synoptics; Matthew and Mark lack this phrase) — the temporal marker apo tou nyn indicates that the enthronement begins from the moment of the passion/exaltation, not only at the Parousia. Kathēmenos ek dexiōn (seated at the right hand — Ps 110:1 allusion; the position of co-regent authority) + tēs dynameōs tou theou (the power of God — dynamis = power; "Power" as a divine circumlocution; "seated at the right hand of Power/God").
22.70: eipan de pantes, Su oun ei ho huios tou theou — they all said: Are you then the Son of God — the council draws the implication from Jesus's Son-of-Man-at-God's-right-hand claim: if you will sit at God's right hand, you are claiming divine Sonship. The oun (then, therefore) is inferential: the Son of Man claim implies the Son of God claim. ho de pros autous ephē, Hymeis legete hoti egō eimi — he said to them: You say that I am — hymeis legete (you say — the second-person emphasis) + hoti egō eimi (that I am — the egō eimi = "I am" formula, though here embedded in a reported speech clause; the absolute egō eimi resonates with the divine self-identification of Exod 3:14). The response neither affirms nor denies but lets their formulation stand as their own conclusion.
22.71: hoi de eipan, Ti eti echomen martyrias chreian? autoi gar ēkousamen apo tou stomatos autou — they said: What further need do we have of testimony? For we ourselves have heard from his own mouth — martyrias chreian (need of testimony — martyria = testimony, witness; chreia = need, necessity; they declare the testimony phase complete) + autoi gar ēkousamen (for we ourselves heard — the emphatic autoi = we ourselves, with no further witnesses needed). Apo tou stomatos autou (from his own mouth — the oral self-testimony that in Jewish law required no corroboration). The Sanhedrin's conclusion closes the chapter with their declaration that the trial is complete and a conviction is reached on Jesus's own words; Luke's readers know the irony that the truth spoken as self-condemnation is the very truth that justifies the condemned.

23.46: pater, eis cheiras sou paratithemi to pneuma mou — Father, into your hands I commit my spirit — Luke's uniquely reported last word from the cross quotes Ps 31:5 (the evening prayer of Jewish piety, recited before sleep as a commendation of the soul to God's keeping). The address Pater (Father) is distinctively Lukan (not in Mark's dereliction-cry; Matthew adds the Ps 22 form); even at death Jesus addresses God as Father, maintaining the intimacy of the Gethsemane prayer (22:42). The death is a trust-filled commendation rather than a defeat.
23.1: anestē hapan to plēthos -- "the whole multitude rose up" -- anestē is the same verb used for resurrection; Luke uses the unified assembly (hapan to plēthos) to signal corporate guilt and the gravity of the moment. The Sanhedrin collectively escorts Jesus to Pilate without delay: formal Jewish process has concluded.
23.2: Accusation triad before Pilate: (1) diastrephounta to ethnos hēmōn -- "subverting/misleading our nation" (diastrephō = perverting, twisting aside; used of false prophets); (2) kōluein phorous Kaisari didonai -- "forbidding giving taxes to Caesar" (a politically explosive charge, directly refuted by Luke 20:25); (3) legonta heauton Christon basilea einai -- claiming to be Christ-king. The charges are carefully framed for a Roman governor, translating religious categories into sedition.
23.3: Su ei ho basileus tōn Ioudāiōn? Pilate's central question. Jesus' response sy legeis ("you say so") is an indirect affirmation -- an idiom of qualified acknowledgment. He neither flatly denies nor loudly claims the title; the kingship he embodies exceeds Pilate's political categories. Compare John 18:36 where Jesus explains his kingdom is not from this world.
23.4: aitia -- the Greek legal term for a charge, cause of accusation, grounds for prosecution. Pilate's first innocence declaration: ouden heuriskō aition en tō anthrōpō toutō = "I find no cause/charge in this man." This begins Luke's triple innocence verdict (v4, v14, v22) plus Herod's concurrence (v15) -- a fourfold juridical clearing that makes the execution a gross miscarriage of justice by design.
23.5: epischyron -- "pressing insistently/vigorously" (only here in NT). The leaders intensify: anaseiōn ton laon = "stirring up the people" -- the same verb used of demagogues. They trace his ministry from Galilee to Jerusalem to establish ongoing agitation. Hearing "Galilee" triggers Pilate's Herod maneuver (v6).
23.6: Pilate's inquiry whether Jesus is a Galilean moves the case to Herod Antipas (tetrarch of Galilee and Perea), who happens to be in Jerusalem for the Passover. Luke's legal instinct: proper jurisdiction matters. This is the only episode in any Gospel where Jesus appears before Herod Antipas.
23.7: epempsen auton pros Hērōdēn -- sent him to Herod. Luke has carefully prepared this: 3:1 (Herod introduced as tetrarch), 9:7-9 (Herod hears of Jesus, is perplexed, wants to see him), 13:31-32 (Jesus warned "go tell that fox"). Now the meeting happens -- and it is profoundly anticlimactic, which is the point.
23.8: echarē liān -- "rejoiced greatly" (liān is an intensifier). Herod has wanted to see Jesus for a long time (ex hikanō chronōu), hoping to witness a sign (sēmeion). This mirrors the demand of the "evil generation" in 11:29 -- a sign-seeking that is spiritually bankrupt. Herod treats Jesus as entertainment rather than encountering him as Lord.
23.9: autōs de ouden apekrinato autō -- "but he answered him nothing." Jesus' silence before Herod echoes the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53:7 ("he opened not his mouth"). He performs no sign, offers no word. To those who approach with cynical curiosity rather than genuine inquiry, the Word himself is silent. The contrast with Jesus' open teaching throughout Luke is stark.
23.10: eistēkeisan de hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis -- the chief priests and scribes stand there, eutonōs katēgorountes autou = "accusing him vigorously/strenuously" (eutonōs = with full tension, at full stretch). Luke preserves the parallel with earlier Sanhedrin opposition (19:47, 20:1) -- they are relentless, while Herod is merely amused.
23.11: exouthenēsas -- "treating with contempt, making of no account" (exouthenēō = set at nothing). Herod with his soldiers mocks Jesus and puts on him esthēta lampran -- "brilliant/shining apparel," a royal robe as mockery. The irony is dense: the king of kings is robed in ironic splendor by a petty tetrarch. Luke notes this is mock-investiture; the real king passes through.
23.12: egenonto de philoi -- "they became friends" (from being enemies). Luke alone records this reconciliation between Pilate and Herod over the shared experience of Jesus. Acts 4:27-28 quotes Psalm 2 and identifies both Herod and Pilate as fulfilling the kings who "conspire together against the Lord and his anointed." Their friendship is complicity.
23.13: Pilate assembles the chief priests, rulers, and people together -- a tripartite audience for his second innocence statement. The political stage is set: Pilate publicly addresses the verdict to the full representation of Jewish leadership and populace.
23.14: anakrinas enōpion hymōn -- "having examined before you" -- anakrino is the technical term for a judicial preliminary hearing. Pilate's finding: ouden heuron en tō anthrōpō toutō aition = no cause at all. This is his second declaration. The phrase hōn katēgoreite kat' autou = "of the things of which you accuse him" shows the formal legal language.
23.15: all' oud' Hērōdēs -- "and not even Herod" found cause. Pilate appeals to Herod's concurrence: anepempsen gar auton pros hēmas = "for he sent him back to us" (implying nothing capital was found). The four-fold verdict (Pilate x3 + Herod x1) reaches its peak here: the whole Roman-Herodian judicial apparatus declares Jesus innocent.
23.16: paideusas -- from paideuō, "to educate, discipline, correct," but in a penal context = "flog/chastise." Pilate's compromise offer: a flogging as warning (paideusis) followed by release. The verb's normal meaning (educate) makes this even more ironic -- the incarnate Wisdom who teaches all is offered a "lesson" by his judge. He repeats this offer in v22.
23.18: anekragon de pamplēthei -- "they cried out all together, with the whole crowd" -- pamplēthei (only here in NT) = all as one mass, all at once. The unified demand: aire touton, apoluson de hēmin ton Barabban = "Away with this one, release Barabbas to us." The crowd has made its verdict before Pilate can finish speaking.
23.19: hostis ēn dia stasin tina genethenēn en tē polei kai phonon -- Barabbas had been thrown in prison for insurrection and murder that took place in the city. Luke's description (dia stasin... kai phonon = because of insurrection and murder) is deliberately ironic in light of the charges against Jesus in v2 -- subverting the nation, claiming kingship. The guilty man goes free; the innocent is executed.
23.20: Pilate addresses them again (palin), wishing to release Jesus -- the imperfect ēthelen shows his sustained desire. His will is thwarted by the crowd, not by any finding of guilt. Luke underscores the injustice: the governor knows the right verdict but lacks the will to enforce it.
23.21: Staurou staurou auton -- "Crucify, crucify him!" The doubled imperative marks the escalation and urgency of the crowd's demand. They do not argue the charges; they simply insist on crucifixion. The repetition enacts the relentlessness that Pilate cannot resist.
23.22: Pilate's third declaration of innocence: ouden aition thanatou heuron en autō = "I found no cause of death in him." The legal term aition thanatou = ground for capital punishment. He offers again to chastise (paideusas) and release. The threefold verdict structure (Lukan artistry) mirrors the three-fold denial of Peter (22:54-62) -- innocent repeatedly declared, yet condemned; loyal repeatedly claimed, yet denied.
23.23: epekeinto phōnais megalais -- "they pressed with loud voices" -- epikeima = lying upon, pressing down; their voices were great and insistent. Katischuon = "they prevailed, were strong enough" (katischō used of the gates of Hades not prevailing against the church in Matt 16:18). The crowd's insistence prevails over Pilate's judgment.
23.24: epekrine Pilatos genesthai to aitēma autōn -- "Pilate decided that their demand should be granted." The judicial irony: the judge rules in favor of the demand (aitēma = request/petition) rather than the evidence. Roman legal process is shown as broken by political pressure.
23.25: ton de Iēsoun paredōken tō thelēmati autōn -- "but Jesus he handed over to their will." The devastating climax of the trial narrative. Paredōken = "handed over" (paradidōmi, the word for betrayal, the word for Judas handing over Jesus). Tō thelēmati autōn = "to their will/desire" -- not to justice, not to Roman law, but to the crowd's will. Yet paradoxically this is also the divine will (22:42) accomplished through human sin.
23.26: epelambononto Simōnos tinos Kyrēnaios -- they seized Simon from Cyrene, erchomenon ap' agrou = "coming in from the countryside/fields." He was drafted, not a volunteer. The compelled cross-bearing of a bystander becomes the living illustration of 9:23 ("take up your cross daily and follow me"). Luke adds epetithēsan autō ton stauron pherein opisthen tou Iēsou -- "placed on him the cross to carry behind Jesus" -- the disciple-following posture is physically enacted by this stranger.
23.27: kopetō kai thrēnō -- two types of mourning: kopetos = beating the breast in grief (from kopto = to strike/cut), thrēnos = a dirge or lamentation chant. The women's mourning is authentic grief, unlike the mockery of the soldiers. Luke's women (also 8:2-3, 23:49, 23:55-56, 24:1-10) witness every stage of the passion, providing unbroken testimony from cross to tomb to resurrection.
23.28: Thugateres Ierousalēm -- "Daughters of Jerusalem" -- a Semitic address form (cf. Song of Solomon, Lamentations). Jesus turns to them (strapheis) to redirect their grief: weep not for me but for yourselves and your children. The coming judgment on Jerusalem (19:41-44, 21:20-24) will be so devastating that those without children will be counted blessed -- a shattering reversal of the highest Jewish blessing.
23.29: Makaria hai steirai -- "Blessed are the barren" -- stark reversal of the beatitude in 1:25 (Elizabeth's barrenness removed) and normal Jewish expectation. Kai hai koiliai hai ouk egenнēsan = "and the wombs that never bore." When the siege comes, those with children will have more to lose, more to suffer. This is not praise of childlessness but a warning of the extremity coming upon Jerusalem.
23.30: Hosea 10:8 ("they will say to the mountains, 'Cover us,' and to the hills, 'Fall on us'") -- an appeal for the mercy of sudden death over prolonged suffering. The same verse appears in Revelation 6:16 in the eschatological judgment. Luke's Jesus applies the prophetic tradition to the coming Roman siege (70 CE).
23.31: hoti ei en tō hygrō xylō tauta poiousin, en tō xērō ti genētai? -- "For if they do these things in/to the green wood, what will happen in the dry?" Proverb form. Hygrōs = moist/green/living wood; xēros = dry/withered wood. Jesus is the living wood -- innocent, full of life. If Roman power and human sin can do this to him, what will happen when Jerusalem is dry, God-forsaken, ripe for destruction? The proverb asks the city to consider its own spiritual state.
23.32: Two others (kakourgoi = evildoers, criminals, a Lukan word for criminals in the passion narrative) are led out with Jesus to be executed -- the plural fulfills Isaiah 53:12 ("numbered with the transgressors"). Luke names them kakourgoi throughout rather than lēstai (robbers/bandits) as in Mark/Matthew.
23.33: Kranion -- the skull-place (Latin: Calvaria, hence "Calvary"). They crucified him there, and the criminals -- one on the right, one on the left. The crucifixion itself receives minimal description (only "they crucified him"), consistent with all the Gospels -- the physical act needs no elaboration; the theological significance is what the evangelists narrate.
23.34: Pater, aphes autois, ou gar oidasin ti poiousin -- "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing." Textually present in most MSS though absent in some early witnesses (P75, B, D*); Lukan in idiom and theology. Jesus prays for his executioners even as they cast lots. The ignorance motif (ou oidasin) connects to Acts 3:17 ("you acted in ignorance") and 13:27 -- the pattern of uncomprehending guilt that can still receive forgiveness. The soldiers cast lots for his garments (Ps 22:18 fulfillment).
23.35: exemykterizou de kai hoi archontes -- "the rulers also sneered" (ekmykterizō = turn the nose up at, mock with contempt). Their taunt: allos esōsen, sōsato heauton = "he saved others, let him save himself." They intended it as mockery; it is Lukan irony -- the very definition of the cross is saving others precisely by not saving himself. Ho Christos tou theou ho eklektos = "the Christ of God, the Chosen One" -- unknowing confession on their lips.
23.36: The soldiers also mock him, offering him oxos (sour wine/vinegar -- a common soldier's drink), saying "If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself" (ei su ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn, sōson seauton). The triple mockery (rulers v35, soldiers v36-37, one criminal v39) mirrors the triple Pilate verdict in reverse -- there innocence was proclaimed three times, here the title is mocked three times.
23.37: Ei sy ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn, sōson seauton -- "If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself." The soldiers repeat Pilate's title from the trial. The irony throughout the crucifixion scene is that Jesus is simultaneously mocked as king and is king -- every taunt is an inadvertent confession. His refusal to save himself from the cross is the mechanism of salvation for others (v43 demonstrates this immediately).
23.38: epigraphē -- the inscription above him. Luke gives the wording simply: Ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn houtos = "This is the King of the Jews." This titulus (Roman practice of posting the charge) becomes an involuntary proclamation. Pilate's inscription, meant as humiliation, is permanent testimony to Jesus' identity.
23.39: eblasphēmei auton -- "was blaspheming him." The criminal echoes the rulers' taunt: "Are you not the Christ? Save yourself and us!" -- a self-interested demand for deliverance. Luke's use of blasphēmeō is deliberate: the word used of speaking against God is applied to speaking against Jesus on the cross, equating the two.
23.40: Ou phobē sy ton theon -- "Do you not fear God?" The second criminal's rebuke begins with the core question of all wisdom literature (fear of God as beginning of wisdom, Prov 1:7). Hoti en tō autō krimati ei = "since you are in the same condemnation." Same judgment, same death -- why compound one's dying with mockery of the innocent?
23.41: Kai hēmeis men dikaios -- "And we indeed justly" -- a remarkable public confession of guilt and acceptance of just punishment. Axios gar hōn epraxamen apolambanomen = "for we are receiving what our deeds deserve." Then the contrast: houtos de ouden atopon epraxen = "but this man has done nothing out of place/wrong." Atopos = "out of place, inappropriate, wrong" -- an understated Greek word that here carries full weight. The dying thief pronounces what the Sanhedrin, Pilate, and Herod all failed to enforce: this man is innocent.
23.42: Mnēsthēti mou hotan elthēs eis tēn basileian sou -- "Remember me when you come into your kingdom." The grammar is significant: elthēs is an aorist subjunctive -- "whenever you come" -- expressing real expectation of a future arrival. Mnēsthēti = remember me (covenant-memory language in LXX: God "remembered" Noah, Abraham, Hannah). The criminal does not ask for rescue from the cross but for inclusion in Jesus' kingdom. His faith is extraordinary -- he believes in the kingship of a crucified man.
23.43: Amēn soi legō, sēmeron met' emou esē en tō paradeiso -- "Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise." Sēmeron (today) -- Luke's signature word of eschatological immediacy: 2:11 (born today), 4:21 (fulfilled today), 19:5 (today I must stay at your house), 19:9 (today salvation has come). Even at the cross, salvation breaks through "today." Paradeisos -- a Persian loanword (pairidaeza = walled garden) adopted into Greek via the LXX to translate the Garden of Eden and divine garden imagery. Jewish usage (2 Enoch, 4 Ezra, Testament of Levi) describes paradise as the dwelling of the righteous dead. Jesus promises immediate post-mortem fellowship: met' emou = "with me."
23.44: ēn de hōsei hōra hektē -- "it was about the sixth hour" (noon). Darkness came over the whole land (skotos egeneto eph' holēn tēn gēn) until the ninth hour (3 pm). Three hours of mid-day darkness recalls the plagues of Egypt (Exod 10:21-23), the Day of the Lord darkness (Amos 8:9: "I will make the sun go down at noon"), and functions as cosmic mourning/judgment sign at the cross.
23.45: tou hēliou ekliponto -- "the sun having failed/ceased" (eklipō = to fail, disappear; from this: eclipse). The temple veil torn in two (eschisthē de to katapetasma tou naou meson = "the curtain of the temple was torn down the middle"). The katapetasma was the inner veil separating the Holy of Holies. Its tearing at Jesus' death signals the end of the barrier between God and humanity -- access opened by his sacrifice (cf. Heb 10:19-20). Luke places this before Jesus' death (unlike Mark/Matthew), perhaps to frame the death as the cause of the opening.
23.47: Ontōs ho anthrōpos houtos dikaios ēn -- "Truly this man was righteous." Luke's centurion pronounces Jesus dikaios -- the Greek word carrying both "innocent" (legal innocence) and "righteous" (covenant faithfulness). Mark/Matthew have "Son of God" (Theou Huios); Luke's dikaios fits his emphasis on the judicial vindication of Jesus and connects to the Servant of Isaiah 53:11 ("the righteous one, my servant"). The centurion edoxazen ton theon = "glorified God" -- a Lukan response phrase (5:25, 13:13, 17:15, 18:43) marking recognition of divine action.
23.48: theōria -- the spectacle, the sight (theōreō = to watch, contemplate). The crowds who had come for the spectacle (epi tēn theōrian tautēn) witness the events and return home typtomenoi ta stēthē = "beating their breasts." This gesture of grief and contrition (cf. 18:13 -- the tax collector's contrition) suggests the crowd is moved beyond morbid spectacle to something like remorse. Luke's crowd here differs from Mark/Matthew's jeering crowd.
