23.49: heistēkeisan de pantes hoi gnōstoi autō -- "all his acquaintances stood at a distance." Luke adds gnōstoi (known ones, acquaintances) beyond "the women." They stand at a distance, watching (horōsai tauta). The women who had followed from Galilee are specifically named as eyewitnesses -- their unbroken presence from cross to tomb to resurrection is Luke's evidentiary chain for the resurrection narrative.
23.50: bouleutēs -- a member of the Boulē (council), i.e., the Sanhedrin. Joseph of Arimathea is described as anēr agathos kai dikaios = "a good and righteous man." Dikaios again -- the word used of Simeon (2:25), Zechariah and Elizabeth (1:6), John the Baptist (1:17), and the centurion's verdict. Luke identifies the righteous who surround this death.
23.51: ou synkatatetheimenos -- "not having consented" -- the perfect participle indicates his ongoing dissent from the Sanhedrin's decision (boulē kai tē praxei autōn = "their counsel and action"). Joseph's righteousness consists partly in what he refused to do. He was waiting expectantly for the kingdom of God (prosdechomenos tēn basileian tou theou) -- the phrase connects him to Simeon (2:25, 2:38) and marks him as genuinely Israel's hope.
23.52: prosēlthen tō Pilatō kai ētēsato to sōma tou Iēsou -- Joseph approaches Pilate and asks for Jesus' body. The request required courage (Mark 15:43: tolmēsas); Joseph uses his political access (as council member) for this final act of respect. The body (sōma) would otherwise be left on the cross or thrown in a common grave.
23.53: sindon -- a linen cloth, fine linen (used for burial wrapping). Joseph wraps the body and places it in a hewn tomb (mnēmati laxeutō = carved/cut from rock) where no one had yet been laid (hou ouk ēn oupō oudeis keimenos = a new, unused tomb). The unused tomb's significance: no confusion of remains is possible; the resurrection will be unambiguous.
23.54: hēmera paraskeuas -- "day of Preparation," i.e., Friday before Sabbath. Sabbaton epephōsken = "the Sabbath was dawning/beginning to shine." The Jewish day begins at sundown; the Sabbath's approach creates urgency. Joseph completes the burial before sundown, observing Torah timing even in grief.
23.55: katakolouthsāsai de hai gynaikes -- "the women who had come with him from Galilee followed after." They see the tomb (to mnēmeion) and how his body was laid (hōs etethē to sōma autou). Luke is establishing the chain of witness: the women are eyewitnesses to (1) the crucifixion (v49), (2) the burial location (v55), and (3) later the empty tomb (24:1-3). Their testimony is foundational.
23.56: hētoimasan arōmata kai myra -- "they prepared spices and ointments/myrrh" for anointing the body. Then they rested on the Sabbath according to the commandment (kata tēn entolēn). Luke alone adds this phrase of Torah observance -- these women are faithful Jews, resting on Sabbath even in grief, then returning to honor Jesus at the first opportunity (24:1: "on the first day of the week, very early"). Their Sabbath obedience makes the resurrection morning witness even more carefully timed.
23.17: anagkēn de eichen apolyein autois kata heotrēn hena -- "For of necessity he must release one to them at the feast." This verse is textually uncertain (absent in P75, Vaticanus, and other early witnesses; present in D, W, and the majority text as a harmonization from Mark 15:6/Matt 27:15). Whether original or a scribal insertion, the content reflects historical Roman practice of a Passover amnesty. Anagkē = necessity, compulsion -- the same word used of divine necessity elsewhere in Luke, here referring to customary obligation.

24.27: arxamenos apo Mouseos kai apo panton ton propheton diermeneuen autois en pasais tais graphais ta peri heautou — beginning from Moses and all the prophets he interpreted to them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself — the risen Christ's Emmaus hermeneutic is Luke's programmatic claim about Scripture: all of it (en pasais tais graphais = in all the scriptures) is about him. The verb diermeneuen (he interpreted, explained) is the technical term for authoritative interpretation; Jesus is his own supreme interpreter. The burning hearts (v.32) indicate that the hermeneutic is not merely cognitive but spiritually transformative: right reading of the Scriptures produces encounter with the risen Christ.
24.44: dei plerothenal panta ta gegrammena en to nomo Mouseos kai tois prophetais kai psalmois peri emou — it is necessary for everything written in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms about me to be fulfilled — Luke's risen-Christ statement is the most comprehensive canonical claim in the Gospels: the tripartite Hebrew canon (Torah + Nevi'im + Ketuvim = the Psalms as the Writings' representative) is entirely oriented toward and fulfilled in Jesus. Dei (it is necessary) indicates divine necessity: not merely that these things happened to correspond with Scripture, but that Scripture's fulfillment was divinely required and superintended. The resurrection is the key that unlocks what was always there.
24.1: tē de mia tōn sabbatōn orthrou batheōs ēlthon epi to mnēma — on the first day of the week, at deep dawn, they came to the tomb. Tē mia tōn sabbatōn (the first of the week — literally the first of the sabbaths; the Jewish week was counted from the Sabbath, so “day one of the week” = Sunday, the day after Sabbath) — the day becomes the Lord’s Day for the church. Orthrou batheōs (at deep/very early dawn — bathos = depth; the deepest point of dawn, before full light) is Luke’s precise time-marker for the earliest possible morning visit. Ta arōmata (the spices they had prepared — 23:56) — the women are completing a burial interrupted by the Sabbath.
24.2: heuron de ton lithon apokekulismenon apo tou mnēmeiou — they found the stone rolled away from the tomb. Apokekulismenon (having been rolled away — perfect passive participle of apokuliō, to roll away) — the completed removal is already accomplished before the women arrive; they are witnesses of the effect, not the event. The stone’s removal is not to let Jesus out (the resurrection is already accomplished) but to let witnesses in; its rolled-away position is evidence, not mechanism.
24.3: eiselthousai de ouch heuron to sōma tou kyriou Iēsou — but when they entered they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus. Tou kyriou Iēsou (of the Lord Jesus) — the post-resurrection title used by Luke in his narrator’s voice already at the empty tomb. The combination of the full title at the moment of the missing body is deliberately ironic: the one whose body cannot be found is identified by the highest Christological title. The absence of the body is the first resurrection evidence: the tomb is empty, and the Lord’s body is gone.
24.4: kai egeneto en tō aporein autas peri toutou kai idou andres dyo epestēsan autais en esthēti astraptousē — while they were puzzled/perplexed about this, suddenly two men in dazzling clothes stood beside them. Aporein (to be perplexed, at a loss — the same root as aporia of 21:25) — the women’s confusion is epistemic: the evidence does not fit their categories. Dyo andres (two men) — Luke consistently represents angelic messengers as “men” (cf. 24:23 where they are called “a vision of angels”). Esthēti astraptousē (dazzling/lightning-like clothing) — the theophanic language of heavenly beings whose garments reflect divine glory.
24.5: emphobōn de genomenōn autōn kai klinousōn ta prosōpa eis tēn gēn eipan pros autas: ti zēteite ton zōnta meta tōn nekrōn — in their fright, the women bowed their faces to the ground. The men said to them: Why do you seek the living among the dead? Ti zēteite ton zōnta meta tōn nekrōn (why do you seek the living one among the dead ones?) — the question is the resurrection’s first announcement, phrased as a gentle reproof of a category error: the women are looking for Jesus among the dead (nekrōn), but he is the living one (ton zōnta — the present participle: the one who is alive, the living one, a title). The question presupposes the resurrection as already accomplished.
24.6: ouk estin hōde, alla ēgerthē — he is not here, he has been raised! The double declaration is Luke’s resurrection kerygma in its most compressed form. Ouk estin hōde (he is not here) — the negative fact: absence from the tomb. Alla ēgerthē (but he has been raised — divine passive of egeirō, to raise; God raised him) — the positive claim: resurrection accomplished by divine action. Mnēsthēte hōs elalēsen hymin eti ōn en tē Galilaia (remember how he spoke to you while he was still in Galilee) — the command to remember redirects the women to Jesus’s own predictions, making the resurrection the fulfillment of his word rather than a surprise.
24.7: legōn ton huion tou anthrōpou hoti dei paradothēnai eis cheiras anthrōpōn hamartolon kai staurōthēnai kai tē tritē hēmera anastēnai — saying that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, be crucified, and on the third day be raised again. The passion-resurrection summary (paradothēnai... staurōthēnai... anastēnai — delivered, crucified, raised) repeats the triadic pattern of the passion predictions (9:22; 9:44; 18:32-33). Dei (must — divine necessity) frames the entire sequence as providentially required, not accidentally suffered. Eis cheiras anthrōpōn hamartolon (into the hands of sinful men) — the human agency of the crucifixion is named without exonerating it.
24.8: kai emnēsthēsan tōn rhēmatōn autou — and they remembered his words. The remembering (emnēsthēsan — aorist of mimnēskomai) is the cognitive breakthrough that makes the evidence intelligible: the empty tomb alone is ambiguous; the empty tomb plus the remembered prediction produces understanding. Luke’s resurrection epistemology consistently pairs evidence with the interpretive key of Jesus’s prior teaching: the disciples need both the fact and the framework. The women’s remembering is the model of post-resurrection faith: connect what you see to what he said.
24.9: kai hypostrepsasai apo tou mnēmeiou apēngeilan tauta panta tois hendeka kai pasin tois loipois — when they returned from the tomb, they told all these things to the Eleven and to all the others. The women are the first witnesses of the resurrection and the first proclaimers of the resurrection message — a culturally significant detail in a context where women’s legal testimony was systematically discounted. Tois hendeka kai pasin tois loipois (the Eleven and all the others) — the broader community of disciples beyond the Twelve (now Eleven after Judas’s defection); the resurrection announcement goes to the whole community.
24.10: ēsan de hē Magdalēnē Maria kai Iōanna kai Maria hē Iakōbou kai hai loipai syn autais — it was Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and the others with them who told this to the apostles. Luke names three of the women witnesses: Mary Magdalene (present also in Mark 16:1; Matt 28:1; John 20:1), Joanna (unique to Luke; also named in 8:3 as the wife of Chuza, Herod’s steward), and Mary the mother of James (also in Mark 16:1). The named women anchor the resurrection tradition in specific, identifiable witnesses; the claim is not anonymous but anchored in known persons.
24.11: kai ephainonto enōpion autōn hōsei lēros ta rhēmata tauta, kai ēpistoun autais — but they did not believe the women, because their words seemed to them like nonsense. Lēros (nonsense, idle talk — a medical term for the delirious chatter of a fever patient; Hippocratic literature uses it for the rambling speech of the seriously ill) — Luke uses the medical vocabulary of delirium to describe the apostles’ dismissal of the resurrection report. The use of this specific term is likely intentional: Luke the physician (Col 4:14) reaches for the clinical word for irrational speech. Ēpistoun autais (they disbelieved them) — the male disciples’ initial unbelief in the female witnesses is a mark of historical honesty; no one inventing a resurrection story would begin it with the primary witnesses being disbelieved.
24.12: ho de Petros anastas edramen epi to mnēmeiou kai parakupsas blepei ta othonia mona — Peter, however, got up and ran to the tomb. Bending over, he saw the strips of linen lying by themselves. Edramen (ran) — Peter’s response to the disbelieved report is immediate and urgent: he runs. Ta othonia mona (the linen wrappings alone, by themselves) — the burial cloths are present but the body is absent; the wrappings have not been removed and carried away with the body, which would indicate theft; they lie as if the body evaporated from within them. Apēlthen pros heauton thaumazōn to gegonos (he went away wondering at what had happened) — Peter’s response is wonder (thaumazōn), not yet belief; he processes the evidence without yet arriving at the resurrection conclusion.
24.13: kai idou dyo ex autōn ēsan poreuomenoi en autē tē hēmera eis kōmēn apechousan stadious hexēkonta apo Ierousalēm — that same day two of them were going to a village called Emmaus, about sixty stadia from Jerusalem. En autē tē hēmera (on that same day) — the Emmaus journey happens on the resurrection day itself; the chronology is tight. Stadious hexēkonta (sixty stadia = approximately 7 miles; a stade is about 607 feet) — Luke gives the precise distance; the walk would take about 2-3 hours each way. Emmaoūs — the village’s exact location is debated; ancient candidates include Nicopolis (modern Imwas, 18 miles) and Qubeibeh (about 7 miles), the latter fitting the 60-stade figure better.
24.14: kai autoi hōmiloun pros allēlous peri pantōn tōn symbebēkotōn toutōn — they were talking with each other about everything that had happened. Hōmiloun (were conversing, talking — the verb from which “homily” derives; a continuous, back-and-forth discussion) — the two disciples are engaged in sustained analysis of the crucifixion events. Tōn symbebēkotōn toutōn (these things that had happened — perfect participle of symbaínō, to come together, to occur) — the events are complete in their occurrence but not yet understood; the disciples are working through the cognitive aftermath of the cross.
24.15: kai egeneto en tō homilein autous kai syzētein kai autos Iēsous engisas syneporeueto autois — as they talked and discussed these things, Jesus himself came near and walked along with them. Autos Iēsous (Jesus himself — the emphatic pronoun marks the dramatic arrival: not a report about Jesus, not a vision of Jesus, but Jesus himself in person) draws near and begins walking with them. Syneporeueto autois (traveled along with them — the verb of journeying together on the same road) — the Emmaus narrative is a journey-with-Jesus story; the risen Lord meets the disciples in the midst of their ordinary travel and their confused conversation.
24.16: hoi de ophthalmoi autōn ekratounto tou mē epignōnai auton — but their eyes were kept from recognizing him. Ekratounto (were kept, held — imperfect passive of krateō, to hold firmly, grip) — the divine passive: God actively prevented their recognition. Tou mē epignōnai auton (from recognizing him — the genitive articular infinitive of purpose/result) — the non-recognition is not a failure of perception but a divinely imposed barrier. The Emmaus narrative builds toward the moment of recognition (v.31) as its structural climax; the prevented recognition creates the space for the Scripture-opening and the eucharistic gesture that produce genuine understanding rather than mere visual identification.
24.17: eipen de pros autous: tines hoi logoi houtoi hous antiballete pros allēlous peripatountes kai estē skythrōpoi — he asked them: What are you discussing as you walk along? They stood still, their faces downcast. Antiballete (you are throwing back and forth, exchanging — the verb for a dialogue where each party responds to the other) — Jesus uses the image of conversational exchange. Estēsan skythrōpoi (they stood, gloomy/sad-faced — skythrōpos is a NT rare word for a gloomy, downcast expression; used also in Matt 6:16 for the ostentatious sad-face of fasting hypocrites) — their physical posture and facial expression embody the grief and confusion of the post-crucifixion period.
24.18: apokritheis de heis onomati Kleopas eipen pros auton: sy monos paroikeis Ierousalēm kai ouk egnōs ta genomena en autē en tais hēmerais tautais — one of them, named Cleopas, answered: Are you the only stranger in Jerusalem who doesn’t know the things that happened there in these days? Kleopas — one of the two named disciples; his companion remains unnamed. Cleopas may be an alternate form of Clopas/Alpheus; some church fathers (Hegesippus via Eusebius) identify him as a brother or relative of Joseph. Sy monos paroikeis (you alone are staying as a stranger/sojourner) — the irony is complete: the one they think is ignorant of recent events is the event himself.
24.19: kai eipen autois: poia; hoi de eipan autō: ta peri Iēsou tou Nazarēnou — what things? he asked. About Jesus of Nazareth, they replied. Iēsou tou Nazarēnou (Jesus the Nazarene — the geographic identification that had become the standard way of distinguishing this Jesus from others; used in the early church as a formal identification, Acts 2:22; 3:6) — the disciples’ identification of Jesus is geographically precise and shows that “Jesus of Nazareth” was already a recognized identity label. Dynatos en ergō kai logō enantion tou theou kai pantos tou laou (powerful in deed and word before God and all the people) — the twofold power of Jesus: the deed-word combination is the prophetic pattern, and the testimony is before both God (coram Deo) and all the people (public, verifiable).
24.20: hopōs te paredōkan auton hoi archiereis kai hoi archontes hēmōn eis krima thanatou kai estaurōsan auton — the chief priests and our rulers handed him over to be sentenced to death, and they crucified him. The disciples’ summary places the responsibility on the Jewish leaders (hoi archiereis kai hoi archontes) who handed him over (paredōkan — the passion’s central verb of betrayal-transfer) and the Roman execution (estaurōsan, they crucified him). The political anatomy of the crucifixion is accurately summarized: Jewish authorities initiated, Roman authorities executed. The disciples state the facts without yet understanding their redemptive meaning.
24.21: hēmeis de elpizomen hoti autos estin ho mellōn lytrousthai ton Israēl — but we had hoped that he was the one who was going to redeem Israel. Ēlpizomen (we were hoping — imperfect indicative, the tense of continuous past action now broken off) — the imperfect marks the death of their hope: “we used to hope, but that hope is now over.” Ho mellōn lytrousthai ton Israēl (the one who was about to redeem Israel — lytrousthai is the verb of ransom-liberation from Exodus typology; Zechariah used it in the Benedictus, 1:68) — they had understood Jesus in terms of national-political redemption; the cross destroyed that particular hope. Their framework was right (redemption) but too narrow (Israel only, this-worldly liberation).
24.22: alla kai gynai kes tines ex hēmōn exestēsan hēmas — in addition, some of our women amazed us. Exestēsan hēmas (they amazed us, astonished us — from existēmi, to stand outside oneself; the verb for astonishment or amazement) — the women’s report has disrupted the disciples’ settled grief without resolving it. The “women’s evidence” is positioned as an intermediate stage: it creates confusion without yet producing faith. The Emmaus disciples are in the uncomfortable position between the crucifixion’s finality and the women’s unbelievable report.
24.23: kai mē heurousai to sōma autou ēlthon legousai kai optasian angelōn heōrakenai hoi legousin auton zēn — but they didn’t find his body. They came and told us that they had seen a vision of angels, who said he was alive. Optasian angelōn (a vision of angels — the word optasia is used for visionary experience; the Emmaus disciples report the women’s encounter as a “vision” rather than as objective events, revealing their own epistemic skepticism) — even the women’s encounter with the angelic messengers is processed through the Emmaus disciples’ doubt-framework as a possibly subjective vision. The report is accurate; the framing as “vision” reveals the hearers’ resistance.
24.24: kai apēlthon tines tōn syn hēmin epi to mnēmēion kai heuron houtōs kathōs kai hai gynaikes eipon, auton de ouk eidon — then some of our companions went to the tomb and found it just as the women had said, but they did not see him. The male disciples’ verification of the tomb condition is corroborated (found it just as the women said: empty) but incomplete (did not see him). Peter’s investigation (v.12) is here placed in a wider corroborating frame. The cumulative weight of evidence — empty tomb, abandoned linen, angels’ testimony, confirmed by multiple witnesses — has not yet produced resurrection faith; the disciples need the Scriptures opened and the Lord recognized.
24.25: kai autos eipen pros autous: ō anoētoi kai bradeis tē kardia tou pisteuein epi pasin hois elalēsan hoi prophētai — he said to them: How foolish you are, and how slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Anoētoi (foolish, without understanding — a = not + noetikos = intelligently reasoning; those who fail to exercise the capacity for rational understanding) — the rebuke is intellectual: the disciples have failed to think through the prophetic testimony. Bradeis tē kardia tou pisteuein (slow of heart to believe — the heart as the seat of understanding in Semitic thought; slowness of heart = reluctance to draw the full implications of what the evidence demands). The prophets had provided sufficient testimony for faith; the disciples’ failure is not lack of evidence but slow-heartedness.
24.26: ouchi tauta edei pathein ton Christon kai eiselthein eis tēn doxan autou — was it not necessary for the Christ to suffer these things and then enter into his glory? The rhetorical question has the force of a Christological argument: ouchi edei (was it not necessary — the dei of divine necessity framing the entire passion-resurrection as the scripturally required pattern). Pathein ton Christon (for the Christ to suffer) — the necessity of a suffering Messiah is the interpretive breakthrough the Emmaus disciples had missed: their Messianic framework excluded suffering, but the prophetic testimony required it. Eiselthein eis tēn doxan autou (to enter into his glory) — suffering is not the Messiah’s defeat but the pathway to glory, the road through which the resurrection glory is reached.
24.28: kai ēngisan eis tēn kōmēn hou eporeuonto, kai autos prosepoieito porrōteron poreuesthai — as they approached the village to which they were going, Jesus acted as if he were going further. Prosepoieito porrōteron poreuesthai (he made as if to go further — prospoieomai with an infinitive: to pretend, to act as if) — Jesus does not force an invitation; he makes a movement that requires the disciples to choose whether to extend hospitality. The hospitality pattern (meinon meth hēmōn, stay with us) is an echo of the OT hospitality traditions and foreshadows the eucharistic recognition that will follow.
24.29: kai parebiasanto auton legontes: meinon meth hēmōn, hoti pros esperan estin kai keklinēken ēdē hē hēmera — but they strongly urged him: Stay with us, for it is nearly evening; the day is almost over. Parebiasanto (they urged strongly, pressed him — para- + biazō = to force alongside; to press with force; the most urgent form of invitation) — the disciples do not merely invite but press Jesus to remain. Pros esperan estin kai keklinēken hē hēmera (it is toward evening and the day has declined/bent down) — the temporal urgency of the invitation: travel after dark is dangerous. The urgency will be fulfilled in a different sense: the day’s ending becomes the moment of recognition and new beginning.
24.30: kai egeneto en tō kataklithēnai auton met autōn labōn ton arton eulogēsen kai klasas epedidou autois — when he was at table with them, he took bread, gave thanks, broke it and began to give it to them. The four eucharistic verbs (labōn: took; eulogēsen: gave thanks/blessed; klasas: broke; epedidou: gave) precisely mirror Luke’s Last Supper account (22:19) and the feeding of the five thousand (9:16). The guest becomes the host; the one who was invited to stay assumes the role of the meal’s presider. Luke frames the Emmaus recognition as a eucharistic event: the breaking of bread is the context of resurrection recognition.
24.31: autōn de diēnoichthēsan hoi ophthalmoi kai epegnōsan auton kai autos aphantos egeneto ap autōn — then their eyes were opened and they recognized him, and he disappeared from their sight. Diēnoichthēsan hoi ophthalmoi (their eyes were opened — divine passive: God opened their eyes) — the miracle of recognition is the reversal of v.16’s miracle of non-recognition; both are divinely controlled. Epegnōsan auton (they recognized him — epiginōskō is the full, complete knowing that goes beyond simple identification) — the recognition is complete. Aphantos egeneto (he became invisible, disappeared — aphantos is a NT hapax meaning unseen, invisible; from a + phaínō = to appear) — the instant disappearance marks the post-resurrection mode of existence: not bound by physical location.
24.32: kai eipan pros allēlous: ouchi hē kardia hēmōn kaiomenē ēn en hēmin hōs elalei hēmin en tē hodō, hōs diēnoigen hēmin tas graphas — they said to each other: Were not our hearts burning within us while he talked with us on the road and opened the Scriptures to us? Hē kardia hēmōn kaiomenē ēn (our heart was burning — imperfect, the continuing inner experience during the journey) — the burning heart is the phenomenology of encounter with the risen Christ through Scripture: before they recognized him visually, they were experiencing the unmistakable inner warmth of genuine divine encounter. Hōs diēnoigen hēmin tas graphas (as he opened the Scriptures to us — dianoigō is to open wide, to unlock what was closed) — the Scripture-opening produces the burning recognition that precedes and enables the visual recognition at the table.
24.33: kai anastantes autē tē hōra hypostrepsan eis Ierousalēm kai heuron ēthroismenous tous hendeka kai tous syn autois — they got up that very hour and returned to Jerusalem. There they found the Eleven and those with them, assembled together. Autē tē hōra (that very hour — the immediacy of the response: they do not wait until morning despite the evening hour) — the burning hearts and the opened minds produce immediate action; the resurrection news cannot be kept. The return journey to Jerusalem reverses the grief-departure: they had been going away from Jerusalem in confusion; they now return to it with joy.
24.34: legontas hoti ontōs ēgerthē ho kyrios kai ōphthē Simōni — saying: It is true! The Lord has risen and has appeared to Simon. The assembled community greets the two Emmaus disciples with their own prior confirmation: ontōs ēgerthē (truly/really he has been raised — ontōs is the adverb of ontological reality: genuinely, in fact, truly) — the resurrection is not rumor but confirmed fact. Ōphthē Simōni (he appeared to Simon — the passive of horaō, to see, used as the technical term for resurrection appearances; cf. 1 Cor 15:5 where the same verb appears) — the appearance to Peter is corroborated in 1 Cor 15:5 as the first individual appearance; Luke mentions it without narrating it, a significant example of restrained tradition.
24.35: kai autoi exēgounto ta en tē hodō kai hōs egnōsthē autois en tē klasei tou artou — then the two told what had happened on the road, and how Jesus was recognized by them in the breaking of the bread. En tē klasei tou artou (in the breaking of the bread) — Luke’s phrase for the eucharistic action that produced recognition. The phrase recurs in Acts 2:42,46 as a regular practice of the early Jerusalem community: the breaking of bread as the habitual context of the risen Christ’s presence with his community. The Emmaus narrative provides the theological grounding: the Lord is known in the eucharistic breaking.
24.36: tauta de autōn lalountōn autos estē en mesō autōn kai legei autois: eirēnē hymin — while they were still talking about this, Jesus himself stood among them and said to them: Peace be with you. Autos estē en mesō autōn (he himself stood in their midst) — the appearance is sudden and unannounced; the closed doors are not mentioned (contrast John 20:19-20) but implied by the suddenness of the appearance. Eirēnē hymin (peace to you — the standard Jewish greeting shalom lekhem) — the risen Lord’s first word to the assembled community is the covenant greeting of wholeness and wellbeing; the peace of his resurrection becomes the peace he gives his community.
24.37: ptoēthentes de kai emphoboi genomenoi edokoun pneuma theōrein — they were startled and frightened, thinking they saw a ghost. Ptoēthentes (startled, terrified — ptoeō is to frighten, to cause to flee in terror; Luke uses it also in 21:9) and emphoboi (full of fear, frightened) — the double fear-word intensifies the disciples’ response: not mere surprise but genuine terror. Pneuma theōrein (they were thinking they were seeing a ghost/spirit) — the disciples’ misidentification is the apologetic occasion: they think they are seeing a disembodied spirit, not a physically resurrected person. Jesus’s response (vv.38-43) is a systematic refutation of that interpretation.
24.38: kai eipen autois: ti tetaragmenoi este kai dia ti dialogismoi anabainousin en tē kardia hymōn — he said to them: Why are you troubled, and why do doubts rise in your minds? Tetaragmenoi (troubled, disturbed — perfect passive participle of tarasso, to stir up, agitate) — the interior disturbance of confused emotion. Dialogismoi anabainousin en tē kardia hymōn (reasonings/doubts rise up in your heart — dialogismos is internal reasoning/deliberation that produces doubt; “rising up” in the heart suggests the involuntary surge of questioning thoughts) — Jesus diagnoses the dual problem: emotional disturbance and cognitive doubt, both of which he will address through sensory evidence in vv.39-43.
24.39: idete tas cheiras mou kai tous podas mou hoti egō eimi autos; psēlaphēsate me kai idete — look at my hands and my feet. It is I myself! Touch me and see. Tas cheiras mou kai tous podas mou (my hands and feet) — the hands and feet bear the marks of crucifixion nails; the invitation to examine them is an invitation to identify the risen Jesus as the crucified Jesus — the resurrection is the resurrection of the same body, not a new spiritual form. Psēlaphēsate me kai idete (touch me and see — psēlaphāō is tactile exploration, the touch of one who wants to verify; the same verb used in Acts 17:27 for seeking God) — touch is the most direct refutation of ghost-interpretation.
24.40: kai touto eipōn edeixen autois tas cheiras kai tous podas — when he had said this, he showed them his hands and feet. The showing of hands and feet is the confirmatory act accompanying the verbal invitation of v.39: Jesus’ words (look, touch) are followed by the gesture (showed) that makes the examination possible. The text is absent from some important Western manuscripts (D, Old Latin) but present in the Alexandrian tradition; most scholars consider it original. The physical display is Luke’s apologetic for bodily resurrection: the risen Lord has identifiable physical marks.
24.41: eti de apistountōn autōn apo tēs charas kai thaumazontōn eipen autois: echete ti brōsimon enthade — and while they still disbelieved from joy and amazement, he asked them: Do you have anything here to eat? Apistountōn apo tēs charas kai thaumazontōn (while they were disbelieving from joy and amazement) — the paradoxical psychological state: the disciples cannot fully process what they are seeing because the joy of it is too overwhelming; disbelief and joy co-exist as the mind struggles to accept what is too good to be true. The request for food is the climax of the bodily-resurrection apologetic: a ghost cannot eat; a risen body can and does.
24.42: hoi de epedōkan autō ichthuos optou meros — they gave him a piece of broiled fish. Ichthuos optou meros (a piece of broiled/grilled fish — optos means cooked by fire, roasted, grilled) — the specific detail (fish, not bread; grilled, not baked) grounds the account in concrete sensory memory. Fish is an appropriate Jerusalem meal (transported from the Sea of Galilee); its preparation (optos) specifies the cooking method. The specificity of the fish and its preparation is a hallmark of eyewitness memory preservation.
24.43: kai labōn enōpion autōn ephagen — and he took it and ate it in their presence. Enōpion autōn (before them, in their presence — in plain sight, with full visibility) — the eating is a public, observable act. Ephagen (ate — the simple aorist: he ate it, fully, completely) — the eating of food is the most direct demonstration of bodily resurrection: a spirit-appearance cannot eat physical food; the risen Jesus eats in full view of the assembled disciples. Luke’s bodily-resurrection apologetic is complete with this verse: visible scars, tangible body, consumed food.
24.45: tote diēnoixen autōn ton noun tou synienai tas graphas — then he opened their minds so they could understand the Scriptures. Diēnoixen autōn ton noun (he opened their mind/understanding — the same verb dianoigō used for the Scripture-opening in v.32) — the opening of the Scriptures (v.32) and the opening of the mind (v.45) are parallel acts of the risen Lord: the external text and the internal capacity for understanding are both opened by him. Without the opened mind, the opened Scriptures cannot be understood; the hermeneutical event requires both the text and the reader’s transformation.
24.46: hoti houtōs gegraptai pathein ton Christon kai anastēnai ek nekrōn tē tritē hēmera — this is what is written: The Messiah will suffer and rise from the dead on the third day. The summary of what is written covers the entire passion-resurrection as a single scriptural statement: pathein ton Christon (the Messiah to suffer) and anastēnai ek nekrōn tē tritē hēmera (to rise from the dead on the third day). No single OT text contains this precise formulation; Jesus is presenting the cumulative witness of the whole canonical pattern. Paul makes the same move in 1 Cor 15:3-4 (kata tas graphas twice: according to the Scriptures, both for death and resurrection).
24.47: kai kērychthēnai epi tō onomati autou metanoian eis aphesin hamartiōn eis panta ta ethnē — and repentance for the forgiveness of sins will be preached in his name to all nations. Metanoian eis aphesin hamartiōn (repentance for/leading to forgiveness of sins — metanoia = the change of mind and direction that is the human response to the gospel; aphesis = release, forgiveness — the term introduced in 4:18 as the Jubilee-release Jesus came to proclaim) — the Lukan summary of the apostolic preaching that will fill Acts: beginning at Jerusalem (arxamenōn apo Ierousalēm), going to all nations (eis panta ta ethnē). The scope is universal; the center is Jesus’s name (epi tō onomati autou).
24.48: hymeis martyres toutōn — you are witnesses of these things. Martyres (witnesses — the legal term for one who testifies to what they have seen; the source of the word “martyr” after witnesses began dying for their testimony) — the apostolic identity as witnesses is the foundation of the church’s mission in Acts. Toutōn (of these things) — the testimony is to the entire preceding account: the passion, the empty tomb, the appearances, the opened Scriptures. The witness-commission without the Spirit’s empowerment (v.49) would be insufficient; the two go together as commission and equipment.
24.49: kai idou egō apostellō tēn epangelian tou patros mou eph hymas — and behold, I am going to send what my Father promised upon you. Tēn epangelian tou patros mou (the promise of my Father) — the promised Spirit (Joel 2:28-32; Isa 32:15; 44:3; Ezek 36:26-27) for which John the Baptist had prepared (3:16: he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire). Luke does not name the Spirit here; the reference is sufficiently clear from the promise tradition. Hymeis de kathisate en tē polei heōs hou endysēsthe ex hypsous dynamin (but you, stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high) — endysēsthe (to be clothed with) is the clothing metaphor for being enveloped by the Spirit’s power; ex hypsous (from on high) echoes Isa 32:15 and anticipates Pentecost.
24.50: exēgagen de autous exō heōs pros Bēthanion kai eparas tas cheiras autou eulogēsen autous — when he had led them out to the vicinity of Bethany, he lifted up his hands and blessed them. The ascension-blessing narrative (unique to Luke among the Gospels) takes place near Bethany, on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives (Acts 1:12 gives the location as the Mount of Olives; Bethany is at the foot of its eastern slope). Eparas tas cheiras autou eulogēsen autous (lifting up his hands he blessed them) — the priestly gesture: the high priestly blessing (Num 6:22-27; Sir 50:20-22) involved raised hands. The ascension is structured as a priestly act of blessing; Jesus departs as priest blessing his people.
24.51: kai egeneto en tō eulogein auton autous diestē ap autōn kai anephereto eis ton ouranon — while he was blessing them, he left them and was taken up into heaven. Diestē ap autōn (he withdrew, separated himself from them — diistamai = to stand apart) and anephereto eis ton ouranon (he was carried up into heaven — anaferō in the passive: to be carried/borne upward; the same verb used for Elijah’s being “taken up” in the LXX tradition) — the ascension is an act of being taken rather than of going: passive verbs throughout. The ascension happens during the blessing, so the disciples are blessed in the moment of departure; the departure is itself a blessing-moment. Luke narrates it briefly here; Acts 1:9-11 gives the fuller account.
24.52: kai autoi proskynēsantes auton hypostrepsan eis Ierousalēm meta charas megalēs — then they worshipped him and returned to Jerusalem with great joy. Proskynēsantes auton (having worshipped him — proskyneō is the full prostration-worship of deity; the disciples worship the ascended Jesus as divine) — the ascension produces worship rather than grief: the departure of the risen Lord is experienced as a cause for joy and worship, not mourning. Meta charas megalēs (with great joy) — the characteristic Lukan theme of joy (also 1:14,44; 2:10; 10:17; 15:7,10; 19:6,37; 24:41) now at its eschatological peak: the resurrection and ascension complete the joy that the births of John and Jesus began.
24.53: kai ēsan dia pantos en tō hierō eulogountes ton theon — and they were continually in the temple, praising God. The Gospel of Luke ends where it began: in the temple (en tō hierō). Zechariah’s priestly service in the temple (1:5-23) opens the narrative; the disciples’ continual temple praise closes it. Dia pantos (continually, always — the same phrase used in 24:10 for the women) — the community of disciples is defined by ceaseless temple-praise. Eulogountes ton theon (blessing/praising God — the Benedictus verb of 1:64,68) completes the inclusio: the blessing of God with which Zechariah’s tongue was released is now the continuous activity of the entire community of resurrection witnesses.

5.1: egeneto de en tō ton ochlon epikesthai autō kai akouein ton logon tou theou — as the crowd was pressing upon him and hearing the word of God — epikeimai (to press upon, lie on) describes the physical pressure of an eager crowd. Luke identifies Jesus's teaching as ton logon tou theou (the word of God), not merely rabbinic instruction; this identification is programmatic in Luke-Acts where the apostolic message is consistently called ho logos tou theou (Acts 6:7; 12:24; 13:7). limné Gennésaret — the Lake of Gennesaret — Luke's preferred name for the Sea of Galilee, derived from the fertile plain on its northwest shore.
5.2: kai eiden ploion dyo hestōta para tēn limnēn — he saw two boats standing by the lake — the fishermen (halieis) had come in after a night's fruitless fishing and were washing their nets (eplynon ta diktya) — daytime net-cleaning after the night-fishing failure Luke has established in advance of the miraculous catch narrative. The detail of net-washing indicates the end of the working night, not the beginning; the fishermen are finished and available.
5.3: embas de eis hen tōn ploiōn, ho ēn Simōnos — stepping into one of the boats, the one belonging to Simon — Jesus requisitions Simon's boat as a teaching platform before the miraculous catch. The boat-as-pulpit (ekathisen ... edidasken = sitting, he was teaching) follows the seated-teacher pattern (cf. 4:20); the lake amplifies the voice for the crowd on the shore. Luke establishes the relationship between Simon and Jesus (the boat-sharing) before the call narrative of v.10-11, making the call the natural consequence of a relationship already begun rather than an abrupt summons to a stranger.
5.4: hōs de epausato lalōn, eipen pros ton Simōna — when he stopped speaking, he said to Simon — the teaching ends and the testing begins. Epanagage eis to bathos kai chalasate ta diktya hymōn eis agran — put out into the deep and let down your nets for a catch — bathos (depth, deep water) is where fish cannot be seen from shore; the daylight instruction contradicts fishing practice (Galilean fishermen fished at night in shallow water with torchlight to attract fish). The command makes no pragmatic sense and is obeyed as an act of trust.
5.5: kai apokritheis Simōn eipen, Epistata, di holēs nyktos kopiasantes ouden elabomen — Simon answered: Master, working hard all night we caught nothing — Epistata (master, commander, overseer) is Luke's exclusive title for Jesus in this pre-discipleship scene; it is not rabbi (teacher) or kurios (Lord) but the word for a military or civil commander. The professional fisherman acknowledges the night's failure with full honesty. epi de tō rhēmati sou chalasō ta diktya — but upon your word I will let down the nets — the transition from objection to obedience: not upon pragmatic expectation but upon rhēma (your specific word, command). This is the faith-response that occasions the miracle.
5.6: kai touto poiēsantes synekleisan plēthos ichthyōn poly — doing this, they enclosed a great multitude of fish — synekleisan (enclosed, caught in a net) indicates surrounding the school from below; the verb is also used in Ezek 47:10 LXX (fishermen with nets on the shores of the restored land). dierrhēgnuto de ta diktya autōn — and their nets were breaking — the miraculous abundance strains the tools of the trade; the provision exceeds the capacity of the instruments. The net-breaking signals the completeness of the miracle rather than a mundane good catch.
5.7: kai kateneusan tois metochois en tō heterō ploiō tou elthontas syllabesthai autois — they signalled to their partners in the other boat to come and help them — kataneuō (to signal by nodding or gesturing) implies they cannot shout over the noise or the distance; the partners in the other boat are summoned by gesture. Metochoi (partners, sharers) indicates a fishing cooperative; James and John are identified as partners (koinōnoi, v.10) of Simon. kai ēlthon kai eplēsan amphotera ta ploia, hōste bythizesthai auta — they came and filled both boats so full they were sinking — bytizō (to sink, submerge) dramatizes the scale; two full boats sinking under fish is the visual of abundance beyond capacity.
5.8: idōn de Simōn Petros prosepesen tois gonasin Iēsou legōn, Exelthe ap emou, hoti anēr hamartolos eimi, Kyrie — when Simon Peter saw, he fell before Jesus's knees saying: Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, Lord — prosepesen tois gonasin (fell at the knees) is the posture of supplication or prostration before the holy. The response echoes Isaiah's throne-room vision: "Woe to me, for I am undone! I am a man of unclean lips" (Isa 6:5) — the encounter with the holy produces consciousness of sin, not just wonder. Hamartolos (sinner) is Luke's most frequent characterization of those who need salvation (5:30,32; 7:34,37,39; 15:1,2,7,10; 18:13; 19:7); it is a self-designation in the presence of recognized holiness.
5.9: thambos gar perieschev auton kai pantas tous syn autō — for astonishment had seized him and all those who were with him — thambos (astonishment, awe) is the reaction to the numinous, the presence of power beyond human category. The same word is used in 4:36 after the exorcism; here it follows a miracle of provision. The astonishment is specifically connected to the catch: epi tē agra tōn ichthyōn hōn synelabon — at the catch of fish which they had taken together. The eyewitness testimony of overwhelm is Luke's authentication device throughout the Gospel.
5.10: homoiōs de kai Iakōbon kai Iōannēn, huious Zebedaiou, hoi ēsan koinōnoi tō Simōni — similarly also James and John, sons of Zebedee, who were partners with Simon — koinōnoi (partners, associates in a business venture) is a commercial term; the apostolic nucleus is a family fishing business. kai eipen pros ton Simōna ho Iēsous, Mē phobou — Jesus said to Simon: Do not be afraid — the comfort-phrase in the presence of the numinous, identical to the angel's words to Zechariah (1:13), to Mary (1:30), and to the shepherds (2:10). apo tou nyn anthrōpous esē zōgrōn — from now on you will be catching people alive — zōgreō (to catch alive, take captive alive — a hunting/fishing term) transforms the vocational metaphor: fish caught die; people "caught" by proclamation gain life.
5.11: kai katagagontes ta ploia epi tēn gēn, aphentes panta, ēkolouthēsan autō — pulling the boats onto the land, leaving everything, they followed him — the response to the call is total abandonment: aphentes panta (having left all things). Luke uses the same phrase at Levi's call (5:28) and later generalizes it (18:28 Peter's claim, 18:29 Jesus's promise). The two boats full of fish — a windfall catch worth a season's income — are left on the shore. This is Luke's discipleship definition at its starkest: the net full of fish abandoned with the nets.
5.12: kai egeneto en tō einai auton en mia tōn poleōn kai idou anēr plērēs lepras — as he was in one of the cities, a man full of leprosy — plērēs lepras (full of leprosy) — Luke's medical term: the disease is at an advanced, total stage. Lepra in the NT encompasses a range of skin conditions classified under Levitical law (Lev 13-14), not only modern Hansen's disease. The man is ritually excluded from community (Lev 13:45-46) and approaches Jesus in violation of social protocol. pesōn epi prosōpon edeēthē autou — falling on his face, he begged him — deeomai (to beg, implore) is the prayer-verb; the leper approaches Jesus as a suppliant approaching God.
5.13: kai ekteinas tēn cheira hēpsato autou legōn, Thelō, katharisthēti — extending his hand, he touched him saying: I am willing; be cleansed — the touch is the scandal: Jesus could have healed by word alone (cf. 4:35,39) but chooses to touch the ritually impure. Under Levitical law, touching a metzora (one with a skin disease) transmitted impurity (Lev 5:3); Jesus does not become impure but transfers purity. Thelō (I am willing) answers the leper's implicit doubt ("if you are willing," v.12); divine will aligns with human need. kai eutheōs hē lepra apēlthen ap autou — and immediately the leprosy departed from him — apēlthen (went away, departed) — the same verb for demon-departure.
5.14: kai autos parēggeilen autō mēdeni eipein — he commanded him to tell no one — the messianic silence command (cf. 4:41); the proclamation of identity through miracles is premature. alla apelthōn deixon seautō tō hierai — but going, show yourself to the priest — Lev 14 prescribed a detailed seven-day purification ritual for the restored leper: the priest examined the healed person, performed two-bird sacrifice, and pronounced cleanness. The instruction to comply with the Mosaic ritual demonstrates Jesus's affirmation of Torah even as he transcends its mechanisms. kai prospheron peri tou katharismou sou kathōs prosetaxen Mōysēs — and offer the things concerning your cleansing as Moses commanded — the restored leper rejoins the covenant community through its own prescribed means.
5.15: diērcheto de mallon ho logos peri autou — the word about him spread all the more — the Lukan irony: silence was commanded (v.14) but news spread further. Ho logos (the word, the report) is Luke's ambiguous term — it will become the technical term for the apostolic gospel proclamation in Acts. kai synērchonto ochloi polloi akouein kai therapeuesthai apo tōn astheneiōn autōn — crowds came together to hear and to be healed from their infirmities — the dual purpose of the crowds is Lukan: they come both to hear (the word) and to be healed (physical restoration). Luke consistently pairs proclamation and healing as twin dimensions of the kingdom's arrival.
5.16: autos de ēn hypochōrōn en tais erēmois kai proseuchomenos — but he was withdrawing in the deserted places and praying — Luke's characteristic prayer note: this is the third Lukan mention of Jesus withdrawing to pray (cf. 3:21 at baptism; 4:42 after the Capernaum healings), and will continue at every major turning point (6:12; 9:18,28-29; 11:1; 22:41-44). Hypochōreō (to withdraw, retire) is stronger than Mark's aperchomai; the imperfect ēn ... proseuchomenos (was regularly praying) indicates a pattern, not a single occasion. Luke presents prayer as the foundation of Jesus's ministry, not an interruption to it.
5.17: kai egeneto en mia tōn hēmerōn kai autos ēn didaskōn — one day as he was teaching — the scene is set: Jesus is teaching in a house. kai ēsan kathēmenoi Pharisaioi kai nomodidaskaloi hoi ēsan elēlythotes ek pasēs kōmēs tēs Galilaias kai Ioudaias kai Ierousalēm — Pharisees and teachers of the law were sitting, who had come from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem — Luke stresses the representative gathering of all three regions: Galilee (north), Judea (south), and Jerusalem (the capital). This is a formal investigative delegation, not a chance encounter. kai dynamis Kyriou ēn eis to iasthai auton — and the power of the Lord was present for him to heal — Luke notes the Spirit's presence as the enabling condition for the miracle that follows.
5.18: kai idou andres pherontes epi klinēs anthrōpon hos ēn paralalymenos — men were bringing a paralysed man on a stretcher — klinē is a bed or stretcher; Luke's version (vs. Mark's krabatton, a pallet) uses slightly more polished Greek vocabulary. Paralalymenos (perfect passive participle of paralyō, to loose at the side — a medical term for limb paralysis) is Luke's medical vocabulary. The four bearers (Mark 2:3 specifies four; Luke does not count them) could not access Jesus because of the crowd; the house is packed.
5.19: kai mē heurontes poias eisenengkōsin auton dia ton ochlon, anabantes epi to dōma — not finding a way to bring him in through the crowd, going up onto the roof — dōma (flat roof, housetop) is the standard Palestinian roof accessible by exterior stairs. Luke's version describes removing roof tiles (dia tōn keramōn, through the tiles — a Hellenistic construction detail vs. Mark's digging through earth and thatch), reflecting an audience familiar with tiled roofs rather than Palestinian mud construction. kathēkan auton syn tō klinidiō eis to meson emprosthen tou Iēsou — they lowered him with the small stretcher into the middle in front of Jesus — the centering detail: right before Jesus, unavoidable.
5.20: kai idōn tēn pistin autōn eipen, Anthrōpe, apheontai soi hai hamartiai sou — seeing their faith, he said: Friend, your sins are forgiven — tēn pistin autōn (their faith) encompasses both the paralyzed man and his bearers; faith visible in action (the creative roof-entry) is what Jesus perceives. Anthrōpe (man, friend) is a direct but not demeaning address. The forgiveness precedes the healing — Luke's ordering makes the theological point: forgiveness is the primary need; physical restoration follows as its sign. Apheontai (have been forgiven, perfect passive) declares completed forgiveness in the divine passive.
5.21: kai ērxanto dialogizesthai hoi grammateis kai hoi Pharisaioi legontes, Tis estin houtos hos lalei blasphēmias — the scribes and Pharisees began to reason: who is this who speaks blasphemy? — dialogizesthai (to reason, deliberate — Luke uses this for internal, often hostile calculation, cf. 1:29; 2:35; 3:15) is internal, not voiced aloud; Jesus perceives it (v.22). The charge of blasphemy is the formal religious accusation: blasphēmia (defaming, reviling) of God — specifically, usurping divine prerogatives. tis dynatai hamartias aphienai ei mē monos ho theos — who can forgive sins except God alone? — this is the correct theological premise; the scribes' error is not the premise but the failure to recognize who stands before them.
5.22: epignous de ho Iēsous tous dialogismous autōn apokritheis eipen pros autous — Jesus, knowing their deliberations, answered and said to them — epignous (having known, perceived — aorist of epiginōskō, intensive knowing) indicates supernatural cognition of unexpressed thoughts, not inference from facial expressions. This is one of Luke's consistent demonstrations of Jesus's divine omniscience (cf. 6:8; 9:47; 11:17). ti dialogizesthe en tais kardiais hymōn — why are you deliberating in your hearts? — the internal reasoning is exposed and addressed directly; the question confronts rather than accuses.
5.23: ti estin eukopōteron, eipein, Apheontai soi hai hamartiai sou, ē eipein, Egeire kai peripatei — which is easier: to say, your sins are forgiven, or to say, rise and walk? — the rhetorical question addresses what is easier to say, not to do. Pronouncing forgiveness is easier to say because it cannot be immediately verified; commanding physical healing is immediately verifiable and the healer is immediately accountable to the result. By performing the visible miracle, Jesus provides public verification of the invisible divine action (forgiveness) that no human can authenticate.
5.24: hina de eidēte hoti ho huios tou anthrōpou exousian echei epi tēs gēs aphienai hamartias — but so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — ho huios tou anthrōpou (the Son of Man) is Jesus's characteristic self-designation in the Synoptics; in Dan 7:13-14 the Son of Man receives from the Ancient of Days universal authority, glory, and dominion. Jesus applies this Danielic title to the one who has exousian ... epi tēs gēs — authority on earth (not merely eschatological authority, but present, operative authority in the physical world). The healing that follows is the public demonstration of this claim.
5.25: kai parachrēma anastas enōpion autōn aras eph ho kateiketo apēlthen eis ton oikon autou doxazōn ton theon — immediately he rose before them, took up what he had lain on, and went home glorifying God — parachrēma (immediately, at once — Luke's preferred term for instantaneous miraculous healing, cf. 1:64; 4:39; 8:44,47; 13:13; 18:43; Acts 3:7; 5:10) signals the completeness of divine power. The former paralytic carries his own stretcher — the instrument of his helplessness becomes the trophy of his healing. Doxazōn ton theon (glorifying God) is the proper response: the man attributes the healing to God, not merely to the healer.
5.26: kai ekstasis elaben hapantas kai edoxazon ton theon — astonishment seized all and they were glorifying God — ekstasis (ecstasy, being outside oneself, displacement by overwhelming experience) is the strongest Greek term for stupefied amazement; the crowd is beyond ordinary response. kai eplēsthēsan phobou legontes hoti eidomen paradoxa sēmeron — they were filled with fear, saying: we have seen remarkable things today — paradoxa (wonderful things, paradoxes — things contrary to ordinary expectation) is a Lukan hapax; the crowd's assessment captures the epistemological shock of divine intrusion into the ordinary. Sēmeron (today) echoes 4:21 — the Jubilee-era of divine favor is the present moment of encounter with Jesus.
5.27: kai meta tauta exēlthen kai etheasato telōnēn onomati Leuin kathēmenon epi to telōnion — after these things he went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax office — telōnēs (tax collector, toll collector) was a contracted collector for Rome, generally detested as both a collaborator and a notorious extortioner; telōnion (tax booth) is the collection point on a road or at a border. Levi (Matthew in Matt 9:9) sat at one of the Capernaum customs stations on the Via Maris trade route. kai eipen autō, Akolouthei moi — and he said to him: Follow me — the call is identical in form to Simon's (cf. v.10); no explanation, no preliminary discussion, one command.
5.28: kai katalipōn panta anastas ēkolouthei autō — leaving everything, he rose and followed him — Levi's response is more total than Simon's in one respect: the tax collector abandoned a profitable licensed business with no possibility of return (a fishing boat can be reclaimed; a tax contract relinquished is gone). Katalipōn panta (having left everything) is Luke's emphatic phrase for complete discipleship abandonment (cf. 5:11; 18:28-29). The imperfect ēkolouthei (was following, began to follow) signals the ongoing nature of the following that begins here.
5.29: kai epoiēsen dochēn megalēn Leuis autō en tē oikia autou — and Levi made a great banquet for him in his house — dochē (reception, banquet — a formal feast given in someone's honor) is a Lukan word (also 14:13); it signals a celebration of the call. kai ēn ochlos polys telōnōn kai allōn hoi ēsan met autōn katakeimenoi — there was a large crowd of tax collectors and others who were reclining with them — katakeimai (to recline at table) is the Greek dining posture for a formal meal. The gathering is Levi's professional network: tax collectors and the socially marginal ("others") who shared their stigma.
5.30: kai egongyzon hoi Pharisaioi kai hoi grammateis autōn pros tous mathētas autou legontes — the Pharisees and their scribes murmured against his disciples saying — egongyzon (were murmuring, grumbling) is the LXX term for Israel's wilderness murmuring against Moses (Exod 15:24; 16:7-8; 17:3; Num 14:2,27; Ps 106:25); Luke's use of this exact word signals the Pharisees as recapitulating Israel's wilderness sin of complaining against God's appointed leader. Dia ti meta tōn telōnōn kai hamartoōlōn esthiete kai pinete — why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners? — the table-fellowship challenge: eating with the am ha-aretz (people of the land) and tax collectors made a rabbi ritually suspect and socially compromised.
5.31: kai apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen pros autous, Ou chreian echousin hoi hygiainontes iatrou alla hoi kakōs echontes — Jesus answered them: those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick — the physician proverb is known from Greek sources (Plutarch, Diogenes Laertius) and is probably a common wisdom saying. Luke's ironic appropriateness: he is addressing the physician-metaphor to a physician's audience (Luke himself). Hoi hygiainontes (those who are well, healthy) vs. hoi kakōs echontes (those who are doing badly, having it badly = the sick) sets up the social analogy Jesus will make explicit in v.32.
5.32: ouk elēlytha kalesai dikaious alla hamartōlous eis metanoian — I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance — kalesai (to call, summon) is the vocabulary of divine election and invitation; the same word is used for God's call of Abraham, Israel, and the prophets. Dikaious (righteous ones) is ironic: the Pharisees consider themselves righteous; Jesus does not contest their self-assessment but reframes the mission. Hamartoōlous eis metanoian — sinners to repentance — metanoia (change of mind, turning) is distinctively Lukan; it occurs more in Luke-Acts than in all other NT writings combined and is the defining characteristic of the kingdom's response requirement.
5.33: hoi de eipen pros auton, Hoi mathētai Iōannou nēsteuousin pyknōs kai deēseis poioyntai — they said to him: John's disciples fast often and make prayers — nēsteuousin pyknōs (fast frequently, regularly) — the Pharisees (m. Taanit) fasted Mondays and Thursdays in addition to the mandatory Day of Atonement fast; John's ascetic movement fasted similarly. Deēseis poiountai (they make/perform prayers) — formal prayer as religious discipline. homoiōs kai hoi tōn Pharisaiōn, hoi de soi esthiousin kai pinousin — and likewise the Pharisees', but yours eat and drink — the accusation is framed as a contrast between two recognized religious traditions (Johannine, Pharisaic) and Jesus's notably different practice.
5.34: ho de Iēsous eipen pros autous, Mē dynasthe tous huious tou nymphōnos en hō ho numphios met autōn estin poiēsai nēsteusai — Jesus said to them: can you make the sons of the bridal chamber fast while the bridegroom is with them? — huioi tou nymphōnos (sons of the bridal chamber = wedding guests, groomsmen) is a Semitic idiom for those belonging to the bridal party. The nymphios (bridegroom) is a well-established image for YHWH in relationship to Israel (Isa 62:5; Jer 2:2; Hos 2:16-20; Ezek 16). Jesus applies the bridegroom image to himself without explanation; the audience would understand the implication: the messianic age has arrived, and fasting (mourning) is incompatible with the bridegroom's presence.
5.35: eleusontai de hēmerai kai hotan aparthē ap autōn ho numphios — but days will come when the bridegroom is taken away from them — aparthē (is taken away, removed by force — aorist passive of apairō) is a passive that implies external force, not natural departure; this is a cryptic Passion reference, the first in Luke. The wedding-feast of the messianic era will be interrupted by the violent removal of the bridegroom — an event only later identified explicitly as the crucifixion. tote nēsteusousin en ekeinais tais hēmerais — then they will fast in those days — fasting in the post-Easter era is legitimate, but as grief for the absent bridegroom, not as religious performance.
5.36: elegen de kai parabolēn pros autous — he was also telling a parable to them — the two parables of incompatible structures address the deeper question behind the fasting-debate: the new era is qualitatively incompatible with the old order, not merely quantitatively different. Oudeis epilamma schismas apo himatiou kainou epiballei epi himation palaion — no one tears a patch from a new garment and sews it on an old garment — the garment parable: the new garment is destroyed to patch the old, and the patch from the new does not match the old anyway (shrinkage difference). The new era cannot be used as a resource to repair the old religious order; it would ruin both.
5.37: kai oudeis ballei oinon neon eis askous palaious — and no one puts new wine into old wineskins — askous (wineskins, leather bags made from goatskin) were used for wine storage; old skins had already been stretched to their limit by previous fermentation. New wine (oinos neos) continues to ferment and expand; old skins cannot accommodate the expansion and burst. ei de mē ge, rēxei ho oinos ho neos tous askous — otherwise the new wine will burst the wineskins — the structural incompatibility is not a judgment on the old but a statement of physical necessity. The new era of the Spirit is not compatible with the containing structures of Second Temple Judaism's religious economy.
5.38: alla oinon neon eis askous kainous blēteon — but new wine must be put into new wineskins — kainous (new in quality, fresh, unused — vs. neos which is new in time) is Luke's word for the eschatologically new. The dei-equivalent blēteon (must be put, verbal adjective of necessity) makes this a structural requirement, not a preference. The new covenant community, the church that Luke will narrate in Acts, is the new wineskin adequate to contain the Spirit's new wine. The parable does not deprecate the old but insists on structural correspondence between content and container.
5.39: kai oudeis piōn palaion thelei neon, legei gar ho palaios chrēstos estin — and no one after drinking old wine desires new, for they say: the old is good — this final saying is Luke's addition (absent from Matthew and Mark) and is often read as ironic comment on the Pharisees' preference for the old order. Chrēstos (good, pleasant, well-suited, palatable) is the vocabulary of practical usefulness rather than moral excellence. The person habituated to the old wine of Second Temple piety does not feel a deficit; the palatability of the familiar obscures the need for the new. Luke presents this as an explanation of why the religious establishment resists Jesus's new era — not malice but acquired taste.

8.1: kai egeneto en tō kathexēs kai autos diōdeuev kata polin kai kōmēn — he was traveling through city after city and village after village — kathexēs (in sequence, successively) is a Lukan word (cf. 1:3; Acts 3:24; 11:4; 18:23) marking ordered narrative progression. Diōdeuō (to travel through, journey through) describes systematic evangelistic itineration. kēryssōn kai euangelizomenos tēn basileian tou theou — proclaiming and announcing the good news of the kingdom of God — Luke pairs kēryssōn (herald-proclamation) and euangelizomenos (good-news announcement); together they cover both the public announcement and the content-specific glad tidings.
8.2: kai gynaikes tines hai ēsan tetherapeumenai apo pneumatōn ponērōn kai astheneiōn — and certain women who had been healed of evil spirits and diseases — Luke alone records the band of women disciples who traveled with Jesus and supported the movement. Tetherapeumenai (perfect passive participle: having been and remaining healed) marks the healings as permanent. Mariam hē kaloumenē Magdalēnē — Mary called the Magdalene — Magdalēnē identifies her town of origin: Magdala/Taricheae on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee, a prosperous fishing-processing center. aph hēs daimonia hepta exelēlythei — from whom seven demons had gone out — seven (hepta) is the number of completeness; the exorcism was total.
8.3: kai Iōanna gynē Chyza epitropou Hērōdou — and Joanna wife of Chuza, Herod's steward — epitropos (steward, manager, administrator) is a high-ranking household official; Chuza managed Herod Antipas's personal estate. Joanna's presence in the apostolic company indicates access to the Herodian court — a significant intelligence and social network connection. hai diekonoun autois ek tōn hyparchontōn autais — they were serving/supporting them out of their own possessions — diakonoun (were serving, ministering — the diakonia vocabulary) and hyparchontōn (possessions, resources) indicate material patronage. Women of means are the financial foundation of Jesus's itinerant movement.
8.4: syniontos de ochlou pollou kai tōn kata polin epiporeouomenōn pros auton — a large crowd coming together and those from each city traveling to him — the composite audience arrives from multiple directions; Luke emphasizes the drawing power of Jesus before the parable begins. eipen dia parabolēs — he spoke by a parable — parabolē (comparison, figurative speech, riddle) is the Synoptic term for Jesus's characteristic teaching mode; it derives from the Hebrew mashal (proverb, parable, simile), which covered wisdom comparisons, riddles, and extended analogies. The parable is not primarily didactic illustration but an invitation to discernment.
8.5: exēlthen ho speirōn tou speirai ton sporon autou — the sower went out to sow his seed — the indefinite article is absent in Greek: ho speirōn is simply "the sower" as a type, not a specific identified individual. Speirōn/speirō (sowing) was broadcast by hand in the ancient Near East; the sower scattered seed widely before plowing, so some inevitably fell on unintended soil. kai en tō speirein auton ho men epesen para tēn hodon — and in his sowing, some fell along the road — para tēn hodon (beside/along the path) is the hard-packed edge of agricultural fields where foot traffic compacted the soil; seed lying on the surface became bird-food.
8.6: kai heteron katepesen epi tēn petran — other seed fell on the rock — petra (bedrock, solid rock) covered by a thin layer of soil; sufficient for germination but not for root development. kai phylen exaranthe dia to mē echein ikmada — and growing up it withered because it had no moisture — ikmada (moisture, dampness) is Luke's unique word here (vs. Matthew/Mark's "depth of earth"); a medical-sounding term for the soil's hydration level. The Palestinian summer rapidly desiccates shallow-rooted seedlings; without moisture access from depth, the young plant cannot survive the dry season.
8.7: kai heteron epesen en mesō tōn akanthōn — other seed fell in the middle of the thorns — akanthai (thorns, thorn-bushes) were perennial plants whose root systems occupied the soil before plowing; their growth season paralleled the grain crop. kai symphyeisai hai akanthai apepnixan auto — and the thorns growing up with it choked it — symphyō (to grow up together with, co-grow) and apopnigō (to choke, strangle, cut off air) describe competitive growth that starves the good plant of light and nutrients. Luke's interpretation (v.14) identifies these as the cares, wealth, and pleasures of life.
8.8: kai heteron epesen eis tēn gēn tēn agathēn kai phyen epoiēsen karpon hekatontaplasiōna — other seed fell into the good earth and growing up produced fruit a hundredfold — gēn tēn agathēn (good earth) — deep, prepared, fertile soil. Hekatontaplasiōna (a hundred times over, hundredfold) is an extraordinary yield; Palestinian grain farming averaged 10-fold returns; 30-fold was good; 100-fold is extraordinary enough to suggest the eschatological abundance of the new era (cf. Gen 26:12 where Isaac reaped hundredfold as divine blessing). tauta legōn ephōnei, Ho echōn ōta akouein akouetō — saying these things he called out: the one having ears to hear, let them hear — the hearing-summons marks the parable as requiring discernment, not merely comprehension.
8.9: epērōtōn de auton hoi mathētai autou tis autē eiē hē parabolē — his disciples were asking him what this parable might mean — epērōtaō (to ask, question, interrogate) indicates persistent inquiry; the imperfect suggests ongoing questioning. Tis autē eiē hē parabolē (what this parable might be — optative mood) is an indirect question in the optative, Luke's more refined literary style (vs. Mark's simpler constructions). The disciples' question is genuine incomprehension, not Socratic pretense; the parable's meaning is not transparent on the surface.
8.10: ho de eipen, Hymin dedotai gnōnai ta mystēria tēs basileias tou theou — he said: to you it has been given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God — ta mystēria (the mysteries, secrets) is the Danielic-apocalyptic term for divine revelations disclosed to the elect but hidden from others (cf. Dan 2:18-19,27-30,47; 1QS III-IV). The passive dedotai (has been given, divine passive) locates the source of understanding in God, not human insight. tois de loipois en parabolais, hina blepontes mē blepōsin kai akouontes mē syniosin — to the rest in parables, so that seeing they may not see and hearing they may not understand — Isa 6:9 (LXX), the commissioning text for prophetic ministry to a hardened audience.
8.11: estin de hautē hē parabolē, Ho sporos estin ho logos tou theou — this is the parable: the seed is the word of God — ho logos tou theou (the word of God) is the identification Luke consistently applies to Jesus's proclamation and later to the apostolic gospel in Acts (Acts 4:31; 6:2,7; 8:14; 11:1; 12:24; 13:7; 17:13; 18:11). The equation seed = the word of God makes the parable an allegory of reception and rejection of divine address. The sower is not identified in Luke's interpretation (unlike some subsequent traditions); the focus is entirely on the soil/reception conditions.
8.12: hoi de para tēn hodon eisin hoi akousantes, eita erchetai ho diabolos kai airei ton logon apo tēs kardias autōn — those along the road are those who heard, then the devil comes and takes the word from their heart — Luke's interpretation names ho diabolos (the devil) explicitly where Mark has "Satan"; this matches Luke's preference established from 4:2. Airei ton logon apo tēs kardias (lifts/takes the word from their heart) explains the birds of the parable as active demonic removal of what was heard; the word never penetrates beyond surface hearing. hina mē pisteusantes sōthōsin — so that they might not believe and be saved — the purpose is damning: the devil's action prevents the believing that leads to salvation (soteria).
8.13: hoi de epi tēs petras, hoi hotan akousōsin meta charas dechovtai ton logon — those on the rock are those who, when they hear, receive the word with joy — meta charas (with joy) is an enthusiastic initial reception; the problem is structural, not motivational. kai houtoi rizan ouk echousin — and these have no root — rizan ouk echousin (have no root) is the depth metaphor for settled conviction; surface enthusiasm without deep formation. pros kairon pisteuousin kai en kairō peirasmou afistantai — they believe for a season and in a time of testing they fall away — kairō peirasmou (time of testing/temptation) is the crucible that reveals the rootlessness; aphistantai (fall away, depart, apostatize — the apostasia root) is Luke's strongest term for abandonment of faith.
8.14: to de eis tas akanthas peson, houtoi eisin hoi akousantes kai hypo merimnōn kai ploutou kai hēdonōn tou biou poreuomenoi symphygontai — the seed fallen among thorns are those who heard and going on their way are choked by anxieties and wealth and pleasures of life — Luke's triad merimna (anxiety, care), ploutos (wealth, riches), and hēdonai tou biou (pleasures of life) maps the competitive forces against the word. Sympnigō (to choke, strangle) describes the gradual suffocation of spiritual vitality. kai ou telesphorousin — and they do not bring to maturity — telesphorō (to bring to full fruition, complete development) is unique to Luke; the image is of fruit that begins but never ripens to harvestable completeness.
8.15: to de en tē kalē gē, houtoi eisin hoitines en kardia kalē kai agathē — those in the good earth are those who have a noble and good heart — kardia kalē kai agathē (noble and good heart) is the Hellenistic moral ideal: kalos (beautiful, honorable) and agathos (good, morally excellent) together form the classical kalos kagathos ideal of the fully formed ethical person. Luke adapts this Hellenistic virtue language for a Greek-speaking audience; Matthew and Mark lack this phrase. akousantes ton logon katechousin kai karpophorous in en hypomonē — having heard the word they hold fast and bear fruit in perseverance — katechō (to hold fast, retain) and hypomonē (endurance, perseverance under pressure) are the two marks of productive reception.
8.16: Oudeis de lychon hapsas kalyptei auton skeuei ē hypokato klinēs tithēsin — no one lighting a lamp covers it with a vessel or places it under a bed — the lamp saying appears in a different context than Matthew's (Sermon on the Mount, Mt 5:15) and Mark's (Mk 4:21); Luke places it after the sower-interpretation to apply the revelation principle: what has been disclosed is for illumination, not concealment. Lychon (lamp, oil lamp) is the household clay lamp; lychnia (lampstand) elevates it for maximum distribution of light. hina hoi eiserchomenoi blepōsin to phōs — so that those entering may see the light — the incoming visitor benefits from the lamp placed for visibility.
8.17: ou gar estin krypton ho ou phaneron genēsetai — for there is nothing hidden that will not become visible — the principle grounds the lamp saying: hiddenness is provisional and purposeful, not permanent. oude apokryphon ho ou mē gnōsthē kai eis phaneron elthē — and nothing concealed that will not be known and come into the open — apokryphos (hidden away, concealed) is the technical term for esoteric knowledge; Luke applies it to the parables' concealment. The revelation of the kingdom's mysteries (v.10) is ultimately public, not permanently esoteric. The saying functions as both promise (truth will emerge) and warning (all concealed things will be disclosed).
8.18: blepete oun pōs akouete — take care therefore how you hear — pōs akouete (how you hear, in what manner you hear) is Luke's distinctive formulation; Mark and Matthew have "what you hear." The quality and manner of hearing determines what is received; the same words produce different effects in different hearers. hos an gar echē, dothēsetai autō; kai hos an mē echē, kai ho dokei echein arthēsetai ap autou — for whoever has, it will be given to him; and whoever does not have, even what he thinks he has will be taken from him — the paradox of spiritual economy: active receptivity generates accumulation; passive possession erodes. Ho dokei echein (what he thinks he has) reveals the illusion of superficial reception.
8.19: parageonto de pros auton hē mētēr kai hoi adelphoi autou — his mother and brothers came to him — the adelphoi (brothers) of Jesus are named in Mk 6:3 as James, Joseph, Judas, and Simon; whether biological brothers, half-brothers, or cousins depends on one's view of Mary's perpetual virginity. kai ouk ēdynanto syntychein autō dia ton ochlon — and they could not reach him because of the crowd — physical inaccessibility creates the occasion for the pronouncement about spiritual family that follows. Luke's version (unlike Mark 3:21,31-35) removes any hint that the family came because they thought Jesus was out of his mind; Luke presents the scene neutrally.
8.20: apēngelē de autō, Hē mētēr sou kai hoi adelphoi sou hestēkasin exō idein se thelontes — it was reported to him: your mother and brothers are standing outside wishing to see you — the message is relayed through the crowd rather than spoken directly; the physical distance mirrors the coming pronouncement about relational distance. Hestēkasin (are standing, perfect participle = have taken a position and remain there) indicates they are waiting, not hostile. The message creates a teaching moment rather than reporting a crisis.
8.21: ho de apokritheis eipen pros autous, Mētēr mou kai adelphoi mou houtoi eisin hoi ton logon tou theou akouontes kai poiountes — he answered and said to them: my mother and my brothers are those who hear the word of God and do it — the redefinition of family is based on the parable's criterion (vv.15, "hear the word and hold fast") now applied relationally. Akouontes kai poiountes (hearing and doing) is the James-and-Jesus ethical summary (cf. Jas 1:22-25); the two-part criterion reflects Deut 6:3 (hear and do). The biological family is not excluded but the covenant family of disciples supersedes biological claims. Ho logos tou theou binds vv.11-21 into a unity.
