8.22: egeneto de en mia tōn hēmerōn kai autos enebē eis ploion kai hoi mathētai autou — one day he got into a boat with his disciples — the sea-crossing narrative begins: the lake crossing to the eastern (predominantly Gentile) shore is a geographical transition with theological significance. kai eipen pros autous, Dielthōmen eis to peran tēs limnēs — he said to them: let us cross to the other side of the lake — to peran (the other side) in the Synoptics regularly marks the crossing to Gentile territory (the Decapolis). kai anēchthēsan — and they set sail — anagō (to bring up, to put to sea) is a nautical term; the verb is common in Luke-Acts for sea voyages (Acts 13:13; 16:11; 18:21; 20:3,13).
8.23: pleonton de autōn aphypnōsen — as they sailed, he fell asleep — aphypnoō (to fall deeply asleep) is used only here in the NT; it implies sound, undisturbed sleep — not fitful dozing. The detail is theologically charged: Jesus sleeps in the storm as Jonah slept in the ship (Jon 1:5-6), but where Jonah's sleep was negligence, Jesus's sleep is sovereignty. kai katebē lailaps anemou eis tēn limnēn kai syneplērounto kai ekindyneuon — a squall came down onto the lake and they were filling with water and were in danger — lailaps (violent squall, whirlwind-storm) is the technical term for the sudden katabatic storms that descend from the Golan Heights onto the Sea of Galilee.
8.24: proselthontes de diegeiron auton legontes, Epistata, epistata, apollymetha — going to him they woke him saying: Master, Master, we are perishing — Epistata doubled (Epistata, Epistata) is the urgent Lukan cry of disciples in crisis (cf. 17:13 ten lepers calling Iēsou epistata); it expresses both urgency and deference to his authority. ho de egertheis epetimēsen tō anemō kai tō klydonī tou hydatos — waking up he rebuked the wind and the surging of the water — epetimēsen (rebuked) is the same verb used against demons (4:35,41; 9:42) and fever (4:39); Luke treats the storm as subject to the same divine command that expels demons. kai egeneto galēnē — and there was a calm — galēnē (calm, tranquility at sea) is the technical Greek term for a windless sea surface.
8.25: eipen de autois, Pou hē pistis hymōn — he said to them: where is your faith? — the question is diagnostic: not "where were you?" but "where is the pistis (faith, trust, reliance) that should have sustained you?" The disciples' failure was not the fear itself but the absence of trust in the one sleeping in the boat. phobēthentes de ethaumasan legontes pros allēlous, Tis ara houtos estin — they were afraid and marveled, saying to one another: who then is this? — Tis ara houtos estin (who therefore is this?) is the Christological question the miracle raises without immediately answering. hoti kai tois anemois epitassei kai tō hydati, kai hypakouousin autō — because he commands even the winds and the water and they obey him — divine control over sea and storm is YHWH's prerogative in the OT (Ps 107:23-32; Job 38:8-11).
8.26: kai katepleusan eis tēn chōran tōn Gerasēnōn — they sailed down to the region of the Gerasenes — Gerasēnōn is the reading of most manuscripts (cf. the textual variant Gadarēnōn in Matthew 8:28 and some Luke manuscripts, and Gergesēnōn in a few). Gerasa (modern Jerash in Jordan) is ~50km from the lake — too far geographically, but may refer to a district rather than the city. hētis estin antiperan tēs Galilaias — which is across from Galilee — Luke's explanatory gloss for Gentile readers who would not know the eastern shore's geography; it marks the crossing to Gentile territory as completed.
8.27: exelthonti de autō epi tēn gēn hypēntēsen anēr tis ek tēs poleōs — when he stepped ashore, a certain man from the city met him — the immediate encounter before Jesus can reach any populated area underscores the severity of the case: the demoniac comes out to meet him. hos eichen daimonia kai chronō hikanō ouk enedysato himation — who had demons and for a long time had not worn clothing — chronō hikanō (for a considerable time, a long time) emphasizes the chronic, severe nature of the possession. Nakedness marks the loss of human dignity and social identity. kai en oikia ouk emenev alla en tois mnēmasin — and he was not staying in a house but in the tombs — dwelling among the dead (the unclean, ritually defiling tombs) is the ultimate marginalization from human community.
8.28: idōn de ton Iēsoun anakraxas prosepesen autō — seeing Jesus he cried out and fell before him — anakrazō (to cry out, shout aloud) is the involuntary vocalization of the demon encountering divine power (cf. 4:33-34). Prosepesen autō (fell before him) is the same prostration used for Peter (5:8) and the leper (5:12); the demon compels the man into the position of a suppliant. kai phōnē megalē eipen, Ti emoi kai soi, Iēsou Huie tou Theou tou Hypsistou — with a loud voice he said: what to me and to you, Jesus Son of God Most High? — Hypsistos (Most High) is the Hellenistic Jewish title for YHWH (Gen 14:18-20; Num 24:16; Dan 3:26), also used by Gentiles for the supreme deity. The title bridges Jewish and Gentile theological categories.
8.29: parēggeilen gar tō pneumati tō akathartō exelthein apo tou anthrōpou — for he had commanded the unclean spirit to come out of the man — Luke explains the demon's outcry by giving the backstory: Jesus had already issued the command before the verbal exchange. The pluperfect parēggeilen (had commanded) places the command prior to the dialogue. pollois gar chronois synerpakei auton — for many times it had seized him — synarpazō (to seize, sweep away — a violent verb for sudden attack) is Luke's medical term for seizure (cf. Acts 6:12; 19:29; 27:15 for "swept away"). The chains and shackles broken by the demoniac's supernatural strength indicate chronic violent episodes known to the community.
8.30: epērōtēsen de auton ho Iēsous legōn, Ti soi onoma estin — Jesus asked him: what is your name? — the question is not for Jesus's information but for the man's own benefit and for the audience's comprehension. Knowing a demon's name was associated in ancient exorcism practice with gaining power over it (cf. PGM IV.1265-74); Jesus does not need the name to exorcise but asks publicly to expose the magnitude of the possession. ho de eipen, Legiōn, hoti eisēlthen daimonia polla eis auton — he said: Legion, because many demons had entered into him — Legiōn is the Latin loanword for a Roman military unit of 5,000-6,000 soldiers; the name simultaneously reveals the severity of possession (a multitude) and, for Luke's audience, evokes Roman military occupation as a demonic presence.
8.31: kai parekalei auton hina mē epitaxē autois eis tēn abysson apelthein — they were begging him not to command them to go into the Abyss — abyssos (the Abyss, the bottomless pit) is the eschatological prison for rebellious spirits (cf. Rev 9:1-2,11; 20:1-3; 1 Enoch 18:12-16; Jubilees 5:6-10). In Jewish apocalyptic, the Abyss is the pre-appointed confinement for fallen angels awaiting final judgment; the demons' terror of it reveals their knowledge of their ultimate fate. Their request to avoid it is a request for a temporary reprieve from their eschatological sentence.
8.32: ēn de ekei agelē choirōn hikanōn boskomenē en tō orei — there was a large herd of pigs feeding on the hillside — pigs (choiroi) are ritually impure animals (Lev 11:7; Deut 14:8) whose presence confirms the Gentile territory; a Jewish settlement would not keep swine herds. Hikanōn (considerable, large) indicates a substantial commercial herd. kai parekaleson auton hina epitrepsē autois eis ekeinous eiselthein — they begged him to allow them to enter into those — the demons negotiate: not the Abyss, but the swine herd. Jesus's permission (epetrepsen autois) raises the question of why he grants it; the outcome (the herd's destruction) may be the answer: even the "concession" serves the man's liberation.
8.33: exelthonta de ta daimonia apo tou anthrōpou eisēlthen eis tous choirous — coming out of the man, the demons entered the pigs — the transfer is immediate upon Jesus's permission. kai hōrmēsen hē agelē kata tou krēmnou eis tēn limnēn kai apepnigē — and the herd rushed down the steep bank into the lake and was drowned — hormaō (to rush, charge — used of a herd stampeding) and krēmnos (steep bank, cliff) describe the uncontrolled plunge. Apepnigē (was choked, drowned) echoes the "choked" plants of v.14 (thorns); the demons' final state is drowning/choking — the same fate they inflict on the word in human hearts. The lake that was a crisis for the disciples (vv.22-25) is now the destruction of the demonic horde.
8.34: idontes de hoi boskontes to gegenēmenon ephygon kai apēngeilan eis tēn polin kai eis tous agrous — seeing what had happened, those tending them fled and reported in the city and in the fields — the herdsmen are eyewitnesses who became involuntary evangelists; their flight and report follow the pattern of those who encounter divine power and cannot contain the news. To gegenēmenon (what had happened, perfect participle: the event now present as a completed reality) is the Lukan summary phrase for miraculous events (cf. 2:15; 24:12). The report goes both to the polis (city, urban center) and to the agroi (fields, rural areas).
8.35: exēlthan de idein to gegenēmenon kai ēlthon pros ton Iēsoun kai heuron kathēmenon ton anthrōpon — they went out to see what had happened and came to Jesus and found the man sitting — the transformation is visible: the man who was naked, among the tombs, shouting, and breaking chains is now kathēmenon himatimusemon kai sōphronounta — sitting, clothed, and in his right mind. Sōphroneō (to be in one's right mind, sane, self-controlled) is the mark of restored humanity; para tous podas tou Iēsou (at the feet of Jesus) is the posture of a disciple or a healed person in grateful proximity. kai ephobēthēsan — and they were afraid — the community's response to the restoration is fear, not joy; the disruption of the known order is more alarming than the restoration of the afflicted man.
8.36: apēngeilan de autois hoi idontes pōs esōthē ho daimonistheis — those who had seen told them how the demon-possessed man had been saved — esōthē (was saved, healed — aorist passive of sōzō) applies Luke's salvation vocabulary to the exorcism: the man's liberation from demonic possession is described with the same term used for forgiveness of sins (7:50) and physical healing (8:48; 17:19; 18:42). The observers' report (hoi idontes = eyewitness testimony) is the basis of the community's knowledge of the event.
8.37: kai ērōtēsen auton hapan to plēthos tēs perichōrou tōn Gerasēnōn apelthein ap autōn, hoti phobō megalō syneichonto — the whole multitude of the surrounding region of the Gerasenes asked him to depart from them, because they were gripped by great fear — hapan to plēthos (the whole multitude) — unanimous rejection. Phobō megalō syneichonto (were gripped by great fear) — sunechō (to grip, compress, constrain) + phobos megas (great fear) describes a community traumatized by an encounter with divine power beyond their framework. The Gerasenes prefer to lose the possessed man than to live with the one who cured him; the power that heals is more threatening than the power that destroys. autos de embas eis ploion hyestrepsen — he got into the boat and returned.
8.38: edeito de autou ho anēr aph hou exelēlythei ta daimonia einai syn autō — the man from whom the demons had gone out begged to be with him — edeito (was begging, entreating — imperfect of earnest request) from the man who has most reason to desire proximity to his deliverer. Einai syn autō (to be with him) is the language of discipleship (cf. Mk 3:14 "that they might be with him"); the man wants to join the traveling company. apelysen de auton legōn — but he sent him away saying — apolyō (to release, send away, dismiss) is a deliberate decision; Jesus does not grant the request to travel with him but issues an alternative commission.
8.39: Hypóstrephe eis ton oikon sou kai diēgou hosa epoiēsen soi ho Theos — return to your house and recount all that God has done for you — the man is commissioned as a witness in his own community. Diēgeomai (to narrate, recount in full) is Luke's historiographical verb (cf. 1:1; Acts 8:33; 9:27); the former demoniac is to give a full account. The commission to Gentile territory contrasts with the silence commands in Jewish contexts (4:41; 5:14; 8:56); the Gerasene region is the appropriate place for a Gentile witness. kai apēlthen kath holēn tēn polin kēryssōn hosa epoiēsen autō ho Iēsous — and he went through the whole city proclaiming what Jesus had done for him — he obeys and expands the commission; "what God did" becomes "what Jesus did" — a Christological equivalence Luke records without comment.
8.40: en de tō hypostrephein ton Iēsoun apedexato auton ho ochlos — when Jesus returned, the crowd welcomed him — the contrast is sharp: the Gerasenes expelled Jesus; the Galilean crowd welcomes him. Apodechomai (to welcome, receive with approval — a word for the proper reception of a guest or dignitary) indicates prepared expectation. ēsan gar pantes prosdokōntes auton — for they were all waiting for him — prosdokaō (to wait, expect, look for) indicates the crowd had remained in anticipation of his return. His absence had not dissipated their attention; it had intensified it.
8.41: kai idou ēlthen anēr ō onoma Iaeiros kai houtos archōn tēs synagōgēs hypērchen — a man came whose name was Jairus, and this man was a ruler of the synagogue — archōn tēs synagōgēs (ruler/head of the synagogue) was the lay administrator responsible for building upkeep, service order, and inviting readers and teachers; a position of social standing and communal trust. Jairus is named — a named supplicant in Luke whose petition we can track. kai pesōn para tous podas Iēsou parekalei auton eiselthein eis ton oikon autou — falling at Jesus's feet he begged him to come to his house — the posture of falling at the feet (pesōn para tous podas) is the posture of the leper (5:12), the demoniac (8:35), and later the grateful Samaritan leper (17:16).
8.42: hoti thygatēr monogenēs ēn autō hōs etōn dōdeka kai autē apethnēsken — because he had an only daughter of about twelve years and she was dying — monogenēs (only-born, only child) is Luke's distinctive term for the special grief of sole-child bereavement (cf. 7:12 the widow's son; 9:38 the epileptic boy — all three unique to Luke). Twelve years (hōs etōn dōdeka) is the age of Bar/Bat Mitzvah preparation — at the threshold of adult covenant membership. en de tō hypagein auton hoi ochloi synepnigon auton — as he was going, the crowds were pressing him — synpnigō (to press together, choke — the same verb as thorns choking the seed in v.7) describes the physical crush; the delay created by the bleeding woman is not coincidental to the intercalation structure.
8.43: kai gynē ousa en rhysei haimatos apo etōn dōdeka — a woman who had been in a flow of blood for twelve years — the twelve-year duration precisely mirrors Jairus's daughter's twelve-year age; Luke's intercalation pairs the two cases. Rhysis haimatos (flow of blood) would have rendered the woman ritually impure continuously under Levitical law (Lev 15:19-30; m. Niddah), excluding her from temple worship, communal meals, and physical contact with others. hētis iathrois prosanalōsasa holon ton bion ouk ischysen therapeuthenai apo oudenos — who had spent her whole livelihood on physicians and could not be healed by anyone — Luke's restraint: he omits Mark's detail that she grew worse under physicians' care (Mk 5:26), perhaps the restraint of a physician-author.
8.44: proselthous opisthen hēpsato tou kraspedou tou himatiou autou — coming up behind him she touched the edge/fringe of his garment — kraspedon (the edge, hem, fringe — in Jewish usage, the tzitzit/tassel mandated in Num 15:38-40 and Deut 22:12 as a visible reminder of Torah commandments). Touching the kraspedon may be deliberate contact with the symbol of covenant faithfulness; the healing flows through what represents Torah observance. kai parachrēma estē hē rhysis tou haimatos autēs — and immediately her flow of blood stopped — parachrēma (immediately) is Luke's signature word for instantaneous miraculous healing; estē (aorist: stopped, stood still) signals the cessation of a twelve-year hemorrhage in an instant.
8.45: kai eipen ho Iēsous, Tis ho hapsamenous mou — Jesus said: who touched me? — the question is not ignorance but pedagogical disclosure: Jesus draws the woman out for a public declaration of what happened and why. arnoumenōn de pantōn eipen ho Petros, Epistata, hoi ochloi synechousin se kai apothlibousin — when all denied it, Peter said: Master, the crowds are pressing and crushing you — Epistata (Master — Luke's title for Jesus in disciple-speech) and Peter's pragmatic observation capture the disciples' puzzlement: physical contact from dozens, yet Jesus asks who touched him. The distinction Jesus draws is between incidental contact and intentional faith-contact.
8.46: ho de Iēsous eipen, Hēpsato mou tis, egō gar egnōn dynamin exelēlythuian ap emou — Jesus said: someone touched me, for I knew power had gone out from me — Dynamin exelēlythuian ap emou (power having gone out from me — perfect participle indicating completed action with present result) is Luke's explanation of the mechanism: dynamis (power, Spirit-energy) flows from Jesus to the recipient. This is not automatic or impersonal — Jesus perceives the flow. The perfect participle emphasizes that the power transfer is already complete; the healing has already occurred before this conversation. Jesus does not identify the woman to shame her but to name the faith that triggered the healing.
8.47: idousa de hē gynē hoti ouk elathen, tremousa ēlthen kai prosesousa autō — the woman, seeing that she had not escaped notice, trembling came and fell before him — tremousa (trembling) is the bodily response to exposure before the holy; she expected, perhaps, to slip away unnoticed after the healing. Prosesousa autō (falling before him) is the posture of reverence before divine power. di hēn aitian hēpsato autou apēngeilen enōpion pantos tou laou kai hōs iathē parachrēma — she declared before all the people why she had touched him and how she was healed immediately — her public testimony is compelled; what had been a private act of faith becomes a public witness.
8.48: ho de eipen autē, Thygater, hē pistis sou sesōken se; poreuou eis eirēnēn — he said to her: Daughter, your faith has saved/healed you; go in peace — Thygater (Daughter) is a term of warm relational inclusion; Jesus addresses her as a member of the family of God, not merely a healed patient. Hē pistis sou sesōken se (your faith has saved/healed you — perfect active: has saved and the salvation remains) uses sōzō, Luke's comprehensive salvation term covering physical healing and spiritual wholeness; the same formula appears at 7:50 (the sinful woman), 17:19 (the Samaritan leper), and 18:42 (blind Bartimaeus). Poreuou eis eirēnēn (go into peace) is the Hebrew shalom dismissal, the state of complete wellbeing.
8.49: eti autou lalountos erchetai tis para tou archisyagōgou legōn hoti Tethnēken hē thygatēr sou — while he was still speaking, someone came from the house of the synagogue ruler saying: your daughter has died — eti autou lalountos (while he was still speaking) — the interruption arrives at the worst moment; the delay caused by the woman's healing has apparently cost the girl's life. Tethnēken (perfect: has died and is dead) is the final verdict. mēketi skyllve ton Didaskalon — do not trouble the Teacher any further — skyllō (to trouble, annoy, harass — originally to flay, then to pester) implies that the messenger considers the situation hopeless; there is no point in continuing to impose on Jesus.
8.50: ho de Iēsous akousas apekrithē autō, Mē phobou, monon pisteue kai sōthēsetai — Jesus hearing answered him: do not fear, only believe and she will be saved/healed — Mē phobou (do not fear) is the divine comfort formula (cf. 1:13,30; 2:10; 5:10); it counters the messenger's despair with the perspective of divine possibility. Monon pisteue (only believe) — the single requirement is trust. Sōthēsetai (future: she will be saved/healed) — Jesus promises salvation before the miracle; the parent is to trust the promise before seeing the evidence. The pattern mirrors Abraham's faith (Gen 15:6).
8.51: elthōn de eis tēn oikian ouk aphēken eiselthein tina syn autō ei mē Petron kai Iōannēn kai Iakōbon — arriving at the house he did not allow anyone to enter with him except Peter and John and James — the inner three disciples (the Petrine nucleus) are privileged witnesses; the same three will witness the Transfiguration (9:28) and, in Mark/Matthew, Gethsemane. kai ton patera tēs paidos kai tēn mētera — and the child's father and mother — the parents are necessary: they are the primary witnesses of their daughter's death and restoration. The crowd of mourners is excluded; the witnesses are carefully selected.
8.52: eklaion de pantes kai ekoptonto autēn — all were weeping and mourning her — koptō (to beat, strike — reflexively to beat one's chest in mourning) is the formal mourning gesture (cf. 23:27,48; Rev 1:7). ho de eipen, Mē klaete, ou gar apethanen alla katheudei — he said: do not weep, for she has not died but is sleeping — katheudō (to sleep) is the euphemism for death in Jewish usage (cf. 1 Thess 4:13-14; 1 Cor 15:6,18,20,51); Jesus uses the metaphor prospectively — she will wake — but the mourners interpret it as denial of the obvious fact. The statement is simultaneously a medical judgment (she is in a death-like state) and a theological declaration (death in the presence of the Life-giver is sleep).
8.53: kai kategelōn autou eidotes hoti apethanen — they were laughing at him, knowing that she had died — katagelō (to laugh at, mock, deride) is the mourners' reaction to what they take as Jesus's absurd denial. Eidotes hoti apethanen (knowing that she had died — perfect: she has died and remains dead) — the mourners' certainty becomes the foil against which the resurrection is measured. Their professional knowledge of death is about to be overturned. The laughter is not condemned by Luke; it is the inevitable response of those who measure reality by natural categories.
8.54: autos de kratēsas tēs cheiros autēs ephōnēsen legōn, Hē pais, egeire — he took her hand and called out saying: Child, get up! — kratēsas tēs cheiros (taking hold of the hand) is direct physical contact with a corpse — ritually defiling (Num 19:11-16); Jesus touches what is impure and instead of being contaminated, imparts life. Ephōnēsen (called out, cried out — aorist of summoning) — not a whisper but a public summons, as Elijah and Elisha prayed over dead children (1 Kgs 17:21; 2 Kgs 4:34). Hē pais, egeire (Child, rise up!) — the diminutive address and the simple command capture both tenderness and authority.
8.55: kai epestrepsen to pneuma autēs kai anestē parachrēma — her spirit returned and she stood up immediately — Epestrepsen to pneuma autēs (her spirit returned) — Luke's account of the mechanism: the pneuma (spirit, breath of life) is what God breathed into Adam (Gen 2:7) and what departs at death (Eccles 12:7; Ps 104:29-30); its return is the reversal of death. Parachrēma (immediately) is Luke's signature word for instantaneous divine action. kai diataxen autē dothēnai phagein — he commanded that something be given her to eat — the practical instruction verifies the reality of the restoration: a ghost does not eat; a truly revived child needs nourishment.
8.56: kai existēsan hoi goneis autēs — her parents were astonished — existēmi (to be beside oneself with astonishment, to lose one's composure) is the strongest Greek amazement word. ho de parēngeilen autois mēdeni eipein to gegenōmenon — he commanded them to tell no one what had happened — the silence command applied to a resurrection: the contrast with the Gerasene demoniac (sent home to proclaim) is deliberate. In Jewish territory Jesus constrains proclamation (4:41; 5:14; 8:56); in Gentile territory he commissions it (8:39). The parents' astonishment, the restoration of a twelve-year-old girl, and the command of silence close the double-miracle intercalation that began with another twelve-year duration.

11.1: kai egeneto en tō einai auton en topō tini proseuchomenon — one day Jesus was praying in a certain place. The prayer-context is Luke’s consistent framing for moments of teaching on prayer; Jesus prays before teaching about prayer (cf. 3:21; 6:12; 9:18,28). The disciple’s request (kyrie, didaxon hēmas proseuchesthai) echoes John’s teaching of his disciples; the request implies a distinctive prayer practice is part of what defines a rabbi’s school. The Lord’s Prayer that follows is shorter in Luke than in Matthew (Matt 6:9-13), without the doxology.
11.2: hotan proseuchēsthe legete: pater, hagiasthētō to onoma sou, elthetō hē basileia sou — when you pray, say: Father, hallowed be your name, your kingdom come. Luke’s version omits Matthew’s hēmōn ho en tois ouranois (our Father in heaven) — simply Pater (Father), the intimate Aramaic Abba. The two kingdom-oriented petitions (hagiasthētō to onoma, let your name be sanctified; elthetō hē basileia, let your kingdom come) are aorist imperatives — definitive, eschatological acts petitioned. Luke’s version also omits Matthew’s “your will be done on earth as in heaven,” though this was likely part of the original prayer.
11.3: ton arton hēmōn ton epiousion didou hēmin to kath hēmeran — give us each day our daily bread. Epiousion is the most debated single word in the New Testament — it appears only here and in Matthew 6:11 and in a 5th-century papyrus. Three main interpretations: (1) for today (epi tēn ousan hēmeran); (2) for tomorrow (epiousa = the coming day); (3) necessary for existence (from epi + ousia = for subsistence). Jerome’s Latin renders it supersubstantialis (supersubstantial) in Matthew but quotidianum (daily) in Luke. The Eucharistic interpretation (the bread from above) has early patristic support (Origen). The MKT “daily” captures the temporal sense; the spiritual surplus resonates throughout the prayer’s eschatological horizon.
11.4: kai aphes hēmin tas hamartias hēmōn, kai gar autoi aphiomen panti opheilonti hēmin — forgive us our sins, for we also forgive everyone who sins against us. Luke uses hamartias (sins) where Matthew uses opheilēmata (debts — the original Aramaic hobayn which means both debts and sins). Luke explains the human forgiveness (aphiomen) with kai gar (for indeed, because) — the human practice of forgiving is offered as a ground for divine forgiveness; or more likely, it is the evidence of the divine forgiveness already received. The verb tense in Luke is present (aphiomen = we forgive, ongoing) vs. Matthew’s aorist (we have forgiven).
11.5: kai eipen pros autous: tis ex hymōn hexei philon kai poreusetai pros auton mesonyktiou — then Jesus said to them: Suppose you have a friend and you go to him at midnight. The Friend at Midnight parable (unique to Luke) is an a fortiori argument from the lesser to the greater: if a reluctant human friend eventually responds to persistent asking, how much more will the willing heavenly Father respond? The midnight timing (mesonyktiou) represents the most inconvenient possible moment; the request for three loaves (treis artous) is for guests who have arrived unexpectedly and must be hosted.
11.6: epeidē philos mou paredeto ex hodou pros me kai ouk echō ho parathēsō autō — a friend of mine on a journey has come to me, and I have nothing to set before him. The social obligation of hospitality in Mediterranean culture was binding and honor-laden: to have a guest arrive and not be able to offer food was a serious social failure. Ouk echō ho parathēsō autō (I have nothing to set before him) — the needy person is asking not for himself but for a third party; the intercession structure (asking on behalf of another) models the pattern of prayer the chapter is teaching.
11.7: ho de esōthen apokritheis eipē: mē moi kopous pareche; ēdē hē thyra kekleistai kai ta paidia mou met emou eis tēn koitēn eisin — the one inside answers: Don’t bother me. The door is already locked, and my children and I are in bed. The first-century peasant house had one room; the whole family slept together on a raised platform. Kekleistai (has been shut, perfect tense — firmly, already closed) and the children already in bed make the request maximally inconvenient. The householder’s reluctance is genuine, not theatrical; the parable requires a genuinely reluctant figure for the a fortiori argument to work.
11.8: dia ge tēn anaideian autou egertheis dōsei autō hosōn chrēzei — because of his shameless audacity he will surely get up and give him as much as he needs. Anaideia (shamelessness, boldness, impudence — a hapax in the NT) is the key word: the persistence that overcomes reluctance. In the honor-shame framework, to keep knocking after being refused once is to risk social humiliation — the asker’s need overcomes his honor-calculation. Some interpreters read anaideia as the householder’s avoidance of shame (getting up to prevent the embarrassment of the neighbor’s continued banging), but the grammatical subject is the asker. Shameless persistence is the parable’s model for prayer.
11.9: kagō hymin legō: aiteite kai dothēsetai hymin, zēteite kai heurēsete, krouete kai anoigēsetai hymin — I say to you: ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you. The three verbs in ascending order of intensity: aiteite (ask, request), zēteite (seek, search actively), krouete (knock, persistent insistent action). All three are present imperatives — continuous, ongoing actions. The divine passive in the responses (dothēsetai, anoigēsetai) identifies God as the giver and opener. The logic is the parable’s conclusion applied universally: what the midnight asking secured from a reluctant friend, the asking-seeking-knocking secures from the willing Father.
11.10: pas gar ho aitōn lambanei kai ho zētōn heuriskei kai tō krouonti anoigēsetai — for everyone who asks receives; the one who seeks finds; and to the one who knocks, the door will be opened. The universal promise (pas = everyone) is stated as a present-tense axiomatic truth: the asking-and-receiving, seeking-and-finding, knocking-and-opening are presented as reliable correspondences, not occasional outcomes. The three pairs form a complete hermeneutic of prayer: it is asking-based, searching-active, and door-oriented (expectant of access to the divine presence).
11.11: tina de ex hymōn ton patera aitēsei ho huios ichthyn kai anti ichthyos ophin autō epidōsei — which of you fathers, if your son asks for a fish, will give him a snake instead? The argument from normal parental love to divine giving employs the fish-snake and egg-scorpion contrasts (Luke’s version differs slightly from Matthew’s). The fish and snake, the egg and scorpion, are similar in external appearance (the serpent might be mistaken for a fish; the scorpion egg-shaped); the parable exploits the similarity to underscore the contrast. A father would never exploit the similarity to harm his child.
11.12: ē kai aitēsei ōon kai epidōsei autō skorpion — or if he asks for an egg, will give him a scorpion? The egg-scorpion comparison is Luke’s unique addition to the fish-snake pair. Epidōsei (will give in response, will hand over to) — the verb emphasizes the deliberateness of the giving; no father would deliberately substitute a harmful creature for a nourishing one. The a fortiori argument prepares for v.13: if imperfect, sinful fathers give good gifts, the perfect Father gives infinitely better.
11.13: ei oun hymeis ponēroi hyparchontes oidate domata agatha didonai tois teknois hymōn, posō mallon ho patēr ex ouranou dōsei pneuma hagion tois aitousin auton — if you then, though you are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him! Luke’s climax differs from Matthew’s (“good things”): here the supreme gift is pneuma hagion (the Holy Spirit). Luke’s Pentecostal theology shapes his version of the prayer-chapter: the ultimate answer to prayer, the best gift the Father can give, is the Holy Spirit — the fulfilment of the kingdom’s promise (Acts 1:4-5; 2:38). Posō mallon (how much more — the standard kal vaḥomer intensifier) makes the gift immeasurably greater than any human father’s.
11.14: kai ēn ekballōn daimonion, kai auto ēn kōphon — Jesus was driving out a demon that was mute. The exorcism (unique to Luke and Matthew in this form; different from Mark’s setting) produces speech in one who was mute (kōphos, deaf-mute). The crowd’s amazement (ethaumasan hoi ochloi) is the expected response; the divided responses (accusation of Beelzebul, demand for a sign from heaven) that follow represent the opposite of amazement — hostile re-categorization of the miracle as demonic power.
11.15: tines de ex autōn eipon: en Beelzeboul tō archonti tōn daimoniōn ekballei ta daimonia — some of them said: By Beelzebul, the prince of demons, he is driving out demons. Beelzeboul — the name derives from “lord of the house” (ba’al zebul) or “lord of dung/flies” (ba’al zebub, 2 Kgs 1:2); the latter is a contemptuous distortion of the Philistine god’s name. The accusation is the most serious possible: attributing Jesus’s exorcistic power not to God but to the chief of demons. This is the context of the Blasphemy against the Holy Spirit saying (12:10).
11.16: heteroi de peiraszontes sēmeion ex ouranou ezētoun par autou — others tested him by asking for a sign from heaven. Peiraszontes (testing — the same root as the Devil’s temptation in 4:2, peirazomenos) — the demand for a sign is hostile, not genuinely seeking. Sēmeion ex ouranou (a sign from heaven, a celestial sign) — the demand for a spectacular supernatural proof, over and above the exorcism just performed, is a refusal to accept the present evidence. Jesus will respond with the sign of Jonah (v.29): the only sign that will be given is the sign of a death and resurrection.
11.17: autos de eidōs autōn ta dianoēmata eipen autois — Jesus knew their thoughts and said to them. Again Luke notes Jesus’s knowledge of unspoken thoughts (cf. 6:8; 9:47) — a recurring Lukan marker of divine perceptiveness. The house-divided argument that follows is a logical reductio: if Satan is expelling his own demons, he is destroying his own kingdom. The argument assumes that Satan’s kingdom is a coherent organized domain, not a chaos; its internal consistency is exactly what the divided-house scenario would undermine.
11.18: ei de kai ho Satanas eph heauton diemeristhē, pōs stathēsetai hē basileia autou — if Satan is divided against himself, how can his kingdom stand? Ho Satanas (the Adversary, the Accuser — the Hebrew śāṭān as a proper name for the cosmic opponent) has a kingdom (basileia) — the rival ordering of reality under the power of darkness. Jesus’s logic: internal division produces collapse; if the exorcisms were demonic, Satan is self-destructing. The parallel structure of Satan’s kingdom and God’s kingdom is implicit throughout; the exorcisms represent territorial conflict at the cosmic level.
11.19: ei de egō en Beelzeboul ekballō ta daimonia, hoi huioi hymōn en tini ekballousin — now if I drive out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your followers drive them out? Jewish exorcists practiced exorcism within the covenant community (Acts 19:13-14 shows the practice). Dia touto autoi hymōn kritai esontai (so then, they will be your judges) — Jesus turns the accusation’s logic against the accusers: if they accept Jewish exorcism as legitimate, they cannot categorize Jesus’s exorcisms as demonic without condemning their own community’s practice.
11.20: ei de en daktylō theou ekballō ta daimonia, ara ephthasen eph hymas hē basileia tou theou — but if I drive out demons by the finger of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you. Daktylō theou (finger of God) — where Matthew reads “Spirit of God” (Matt 12:28), Luke uses “finger of God” — the LXX phrase used by Pharaoh’s magicians when they recognized the plagues as divine action (Exod 8:19) and for the writing of the Ten Commandments (Exod 31:18; Deut 9:10). The Exodus resonance is deliberate: the exorcisms are the new plagues, liberating the captives of the new Pharaoh (Satan). Ephthasen (has arrived, come upon — aorist of anticipatory arrival) is Luke’s strongest “realized eschatology” statement.
11.21: hotan ho ischyros kathōplismenos phylassē tēn heautou aulēn — when a strong man, fully armed, guards his own house, his possessions are safe. The strong man parable (ho ischyros, the strong/mighty one) depicts Satan as an armed householder guarding his domain. Kathōplismenos (fully armed, equipped with full armor — a military term) and ta huparchonta autou en eirēnē estin (his possessions are in peace) — the ironic use of eirēnē (peace) for the security of Satan’s domain. The exorcisms are not Satan expelling his own forces but the stronger one (ho ischyroteros) overpowering the strong one.
11.22: epan de ho ischyroteros autou epelthōn nikēsē auton — but when someone stronger attacks and overpowers him. Ho ischyroteros (the stronger one) — the comparative deliberately echoes John the Baptist’s announcement (3:16 — “one more powerful than I is coming”). Jesus is the stronger one who has come to overpower the strong man. Tēn panoplian autou airei (he takes away his full armor) — panoplia (full armor, the same term in Eph 6:11,13 for the Christian’s armor) — the plundering of the strong man’s armor represents the dismantling of Satan’s capacity to defend his domain. The exorcisms are armor-stripping operations.
11.23: ho mē ōn met emou kat emou estin, kai ho mē synagōn met emou skorporizei — whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters. The binary of gathering and scattering frames the exorcism controversy’s conclusion: there is no neutral ground in the cosmic conflict. Met emou / kat emou (with me / against me) — the prepositions are the only difference; neutrality is logically impossible in a spiritual conflict where inaction is itself an act. The gathering-scattering pair is harvest imagery: the eschatological harvest demands workers who gather; those who do not gather by definition scatter the harvest.
11.24: hotan to akatharton pneuma exelthē apo tou anthrōpou, dierchetai di anydrou topōn zētoyn anapausin — when an impure spirit comes out of a person, it goes through waterless places seeking rest. The exorcism’s sequel-warning: an expelled demon wanders through arid/waterless places (anydrous topous) — the desert as demonic space (cf. Isa 34:14; Bar 4:35; Rev 18:2). The failure to find rest (anapausin) drives the return to the former host. The warning context (following the Beelzebul controversy) applies this to Israel: the generation that has witnessed exorcisms but refuses to respond is in danger of a worse possession than before.
11.25: kai elthon heuriskei sesarōmenon kai kekosmēmenon — when it arrives, it finds the house swept clean and put in order. Sesarōmenon (swept) and kekosmēmenon (put in order, adorned) — both perfect participles indicating a completed state. The house is emptied of evil but not filled with good; reformation without regeneration leaves a vacuum. The clean-but-empty house is more vulnerable than an occupied house: the demon can reenter without resistance. The parable applies to religious reform that stops at external correction without the Spirit’s indwelling.
11.26: tote poreuetai kai paralambanei hetera pneumata ponērotera heautou hepta — then it goes and takes seven other spirits more wicked than itself. Seven spirits (the number of completeness) — the final state is a sevenfold intensification of the first. Hē eschate tēs anthrōpou ekeinēs ginetai cheirōn tēs prōtēs (the final condition of that person is worse than the first) — the warning applies to “this evil generation” (v.29): having experienced the liberating power of Jesus’s ministry and rejected it, the spiritual vacancy leaves them susceptible to a worse condition than before Jesus came.
11.27: egeneto de en tō legein auton tauta eparasa phōnēn gynē tis ek tou ochlou eipen autō: makaria hē koilia hē bastasasa se — as Jesus was saying these things, a woman in the crowd called out: Blessed is the womb that bore you. The woman’s beatitude is a maternal blessing — praising Jesus by praising the physical maternity that produced him. Makaria hē koilia (blessed the womb) and hoi mastoi hous ethēlasas (the breasts you nursed from) — the specifically physical blessing is not rejected by Jesus but transcended: the true blessedness is not physical proximity to Jesus but covenant response to his word.
11.28: autos de eipen: menoun makarioi hoi akouontes ton logon tou theou kai phylassontes — he replied: Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and obey it. Menoun (rather, on the contrary — a corrective particle that does not negate but reorients) — Jesus does not deny his mother’s blessing but redirects it to a greater beatitude. The hearing-and-keeping (akouontes... kai phylassontes) formulation echoes the Shema structure (hear and keep) and Deuteronomy’s covenant pattern. The biological connection to Jesus is surpassed by the covenant connection to his word; Mary’s truest beatitude is her hearing and obeying (1:38), not her biological maternity.
11.29: tōn de ochlōn epathroizomenōn ērxato legein: hē genea hautē genea ponēra estin; sēmeion zētei, kai sēmeion ou dothēsetai autē ei mē to sēmeion Iōna — this is a wicked generation. It asks for a sign, but none will be given it except the sign of Jonah. Hē genea hautē (this generation) — Luke’s characteristic judgment-target. Sēmeion Iōna (the sign of Jonah) — in Matthew, Jesus explains this as Jonah’s three days in the fish = the Son of Man’s three days in the earth. Luke’s version moves differently: the comparison is to Jonah as a sign to the Ninevites (v.30 — Jonah himself was the sign, not the fish-episode). The preaching that produced Nineveh’s repentance is the parallel to Jesus’s preaching to this generation.
11.30: kathōs gar egeneto Iōnas tois Nineuītais sēmeion, houtōs estai kai ho huios tou anthrōpou tē genea tautē — for as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so also will the Son of Man be to this generation. Luke’s Jonah-comparison focuses on the prophetic preacher as sign: Jonah’s arrival in Nineveh (a man from the sea, changed by the experience) was itself the sign that moved the city to repentance. Jesus as the Son of Man — the Danielic figure of cosmic authority — is the sign to this generation; his presence, his words, his miracles, and ultimately his death and resurrection are the sign. The generation that fails to respond condemns itself.
11.31: basilissa notou egerthēsetai en tē krisei meta tōn andrōn tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinei autous — the Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with the people of this generation and condemn them. The Queen of Sheba (1 Kgs 10:1-13) traveled from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon’s wisdom. Idou pleion Solomōnos hōde (and now something greater than Solomon is here) — the comparative pleion (greater, more) is neuter, not masculine: the greater thing is the kingdom and its wisdom, which is present in Jesus. The Gentile queen’s response to lesser wisdom condemns a generation that refuses greater wisdom.
11.32: andres Nineuī anastēsontai en tē krisei meta tēs geneas tautēs kai katakrinousin autēn — the men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it. The Ninevites repented at Jonah’s preaching — a single prophet’s message with no miracles, no royal authority. Idou pleion Iōna hōde (and now something greater than Jonah is here) — the double pleion (greater than Solomon, greater than Jonah) brackets the two OT comparison-figures. If Nineveh repented at less and Jerusalem refuses more, the comparison is self-condemning. Judgment by the Gentiles who responded is the inversion the wicked generation deserves.
11.33: oudeis lychon hapsas eis kryptēn tithēsin — no one lights a lamp and puts it in a place where it will be hidden. The lamp-on-the-stand saying follows the sign-of-Jonah discourse: the light of Jesus’s ministry has not been hidden but publicly proclaimed. Hina hoi eisporeuomenoi to phōs blepōsin (so that those who come in may see the light) — the lamp is for the benefit of those entering the space. The application to Jesus: his ministry is the lamp that the householder has placed on the stand; the question is whether the entering generation has eyes to see it.
11.34: ho lychnos tou sōmatos estin ho ophthalmos sou; hotan ho ophthalmos sou haplous ē, kai holon to sōma sou phōteinon estin — your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eyes are healthy, your whole body also is full of light. Haplous (single, healthy, generous — the word means both “single/simple” and “sound/healthy”; in the Semitic tradition, the “good eye” means generosity and the “evil eye” means miserliness) and ponēros (evil, sick, unhealthy eye). The spiritual interpretation: the eye as the organ of moral perception determines whether the whole person is illuminated by the light of the kingdom or darkened. The Beelzebul accusers have a diseased eye — they see the works of God and categorize them as demonic.
11.35: skopei oun mē to phōs to en soi skotos estin — see to it, then, that the light within you is not darkness. The self-examination command (skopei, watch yourself, take heed) is urgent: the danger is not obvious external darkness but internal darkness masquerading as light. The paradox of “the light within you is darkness” applies to those who have religious certainty (the Pharisees) but whose moral perception is inverted: they see demonic in divine action and divine in their own tradition. Self-examination is required precisely by those who are most confident of their light.
11.36: ei oun to sōma sou holon phōteinon, mē echon meros ti skoteinon, estai holōs phōteinon hōs hotan ho lychnos tē astrapē phōtizē se — if your whole body is full of light, no part of it dark, it will be just as full of light as when a lamp shines its light on you. The positive conclusion: the body fully illuminated is like the moment when a lamp floods the room with complete brightness. The eschatological implication: full illumination is the kingdom’s telos — the comprehensive light that leaves no dark corners. The lamp metaphor closes with the promise of total illumination for those whose eye is haplous.
11.37: en de tō lalēsai erōta auton pharisaios hopos aristēsē par autō — when Jesus had finished speaking, a Pharisee invited him to eat with him. The second dinner-with-a-Pharisee scene (cf. 7:36-50) provides the setting for the woe-oracles. Luke’s structuring of the woes within a meal-setting is socially pointed: the Pharisee invites Jesus to his table; Jesus responds with a public critique. Eiselthōn de anepesen (he went in and reclined) — Jesus accepts the invitation without social ceremony; the lack of pre-meal hand-washing that follows is the immediate provocation.
11.38: ho de Pharisaios idōn ethaumasen hoti ou prōton ebaptisthē pro tou aristou — the Pharisee was surprised that Jesus did not first wash before the meal. Ebaptisthē (was baptized, ritually immersed) — the pre-meal immersion washing (not merely hand-washing but a full ritual bath for some Pharisaic schools) was a purity practice going beyond Torah requirements; it was a “tradition of the elders” (cf. Mark 7:3-4). Jesus’s omission is deliberate: the body-cleaning without heart-cleaning is precisely what his following critique addresses.
11.39: eipen de ho kyrios pros auton: nyn hymeis hoi pharisaioi to exōthen tou potēriou kai tou pinakos katharizete, to de esōthen hymōn gemei harpagēs kai ponērias — the Lord said: Now you Pharisees clean the outside of the cup and dish, but inside you are full of greed and wickedness. The cup-and-dish metaphor makes the critique concrete: the scrupulous cleaning of vessels (to exōthen, the outside) while the inner person (to de esōthen hymōn) is full of harpagē (greed, extortion — the word for seizing property unlawfully) and ponēria (wickedness, evil). The Pharisaic purity system has become an external ritual that conceals and perhaps enables internal moral corruption.
11.40: aphrones, ouch ho poiēsas to exōthen kai to esōthen epoiēsen — you foolish people! Did not the one who made the outside make the inside also? Aphrones (fools, those without wisdom — a strong address) — the argument from creation: the Creator God who made the exterior of the vessel also made the interior; a theology that attends to the exterior without the interior has missed the Creator’s design. The rhetorical question expects the answer “yes”: of course the Creator made both — which means both are accountable to him.
11.41: plēn ta enonta dote eleēmosynēn, kai idou panta kathara hymin estin — but now as for what is inside you — be generous to the poor, and everything will be clean for you. The solution to the inside-outside purity problem is not additional external ritual but generosity (eleēmosynēn, almsgiving). Ta enonta (what is inside, what you have within/at hand) — use what you possess for the poor. Luke’s version of the cleanness-teaching makes generosity the measure of purity: the cleaned inside manifests as open-handed giving. The purity system is redirected from ritual compliance to moral transformation expressed in economic sharing.
11.42: alla ouai hymin tois Pharisaiois, hoti apodekateute to hēdyosmon kai to pēganon kai pan lachanon — woe to you Pharisees, because you give God a tenth of your mint, rue and all other kinds of garden herbs, but you neglect justice and the love of God. Apodekateute (you tithe) — the tithe of garden herbs extended the Torah’s grain-oil-wine tithe (Deut 14:22-23) to the level of individual stalks of herbs; the meticulous attention to micro-tithing. Parerchesthe tēn krisin kai tēn agapēn tou theou (you pass over justice and the love of God) — krisis (justice, judgment) and agapē tou theou (the love of God — either love toward God or God’s own love enacted through them) are the weightier matters that the herb-tithe has displaced. Luke reads “love of God” where Matthew reads “mercy and faithfulness” (Matt 23:23).
11.43: ouai hymin tois Pharisaiois, hoti agapate tēn prōtokathedrian en tais synagōgais kai tous aspasmous en tais agorais — woe to you Pharisees, because you love the most important seats in the synagogues and respectful greetings in the market-places. Prōtokathedria (first seat, seat of honor at the front of the synagogue) and aspasmoi en tais agorais (greetings in the market-places — the ceremonial public greetings of honor expected by religious authorities). The love of public status (agapate) inverts the love-command: the Pharisees who are commanded to love God and neighbor have channeled their love into loving public honor. Status-seeking has replaced covenant-love.
11.44: ouai hymin, hoti este hōs ta mnēmeia ta adēla kai hoi anthrōpoi hoi peripatountes epanō ouk oisin — woe to you, because you are like unmarked graves, which people walk over without knowing it. Ta mnēmeia ta adēla (unmarked graves, invisible tombs) — in Jewish law, contact with a grave or tomb caused ritual impurity; unmarked graves were especially dangerous because travelers could unknowingly walk over them. The Pharisees are hidden sources of defilement: their outward respectability (adēla, invisible, undetected) conceals the contagion within. Those who come into contact with them — their students, their communities — are unknowingly defiled. Matthew’s parallel reads “whitewashed tombs” (Matt 23:27); Luke’s unmarked graves emphasize the hidden rather than the hypocritical.
11.45: apokritheis de tis tōn nomikōn legei autō: didaskale, tauta legōn kai hēmas hybrizeis — one of the experts in the law answered him: Teacher, when you say these things, you insult us also. The legal scholars (nomikoi, experts in the Torah — distinct from Pharisees as a group, though related) recognize that the critique includes them. Hybrizeis (you insult, treat with contempt — the verb of violent public dishonor) — the scholar’s protest that the woes constitute social humiliation. Jesus’s response is to add three more woes specifically for the lawyers.
11.46: ho de eipen: kai hymin tois nomikois ouai, hoti phortizete tous anthrōpous phortia dysbastakata — and you experts in the law, woe to you, because you load people down with burdens they can hardly carry. Phortia dysbastakata (burdens that are hard to bear, difficult loads) — the halakhic elaborations that the legal scholars had developed (the “fence around the Torah”) had become oppressive weight on ordinary people who could not keep all the interpretive rulings. Autoi de heni tōn daktylōn hymōn ou prospsauete tois fortiois (you yourselves do not touch the loads with one of your fingers) — they impose but do not carry; the scholars are exempt from the practical burdens they assign to others.
11.47: ouai hymin, hoti oikodomeite ta mnēmeia tōn prophētōn, hoi de pateres hymōn apekteinan autous — woe to you, because you build tombs for the prophets, and it was your ancestors who killed them. The prophet-tomb building is on its surface an act of honor; Jesus’s indictment exposes the irony: building tombs for the prophets one’s ancestors killed is not repentance but monument-making. The descendants inherit the ancestors’ violence while honoring its victims in stone — a gesture that serves the present generation’s self-image rather than repairing the injustice.
11.48: ara martyres este kai syneudokeite tois ergois tōn paterōn hymōn, hoti autoi men apekteinan autous hymeis de oikodomeite — so you testify that you approve of what your ancestors did; they killed the prophets and you build their tombs. The indictment uses the witnesses’ own action as testimony against them: the tomb-building is taken as evidence of approval (syneudokeite, you agree with, endorse), not honor. The generation that is about to kill Jesus completes the pattern: rejecting the living prophet while honoring the dead ones is the consistent behavior of the prophetic-rejection tradition.
11.49: dia touto kai hē sophia tou theou eipen: apostelō eis autous prophētas kai apostolous, kai ex autōn apoktenousin kai diōxousin — because of this, God in his wisdom said: I will send them prophets and apostles, some of whom they will kill and others they will persecute. Hē sophia tou theou (the wisdom of God) — a personified divine speech (not a quotation from a specific text; possibly an apocryphal source or a free composite); Jesus may be identifying himself as divine Wisdom speaking here. The sending of prophets and apostles who will be killed and persecuted is both prediction and indictment: the divine patience in sending successive messengers makes the rejection increasingly culpable.
11.50: hina ekzētēthē to haima pantōn tōn prophētōn to ekkechymenon apo katabolēs kosmou apo tēs geneas tautēs — therefore this generation will be held responsible for the blood of all the prophets that has been shed since the beginning of the world. The “blood of all the prophets” is the cumulative debt of the rejection-and-killing tradition; “since the beginning of the world” (apo katabolēs kosmou) frames the entire span of prophetic history. The generation that kills Jesus becomes the culminating instance — the generation in which the full weight of the accumulated guilt falls because the ultimate messenger has been rejected.
11.51: apo haimatos Abel heōs haimatos Zachariou tou apolomenou metaxy tou thysiastēriou kai tou oikou — from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who was killed between the altar and the sanctuary. Abel (Gen 4) and Zechariah (2 Chr 24:20-21) are the first and last murder victims in the Hebrew canonical order (Genesis to Chronicles). The bookending of canonical history by two murders creates a comprehensive indictment: the history of prophetic rejection spans the entire Bible. Nai legō hymin, ekzētēthēsetai apo tēs geneas tautēs (yes I tell you, it will be required of this generation) — the generation witnessing Jesus is the terminus of the accumulated blood-guilt.
11.52: ouai hymin tois nomikois, hoti ērate tēn kleida tēs gnōseōs — woe to you experts in the law, because you have taken away the key to knowledge. Tēn kleida tēs gnōseōs (the key of knowledge) — the legal scholars possess the interpretive key to the Torah that should unlock the way to God; instead, they have locked the door. Autoi ouk eisēlthate kai tous eiserchomenous ekōlysate (you yourselves have not entered, and you have hindered those who were entering) — the scholars neither benefit from the key themselves nor allow others to benefit. Matthew’s parallel (23:13) speaks of shutting the kingdom of heaven in people’s faces; Luke’s “key of knowledge” emphasizes the epistemological function of the scribal tradition that has been weaponized against access to God.
11.53: kakeithen exelthontos autou ērxanto hoi grammateis kai hoi pharisaioi deinōs enechein kai apostomatizein auton peri pleionōn — when Jesus went outside, the Pharisees and teachers of the law began to oppose him fiercely and to besiege him with questions. Deinōs enechein (fiercely resenting, holding a deep grudge — the compound of deinōs terrible/fierce and enechein to hold/be seized by) conveys the intensity of the religious establishment’s response to the woes. Apostomatizein (to interrogate point by point, to question systematically — possibly to make him speak off the top of his head in order to catch him) is a forensic term: the opponents are constructing a case.
11.54: enedreouontes auton thēreusai ti ek tou stomatos autou — waiting to catch him in something he might say. Enedreouontes (lying in wait, setting an ambush — the ambush vocabulary of military or hunting) — the Pharisees and lawyers are now formally in the opposition role. Thēreusai (to hunt, to catch in a trap) is the hunter’s term applied to entrapment through speech. The chapter ends with the religious establishment in open, organized opposition — the trajectory toward the passion is now clearly established.

12.1: En hois episynachtheisōn tōn myriasōn tou ochlou — while ten-thousands of the crowd had gathered — myrias (myriad, ten thousand — often used hyperbolically for an uncountable crowd) marks the mass context for the following private instruction to disciples. ērxato legein pros tous mathētas autou prōton — he began to say to his disciples first — the audience distinction is structural: vv.1-12 address disciples privately; vv.13ff address the crowd. Prosexete heautois apo tēs zymēs, hētis estin hypokrisis, tōn Pharisaiōn — guard yourselves from the yeast, which is hypocrisy, of the Pharisees — zymē (yeast, leaven — a small agent that pervades the whole) is Paul's metaphor for sin in 1 Cor 5:6-8; Gal 5:9. Luke defines the yeast explicitly: hypokrisis (hypocrisy, playing a role, mask-wearing — the actor's term) is the gap between public presentation and inner reality.
12.2: ouden de synkekalymmenon estin ho ouk apokalyphthēsetai — nothing has been covered that will not be uncovered — the aphorism (cf. 8:17) grounds the call to genuine rather than performed religion: ultimate disclosure makes hypocrisy futile. Synkalyptō (to cover over, conceal thoroughly) and apokalyptō (to uncover, reveal — the apocalypsis root) are the antonyms of concealment and disclosure. kai krypton ho ou gnōsthēsetai — and hidden what will not be known — the passive futures (apokalyphthēsetai, gnōsthēsetai) are divine passives: God is the one who will disclose and make known. The eschatological disclosure renders all present concealment temporary.
12.3: anth hōn hosa en tē skotia eipate en tō phōti akousthēsetai — therefore what you have said in the dark will be heard in the light — the spatial metaphors (dark/light) and acoustic metaphors (whispered/proclaimed) illustrate the principle of v.2. En tois tameious (in the inner rooms, store-rooms — private chambers without windows) — the most private location imaginable. kai ho pros to ous elalesate en tois tameois kērychthēsetai epi tōn dōmatōn — what you whispered in the ear in the inner rooms will be proclaimed on the rooftops — the flat Palestinian rooftop was the public announcement platform; the herald's position. What was whispered privately will be heralded publicly — the complete reversal of secrecy.
12.4: Legō de hymin tois philois mou — I say to you, my friends — philois (friends — a term of close relational warmth, used by Jesus for the disciples in John 15:13-15) personalizes the following command. Mē phobēthēte apo tōn apokteinontōn to sōma kai meta tauta mē echontōn perissoteron ti poiēsai — do not fear those who kill the body and after that have nothing more they can do — the limit of human violence is the body; death exhausts the persecutor's power. The grammar is emphatic: mē echontōn perissoteron ti poiēsai (having nothing more, nothing exceeding, to do) — after death, their power is spent.
12.5: hypo deixō de hymin tina phobēthēte — but I will show you whom to fear — the contrast is sharp: the one to fear is not the executioner but the Judge. phobēthēte ton meta to apokteinai echonta exousian embalein eis tēn geennan — fear the one who after killing has authority to throw into Gehenna — geenna is the Greek form of Ge-Hinnom (Valley of Hinnom), the ravine south of Jerusalem where child sacrifice occurred (Jer 7:31; 2 Chr 28:3; 33:6) and later became the city's refuse dump; in Second Temple Jewish thought it became the metaphor for final divine judgment. Exousia embalein eis tēn geennan — authority to throw into Gehenna — the one who has this post-mortem authority is God. nai, legō hymin, touton phobēthēte — yes, I tell you, fear this one.
12.6: Ouchi pente strouthia pōlountai assariōn dyo — are not five sparrows sold for two assaria? — strouthion (sparrow — the cheapest available bird in the market; Mt 10:29 gives two for one assarion, but Luke's five for two assaria is a bulk-discount pricing: buy two get one free). Assarion is a small copper Roman coin, 1/16 of a denarius — the bare minimum of purchasing power. kai hen ex autōn ouk estin epilelēsmemon enōpion tou theou — yet not one of them is forgotten before God — epilanthanō (to forget, overlook — perfect passive: has been and remains forgotten) with the negation: God's attention to the sparrow is permanent and complete. The argument is a fortiori: if God holds sparrows in continuous awareness, how much more his human image-bearers.
12.7: alla kai hai triches tēs kephalēs hymōn pasai ērithmēntai — but even the hairs of your head are all numbered — ērithmēntai (perfect passive: have been numbered and remain counted) — not merely that God could count them but that they are presently enumerated. The hyperbolic precision (the most individually variable and untracked aspect of a person's body) illustrates total divine knowledge of particulars. mē phobou; pollōn strouthiōn diapherete — do not fear; you are worth more than many sparrows — diapherō (to differ from, be worth more than, surpass in value) grounds the reassurance in comparative worth, not in absence of danger.
12.8: Legō de hymin, pas hos an homologēsē en emoi emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — I say to you, everyone who confesses in me before men — homologeō en emoi (to confess in me, acknowledge publicly — the en construction is unusual: "in me" implies confessing from within union with Jesus, not merely about him) is the public acknowledgment in hostile circumstances. kai ho huios tou anthrōpou homologēsei en autō emprosthen tōn aggelōn tou theou — the Son of Man will also confess in him before the angels of God — the eschatological endorsement is symmetrical: present confession in Jesus → future acknowledgment by the Son of Man before God's heavenly court. The angels are the divine council witnesses.
12.9: ho de arnēsamenos me enōpion tōn anthrōpōn aparnēthēsetai enōpion tōn aggelōn tou theou — the one who denies me before men will be denied before the angels of God — arneomai (to deny, disown) and the intensified aparneomai (to deny thoroughly, disown completely) create an asymmetry: the human denial is simple; the divine denial is emphatic. The passive aparnēthēsetai (will be denied) is divine passive: God/the Son of Man will do the denying. The courtroom imagery (before men/before the angels) structures the parallel.
12.10: kai pas hos erei logon eis ton huion tou anthrōpou, aphethēsetai autō — everyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man, it will be forgiven him — the contrast is theologically precise: speaking against the Son of Man (his earthly form, his human vulnerability, his apparent weakness) is forgivable — the disciples' failure of nerve during the Passion is included in this. tō de eis to hagion pneuma blasphēmēsanti ouk aphethēsetai — but to the one who blasphemes against the Holy Spirit it will not be forgiven — blasphēmia kata tou pneumatos is the deliberate, persistent rejection of the Spirit's testimony about Jesus; in context (cf. Mk 3:22-30) it is attributing the Spirit's works to demonic agency. It is the sin of hardened finality, not a momentary lapse.
12.11: Hotan de eispherōsin hymas epi tas synagōgas kai tas archas kai tas exousias — when they bring you before synagogues and rulers and authorities — the threefold escalation: synagōgai (Jewish community courts), archai (magistrates, principalities — civic officials), exousiai (powers, authorities — Roman imperial power). The progression mirrors Acts: Stephen before the Sanhedrin (Acts 6-7), Paul before Jewish synagogues, then Roman proconsuls, then kings. mē merimnēsēte pōs ē ti apologēsēsthe ē ti eipēte — do not be anxious about how or what you should say in defense — apologeomai (to make a defense, give an apologia) is the legal defense speech; the disciples are not to prepare it in advance.
12.12: to gar hagion pneuma didaxei hymas en autē tē hōra ha dei eipein — for the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what it is necessary to say — to hagion pneuma is the promised advocate (cf. Jn 14:26 where the Paraclete teaches); the Spirit's role in legal defense before authorities is fulfilled in Acts 4:8 (Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, addressing the Sanhedrin), Acts 6:10 (Stephen's opponents unable to withstand his Spirit-given wisdom), Acts 13:9 (Paul filled with the Spirit rebuking Elymas). En autē tē hōra (in that very hour) — the provision is contemporaneous with the need, not advance preparation.
12.13: Eipen de tis ek tou ochlou autō, Didaskale, eipe tō adelphō mou merisasthai met emou tēn klēronomian — someone from the crowd said to him: Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me — the interruption is abrupt and jarring after the teaching on persecution and the Spirit. The petition is a property dispute about inheritance (klēronomia), the division of an estate. Jewish inheritance law was complex; the firstborn received a double share (Deut 21:17); disputes required a recognized authority. The man addresses Jesus as Didaskalos (Teacher), expecting him to function as a rabbinic arbitrator.
12.14: ho de eipen autō, Anthrōpe, tis me katestēsen kritēn ē meristēn eph hymas — he said to him: Man, who appointed me a judge or a divider over you? — kritēs (judge) and meristēs (divider, arbitrator — unique to this passage in the NT) are the roles Jesus declines. The refusal is not indifference to justice but a redirection: the rabbinic arbitration role is not his commission. The echo of Moses's refusal to be a judge in Exod 2:14 is deliberate; Jesus will not be drawn into property adjudication. His question implies that no one has made him their judge — a comment on the nature of his authority.
12.15: Eipev de pros autous, Horate kai phylassesthe apo pasēs pleonexias — he said to them: See and guard yourselves from all covetousness/greed — pleonexia (greediness, the desire for more, covetousness) is listed by Paul among the fundamental vices (Col 3:5; Eph 5:3) and identified with idolatry (Col 3:5). hoti ouk en tō perisseuein tini hē zōē autou estin ek tōn hyparchontōn autou — because life does not consist for anyone in the abundance of his possessions — perisseuo (to abound, overflow, be in excess) and hyparchonta (possessions, property — literally "what one already has, what lies beneath") define the equation the crowd assumes: more possessions = more life. Jesus denies the equation categorically.
12.16: Eipen de parabolēn pros autous legōn — he spoke a parable to them saying — the parable of the Rich Fool is unique to Luke and serves as the narrative illustration of v.15's principle. Anthrōpou tinos plousiou euphorēsen hē chōra — a certain rich man's land yielded abundantly — euphoreo (to bear well, produce abundantly — a hapax in the NT) is the agricultural term for an exceptional harvest. The man is already rich (plousios); the abundant harvest is surplus beyond surplus. The problem the parable addresses is the management of unexpected abundance.
12.17: kai dielogizeto en heautō legōn, Ti poiēsō, hoti ouk echō pou synaxō tous karpous mou — and he was deliberating within himself saying: what shall I do? for I have nowhere to store my crops — the internal deliberation (dielogizeto en heautō) reveals a man who consults no one — not God, not community, not the poor. The question "what shall I do?" (Ti poiēsō) is answered entirely in terms of storage expansion; no possibility of distribution occurs to him. Synaxō tous karpous mou (gather my crops) — the possessive pronoun mou (my) appears five times in vv.17-19 as the self-referential grammar of covetousness.
12.18: kai eipen, Touto poiēsō — and he said: this is what I will do — the decision is immediate and self-generated. kathelō mou tas apothēkas kai meizōnas oikodomēsō — I will pull down my barns and build larger ones — apothēkē (storehouse, barn, granary — literally a place to put things away) is the storage facility that determines the scope of the man's imagination. Bigger storage = more retained wealth = greater security. The solution to surplus is more capacity for accumulation. kai synaxō ekei panta ton siton kai ta agatha mou — and I will gather there all my grain and all my goods — ta agatha mou (my good things) is the Epicurean vocabulary of the pleasant life.
12.19: kai erō tē psychē mou, Psychē, echeis polla agatha keimena eis etē polla — and I will say to my soul: Soul, you have many good things laid up for many years — the self-address to psychē (soul, life-force, self) treats the soul as an entity to be managed and satisfied through material provision. Keimena eis etē polla (laid up for many years) — the projected time-horizon of the stored goods determines the man's sense of security. anapauou, phage, pie, euphrainou — rest, eat, drink, be merry — the four imperatives of Epicurean ataraxia (tranquility): withdrawal from striving into enjoyment of accumulated goods. The formula echoes Eccles 8:15; the man has made Qohelet's resignation into his life plan.
12.20: eipen de autō ho Theos, Aphrōn, tautē tē nykti tēn psychēn sou apaitousin apo sou — God said to him: Fool, this very night they demand your soul back from you — Aphrōn (fool — one who lacks practical wisdom and moral discernment, not merely unintelligent; cf. Ps 14:1 "the fool says in his heart, There is no God") is God's one-word verdict on the life-plan. Apaitousin (they demand back — present tense: even now demanding; plural subject is the Semitic idiom for impersonal divine action, "one demands of you") is the creditor language of reclaiming a loan: the soul was never the man's property. ha de hētoimasas, tini estai — what you prepared, whose will it be? — the question the man never asked while preparing.
12.21: houtōs ho thēsaurizōn heautō kai mē eis theon ploutōn — so it is with the one treasuring for himself and not being rich toward God — thēsaurizō heautō (treasuring up for oneself) is the hoarding grammar, contrasted with eis theon ploutōn (being rich toward God — a unique Lukan phrase). The preposition eis (toward, into) with theon defines the direction of wealth: toward God means used generously according to divine priorities (cf. 12:33; 16:9; 18:22; 21:1-4). The parable does not condemn wealth but the direction of accumulation: self-ward vs. God-ward.
12.22: Eipen de pros tous mathētas autou — he said to his disciples — the audience shifts back to disciples (from the crowd's parable, vv.13-21). Dia touto legō hymin, mē merimnate tē psychē hymon ti phagēte, mēde tō sōmati hymon ti endysēsthe — therefore I say to you, do not be anxious about your life what you will eat, nor about your body what you will wear — merimnō (to be anxious, divided in mind — from merizō to divide; anxiety as a mind divided against itself by competing concerns) is the subject of the following argument. The body-soul pair (food for the soul/clothes for the body) sets the two axes of survival anxiety.
12.23: hē gar psychē pleion estin tēs trophēs kai to sōma tou endymatos — for the soul/life is more than food and the body more than clothing — the argument from greater to lesser: if God gave the greater (life itself, the body), is he not also capable of supplying the lesser (food, clothing)? The priorities of divine provision are established by the greater gift; the creator-giver of life is not indifferent to its sustenance. The verse is the logical premise for all of vv.24-31.
12.24: katanoeate tous korakas, hoti ou speirousin oude therizousin — consider the ravens, because they do not sow or reap — katanoeō (to perceive thoroughly, observe carefully, contemplate — a stronger word than simply "look") indicates sustained reflective attention to the natural object as a theological text. Luke uses ravens (korax) where Matthew has "birds of the air" (Mt 6:26); ravens are ritually impure (Lev 11:15) and therefore an unexpected illustration of divine care — God feeds even the unclean birds. hois ouk estin tameion oude apothēkē, kai ho Theos trephei autous — who have no storeroom or barn, and God feeds them — the ravens have none of the storage infrastructure the rich fool built; their lack of storage is precisely what illustrates trust in direct divine provision.
12.25: tis de ex hymōn merimnōn dynatai epi tēn hēlikian autou prostheinai pēchyn — which of you by being anxious can add a cubit to his lifespan? — hēlikia (stature, age, lifespan — ambiguous between physical height and duration of life; in context "lifespan" fits better) is what anxiety cannot extend. Pēchys (cubit — a unit of length, ~45cm) applied to lifespan is the metaphor of a very small addition to what is already given. The rhetorical question expects "none of you"; anxiety is not merely useless but specifically powerless over the things it fears most.
12.26: ei oun oude elachiston dynasthe, ti peri tōn loipōn merimnate — if therefore you cannot even do the least, why are you anxious about the rest? — the argument from inability: anxiety fails even at the smallest extension of life; how then can it address the larger provision questions? Elachiston (the least, the smallest) — adding a cubit to one's height/lifespan is described as the "least" concern; the rhetorical point is that anxiety is impotent even at the minimum level of its aspirations. The conclusion is that anxiety achieves nothing and therefore ought not to replace trust.
12.27: katanoeisate ta krina pōs auxanei, ou kopia oude nēthei — consider how the lilies grow; they do not toil or spin — krina (lilies — probably the anemone or other wildflowers of the Galilean hillsides, not specifically water-lilies). The double negative: no agricultural labor (kopiaō = to toil, labor wearily) and no textile production (nēthō = to spin thread). legō de hymin, oud Solomōn en pasē tē doxē autou periebaleto hōs hen toutōn — I tell you, not even Solomon in all his glory was clothed like one of these — Solomon at the height of imperial wealth (1 Kgs 10) cannot match the wildflower's clothing; the comparison makes divine provision appear more lavish than human achievement.
12.28: ei de en agrō ton chorton onta sēmeron kai aurion eis klibanon ballomenon ho Theos houtōs amphiazei — if God so clothes the grass that is in the field today and tomorrow is thrown into the oven — klibanon (clay oven — dried grass and thorns were used as fuel for baking ovens in Palestinian villages; grass is literally a disposable fuel). The argument runs from inferior to superior: grass is temporary fuel; yet God clothes it lavishly. Disciples are permanent image-bearers. posō mallon hymas, oligopistoi — how much more you, O you of little faith — oligopistoi (little-faiths — a Matthean word; Luke uses it only here) gently rebukes anxious disciples as those who trust too little.
12.29: kai hymeis mē zēteite ti phagēte kai ti piēte kai mē meteorrizesthe — do not seek what you will eat and what you will drink, and do not be anxious/suspended — meteōrizō (to suspend in mid-air, to be in suspense, to be unsettled — a hapax in the NT, from meteōros = lifted up, suspended; used of ships tossed at sea or minds suspended in uncertainty) gives the specific texture of food-and-drink anxiety: an unsettled, floating mental state that cannot rest. The instruction is not against planning but against the anxious suspension that makes provision the center of one's existence.
12.30: tauta gar panta ta ethnē tou kosmou epizētousin — for all these things the nations/Gentiles of the world seek after — ta ethnē (the nations, the Gentiles — those without covenant relationship with YHWH) are defined by their preoccupation with food and drink: survival anxiety as the organizing principle of life without God. hymōn de ho patēr oiden hoti chrēzete toutōn — but your Father knows that you need these things — the counter to Gentile anxiety: the disciples' Father (ho patēr — the intimate address presupposing covenant relationship) already knows the need. Prior divine knowledge removes the anxious need to secure what is already known and provided for.
12.31: plēn zēteite tēn basileian autou, kai tauta prostethēsetai hymin — but seek his kingdom, and these things will be added to you — plēn (but, however — Luke's adversative pivot) redirects from anxious material-seeking to kingdom-seeking. Zēteite tēn basileian (seek the kingdom) is the single positive command; everything else is addition to it. Prostethēsetai (will be added, divine passive — God will add) promises material provision as the byproduct of kingdom-priority. The economy of the kingdom reverses the economy of anxiety: seek what is primary, receive what is secondary as gift.
12.32: Mē phobou, to mikron poimnion, hoti eudokēsen ho patēr hymōn dounai hymin tēn basileian — do not fear, little flock, for your Father has been pleased to give you the kingdom — to mikron poimnion (the little flock) is the only occurrence of "flock" applied to the disciples in Luke; it combines smallness with pastoral intimacy. Against the world's great powers and institutions, the disciples are a small and vulnerable group. Eudokēsen (was pleased, delighted to — aorist: a completed decision expressing the Father's sovereign pleasure) grounds the gift of the kingdom in divine initiative and delight, not in the disciples' worthiness or size. The past tense ("was pleased") indicates a settled divine commitment.
