12.33: Pōlēsate ta hyparchonta hymōn kai dote eleēmosynēn — sell your possessions and give alms — the instruction is radical and concrete: the anxiety about possessions is resolved not by securing more but by releasing what one has. Eleēmosynē (almsgiving, merciful giving — from eleos, mercy) is the covenantal obligation of those in community with the needy. Poiēsate heautois ballantia mē palaiomena, thēsauron anekleipton en tois ouranois — make for yourselves purses that do not wear out, an inexhaustible treasure in the heavens — ballantion mē palaioumenon (purse that does not grow old, deteriorate) contrasts with earthly containers that rot, rust, or are stolen. The heavenly treasure is anekleiptos (inexhaustible — unique to this passage in the NT).
12.34: hopou gar estin ho thēsauros hymōn, ekei kai hē kardia hymōn estai — for where your treasure is, there also will be your heart — kardia (heart — in Hebrew anthropology, the center of will, desire, and decision-making, not merely emotion) follows the location of the treasure: the heart is attracted to what it values most, and values what it attends to. The aphorism runs in both directions: where you place your treasure determines the location of your heart; and where your heart already is reveals where your true treasure lies. Almsgiving (v.33) redirects the treasure and thereby reshapes the heart's orientation.
12.35: Estōsan hymōn hai osphyes periezōsmenai kai hoi lychnoi kaiomenoi — let your loins be girded and your lamps burning — osphyes periezōsmenai (loins girded) is the Exodus posture: Israel ate the Passover "with your belt fastened" (mizrayin, Exod 12:11 LXX — girded loins), ready to depart at any moment. Lychnoi kaiomenoi (lamps burning) — the continuous burning lamp signals active readiness through the dark hours; the unprepared lamp means one will be caught in darkness. The two images combine: body ready for action, light for the journey.
12.36: kai hymeis homoioi anthrōpois prosdechomenois ton kyrion heautōn — and you like men waiting for their lord — prosdechomai (to wait for, expect with readiness — active expectation, not passive waiting) characterizes the servant's posture. hotan anastasē ek tōn gamōn, hina elthontos kai krousantos eutheōs anoixōsin autō — when he returns from the wedding banquet, so that when he comes and knocks they may open to him immediately — gamoi (wedding celebrations — plural, the extended festivities lasting multiple days in the ancient world; the return time is genuinely uncertain). Eutheōs anoixōsin (they immediately open) — no delay when the expected lord arrives; instant response marks the ready servant.
12.37: makarioi hoi douloi ekeinoi, hous elthōn ho kyrios heurēsei grēgorountas — blessed are those servants whom the lord, coming, will find watching — grēgoreō (to stay awake, keep watch — from egeirō: to wake up) is the New Testament's eschatological alertness word (cf. Mt 24:42-43; 1 Thess 5:6; Rev 3:3; 16:15). The makarios (blessed) is the beatitude form, declaring the eschatological status of those found in readiness. amēn legō hymin hoti perizōsetai kai anaklinei autous kai parelthōn diakonēsei autois — truly I tell you he will gird himself and seat them and coming to them he will serve them — the role-reversal is astonishing: the returning Lord serves his faithful servants at table.
12.38: kan en tē deutera kan en tē tritē phylakē elthē kai heurē houtōs, makarioi eisin ekeinoi — even if he comes in the second or in the third watch and finds them so, blessed are those — the night watches: Roman military custom divided the night into four 3-hour watches (6-9pm, 9-midnight, midnight-3am, 3-6am); Jewish custom used three watches. The second and third watches (midnight through the early morning hours) are the most demanding times to maintain alertness. The blessing extends to those found watching at the most inconvenient hours of waiting — a comment on the cost and honor of prolonged eschatological readiness.
12.39: touto de ginōskete, hoti ei ēdei ho oikodespotēs poia hōra ho kleptēs erchetai — but know this: if the householder had known at what hour the thief was coming — oikodespotēs (master of the house, householder — compound of oikos house and despotēs master) is the responsible guardian of the household. The thief analogy introduces a different aspect of the Lord's coming: not the welcomed master-servant relationship of vv.36-38 but the unpredictability of the timing. ouk aphēken diorychthēnai ton oikon autou — he would not have allowed his house to be broken into — dioryssō (to dig through — burglars in ancient Palestine dug through mud-brick walls rather than breaking in through doors).
12.40: kai hymeis ginesthe hētoimoi, hoti hē hōra ou dokeite ho huios tou anthrōpou erchetai — and you must be ready, because the Son of Man is coming at an hour you do not expect — ginesthe hētoimoi (become ready, be ready — present imperative: continuous readiness) is the practical conclusion from both the servant-parable (vv.35-38) and the thief-analogy (v.39). Hē hōra ou dokeite (the hour you do not expect, do not think) — the timing's unexpectedness is the consistent point; readiness cannot be contingent on predicted timing. Ho huios tou anthrōpou erchetai — the Son of Man comes (present tense in Greek, conveying imminent certainty).
12.41: Eipen de ho Petros, Kyrie, pros hēmas tēn parabolēn tautēn legeis ē kai pros pantas — Peter said: Lord, are you telling this parable to us or to everyone? — Peter's question is characteristically practical and boundary-setting: is this demand for readiness directed specifically to the apostolic circle or to the entire crowd? The address Kyrie (Lord) marks the heightened awareness of the teaching's seriousness. Jesus does not answer Peter's question directly but deepens and expands the image, implicitly directing the heightened responsibility to those in leadership positions.
12.42: kai eipen ho Kyrios, Tis ara estin ho pistos oikonomos ho phronimos — the Lord said: who then is the faithful and wise manager? — oikonomos (estate manager, steward — responsible for managing the master's household and distributing provisions to servants; a position of delegated authority and accountability) is the image Luke uses for responsible leadership. Pistos (faithful, trustworthy) and phronimos (wise, prudent, practically discerning) are the two qualities the master values. hon katastēsei ho kyrios epi tēs therapeias autou tou didonai en kairō to sitometrion — whom the lord will put in charge of his household staff to give them their food allowance in due time — sitometrion (grain-ration, food-allowance) is the regular provision the steward distributes.
12.43: makarios ho doulos ekeinos, hon elthōn ho kyrios autou heurēsei poiounta houtōs — blessed is that servant whom his lord coming will find doing so — the beatitude here is not for passive waiting but for active faithful service: the manager found distributing the household's food on schedule. The eschatological arrival finds the servant in the middle of ordinary faithful work — not in a posture of dramatic preparation but in the rhythm of daily responsibility. This is the Lukan theology of faithful stewardship: eschatological readiness looks like continued doing of one's assigned work.
12.44: alēthōs legō hymin hoti epi pasin tois hyparchousin autou katastēsei auton — truly I tell you he will put him in charge of all his possessions — the reward for faithful stewardship of limited responsibility is expanded responsibility. The pattern mirrors the Parable of the Pounds (19:11-27): faithful management of a portion leads to superintendence of all. Epi pasin tois hyparchousin autou (over all his possessions) is the maximum delegation the master can grant; the faithful servant becomes the master's full proxy. In the Lukan community context, this addressed leaders of house-churches and the post-resurrection mission.
12.45: ean de eipē ho doulos ekeinos en tē kardia autou, Chronizei ho kyrios mou erchesthai — but if that servant says in his heart: my lord is delayed in coming — the servant's inner reasoning (in the heart = a private decision, not announced) that the master's delay is permanent creates the occasion for abuse. Chronizei (is delayed, is taking long) — the assumption that the delay will continue indefinitely is the theological error that liberates bad behavior. kai arxētai typtein tous paidas kai tas paidikas esthiein te kai pinein kai methyskesthai — and begins to beat the male and female servants and to eat and drink and get drunk — the three abuses: violence against subordinates, self-indulgence, and excess.
12.46: hēxei ho kyrios tou doulou ekeinou en hēmera hē ou prosdokā kai en hōra hē ou ginōskei — the lord of that servant will come on a day he does not expect and at an hour he does not know — the timing certainty is not disclosed but the surprise is certain. kai dichotomēsei auton kai to meros autou meta tōn apistōn thēsei — and he will cut him in two and put his portion with the unfaithful — dichotomeo (to cut in two, bisect — a severe and graphic punishment term; perhaps a legal idiom for severe flogging or for dismissal with permanent separation from the household) is the most extreme consequence in the parable. Meta tōn apistōn (with the unbelievers, the faithless) is the final category: the unfaithful servant is reclassified as a non-disciple.
12.47: ekeinos de ho doulos ho gnous to thelēma tou kyriou autou kai mē hetoimasas ē poiēsas pros to thelēma autou darthēsetai pollas — that servant who knew his master's will and did not prepare or act according to his will will be beaten with many blows — the principle of proportionate accountability: knowledge of the master's will creates proportionate responsibility. Darthēsetai pollas (will be beaten with many — scourged many times; dairō = to flay, beat) — the punishment calibrated to knowledge. The implication for teachers and religious leaders who "know" the scriptures is direct: their accountability is heightened by their knowledge.
12.48: ho de mē gnous, poiēsas de axia plēgōn, darthēsetai oligas — the one who did not know, and did things worthy of punishment, will be beaten with few — the ignorant wrongdoer is punished less severely because knowledge is the multiplier of accountability. panti de hō edothē poly, poly zētēthēsetai par autou — from everyone to whom much was given, much will be required of him — the programmatic statement of stewardship theology: gift creates obligation; scale of gift determines scale of responsibility. kai hō parethento poly, perissoteron aitēsousin auton — and to whom they entrusted much, they will ask all the more of him — the plural subject ("they will ask") is the Semitic impersonal divine passive for God's accounting.
12.49: Pyr ēlthon balein epi tēn gēn, kai ti thelō ei ēdē anēphthē — I came to cast fire on the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled! — pyr balein epi tēn gēn (to cast fire on the earth) is the eschatological fire of divine purification: Isa 66:15-16 (YHWH coming with fire to judge), Joel 2:30-31 (fire as sign of the day of the LORD), Mal 3:2-3 (the Messenger like a refiner's fire). The longing expressed (ti thelō ei ēdē anēphthē — what I wish, if only it were already kindled!) is Jesus's desire for the completion of his mission: the eschatological fire will not come without the cross. The saying stands as the strongest expression of Jesus's emotional longing for the fulfillment of his work.
12.50: baptisma de echō baptisthēnai, kai pōs synechomai heōs hotou telesthē — but I have a baptism to be baptized with, and how constrained I am until it is completed! — baptisma ... baptisthēnai (a baptism to be baptized with) is the metaphorical use of immersion for overwhelming experience, specifically death: being plunged into suffering and death as into deep water (cf. Ps 42:7; 69:1-2; Isa 43:2 "when you pass through the waters"). Synechomai (I am constrained, hemmed in, pressed — from synechō: to hold together under pressure; Luke uses it for illness's grip in 4:38 and fever's constraint) expresses the pressing weight of the approaching Passion. Heōs hotou telesthē (until it is completed/accomplished — teleō: brought to its appointed end) — the same root as "it is finished" (Jn 19:30).
12.51: Dokeite hoti eirēnēn paregenomēn dounai en tē gē — do you think I came to give peace on earth? — the question confronts the assumption of a peaceful Messianic program. The OT shalom (peace, wholeness) promised for the messianic age (Isa 9:6-7; Mic 5:5; Zech 9:10) might lead to the expectation of social harmony. ouchi, legō hymin, all ē diamerismon — no, I tell you, but rather division — diamerismos (division, splitting apart — from diamerizō: to divide through, distribute) is not Jesus's purpose but his effect: the proclamation of the kingdom divides those who receive from those who reject, and that division runs through families (v.52-53).
12.52: esontai gar apo tou nyn pente en heni oikō diamemerismenoi — for from now on there will be five in one household divided — apo tou nyn (from now on) is the Lukan present-eschatological marker for the new era Jesus's ministry inaugurates (cf. 1:48; 5:10; 22:18,69). treis epi dysin kai dyo epi trisin — three against two and two against three — the odd numbers create the impossibility of a stable majority within the family unit: no side can win or absorb the other. The household, the basic unit of ancient Mediterranean social stability, is fractured by the advent of Jesus.
12.53: diamerithesuontai patēr epi huiō kai huios epi patri — father against son and son against father — the family division follows generational lines with the possibility of the division running in either direction: parent may reject child who follows Jesus, or child may reject parent who does. The pairs: father/son, mother/daughter, mother-in-law/daughter-in-law cover both genders and both directions of the adult family relationships. The language echoes Mic 7:6 (a man's enemies are the members of his own household), applied to the prophetic mission and now fulfilled in Jesus's ministry.
12.54: Elegen de kai tois ochlois — he was saying also to the crowds — the audience shift from disciples (vv.22, 35, 41) back to the crowds; the following sayings are directed at the broader public. Hotan idēte tēn nephalēn anatellousari apo dysmōn, eutheōs legete hoti Ombros erchetai — when you see a cloud rising from the west, immediately you say: a shower is coming — the Mediterranean weather pattern: clouds from the west brought rain off the sea; south wind brought desert heat. The crowd's meteorological competence is established as the baseline for the comparison.
12.55: kai hotan noton pneonta, legete hoti Kauson estai, kai ginetai — and when the south wind blows, you say: it will be scorching, and it is — notos (the south wind — from the Negev and Arabian desert, bringing hot dry air; the hamsin or sirocco) reliably produces high temperatures. The twice-confirmed accuracy of the crowd's weather reading establishes that they are capable of interpreting natural signs correctly.
12.56: Hypokritai, to prosōpon tēs gēs kai tou ouranou oidate dokimazein, ton kairon de touton pōs ouk oidate dokimazein — Hypocrites! You know how to interpret the face of the earth and the sky; how is it you do not know how to interpret this present time? — Hypokritai (Hypocrites — actors playing a role; here those who perform meteorological competence while refusing the harder discernment of the present moment) is the accusation. Ton kairon de touton (this present time — kairos, the opportune, significant moment, not just chronological time) is the time of the Messiah's presence, the year of the Lord's favor (4:19). Reading weather requires no moral commitment; reading the kairos requires decision.
12.57: Ti de kai aph heautōn ou krinete to dikaion — why do you not also judge from yourselves what is right? — the capacity for just judgment (to dikaion, the just/right thing) is presented as available within the audience: they have sufficient moral perception to discern the right course without external compulsion. The question implies that the failure to read the times (v.56) is a failure of moral will, not of intellectual capacity. Aph heautōn (from yourselves, on your own initiative) — independent moral discernment is not only possible but expected.
12.58: hōs gar hypageis meta tou antidikou sou ep archonta, en tē hodō dos ergasian apēlachthai ap autou — for as you are going with your adversary before the magistrate, on the way make every effort to be released from him — antidikos (adversary in a lawsuit — literally "one who works against you in court") and archōn (ruler, magistrate) set the legal context. Dos ergasian (give effort, apply energy — an idiomatic phrase for "make every effort") before reaching the court. mēpote katasyrē se pros ton kritēn kai ho kritēs se paradōsei tō praktori — lest he drag you to the judge and the judge deliver you to the collector — praktōr (debt-collector, enforcement officer — a Lukan hapax) who enforces payment.
12.59: legō soi, ou mē exelthēs ekeithen heōs kai to eschaton lepton apodōs — I tell you, you will not get out from there until you pay the last lepton — lepton (the smallest Jewish copper coin, worth 1/2 of a Roman quadrans — the "widow's mite" of 21:2) specifies the minimum unit of debt. Ou mē exelthēs (you will certainly not get out — double negative for emphatic certainty) is the final word of warning. Whether the parable is purely wisdom-instruction about legal prudence or an allegory of eschatological urgency (settle with God before the judgment) is left open; Luke's placement after the kairos-interpretation saying (vv.56-57) supports the latter reading.

13.1: parēsan de tines en autō tō kairō apangellontes autō peri tōn Galilaion — some were present at that very time reporting to him about the Galileans — en autō tō kairō (at that very time — the kairos of 12:56, the opportune time of the Messiah's presence) links this scene to the preceding. The report concerns Galileans whose blood Pilate had mixed with their sacrifices (ta thymata autōn = their sacrificial animals) — a brutal act of political repression during a temple sacrifice, probably in the Passover context. Pilate had history of violent suppression (cf. Josephus, Ant. 18.60-62, the aqueduct riot).
13.2: kai apokritheis eipen autois, Dokeite hoti hoi Galilaioi houtoi hamartōloi para pantas tous Galilaious egenonto — answering them he said: do you think these Galileans were sinners more than all other Galileans? — the question confronts the popular theological assumption (cf. Job 4:7; John 9:2) that exceptional suffering indicates exceptional sin. The suffix para pantas (more than all, in comparison with all) is the specific form of the retribution-theology assumption: their worse fate implies their worse sin. Jesus rejects this interpretive framework directly.
13.3: ouchi, legō hymin, all ean mē metanoēte, pantes homoiōs apoleisthe — no, I tell you, but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish — the counter-move is twofold: first, the denial of the sin-suffering equation; second, the redirection of the lesson. Metanoeō (to repent, change one's mind and direction — Luke's central call; cf. 5:32; 15:7,10; 16:30; 17:3,4; 24:47) is the universal requirement. Pantes homoiōs apoleisthe (you will all likewise perish) — the "likewise" does not predict death by Roman violence for all but perishing in the general eschatological judgment; the specific mode of the Galileans' death illustrates the reality of human vulnerability, not the character of God's judgment.
13.4: ē ekeinoi hoi dekaokto eph hous epesen ho pyrgos en tō Silōam kai apekteinen autous — or those eighteen on whom the tower in Siloam fell and killed them — the Tower of Siloam is otherwise unattested; the pool of Siloam (south of the City of David) was part of Hezekiah's water-tunnel system. A tower collapse was an accident — no human agency, no political violence. The pairing of the two incidents (Roman political violence, architectural accident) covers both categories of violent death: human malice and random disaster. dokeite hoti autoi opheiletai egenonto para pantas tous anthrōpous tous katoikountas Ierousalēm — do you think they were debtors more than all the people living in Jerusalem?
13.5: ouchi, legō hymin, all ean mē metanoēte, pantes hōsautōs apoleisthe — no, I tell you, but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish — the exact same warning repeated for emphasis, now applied to the Jerusalem audience. The repetition of the call to repentance (metanoēsēte) grounds both cases in the same conclusion: the lesson from accidental and political deaths is not "they deserved it" but "mortality is universal and the time for repentance is now." The urgency is eschatological: kairos (12:56) is passing.
13.6: Elegen de tautēn tēn parabolēn — he told this parable — the parable of the Barren Fig Tree follows the double repent-or-perish warning as its narrative elaboration. sykēn eichen tis en tō ampelōni autou pephuteumenēn — a man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard — the fig tree planted in a vineyard (ampelōn) is an unusual detail; figs were planted in vineyards (Mic 4:4; 1 Kgs 4:25) or separately. The vineyard is Israel's consistent OT symbol (Isa 5:1-7; Ps 80:8-16; Jer 2:21; Ezek 15); the fig tree within it represents a particular element of Israel (possibly Jerusalem or its leadership). kai ēlthen zētōn karpon en autē kai ouch heuren — and came seeking fruit on it and found none.
13.7: eipen de pros ton ampelourgo, Idou tria etē aph hou erchomai zētōn karpon en tē sykē tautē kai ouch heuriskō — he said to the vinedresser: behold, three years I have been coming seeking fruit on this fig tree and finding none — tria etē (three years) has prompted allegorical readings (three years of Jesus's ministry); structurally, three attempts represents a patient but not indefinitely patient owner who has given the tree multiple seasons. ekkoson autēn; hina ti kai tēn gēn katargei — cut it down; why does it even waste the ground? — katargeo (to make inoperative, render useless, waste — Paul's favorite word for the abolition of what has served its purpose) applies to soil exhausted by an unproductive tree; the tree's presence prevents productive use of the land.
13.8: ho de apokritheis legei autō, Kyrie, aphes autēn kai touto to etos — he answered him: Sir, leave it also this year — the vinedresser's intercession is the parable's pivot: a third party pleads for continued opportunity. heōs hotou skapsō peri autēn kai balō kopria — until I dig around it and throw manure — skaptō (to dig — to aerate and loosen compacted soil around the roots, allowing moisture and nutrients to penetrate) and kopria (dung, manure — the fertilizer that accelerates growth) are the two interventions the vinedresser will provide. The intensification of care before the final deadline: the tree will be given every possible advantage before the verdict of fruitlessness becomes final.
13.9: kan men poiēsē karpon eis to mellon — if it then bears fruit in the coming year — the conditional is hopeful but not guaranteed; the vinedresser pleads for a chance, not a certainty. ei de mē ge, ekkopseis autēn — but if not, you will cut it down — the deadline is deferred, not abolished; the owner's patience accommodates the vinedresser's intercession but does not abandon the fruitfulness requirement. The parable ends without resolution: the audience must supply the ending. In the narrative context, Israel is at the year of deferral — the three years of Jesus's ministry giving way to one final year of intensified opportunity.
13.10: Ēn de didaskōn en mia tōn synagōgōn en tois sabbasin — he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the Sabbath — the Sabbath healing of the bent woman (vv.10-17) is unique to Luke. The setting — synagogue, Sabbath — is the intersection of maximum communal witness and maximum halakhic scrutiny. The woman's presence in the synagogue despite her severe disability (pneumati astheneia, v.11) implies she maintained her religious practice throughout the eighteen years of her affliction.
13.11: kai idou gynē ēn pneumati echousa astheneia etē dekaokto — and there was a woman who had a spirit of weakness for eighteen years — pneuma astheneia (spirit of infirmity/weakness) — Luke names a spiritual dimension to the physical condition (v.16 confirms: "bound by Satan"); this does not eliminate natural causation but adds a demonic oppression diagnosis. Dekaokto etē (eighteen years) — the same number as the tower victims (v.4), an intentional echo: these eighteen years of bondage parallel those eighteen deaths, both serving to illustrate the condition from which Jesus comes to liberate. kai ēn sygkyptousa kai mē dynamenē anakupsai eis to panteles — she was bent double and could not straighten up at all.
13.12: idōn de autēn ho Iēsous prosephōnēsen kai eipen autē — seeing her, Jesus called to her and said — the initiative is entirely Jesus's: the woman makes no request, no approach. Prosephōnēsen (called out to, summoned by calling) is a public summons across the synagogue. Gynai, apolelalysai tēs astheneia sou — Woman, you have been released from your weakness — apolelalysai (perfect passive: you have been and remain released — the release is an accomplished fact declared before the laying on of hands) announces freedom before the physical restoration. The perfect tense is the word of liberation: the sentence is pronounced, the bondage broken.
13.13: kai epethēken autē tas cheiras — and he laid his hands on her — the laying on of hands (epitithēmi tas cheiras) is the gesture of blessing and transfer: the same gesture used for healing (4:40), commissioning (Acts 6:6; 13:3), and gift of the Spirit (Acts 8:17-19). kai parachrēma anōrthōthē kai edoxazen ton Theon — and immediately she was made straight and was glorifying God — anorthoō (to straighten up, erect again — medical term for correcting a bent or collapsed structure; cf. Acts 15:16 "raise up the tent of David that has fallen") is the physical restoration. Parachrēma (immediately) is Luke's healing-stamp. Edoxazen ton Theon (was glorifying God) — the imperfect of continuous praise: she did not stop.
13.14: apokritheis de ho archisyagōgos, aganaktōn hoti tō sabbatō etherapeusen ho Iēsous — the synagogue ruler answered, indignant because Jesus had healed on the Sabbath — aganakteō (to be indignant, greatly displeased — a strong word for righteous anger turned against Jesus) frames the synagogue ruler's objection as a principled defense of Sabbath law. eischen tō ochlō, Hex hēmerai eisin en hais dei ergazesthai; en autais oun erchomenoi therapeuesthe kai mē tē hēmera tou sabbatou — he said to the crowd: there are six days on which it is necessary to work; in those come and be healed and not on the Sabbath day — the reproof is addressed to the crowd (not directly to Jesus), probably to avoid direct confrontation; the halakhic point is technically sound within a strict Shammaite framework.
13.15: apekrithē de autō ho Kyrios kai eipen, Hypokrita, hekastos hymōn tō sabbatō ou lyei ton boun autou ē ton onon apo tēs phatnēs kai apagagōn potizei — the Lord answered him and said: Hypocrites! Does not each of you on the Sabbath untie his ox or donkey from the manger and lead it away to water? — Hypokritai (Hypocrites — the plural indicts the synagogue leadership in general, not just the ruler). The argument is qal va-homer (light to heavy, a fortiori): if one may perform the labor of untying and leading animals on the Sabbath for their welfare (a permissive halakhic ruling accepted by most schools, m. Eruvin 2:1-4), how much more may one release a human being from bondage.
13.16: tautēn de, thygatera Abraam ousan, hēn edēsen ho Satanas idou dekaokta etē — and this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan has bound for eighteen years — thygatera Abraam (daughter of Abraham) restores the woman's full covenant identity: she is not defined by her disability or her gender's exclusion from the inner synagogue space but by her Abrahamic inheritance. Hon edēsen ho Satanas (whom Satan has bound — aorist: a specific binding at a point in time, followed by eighteen years of captivity) names the spiritual agency behind the physical condition. ouk edei lythēnai apo tou desmou toutou tē hēmera tou sabbatou — ought she not be loosed from this bond on the Sabbath day? — dei (it is necessary, it is morally required) inverts the ruler's "six working days" argument.
13.17: kai tauta legontos autou katēschynonto pantes hoi antikeimenoi autō — and as he was saying these things all his opponents were put to shame — kataischynō (to put to shame, dishonor publicly — cf. Isa 41:11; 54:4 LXX) is the reversal of honor: those who claimed halakhic superiority are publicly shamed. kai pas ho ochlos echaire epi pasin tois endoxois tois ginomenois hyp autou — and all the crowd was rejoicing at all the glorious things being done by him — endoxois (glorious, magnificent things) is Luke's word for miraculous events that display divine glory (cf. 7:25; Acts 10:46). The crowd's joy is the positive counterpart to the opponents' shame.
13.18: Elegen oun, Tini homoia estin hē basileia tou Theou kai tini homoiōsō autēn — he said therefore: what is the kingdom of God like? and what shall I compare it to? — homoios/homoioō (like, to liken — the comparison-formula for parables) introduces the two kingdom-parables. The double question (Tini homoia... kai tini homoiōsō) is the rhetorical invitation to thought before the comparison is offered; it solicits the audience's creative engagement. The kingdom-parables follow the Sabbath healing as its interpretation: the liberation of the bent woman is an instance of the kingdom's arrival, now described through the lens of growth and pervasion.
13.19: homoia estin kokkō sinapeōs, hon labōn anthrōpos ebalen eis kēpon heautou — it is like a mustard seed which a man took and threw into his garden — the mustard (sinapi) seed is proverbially small in rabbinic literature (b. Sanhedrin 91a: "as small as a grain of mustard"). Kēpos (garden — Luke's word; Matthew/Mark have "field") may reflect a Hellenistic context. kai ēyxēsen kai egeneto eis dendron — and it grew and became a tree — Luke's dendron (tree) is a larger growth than Mark's "larger than all garden plants"; Luke's version is more clearly hyperbolic. kai ta peteina tou ouranou kateskēnōsen en tois kladois autou — and the birds of the air nested in its branches — an allusion to Dan 4:12,21 (Ezek 17:23; 31:6) where a great tree shelters the nations: the kingdom's growth will accommodate all peoples.
13.20: kai palin eipen, Tini homoiōsō tēn basileian tou Theou — again he said: what shall I compare the kingdom of God to? — the second question and second kingdom-parable maintains the paired structure (cf. the lost sheep/lost coin parables in ch.15; the two sons/prodigal in ch.15). The repetition of the question (palin = again) marks the separate parable; the yeast-parable supplements the mustard-seed by describing the kingdom's mode of pervasion rather than its mode of growth.
13.21: homoia estin zymē, hēn labousa gynē enekrypsen eis aleurou sata tria heōs hou ezymōthē holon — it is like yeast which a woman took and hid in three measures of flour until it was all leavened — zymē (yeast, leaven) here is used positively, in contrast to 12:1 (the leaven of hypocrisy). The woman hides (enkryptō — to conceal, bury within) the yeast: the kingdom works invisibly from within, not through external imposition. Sata tria (three seahs — approximately 40 liters, enough flour for a feast for 100+ people; cf. Gen 18:6 where Sarah prepares three seahs for the divine visitors). The large quantity implies communal, abundant transformation. Heōs hou ezymōthē holon (until the whole was leavened) — complete, total pervasion.
13.22: kai dieporeueto kata poleis kai kōmas didaskōn kai poreian poioumenos eis Ierousalēm — he was traveling through cities and villages teaching and making his way to Jerusalem — this verse is Luke's narrative hinge reminding the reader that the entire discourse of chs.12-13 occurs within the travel narrative (9:51-19:44). Poreian poioumenos eis Ierousalēm (making his journey toward Jerusalem) — poreian poieomai (to make a journey, pursue a course) uses the journey-language that defines the central section of Luke's Gospel. Every teaching occurs under the shadow of the destination.
13.23: eipen de tis autō, Kyrie, ei oligoi hoi sōzomenoi — someone said to him: Lord, are only a few being saved? — the question was debated in Second Temple Judaism: how many of Israel would be saved in the eschatological age? Some traditions held that "all Israel has a share in the world to come" (m. Sanhedrin 10:1); others expected only a remnant (4 Ezra 8:1-3: "the Most High made this world for the sake of many, but the world to come for the sake of few"). Jesus refuses to answer the numerical question directly and redirects to the urgency of personal decision.
13.24: ho de eipen pros autous, Agōnizesthe eiselthein dia tēs stenēs thyras — he said to them: strive to enter through the narrow door — agōnizomai (to strive, struggle, compete — the athletic-contest word; from agōn, the Greek athletic competition; used by Paul in 1 Cor 9:25; Col 1:29; 1 Tim 6:12) describes effortful, concentrated striving. Stenē thura (narrow door — Matthew's version has "narrow gate" in a different context, Mt 7:13-14). The narrowness does not mean restriction to a few but difficulty of entry: the door requires focused intention. hoti polloi, legō hymin, zētēsousin eiselthein kai ouk ischysousin — because many, I tell you, will seek to enter and will not be able.
13.25: aph hou an egerthē ho oikodespotēs kai apokleisē tēn thyran — once the master of the house gets up and shuts the door — the closing of the door is the definitive moment of eschatological division: before its closing, entry is possible; after, impossible. Apokleisē (to shut, lock — to close definitively against reentry) is the aorist of finality. kai arxēsthe exō hestanai kai krouein tēn thyran legontes, Kyrie, anoixon hēmin — and you begin to stand outside and knock at the door saying: Lord, open to us — Kyrie (Lord) is addressed to the one who has shut the door; the address is that of those who assumed relationship but lacked entry. kai apokritheis erei hymin, Ouk oida hymas pothen este — answering he will say: I do not know you, where you are from.
13.26: tote arxesthe legein, Ephagomen enōpion sou kai epiomen kai en tais plateiais hēmōn edidaxas — then you will begin to say: we ate and drank before you and you taught in our streets — the three claims are social proximity to Jesus: table-fellowship (ephagomen enōpion sou = we ate before/with you), shared drink, and reception of his teaching. None of these constitute the relationship of discipleship; proximity without personal commitment and transformation is not the basis of entry. The claims echo Lk 4:23 ("whatever we heard done in Capernaum, do here also") — familiarity without faith.
13.27: kai erei legōn hymin, Ouk oida hymas pothen este; apostēte ap emou, pantes ergatai adikias — and he will say, speaking to you: I do not know you, where you are from; depart from me, all workers of iniquity — Ouk oida hymas pothen este (I do not know you, where you are from) repeated for emphasis: the denial of relationship is total. Ergatai adikias (workers of iniquity — a phrase from Ps 6:8 LXX: "depart from me all workers of iniquity") identifies those who claimed proximity to Jesus but practiced injustice as Psalm 6's evildoers. The citation grounds the saying in the Psalter's lament-tradition.
13.28: ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn — there will be the weeping and the gnashing of teeth — the stereotyped eschatological expression of grief and rage in final exclusion (Matthew uses it 6x; Luke only here). hotan opsēsthe Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb kai pantas tous prophētas en tē basileia tou Theou, hymas de ekballomenous exō — when you will see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God and yourselves thrown out — the excluded see the patriarchs inside the kingdom; the vision of inclusion they assumed for themselves belongs to others while they are ekballomenous exō (thrown outside).
13.29: kai hēxousin apo anatolōn kai dysmōn kai apo borra kai notou kai anaklithēsontai en tē basileia tou Theou — and they will come from east and west and from north and south and will recline at table in the kingdom of God — apo anatolōn kai dysmōn kai apo borra kai notou (from east and west and from north and south) is the four-direction formula for universal inclusion (cf. Isa 49:12; Ps 107:3; Zech 8:7). Anaklithēsontai (will recline at table) — the banquet posture; the kingdom is the eschatological feast where the unexpected guests from the margins of the world have places set for them. The reversal operates at the macro scale: those who thought themselves insiders will watch outsiders dine.
13.30: kai idou eisin eschatoi hoi esontai prōtoi kai eisin prōtoi hoi esontai eschatoi — and behold, there are last who will be first and first who will be last — the reversal aphorism (cf. 18:14; Mt 20:16; Mk 10:31) summarizes the kingdom's counter-intuitive economy. Eschatoi/prōtoi (last/first) applies both to the unexpected Gentile guests reclining in the kingdom (v.29) and to the presumptuous insiders excluded (v.28). The reversal is not guaranteed for everyone outside or denied to everyone inside; it is the description of the kingdom's surprise: ethnic and religious advantage does not convert to eschatological position without the faith-response.
13.31: En autē tē hōra prosēlthon tines Pharisaioi legontes autō, Exelthe kai poreuou enteuthen, hoti Hērōdēs thelei se apokteinai — at that very hour some Pharisees came saying to him: get out and go from here, because Herod wants to kill you — a surprising warning from Pharisees who are typically hostile (though some Pharisees are hospitable in Luke: 7:36; 11:37; 14:1). The warning may be genuine concern, may be a Herodian attempt to drive Jesus from Perea (Herod's territory), or may be both. Herod Antipas had already killed John the Baptist (3:19-20; 9:9) and had desired to see Jesus (9:9; 23:8).
13.32: kai eipen autois, Poreuthentes eipate tē alōpeki tautē — go and say to that fox — alōpēx (fox) is a term of contempt in both Greek and Jewish usage: cunning, destructive to small animals, not the noble lion. Jesus's public characterization of Herod as a fox is political: it names the petty-prince quality of Herod's threats. Idou ekballō daimonia kai iaseis apotelō sēmeron kai aurion kai tē tritē teleioumai — behold, I cast out demons and complete healings today and tomorrow and on the third day I am perfected — teleioumai (I am perfected, completed, brought to my goal — cf. Heb 2:10; 5:9; 12:23 for the same "perfection through suffering" language) is a Passion reference: the "third day" is the resurrection day that completes the journey.
13.33: plēn dei me sēmeron kai aurion kai tē echomenē poreuesthai — but it is necessary for me today and tomorrow and the next day to travel — dei (divine necessity — Luke's strongest compulsion word) appears again: the journey to Jerusalem is not chosen but appointed. hoti ouk endechetai prophētēn apolesthai exō Ierousalēm — because it is not acceptable for a prophet to perish outside Jerusalem — ouk endechetai (it is not acceptable, not possible, not fitting — an ironic statement) frames Jerusalem's murderous history with prophets as the destination for the prophet who must die. The irony is double: Jerusalem is simultaneously the city of the temple (God's presence) and the city that kills his messengers.
13.34: Ierousalēm, Ierousalēm, hē apokteinousa tous prophētas kai lithobologousa tous apestalmenous pros autēn — Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the one killing the prophets and stoning those sent to her — the double vocative (Ierousalēm, Ierousalēm) is the mourning address, the form of lament (cf. 2 Sam 18:33 "O Absalom, my son"; Jer 22:29 "O land, land, land"). Hē apokteinousa ... kai lithobologousa (the one killing ... and stoning) — present participles: the city is characterized by its persistent rejection of prophetic address. posakis ēthelēsa episynaxai ta tekna sou hon tropon ornis tēn heautēs nossian hypo tas pterygas — how often I desired to gather your children as a bird her nest under her wings — the maternal-bird image (Deut 32:11; Ps 91:4; Ruth 2:12; Isa 31:5) of YHWH's protective sheltering; Jesus speaks with divine longing.
13.35: kai ouk ēthelēsate — and you were not willing — the single counter to the divine desire: human refusal. The perfect freedom of rejection stands against the perfect compassion of the divine longing. idou aphietai hymin ho oikos hymōn — behold, your house is left to you desolate — ho oikos hymōn (your house) is the temple (cf. Jer 12:7; 22:5 LXX "your house is left desolate"); the divine presence departs from the house that rejects its Lord. legō de hymin, ou mē idēte me heōs [hēxei hote] eipēte, Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati Kyriou — I tell you, you will not see me until you say: Blessed is the one coming in the name of the Lord — Ps 118:26 (LXX), the Hallel Psalm quoted at the triumphal entry (19:38). The suspension of the divine presence ends when Jerusalem greets its King; the verse is simultaneously a judgment and a promise.

20.1: kai egeneto en mia tōn hēmerōn didasontos autou ton laon en tō hierō kai euangelizomenou — one of those days as he was teaching the people in the temple and proclaiming the good news — euangelizomenos (proclaiming good news) paired with didaskontos (teaching): the twin activities of Jesus's Jerusalem ministry. epestēsan hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis syn tois presbyterois — the chief priests and the scribes with the elders stood before him — the formal approach of the Jerusalem Sanhedrin: archiereis (high priests), grammateis (scribes), and presbyteroi (elders) constitute the three components of the Sanhedrin (cf. Mk 14:53; Acts 4:5). Their standing (epestēsan) before him in the temple court is an official confrontation.
20.2: kai eipan legontes pros auton, Eipov hēmin, en poia exousia tauta poieis, ē tis estin ho dous soi tēn exousian tautēn — they said to him: tell us by what authority you do these things, or who is the one who gave you this authority — the double-form of the question covers both the question of kind (en poia exousia = in what kind of authority) and the question of source (tis estin ho dous = who gave it). The temple cleansing (19:45-46) and the continuing teaching are both in view. Exousia (authority) — the same word that amazed the synagogue in Capernaum (4:32); now the Jerusalem leadership demands its authorization. Without rabbinic credentials, a priestly appointment, or official commission, Jesus's authority was unexplained by normal categories.
20.3: apokritheis de eipen pros autous, Erōtēsō hymas kagō logon, kai eipate moi — he answered and said to them: I also will ask you a word; tell me — the counter-question is the Socratic-rabbinic device of answering a question with a question. Kagō erōtēsō hymas (I also will ask you — the kai + egō is emphatic: I too, not just you, will ask) makes the exchange symmetrical and competitive. The counter-question is not evasion but a revelatory move: Jesus's question about John forces the authorities to reveal the epistemological framework that renders his authority question unanswerable.
20.4: To baptisma Iōannou ex ouranou ēn ē ex anthrōpōn — John's baptism — was it from heaven or from men? — the dilemma is perfectly constructed. Ex ouranou (from heaven) = divinely authorized (heaven is the Semitic circumlocution for God, cf. 15:18); ex anthrōpōn (from human origin) = self-claimed without divine commission. John's baptism was universally acknowledged as morally powerful (3:10-14; 7:29-30) and his credentials as a prophet were established. The question forces the Jerusalem establishment to take a position on divine authority — the same category in which Jesus operates.
20.5: hoi de synelogisanto pros heautous legontes hoti Ean eipōmen, Ex ouranou, erei, Dia ti ouk episteusate autō — they deliberated with one another saying: if we say, From heaven, he will say, Why did you not believe him? — synelogisanto (deliberated together — the compound prefix syn- indicates shared deliberation; logizomai = to calculate, reason; the council calculates its options). The first horn of the dilemma: affirming John's divine authorization immediately requires explaining why they did not respond to his baptism — a damning admission. Episteuein (to believe, trust, respond in faith) to John meant submitting to his call to repentance and his baptism.
20.6: ean de eipōmen, Ex anthrōpōn, ho laos hapas katalithasei hēmas — but if we say, From men, all the people will stone us — katalithasō (to stone, stone to death) — the crowd's conviction of John's prophetic status (3:15; 7:29-30) made any denial of his divine authorization dangerous. pepeismenoi gar eisin Iōannēn prophētēn einai — for they are convinced that John is a prophet — pepeismenos (perfect passive participle: having been persuaded and remaining convinced) — the crowd's conviction about John is settled; it cannot be moved by the authorities' denial. The second horn is politically fatal.
20.7: kai apekrithēsan mē eidenai pothen — they answered that they did not know where it was from — the authorities' non-answer is an admission of epistemological paralysis or dishonest calculation. They do not say "we don't know" because they are genuinely uncertain; they refuse to answer to preserve their political position. The public non-answer in the temple courts, before "all the people" (v.9), is a public humiliation: the recognized religious authorities of Israel cannot or will not answer a question about John's authority.
20.8: kai ho Iēsous eipen autois, Oude egō legō hymin en poia exousia tauta poiō — and Jesus said to them: neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things — the response is symmetrical: they will not answer his question; he will not answer theirs. But the exchange has already answered the question: if they cannot evaluate John's authority (which Jesus implicitly identifies as the same category as his own), they lack the framework to evaluate Jesus's authority. The non-answer is the answer: Jesus's authority is from heaven, as John's was — the source they refused to acknowledge.
20.9: Ērxato de pros ton laon legein tēn parabolēn tautēn — he began to tell the people this parable — the audience shifts from the authorities (vv.1-8) to the people (laos); the parable is told publicly, not privately. Anthrōpos ephyteusen ampelōna — a man planted a vineyard — the vineyard is Israel's classic OT symbol (Isa 5:1-7; Ps 80:8-16; Jer 2:21); the allusion is immediate for those familiar with the Isaianic "Song of the Vineyard." kai exedeto auton geōrgois — and rented it to farmers — geōrgoi (farmers, tenant-farmers) — the tenants who receive the vineyard on lease and owe the owner a share of the harvest; in the allegorical reading, the tenant-farmers are Israel's leaders responsible for the covenant community. kai apedēmēsen chronous hikanous — and went away for a long time.
20.10: kai en kairō apesteilen pros tous geōrgous doulon hina apo tou karpou tou ampelōnos dōsousin autō — at the proper time he sent a servant to the farmers so they would give him some of the fruit of the vineyard — en kairō (at the appointed time, the right time — harvest season) signals the legitimate expectation of return. The servant represents the prophetic emissaries sent to Israel throughout its history. hoi de geōrgoi exapesteilan auton deirantes kenon — but the farmers beat him and sent him away empty-handed — deirō (to beat, flay — the word for flogging; the same used in 12:47-48 for punishment) and kenon (empty — without the fruit owed). The pattern of prophetic rejection: sent, beaten, dismissed.
20.11: kai prosetheto heteron pempsa doulon, hoi de kakeinon deirantes kai atimasantes exapesteilan kenon — and he proceeded to send another servant; them also beating and dishonoring him they sent away empty-handed — prosetheto ... pempsa (he added, he proceeded to send — the idiomatic Lukan construction for sequential action; cf. 20:12). Atimasantes (dishonoring — treating with contempt, stripping of honor) adds a dimension beyond physical beating: the servant is publicly shamed, a serious violation in an honor-shame culture. The prophetic-rejection pattern continues: each emissary receives worse treatment.
20.12: kai prosetheto triton pempsa, hoi de kai touton traumatisantes exebalon — he proceeded to send a third, and they also wounding this one threw him out — traumatizō (to wound — from trauma, wound; a medical term Luke employs precisely) is a more severe injury than beating (deirō in vv.10-11); escalating violence. Exebalon (threw him out, cast him outside) removes him from the vineyard itself — the escalation from dismissal to expulsion. The three-servant sequence recalls the three-year search for fruit (13:7) and generally represents the full prophetic history of Israel.
20.13: eipen de ho kyrios tou ampelōnos, Ti poiēsō — the lord of the vineyard said: what shall I do? — the owner's self-deliberation (an internal monologue, as in the Rich Fool 12:17) reveals not weakness but the extraordinary patience of one who has extended every reasonable option. Pempsō ton huion mou ton agapēton — I will send my beloved son — ton huion ton agapēton (the beloved son) — the exact phrase from the baptism voice (3:22: su ei ho huios mou ho agapētos) and the Transfiguration (9:35: ho huios mou ho eklelegmenos). The allegorical identification is transparent: the owner's beloved son is the Son whom God sent. Isōs touton entrapēsontai — perhaps they will respect him (entrepō: to turn toward, show regard for, feel shame before).
20.14: idontes de auton hoi geōrgoi dielogizonto pros allēlous legontes, Houtos estin ho klēronomos — seeing him, the farmers deliberated with one another: this is the heir — dielogizonto (deliberated, reasoned among themselves — the sinister planning of those who recognize the heir and see an opportunity). Klēronomos (heir, inheritor — the one who will inherit the estate upon the owner's death) — the tenants' recognition of the heir is paradoxical: knowing who he is does not produce submission but determines the plan of murder. apokteinōmen auton, hina hēmōn genētai hē klēronomia — let us kill him so that the inheritance may become ours — the logic of seizure: remove the heir, become heirs yourselves. The greed of the tenants has become murderous.
20.15: kai ekbalontes auton exō tou ampelōnos apekteinan — throwing him out of the vineyard they killed him — the sequence is significant: ekbalontes exō tou ampelōnos (throwing him outside the vineyard) before killing recalls Heb 13:12 ("Jesus also suffered outside the gate to sanctify the people through his own blood") — crucified outside Jerusalem's walls. The allegory maps precisely: the son killed outside the vineyard = the Messiah crucified outside the city. ti oun poiēsei autois ho kyrios tou ampelōnos — what then will the lord of the vineyard do to them? — the question Jesus poses to the audience, inviting them to judge the tenants and thereby judge themselves.
20.16: eleusetai kai apolesei tous geōrgous toutous kai dōsei ton ampelōna allois — he will come and destroy those farmers and give the vineyard to others — the judgment on the tenants (the Jerusalem leadership) and the transfer of stewardship ("give to others") points to the post-resurrection mission: the vineyard's management passes from the temple establishment to the apostolic community. akousantes de eipan, Mē genoito — hearing this, they said: may it never be! — Mē genoito (may it not happen! — the strongest Greek negative wish, Paul's characteristic "by no means!" in Romans; LXX: mē genoito for "God forbid"). The audience recoils from the judgment they have just endorsed.
20.17: ho de emblepsas autois eipen, Ti oun estin to gegrammenon touto — looking at them he said: what then is this that is written? — emblepsas (looking intently, fixing his gaze on them) — the penetrating gaze that follows their protest. Lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomomountes, houtos egenēthē eis kephalēn gōnias — The stone which the builders rejected, this became the cornerstone — Ps 118:22 (LXX) — the rejection-of-the-builder's-stone is a wisdom paradox: the builders are experts in judging stone quality, yet the stone they discarded as unsuitable becomes the kephalē gōnias (head/top of the corner — either the cornerstone at the foundation or the capstone at the top of an arch; either way, the structurally critical stone). The Psalm citation was already used in v.38 (Ps 118:26); Jesus uses Psalm 118 as a unity.
20.18: pas ho pesōn ep ekeinon ton lithon synthlastesetai — everyone falling on that stone will be shattered — synthlō (to shatter, crush to pieces) — the stone that is the cornerstone is also a stone of stumbling (cf. Isa 8:14; Rom 9:32-33; 1 Pet 2:8). eph hon d an pesē, likmēsei auton — and on whomever it falls, it will crush him to powder — likmaō (to winnow, scatter like chaff; then, to grind to powder) — the image moves from stumbling to being crushed by a falling stone; both describe the catastrophic consequences of rejecting the Messiah-cornerstone. Dan 2:44 (the stone that grinds the statue to powder) is the background.
20.19: kai ezētēsan hoi grammateis kai hoi archiereis epibalein ep auton tas cheiras en autē tē hōra — the scribes and chief priests sought to lay hands on him in that very hour — the authorities recognize themselves as the tenants of the parable; the immediate consequence is intensified desire for arrest. En autē tē hōra (in that very hour) — the recognition of the allegory's target produces instant hostility. kai ephobēthēsan ton laon — and they feared the people — the same crowd-protection as 19:48; the popular regard for Jesus prevents open action. egnōsan gar hoti pros autous tēn parabolēn tautēn eipen — for they knew that he spoke this parable against them.
20.20: kai paratērēsantes apesteilan enkathetus hypokrinoumenous heautous dikaiious einai — watching closely they sent spies pretending to be righteous — enkathetos (a spy, a plant in the audience — literally "one set in place secretly"; the only NT use of this word) underlines the deliberate deception. Hypokrinoumenous heautous dikaiious einai (pretending to be righteous — hypokrinō = to play a role, act; they perform righteousness while pursuing entrapment). hina epilabōntai autou logou, hōste paradounai auton tē archē kai tē exousia tou hēgemonos — in order to catch him in a word, so as to deliver him to the authority and power of the governor — hēgemon (governor — the Roman Prefect/Procurator; Pontius Pilate) — the goal is to get a statement that constitutes maiestas (Roman treason).
20.21: kai epērōtēsan auton legontes, Didaskale, oidamen hoti orthōs legeis kai didaskeis kai ou lambaneis prosōpon — they questioned him saying: Teacher, we know that you speak and teach correctly and do not accept personal appearances — ou lambaneis prosōpon (you do not take up a face = you do not show partiality, you do not play favorites; prosōpolēpsia is the NT term for favoritism) — the flattery establishes what they hope he will demonstrate: honest speech regardless of political consequences. all ep alētheias tēn hodon tou Theou didaskeis — but you teach the way of God according to truth — tēn hodon tou Theou (the way of God) is used later for the Christian movement itself (Acts 9:2; 19:9,23; 22:4; 24:14,22).
20.22: Exestin hēmin Kaisari phoron dounai ē ou — is it lawful for us to pay taxes to Caesar or not? — phoros (tribute, direct tax — the provincial head-tax levied by Rome on subject peoples, distinct from the telos/toll which was indirect). The question is a political trap: answer "yes" and Jesus alienates the Jewish nationalist crowd who resented Roman tribute; answer "no" and he commits sedition against Rome. The Zealot movement had been founded on the principle that paying tribute to Caesar was treason against YHWH's sole kingship over Israel (Josephus, Ant. 18.23).
20.23: katanosas de autōn tēn panourgiav eipen pros autous — perceiving their craftiness he said to them — katanoeō (to perceive thoroughly, observe carefully — the same word for contemplating the ravens in 12:24 and the speck in 6:41) applied to the detection of malicious intent. Panourgia (craftiness, unscrupulous cunning — from pan + ergon: doing everything/anything to achieve one's end; a morally negative term in biblical usage, cf. 1 Cor 3:19; 2 Cor 4:2) — Jesus names their scheme before addressing it.
20.24: Deixate moi dēnarion; tinos echei eikona kai epigraphēn — show me a denarius; whose image and inscription does it have? — dēnarion (a Roman silver coin worth a day's wage for a laborer; the standard wage in Mt 20:2). The Tiberian denarius bore an obverse image (eikōn) of Tiberius Caesar with the inscription TI CAESAR DIVI AVG F AVGVSTVS (Tiberius Caesar, Son of the Divine Augustus) and a reverse image of the seated Pontifex Maximus. Eikōn kai epigraphē (image and inscription) — the coin carries both the ruler's portrait and his title. hoi de eipan, Kaisaros — they said: Caesar's.
20.25: ho de eipen pros autous, Toinyv apodote ta Kaisaros Kaisari kai ta tou Theou tō Theō — he said to them: then give back to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's — apodidōmi (to give back, return, pay what is owed — not merely "give" but "return what belongs to another") is the key verb: both Caesar's coin and God's image-bearers are to be returned to their rightful owners. The answer does not resolve the tension between political obligation and theological sovereignty but disarms the trap: using Caesar's coin implies operating within his economic system; operating within his system entails some obligation. The distinction between Caesar's sphere and God's sphere is left unstated — its content is the ongoing theological question.
20.26: kai ouk ischysan epilabesthai autou rēmatos enantov tou laou — they were unable to catch him in his word before the people — epilambanō (to catch, seize, lay hold of — here used for verbal trapping) — the trap failed. kai thaumasantes epi tē apokrisei autou esigesav — and marveling at his answer they fell silent — thaumazontes (marveling, astonished) indicates genuine surprise; the questioners had not anticipated the answer's elegance. Esigesan (they fell silent — aorist: a sudden complete silence) marks the end of this line of attack; the spies withdraw defeated.
20.27: Proselthontes de tines tōn Saddoukaiōn, hoi antilegontes anastasin mē einai — approaching, some of the Sadducees, who deny that there is a resurrection — Luke's parenthetical gloss on the Sadducees (antilegontes anastasin mē einai = saying in opposition that there is no resurrection) is a reader-orientation note for Gentile audiences unfamiliar with the Sadducean position. The Sadducees accepted only the written Torah (Pentateuch) as authoritative and rejected the Pharisaic oral tradition and doctrines of resurrection and angels (Acts 23:8). The resurrection debate places Jesus squarely in agreement with the Pharisees against the Sadducean aristocratic priesthood.
20.28: epērōtēsan auton legontes, Didaskale, Mōysēs egrapsen hēmin — questioned him saying: Teacher, Moses wrote for us — the opening invocation of Moses's written authority signals Sadducean method: scripture (specifically Torah) is the basis of the argument. ean tinos adelphos apothanē echōn gynaika kai houtos ateknos ē, hina labē ho adelphos autou tēn gynaika kai exanastēsē sperma tō adelphō autou — if a man's brother dies having a wife and he is childless, the brother should take the wife and raise up offspring for his brother — Deut 25:5-10 (levirate marriage, yibbum): the obligation of the surviving brother to marry the widow and produce an heir for the deceased brother's name.
20.29: hepta oun adelphoi ēsan — there were seven brothers — the number seven (hepta) is deliberately extreme: a maximally absurd scenario designed to expose the resurrection doctrine as logically incoherent within their reductio ad absurdum. The Sadducees construct a case that makes resurrection appear to violate Mosaic law (a woman cannot simultaneously be married to seven husbands). kai ho prōtos labōn gynaika apethanen ateknos — and the first married a woman and died childless — ateknos (childless, without offspring — from a- + teknon) activates the levirate obligation for the second brother.
20.30: kai ho deuteros — and the second — Luke's brief notation (vs. Matthew and Mark's slightly fuller version) of the second brother's marriage and death. The pattern is established by the first brother and repeated six times: marry, die childless, leaving the widow for the next. Luke's compressed account of brothers two through six (vv.30-31) is characteristic of his editorial economy when the point is established; the reader understands the pattern without full repetition.
20.31: kai ho tritos elaben autēn, hōsautōs de kai hoi hepta ou katélipon tekna kai apethanon — the third took her, and likewise also all seven left no children and died — hōsautōs (in the same way, likewise) abbreviates the repetition; the pattern holds through all seven brothers. Ou katelipon tekna (left no children) — the levirate obligation was never fulfilled; no heir was produced. The scenario is designed to be maximally embarrassing for resurrection doctrine: if she is resurrected, she simultaneously belongs to seven husbands under Moses's law.
20.32: hysteron kai hē gynē apethanen — finally the woman also died — the widow's death (last in the sequence) completes the scenario. All eight principals (seven brothers + the widow) have died, creating the theoretical resurrection problem. The Sadducean question assumes: (a) resurrection involves resurrection of the same relational configuration as earthly life, and (b) therefore levirate marriage creates an insoluble legal problem in the resurrection. Both assumptions Jesus will correct.
20.33: hē gynē oun en tē anastasei, tinos autōn ginetai gynē — in the resurrection therefore, whose wife will she be? for the seven had her as wife — the question is the payoff of the elaborate scenario: tinos autōn ginetai gynē (of which of them does she become wife? — the present ginetai makes it an ongoing relational question, not merely a legal technicality). The scenario assumes that resurrection implies continuation of earthly legal statuses; the answer will challenge this assumption fundamentally.
20.34: kai eipen autois ho Iēsous, Hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou gamousin kai gamiskontai — Jesus said to them: the sons of this age marry and are given in marriage — hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou (the sons of this age — Lukan term for those whose life is defined by the present age, as opposed to the sons of the coming age in v.35). Gamousin kai gamiskontai (marry and are given in marriage — the active and passive of the wedding: men take wives; women are given by fathers) — marriage is the institutional structure of the present mortal age, necessary for biological continuity through death.
20.35: hoi de kataxiōthentes tou aiōnos ekeinou tychein kai tēs anastaseōs tēs ek nekrōn — but those who are counted worthy of that age and of the resurrection from the dead — kataxiōthentes (those counted worthy, deemed worthy — the divine passive indicating God's judgment of fitness for the age to come) — the resurrection life is not automatic or universal in Jesus's framing but a qualification (axios = worthy; the same root as in Mt 10:37-38). oute gamousin oute gamizontai — neither marry nor are given in marriage — the resurrection life does not require marriage because its biological-continuity function (producing offspring to carry on after death) is rendered obsolete when death itself is abolished (v.36).
20.36: oude gar apothanein eti dynantai, isangeloi gar eisin kai huioi eisin Theou tēs anastaseōs huioi ontes — for they can no longer die; for they are like angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection — isangeloi (like/equal to angels — a compound hapax in the NT; the only NT description of the resurrection state as angel-like) — not that humans become angels but that they share the angels' deathlessness. Apothanein eti dynantai (they can no longer die) — the abolition of death (1 Cor 15:26; Rev 21:4) removes the need for marriage's biological function. Huioi Theou (sons of God) — in the resurrection, the divine-adoption that believers receive now (Rom 8:14-17; Gal 4:6-7) reaches its fullness.
20.37: hoti de egeirontai hoi nekroi, kai Mōysēs emēnusen epi tēs batou, hōs legei Kyrion ton Theon Abraam kai Theon Isaak kai Theon Iakōb — that the dead are raised, even Moses showed in the passage about the burning bush, as he calls the Lord the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — Jesus uses Sadducean methodology against them: they accept only Moses; Moses himself teaches resurrection. The burning bush passage is Exod 3:6. The argument is grammatical and theological: YHWH identifies himself in the present tense (egō eimi) as the God of three patriarchs who were already long dead by Moses's time. YHWH's self-identification with the living patriarchs implies they are alive to him.
20.38: theos de ouk estin nekrōn alla zōntōn, pantes gar autō zōsin — he is not the God of the dead but of the living, for all are alive to him — the theological principle derived from Exod 3:6: YHWH's covenant relationship with the patriarchs is a present reality, not merely a historical record. Pantes gar autō zōsin (for all are alive to him) — the phrase may mean "all will be alive in his sight at the resurrection" (proleptic) or "all are already alive in his perspective" (eternal present); both support the resurrection. The argument is from the nature of God as covenant-keeper: a God who identifies himself by his relationship with people maintains that relationship, including through death.
20.39: apokrithentes de tines tōn grammateōn eipan, Didaskale, kalōs eipas — some of the scribes answered: Teacher, well said — the scribes' approval (kalōs eipas = you spoke well, beautifully) reflects the Pharisaic scribes' agreement with the resurrection teaching against the Sadducees. The cross-party dynamics of Jerusalem: the scribes (typically Pharisaic) enjoy Jesus's defeat of the Sadducees. The momentary alliance of scribal approval does not mean acceptance of Jesus as Messiah; it means the argument about resurrection was compelling and scored against a theological opponent.
20.40: ouketi gar etolmōn eperōtan auton ouden — for they no longer dared to ask him anything — etolmaō (to dare, have the courage — implying that questions now involved too much risk of public defeat; the authorities' line of interrogation has been exhausted). Three challenges have failed: the authority question (vv.1-8), the tax question (vv.20-26), and the resurrection question (vv.27-38). Jesus has answered all three in ways that expose his questioners and silence further inquiry. The silence signals the shift of initiative: Jesus now asks the question (v.41).
20.41: Eipen de pros autous, Pōs legousin ton Christon einai Dauid huion — he said to them: how do they say the Messiah is David's son? — Jesus now takes the offensive with a Christological question about David's descendant. Pōs legousin (how do they say) — the impersonal "they" refers to scribal teaching; the expectation was that the Messiah would be a Davidic descendant (2 Sam 7:12-16; Isa 11:1-10; Jer 23:5-6; Ezek 37:24-25). This is not in dispute; what Jesus raises is whether "son of David" adequately describes the Messiah's nature and identity.
20.42: autos gar Dauid legei en biblō Psalmōn — for David himself says in the Book of Psalms — en biblō Psalmōn (in the Book of Psalms — Luke uses this fuller designation vs. Matthew/Mark's "David himself said in the Spirit"). Ps 110:1 is the most frequently cited OT text in the NT (Mt 22:44; Mk 12:36; Acts 2:34-35; Heb 1:13; 10:13; 1 Pet 3:22; Rev 3:21). Eipen Kyrios tō Kyriō mou, Kathou ek dexiōn mou — the Lord said to my Lord: sit at my right hand — Kyrios ... tō Kyriō mou — the LXX renders YHWH as Kyrios and Adonai also as Kyrios; David's "my Lord" (his Adonai) is thus addressed by YHWH himself, creating two divine referents.
20.43: heōs an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou — until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet — Ps 110:1b — the footstool image (hypopodion) is the posture of total subjection: conquered enemies placed under the feet of the victor (Josh 10:24; Ps 8:6). The waiting posture ("sit at my right hand until") implies an interim period between the enthronement and the completion of the subjection — precisely the period of the church's mission in Acts. The enemies' subjection is the Father's action, not the enthroned Son's; the Son waits and reigns while the Father acts.
20.44: Dauid oun auton Kyrion kalei, kai pōs autou huios estin — David therefore calls him Lord; how then is he his son? — the dilemma Jesus poses: if David calls the Messiah "my Lord" (Kyrios), the Messiah cannot be merely David's descendant in the ordinary sense (descendants were never "lord" over their ancestors in ancient Near Eastern culture; the social flow ran the other direction). The question does not deny Davidic descent (Luke's genealogy has just established it, 3:31) but insists that Davidic sonship does not exhaust the Messiah's identity. Jesus does not answer the question; he leaves the Christological implication for the audience to resolve.
20.45: Akouontos de pantos tou laou eipen tois mathētais autou — while all the people were listening he said to his disciples — the public setting ("all the people listening") and the directed address ("to his disciples") creates a dual audience: the warning about the scribes is given to disciples but intended for the crowd to overhear. Pantos tou laou (all the people) is the full Jerusalem crowd that has been listening throughout the temple controversy.
20.46: Prosechete apo tōn grammateōn tōn thelontōn peripatein en stolais kai philountōn aspasmous en tais agorais kai prōtokathedrias en tais synagōgais kai prōtoklisias en tois deipnois — beware of the scribes who like to walk around in long robes and love greetings in the marketplaces and the first seats in the synagogues and the first places at banquets — three markers of honor-seeking: stolai (long, flowing robes — the distinctive dress of scribal scholars that signaled status; cf. Mk 12:38), aspasmoi en tais agorais (greetings/salutations in the public squares — public deferential address), prōtokathedriai (first/front seats of honor in synagogues) and prōtoklisiai (first reclining-places, the host's-right positions at banquets — the highest-honor dining positions).
20.47: hoi katesthiousin tas oikias tōn chērōn kai prophasei makra proseuchontai — who devour the houses of widows and for a pretext make long prayers — katesthiousin tas oikias tōn chērōn (devour the houses of widows) — the consumption of widows' estates while acting as religious advisors or executors of estates; a form of economic exploitation beneath a veneer of piety (cf. Isa 10:2; Ezek 22:25). Prophasis (pretext, cover, facade — prayer used as a reputation-building display rather than sincere address to God). houtoi lēpsontai perissoteron krima — these will receive greater judgment — perissoteron krima (more judgment, greater condemnation — the proportional accountability principle of 12:47-48 applied to religious leaders who exploit their position). The scribal warning closes the Jerusalem controversy section.

14.1: en tō elthein auton eis oikon tinos tōn archontōn tōn Pharisaiōn sabbatō phagein arton — the construction en tō + infinitive is Luke's favored temporal frame (also 3:21; 9:18; 9:29); here it introduces a Sabbath dinner at the house of a ruling Pharisee. Archontōn (rulers, leaders — from archō = rule) denotes a senior Pharisee; this is not an ordinary householder but a prominent figure in the local community. autoi ēsan paratērumenoi auton — they were watching him — paratēreō (watch carefully, observe closely — from para- + tēreō = keep, guard; watching alongside) has a hostile surveillance nuance throughout Luke (6:7; 20:20); they are monitoring for violations, not simply curious.
14.2: anthrōpos tis ēn hydrōpikos — a certain man was dropsical — hydrōpikos (suffering from dropsy, abnormal fluid retention — from hydōr = water) is a hapax legomenon in the NT; this is the only occurrence of the term in the entire NT. Luke's medical vocabulary (cf. puretos at 4:38-39; lepra at 5:12; paralytikos at 5:24) reflects the tradition of his medical interest. Dropsy (hydrops in ancient medicine) is excess fluid in the body, often a symptom of cardiac, renal, or liver failure; visible, chronic, and socially limiting in an honor-shame society.
14.3: exestin tō sabbatō therapeusai ē ou — is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath or not — Jesus's direct question short-circuits the trap; instead of acting first and being accused, he poses the legal question to the legal experts. Exesti (it is lawful, permissible) is the standard Sabbath-dispute verb (also 6:2, 4, 9; 14:3); the question places the burden of answer on the Pharisees. The ē ou (or not) forces a binary judgment where any answer creates legal difficulty for them.
14.4: hoi de hēsychasan — they remained silent — hēsychasan (aorist of hēsychazō = be quiet, be still; also 23:56 for the women resting on the Sabbath) signals deliberate non-response. The legal experts who are there to trap Jesus cannot answer his question without entangling themselves. epilabomenos iāsato auton kai apelysen — taking hold, he healed him and released him — epilambanō (take hold of, grasp) is the deliberate physical contact that initiates healing; apolyō (release, let go, send away) dismisses the man after healing, ending the episode cleanly.
14.5: tinos hymōn huios ē bous eis phrear peseitai — whose son or ox falls into a well — the a fortiori argument (qal wahomer): if you rescue an animal on the Sabbath, how much more rescue a person? Textual variants exist: some manuscripts read onos (donkey) instead of huios (son); the donkey-ox pair matches Matt 12:11 more closely, while son-ox is the harder reading (more dramatic and therefore more likely original). Phrear (pit, well — also John 4:11-12 for Jacob's well) is the specific emergency that Sabbath law already acknowledged: danger to life supersedes Sabbath restrictions.
14.6: kai ouk ischusan antapokrithēnai pros tauta — they were not able to reply against these things — antapokrinomai (answer in return, reply against — compound: anti- + apo- + krinomai) describes a formal legal counter-argument; they cannot mount one. Pros tauta (against these things) — the demonstration of Sabbath mercy logic has left them without legal ground. The silence of v.4 (strategic) and the inability of v.6 (logical) together constitute Luke's portrait of religious expertise that cannot answer Jesus.
14.7: elegon de pros tous keklēmenous parabolēn — he told a parable to those who were invited — parabolē (parable — from paraballō = throw alongside, compare) is the comparison-teaching form. epechōn pōs tas prōtoklisias exelegonto — noticing how they chose the places of honor — epechō (hold toward, pay attention to) + prōtoklisia (first-reclining-place, place of honor at table — from prōtos + klisia = first leaning-position); the choice of table position is an honor-competition visible to all observers. The dinner table in Mediterranean antiquity is a theater of social rank.
14.8: mē kataklithēs eis tēn prōtoklisian — do not recline at the place of honor — kataklithēs (aorist passive subjunctive of kataklinō = make recline, recline) is the formal dining posture. Mēpote entimoteros sou ē keklēmenos hyp autou — lest a more honorable person than you has been invited — entimos (honored, esteemed — from en + timē = in honor) frames the social comparison: one's position at table must match one's social ranking, and presuming too high a position creates public shame when corrected.
14.9: dos toutō topon — give this person your place — topos (place) is both the physical seat and the social position; the displacement is public and shaming. meta aischunēs ton eschaton topon katechein — to take the last place with shame — aischunē (shame, disgrace — the social negative of timē/honor) is the emotion of public dishonoring; eschaton topon (last place) is the social nadir. The sequence is: presumption → public correction → shame, the honor-shame culture's social worst-case.
14.10: anabase epanō — move up higher — the ascending movement (anabainō = go up) in response to the host's invitation reverses the shame dynamic. tote estai soi doxa — then there will be glory/honor for you — doxa (glory, honor, repute — the highest honor-word) in the presence of all the other guests (enōpion pantōn tōn sunanakeimenenōn); the public scope of the honor is what makes it worth having. The parable maps the honor-culture's logic but redirects it: humble positioning before the host, not strategic self-promotion, wins honor.
14.11: hoti pas ho hypsōn heauton tapeinōthēsetai kai ho tapeinōn heauton hypsōthēsetai — for everyone who exalts himself will be humbled and everyone who humbles himself will be exalted — this is Luke's recurring reversal formula (also 18:14; cf. 1:52); it occurs in Matthew without the pas (all/everyone). Hypsoō (exalt, lift high) and tapeinoō (humble, lower) are antonymous divine-action verbs in the LXX (Ezek 21:26; Job 5:11; Ps 75:7); the divine passive (future passives = God acts) declares that the reversals are not social happenstance but divine governance.
14.12: mē phōnei tous philous sou mēde tous adelphous sou — do not invite your friends or your brothers — Jesus redefines the logic of hospitality from reciprocal exchange to non-reciprocal gift. The standard hospitality pattern in Mediterranean culture was charis-exchange: give to receive; the invited guest becomes socially obligated (antapodoma soi genētai = there will be a return-gift to you). Jesus's counter-instruction breaks the reciprocity cycle that structures the social order of honor exchange.
14.13: ptōchous anapeirous chōlous typhlous kale — invite the poor, crippled, lame, blind — the four categories (ptōchos, anapeiros, chōlos, typhlos) are the same grouping that appear in the Great Banquet parable (v.21) and mirror the eschatological inclusion list of Isa 35:5-6 and Isa 61:1-2. Anapeiros (crippled, disabled — from ana- + pēros = maimed) appears only here and v.21 in the NT; it denotes a disability that prevents participation in normal social and economic life.
14.14: makarios de esē hoti ouk echousin antapodounai soi — blessed are you because they cannot repay you — the beatitude (makarios) reframes the hospitality calculation: absence of repayment-ability is the condition of blessing, not a liability. antapodothēsetai gar soi en tē anastasei tōn dikaiōn — it will be repaid to you at the resurrection of the righteous — antapodidōmi (repay, recompense — compound of anti- + apo- + didōmi) is the reciprocity verb, but relocated to the eschaton: the divine repayment at the resurrection replaces the immediate social return.
