Anastasis tōn dikaiōn (resurrection of the righteous) reflects the Jewish apocalyptic expectation of a selective first resurrection (Dan 12:2; Acts 24:15).
14.15: makarios hostis phagetai arton en tē basileia tou theou — blessed is the one who will eat bread in the kingdom of God — the table-guest's exclamation combines the makarios-formula with the kingdom-feast motif (Isa 25:6; also 13:29); it is a pious cliché that Jesus will immediately reframe. Phagetai arton (eat bread — idiomatic for "share a meal") in the eschatological context echoes the messianic banquet tradition in Second Temple Jewish texts (1 Enoch 62:14; 4 Ezra 7:36). Jesus's parable response implies: the question is not whether the feast happens but whether the invited will show up.
14.16: anthrōpos tis epoiei deipnon mega kai ekalesen pollous — a certain man made a great banquet and invited many — deipnon (the main meal, dinner — the evening banquet; contrast ariston = morning meal) is the formal feast. The mega (great) qualifier signals extravagance and preparation. Pollous (many) identifies the invited group as a large, pre-arranged guest list; in Middle Eastern practice, formal invitations were extended in advance, with a second summons at the time of the meal to confirm attendance.
14.17: erchou hoti ēdē hetoima estin panta — come, because everything is now ready — the two-stage invitation structure (invitation in advance + the servant's summons at serving-time) is the standard of formal ancient hospitality; the second summons is the moment of actual commitment. Refusing the second summons after accepting the first is a serious social offense — it dishonors the host publicly. Ēdē hetoima (already ready) emphasizes that the delay is purely on the guests' side; the feast is prepared and waiting.
14.18: kai ērxanto apo mias pantes paraiteisthai — they all alike began to make excuses — apo mias (from one, all at once — possibly mias gnōmēs = one mind; from one voice/accord) is a Semitic idiom for unanimity. Paraiteisthai (beg off, excuse oneself — from para- + aiteomai = request alongside; to ask to be excused) is the polite refusal verb. The first excuse: agron ēgorasa (I bought a field) + exelthein idein (I need to go see it) — the field was already purchased; this is not urgent, merely convenient.
14.19: zeugē boōn ēgorasa pente kai poreuomai dokimasai auta — I bought five yoke of oxen and I'm going to try them out — zeugē boōn (yoke of oxen — zeugos = paired/yoked; a yoke of oxen is a working pair) × 5 = ten oxen, a large agricultural investment. Dokimasai (test, examine, prove — from dokimazō; the word for evaluating a coin's genuineness) implies field-testing new equipment. The purchase was already made; the testing is post-hoc rather than time-critical. The excuse pattern mirrors the first: acquisition already complete, inspection merely convenient.
14.20: gynaika egēma kai dia touto ou dunamai elthein — I married a wife and therefore I cannot come — the third excuse inverts the polite formula of the first two (the others apologized: erōtō se, echē me parēitēmenon = please have me excused); this one is a flat refusal without apology. Deut 20:7 and 24:5 exempted a newly married man from military service, but a private banquet is not war. The marriage excuse is the most brazen: no apology offered, no return-commitment made. The escalating rudeness of the three excuses narrates the guests' growing disrespect for the host.
14.21: tote orgistheis ho oikodespotēs eipen tō doulō autou — the master became angry and said to his servant — orgizō (be angry — the full-anger word; also Matt 18:34; 22:7) signals that the honor violation is complete. exelthe tacheōs eis tas plateias kai rhumas tēs poleōs — go out quickly into the streets and alleys of the city — plateia (broad street, main avenue) vs rhymē (narrow alley, lane — the back streets); the servant is to scour both the public main streets and the private back alleys. The replacement guests are the same four categories as v.13: poor, crippled, blind, lame — the socially excluded become the feast's recipients.
14.22: gegonen ho epetaxas kai eti topos estin — what you ordered has been done and there is still room — topos (place, room, space) — the banquet hall has more capacity than the replacement guests have filled; the servant reports both compliance and remaining availability. The ongoing vacancy is the narrative setup for the third dispatch (v.23), expanding the invitation beyond the city to the roads and countryside. The feast's generosity exceeds even the first replacement population.
14.23: anankason eiselthein — compel them to come in — anankason (aorist active imperative of anankazō = compel, force, constrain — from anankē = necessity) is the disputed word. In the patristic period Augustine cited this verse for compelling heretics into the church; Calvin and others rejected this use. The Greek sense is "urge strongly, press insistently" — the cultural background is the Middle Eastern hospitality dynamic where hosts must overcome their guests' polite refusals with persistent invitation. Hina gemisthē mou ho oikos — so that my house will be full — the goal is fullness, abundance, the eschatological feast completed.
14.24: oudeis tōn andrōn ekeinōn tōn keklēmenōn geusetal mou tou deipnou — not one of those men who were invited will taste my banquet — geusasthai (taste, experience — from geuomai) with the genitive tou deipnou: taste of my banquet (partitive genitive). The guests who were initially invited and refused are now permanently excluded from what they declined. The Lukan context makes the parable's referent clear: the Jewish leadership who have been invited to the kingdom feast and refused will not participate, while those they considered socially unworthy fill the hall.
14.25: suneporeuonto de autō ochloi polloi — large crowds were travelling with him — the transitional verse marks a shift of audience from the Pharisee's dinner table to the road crowds. Suneporeuomai (travel together with — compound of sun- + poreuomai) implies the crowds are joining the journey movement; this is the Travel Narrative context (9:51ff). Strapheis eipen pros autous — turning he said to them — Luke's characteristic physical gesture-before-teaching (strephō = turn, also 7:9; 9:55; 10:23); the turn signals a direct and personally addressed teaching.
14.26: ei tis erchetai pros me kai ou misei ton patera heautou kai tēn mētera — if anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother — misei (hates — present indicative of miseō) is the strong word; its Semitic idiom background (comparative love: "hate" = "love less") is confirmed by the Matthew parallel (10:37: ho philōn patera ē mētera hyper eme = "who loves father or mother more than me"). The MKT "hate" renders the Aramaic underlying idiom faithfully while the Matthean version interprets it. Eti te kai tēn psychēn heautou — yes, even his own life — psychē (life, self) is added to the family list; self-attachment is the final attachment to be relativized by attachment to Jesus.
14.27: hostis ou bastazei ton stauron heautou — whoever does not carry his own cross — bastazō (carry, bear — the carrying verb for burdens; cf. Gal 6:5; John 19:17 for Jesus carrying his cross) rather than Luke 9:23's airō (take up); both verbs describe cross-bearing but with different emphases: airō = taking up, bastazō = sustained carrying. The cross-bearing here is the discipleship condition stated before the two parables of cost-counting (tower, king); it frames the calculations as the concrete form of cross-bearing.
14.28: tis ex hymōn thelōn purgon oikodomēsai — who among you, wanting to build a tower — purgos (tower — a watchtower, defensive tower, or tall farm-structure for guarding fields) as the construction project; the point is not architectural grandeur but any project requiring advance resource assessment. Kathisas prōton psēphizei tēn dapanēn — first sitting down counts the cost — psēphizō (count, calculate — from psēphos = pebble, voting-stone, used as a counting-token) is the financial calculation verb; dapanē (cost, expense — from dapanaō = spend, consume) is the sum to be counted before beginning.
14.29: hina mēpote thentos autou themelion kai mē ischuontos ektelesai — lest after laying the foundation and not being able to finish — themelion (foundation — from themis = law, established order; the base on which something stands) + ektelesai (finish completely — intensive compound of ek- + teleō = complete thoroughly). The incomplete tower is the parable's embarrassing failure case; the Lukan context suggests that half-committed discipleship — starting without counting the cost — produces the same visible failure.
14.30: houtos ho anthrōpos ērxato oikodomein kai ouk ischusen ektelesai — this man began to build and was not able to finish — the imperfect ērxato (began — past inception) + aorist ischusen (was able) creates the completed failure: he started (imperfect progress) but could not finish (aorist finality). Empaizein autō — mock him — empaizō (mock, ridicule — from en + paizō = play; playing at someone's expense) is the honor-destruction verb; the same word is used for the soldiers mocking Jesus (22:63; 23:11, 36).
14.31: ē tis basileus poreuomenos hetō basilei symbalein eis polemon — or what king, going to engage another king in war — the second parable of calculation shifts from private construction to royal military strategy. Symballō (engage, meet — literally "throw together"; the military idiom is "engage in battle") with eis polemon (into war). Ei dynatai en deka chiliasin hypantēsai tō meta eikosi chiliadeōn — whether he is able with ten thousand to meet the one coming with twenty thousand — the force ratio (1:2) makes the calculation concrete; the weaker king must assess before the armies meet.
14.32: ei de mēge, eti autou porrō ontos presbeian aposteilas erōta ta pros eirēnēn — if not, while the other is still far away, sending an embassy he asks for peace terms — presbeia (embassy, delegation — from presbys = old, elder; an embassy of elders) + aposteilas (having sent) + erōtaō ta pros eirēnēn (ask for the things toward peace). The king who cannot win asks for terms before the battle; the discipleship application is that following Jesus requires calculating whether one can sustain what is required — and if not, to surrender fully rather than partially.
14.33: pas ex hymōn hos ouk apotassetai pasin tois heautou hyparchousin — everyone who does not renounce all his own possessions — apotassomai (renounce, take leave of, bid farewell to — from apo- + tassō = put in order; to arrange for departure, hence renounce/relinquish) appears also in 9:61 (the third would-be disciple asking to say goodbye). Tois heautou hyparchousin (all his own belongings — hyparchō = exist, belong to) is the possessions-word used throughout Luke (8:3; 12:15, 33, 44; 16:1; 19:8). The application lands harder than the parables: the tower-builder counts the cost before building; the disciple must count the cost and then relinquish everything.
14.34: kalon oun to halas, ean de kai to halas mōranthē — salt is good, but if the salt becomes foolish/tasteless — halas (salt — the Greek word; also hals in some forms) is the agricultural and preservation material; in Galilee it was often impure, mixed with other minerals, and could lose its sodium chloride content through leaching, leaving only the tasteless mineral residue. Mōrainō (become foolish, become tasteless — from mōros = foolish, dull; the same root as English "moron") is the double-meaning word: salt that loses its saltiness has become "foolish" — the metaphor and the moral judgment are encoded in the same word.
14.35: oute eis gēn oute eis koprian eutheton estin — it is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile — euthetos (fit, suitable, useful — from eu- + tithēmi = well-placed) with the double negative: tasteless salt fails at both its primary use (seasoning, preservation) and as a soil amendment (salt was sometimes used in compost in small quantities, but insipid mineral residue has no value). ho echōn ōta akouein akouetō — whoever has ears to hear, let them hear — Luke's standard call-to-attention formula (also 8:8; 13:9); the salt metaphor concludes the discipleship section of chapter 14 with the same urgency-marker as the parable section of chapter 8.

16.1: oikonomos — manager, steward — oikonomos (manager, steward of a household — from oikos = house + nomos = law/management; the one who manages the house's affairs; from which "economy") is the key term for the entire parable. The manager does not own the property but is entrusted to administer it; his accountability to the master is the parable's framing tension. Diaskorpizōn ta hyparchonta autou — squandering his possessions — the same diaskorpizō (scatter, squander) used for the prodigal son's waste (15:13); the parable is deliberately set after the prodigal and echoes its vocabulary.
16.2: apodos ton logon tēs oikonomias sou — give an account of your management — logon tēs oikonomias (account of the stewardship — logos = word, account, reckoning; oikonomia = management, stewardship; from which "economy") is the formal audit demand. Ouketi dunasai oikonomein — you can no longer be manager — the dismissal is announced before the account is given, based on the accusation alone. The manager's crisis is not whether the accusation is true but what to do now that his position is ending: the parable's cleverness lies in the manager's resourceful response to irreversible termination.
16.3: ti poiēsō, hoti ho kyrios mou aphaireitai tēn oikonomian ap emou — what shall I do, since my master is taking away the management from me — the interior monologue (eipen en heautō = said to himself; also 12:17 for the rich fool; 15:17 for the prodigal) is Luke's device for revealing the character's reasoning process. Skaptein ouk ischuō, epaitein aischunomai — I cannot dig, I am ashamed to beg — skaptō (dig — manual labor; the manager is a white-collar figure, not a field-worker) + epaitō (beg — from epi- + aitō = ask for; beg insistently); the two rejected options frame his social position: above manual labor by profession but too proud for begging.
16.4: egnōn ti poiēsō — I know what I will do — the manager's decision arrives as a flash of insight (egnōn = I know/came to know — aorist of ginōskō; the moment of recognition). Hina hotan metastathō tēs oikonomias dexōntai me eis tous oikous autōn — so that when I am removed from the stewardship they will receive me into their homes — metastathō (when I am removed — aorist passive subjunctive of methistēmi = remove, displace; also Acts 13:22 for removing Saul) + dechomai eis oikous (receive into homes — the hospitality word): by generating debtor-gratitude before dismissal, he secures future hospitality.
16.5: proskalesmenos hena hekaston tōn chreopheiletōn tou kyriou heautou — calling in each one of his master's debtors — chreopheiletēs (debtor — from chreos = debt + opheiletēs = one who owes; the compound = one who owes a debt; hapax in Luke, also 7:41 in the two-debtors parable). The manager calls each debtor privately (hena hekaston = each one, one by one) — the process is individual and confidential, suggesting the manager wants to minimize visibility of his scheme. Posō opheileis tō kyriō mou — how much do you owe my master — the inquiry is the setup for the reduction.
16.6: hekaton batous elaiou... pentēkonta — a hundred measures of oil... fifty — batos (bath — a Hebrew liquid measure, approx. 8-9 gallons / 30-35 liters; 100 baths ≈ 800+ gallons of olive oil, a large commercial quantity). The reduction from 100 to 50 is 50% — a massive forgiveness of debt. Dexai sou ta grammata kai kathisas tacheōs grapson pentēkonta — take your bill, sit down quickly, and write fifty — gramma (written document, bill — from graphō; the bond or promissory note that establishes the debt) must be rewritten by the debtor himself (not the manager), which may be important legally — the debtor re-writes the obligation and thus creates a new document.
16.7: hekaton korous sitou — a hundred kors of wheat — koros (cor — a Hebrew dry measure, approx. 10-12 bushels; 100 cors ≈ 1000-1200 bushels of wheat, a very large agricultural quantity). The reduction to 80 (20% less than oil's 50% reduction) may reflect different interest rates on the two commodities, or different profit margins the manager had taken. Various interpretations exist: (1) the debts represent illegal interest (usury) that the manager removes, legitimizing the reduction; (2) the manager forgoes his own commission, which was built into the inflated debt; (3) simple forgiveness to generate gratitude.
16.8: epēnesen ho kyrios ton oikonomon tēs adikias — the master commended the dishonest manager — the interpretive crux: who is ho kyrios (the master)? If the master of the parable, the commendation is for shrewdness in crisis management; if Jesus himself is speaking as kyrios, it is direct authorial commendation. Phronimōs epoiēsen — acted shrewdly — phronimōs (shrewdly, wisely — from phronimos = prudent, practical-minded; the "shrewd" word in the parable of the ten virgins, Matt 25:2-9) is the virtue of practical intelligence, not ethical virtue. Hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou phronimōteroi... eisin — the sons of this age are shrewder than the sons of light — the comparison is pragmatic: worldly people apply more ingenuity to their material affairs than believers do to their eternal affairs.
16.9: poiēsate heautois philous ek tou mamōna tēs adikias — make friends for yourselves from the mammon of unrighteousness — mamōnas (Mammon — Aramaic loanword māmōnāʾ = wealth, money, property; appears only at Matt 6:24 and Luke 16:9, 11, 13 in the NT; not a proper name or deity in Second Temple Judaism but an Aramaic term for material wealth that Jesus uses as if it were a master, hence the personification). Tēs adikias (of unrighteousness — the genitive characterizes the nature of worldly wealth: it belongs to the unrighteous age and participates in systemic injustice). Skēnas aiōnious (eternal tents/dwellings — skēnē = tent; the eternal version echoes the heavenly dwelling).
16.10: ho pistos en elachistō kai en pollō pistos estin — the faithful in very little is also faithful in much — the faithfulness principle: pistos (faithful, trustworthy — from pisteō = trust, believe; pistos as an adjective of character = one who can be trusted) in the elachistō (least, smallest — superlative of mikros) predicts faithfulness in pollō (much). The logic is character-consistency: how one handles small matters reveals how one will handle large ones. The faithful-unfaithful antithesis repeats the manager's failure: he was unfaithful in small things (embezzlement) and would be unfaithful in large things (true wealth).
16.11: ei oun en tō adikō mamōna pistos ouk egenesthe — if then you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon — adikos mamōnas (unrighteous mammon = worldly wealth, the money of the current age) is the test case for faithfulness: handling money honestly reveals whether one can be trusted with to alēthinon (the true, genuine — from alētheia = truth; the "real" as opposed to the "transient"). The contrast is between adikos mamōnas (unrighteous mammon = money) and to alēthinon (the genuine wealth = spiritual/eternal goods). Money is the training ground for the eternal.
16.12: en tō allotriō pistos ouk egenesthe, to hymeteron tis hymin dōsei — if you have not been faithful with someone else's property, who will give you what is your own — allotrion (another's property, what belongs to another — from allos = other; what is alien to oneself) vs hymeteron (your own — the possessive pronoun): worldly wealth is not "ours" but belongs to another (God's world, administered for a time); the "our own" (to hymeteron) is what is genuinely ours in eternity. The faithful manager of another's property earns what will be truly his own.
16.13: oudeis oiketēs dunatai dusi kyriois douleuein — no household servant can serve two masters — oiketēs (household servant — from oikos = house; one who belongs to the household, a domestic slave; the domestic version of doulos; also Acts 10:7; Rom 14:4; 1 Pet 2:18) rather than doulos (slave) — the distinction emphasizes the household-allegiance dimension. ē gar ton hena misēsei kai ton heteron agapēsei — for he will hate the one and love the other — miseō/agapaō (hate/love) as comparative-preference antonyms: complete devotion to one master produces functional hatred of the other. Theō douleuein kai mamōna — serve God and Mammon — the personification of Mammon as a rival kyrios (master) makes wealth-love equivalent to idolatry.
16.14: ēkouon de tauta panta hoi Pharisaioi philargyroi ontes kai exemyktērizon auton — the Pharisees, who were money-lovers, heard all these things and sneered at him — philargyros (money-lover — from phileō = love + arguros = silver/money; the compound = lover of money; also 2 Tim 3:2) is Luke's diagnosis; the Pharisees' monetary devotion makes them the illustration of serving Mammon. Exemyktērizon (sneered at, turned up the nose at — from ek- + myktērizō = to turn up the nose, sneer; from myktēr = nostril; the nose-turning gesture of contempt; also Gal 6:7 where God is not mocked/myktērizetai).
16.15: hymeis este hoi dikaiountes heautous enōpion tōn anthrōpōn — you are the ones who justify yourselves before men — dikaioō (justify, declare righteous — from dikaios = just, righteous; the justification verb of Pauline theology, here used by Jesus for self-justification before human audiences). The Pharisees' dikaioō heautous (self-justification) before human observers (enōpion tōn anthrōpōn) is contrasted with God's knowledge of hearts. ho de theos ginōskei tas kardias hymōn — God knows your hearts — kardiognōstēs (heart-knower) is used for God in Acts 1:24 and 15:8; here the verb form: God's heart-knowledge penetrates self-presentation.
16.16: ho nomos kai hoi prophētai mechri Iōannou — the Law and the Prophets until John — the canonical phrase ho nomos kai hoi prophētai (the Law and the Prophets) = the OT as a whole; the temporal boundary is John the Baptist. Apo tote hē basileia tou theou euangelizetai — from then the kingdom of God is proclaimed — euangelizō (proclaim good news — the proclamation verb of Luke-Acts; also 4:18; 7:22; 9:6; 16:16). kai pas eis autēn biazetai — and everyone forces his way into it — biazetai (forces/is forced — from biazō = to use force; the verb can be middle [forces his way] or passive [is forced in]; the meaning is disputed: either eagerness/urgency of entry or violent opposition against the kingdom). The Matthew parallel (11:12: hē basileia tōn ouranōn biazetai) supports the violence/forcing reading.
16.17: eukopōteron de estin ton ouranon kai tēn gēn parelthein — it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away — eukopoteros (easier — comparative of eukopos = easy; from eu + kopos = good-effort; the easy word; "easier" appears also in the camel-needle saying 18:25). Parelthein (pass away, pass by — aorist of parerchomai = pass beside, go past; also used for Jesus's words not passing away, 21:33). Ē tou nomou mian keraian pesein — than for one stroke of the pen to fall from the Law — keraia (stroke, horn-like mark — the tiny decorative mark on a Hebrew letter; the smallest unit of the Torah text; also Matt 5:18 in the "not one iota or keraia" saying). The Torah's permanence is established by comparison to cosmic stability.
16.18: pas ho apolyōn tēn gynaika autou kai gamōn heteran moicheuei — everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery — apolyō (release, dismiss, divorce — the standard divorce verb; the formal release of a wife from the marriage bond under Jewish law). The saying appears here in Luke without the Matthean exception clause (parektos logou porneias = except for sexual immorality, Matt 5:32; 19:9). Luke's absolute form of the saying is a Greek legal formulation without the Jewish exception-clause debate context; it may address a different situation (Gentile divorce practice). Kai ho apolelumenēn apo andros gamōn moicheuei — and the man who marries a woman divorced from a husband commits adultery — the second clause extends the prohibition to the man who marries the divorced woman.
16.19: anthrōpos de tis ēn plousios kai enedidysketo porphyran kai bussinon — there was a rich man who was dressed in purple and fine linen — plousios (rich — from ploutos = wealth; the rich-man word throughout Luke as the economic counterpart to ptōchos = poor). Porphyran (purple — the color of wealth and royalty; porphyra = the Tyrian purple dye extracted from sea snails at enormous expense; 10,000 snails for one gram of dye; the fabric therefore signaled extreme wealth) + bussinon (fine linen — from byssos = Egyptian fine linen, imported luxury fabric; also used for the Jerusalem temple curtain and the clothing of the righteous in Rev 19:8). Euphrainomenos kath hēmeran lampros — feasting sumptuously every day — the daily (kath hēmeran) sumptuousness (lampros = brilliantly, magnificently) characterizes excess rather than occasional celebration.
16.20: ptōchos de tis onomati Lazaros ebeblēto pros ton pylōna autou — a poor man named Lazarus was laid at his gate — Lazaros (the Hellenized form of Hebrew El-āzār = God helps) is the only named character in any of Jesus's parables — the detail is significant; the rich man is unnamed throughout. Ebeblēto (pluperfect passive of ballō = throw; the pluperfect indicates a state already established: "was lying placed/thrown") at the gate (pylōn = gate, gateway — the grand entrance of a wealthy estate). The begging-posture (ptōchos = beggar, destitute — from ptōssō = crouch/cower; the destitute-beggar word, not merely economically poor) at the gate is the maximum social contrast to the purple-clad feaster within.
16.21: epithymōn chortasthēnai apo tōn piptontōn apo tēs trapezēs tou plousiou — longing to be filled from what fell from the rich man's table — epithumeō (long for earnestly — the strong desire verb; also 15:16 for the prodigal's longing for pig-pods; 22:15 for Jesus's Passover longing) + chortasthēnai (to be filled/satisfied — the animal-satiety word; also 15:16; the miraculous feeding verb). All kai hoi kynes erchomenoi apeleichon ta helkē autou — even the dogs were coming and licking his sores — helkos (sore, wound, ulcer) + apoleichō (lick off — compound of apo- + leichō = lick). The dog-licking is ambiguous in the Greek: torment (unclean animals touching his wounds) or comfort (the only contact he receives).
16.22: egeneto de apothanein ton ptōchon kai apenechthēnai auton hypo tōn angelōn — the poor man died and was carried by the angels — apenechthēnai (to be carried away — aorist passive infinitive of apopherō = carry away, bring away; the angelic transport verb) contrasts with the rich man's burial (etaphē = was buried). No burial is mentioned for Lazarus — in antiquity, burial was the transition to the honored afterlife; Lazarus's burial is omitted, foregrounded by angelic transport instead. eis ton kolpon Abraam — into Abraham's bosom/side — kolpos (bosom, chest, side — also John 1:18 for the Son "in the Father's bosom"; 13:23 for the Beloved Disciple reclining in Jesus's bosom at the Last Supper): the closest possible position to the covenant patriarch.
16.23: en tō hadē eparas tous ophthalmous autou — in Hades, lifting up his eyes — hadēs (Hades — the Greek underworld/realm of the dead; adopted into Jewish afterlife vocabulary; in the LXX it translates sheol; in the NT, distinct from gehenna as the permanent place of destruction; Hades is the intermediate state or general underworld). Eparas tous ophthalmous (lifting up his eyes — the gesture of deliberate upward vision, also 6:20; 18:13) from below to see Abraham above. hora Abraam apo makrothen kai Lazaron en tois kolpois autou — he sees Abraham far away and Lazarus in his bosom — the spatial reversal is complete: the one who was at the gate below is now in the place of honor above; the rich man who feasted daily is now in torment below.
16.24: pater Abraam, eleēson me — Father Abraham, have mercy on me — eleēson me (have mercy on me — aorist imperative of eleaō = show mercy/compassion; the same cry of the blind men and the Syrophoenician woman throughout the Gospels; the kyrie eleison of liturgical tradition) addressed to pater Abraam (Father Abraham — the Abrahamic-family claim). Pempson Lazaron — send Lazarus — the rich man still addresses Lazarus only through Abraham; he treats Lazarus as an errand-runner even in the afterlife, suggesting his social perception has not changed. Hina bapsē to akron tou daktylou autou hydatos kai katapsyxē tēn glōssan mou — to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue.
16.25: teknon, mnēsthēti hoti apelabas ta agatha sou en tē zōē sou — child, remember that you received your good things in your lifetime — teknon (child — Abraham's address to the rich man is the same affectionate word the father used for the older son in 15:31; the warmth of the address sharpens the severity of the content). Apelabas (you received in full — aorist of apolambanō = receive back, receive in full; the prefix apo- implies complete receipt, payment-in-full). The reversal logic: goods received fully in this life; now the opposite. Kai Lazaros homoiōs ta kaka nun de hōde parakaleitai — Lazarus likewise received bad things, but now here he is comforted — parakaleō (be comforted, be encouraged — the Paraclete verb in its comfort function).
16.26: en pasi toutois metaxy hēmōn kai hymōn chasma mega estēriktai — in all this, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed — chasma (chasm, gulf — from chainō = yawn, gape; a gaping void; only NT occurrence; the specific word for an unbridgeable geological void) + mega (great) + estēriktai (has been fixed/established — perfect passive of stērizō = set firm, establish; the same verb for Jesus setting his face toward Jerusalem at 9:51). The permanence (stērizō perfect) of the separation is its defining characteristic: the chasm is not temporary but set in place once death has established the final state.
16.27: erōtō se oun, pater, hina pempsēs auton eis ton oikon tou patros mou — I beg you then, father, send him to my father's house — the rich man's second request shifts from personal relief to family warning; the petition is still through Abraham (pempsēs auton = send him), still treating Lazarus as a servant. Erōtō (I ask, I beg — from erōtaō; formal request verb) used for a serious petition. The family concern is genuine but comes too late and through the wrong channel; he cannot direct Lazarus any more than he could ignore him at his gate.
16.28: echō gar pente adelphous, hopōs diamartyretai autois — for I have five brothers, so that he may warn them — diamartyromai (testify solemnly, warn emphatically — from dia- + martys = witness through; intensive testimony verb; also Acts 2:40; 8:25; 18:5; 20:23-24; 28:23) — the request is for solemn warning testimony to prevent the brothers from arriving at the same place of torment. The rich man's concern for others emerges only in extremis, and even here it is framed in terms of his own family rather than universal compassion.
16.29: echousin Mōysea kai tous prophētas — they have Moses and the Prophets — the canonical phrase reappears (v.16): the OT witness (Torah + Prophets) as already sufficient. Akousatōsan autōn — let them listen to them — akouō (hear, listen, obey — the comprehensive hearing verb of Deuteronomy: shema = hear, obey) in the imperative. Abraham's point is that the Scriptures already contain everything necessary for repentance; the rich man's brothers have what is needed — they need only listen. The parable's argument is that the problem is not insufficient evidence but unwillingness to hear what is already available.
16.30: ouchi, pater Abraam, all ean tis apo nekrōn poreuthē pros autous, metanoēsousin — no, Father Abraham, but if someone from the dead goes to them they will repent — metanoeō (repent, change mind — from meta- + noeō = change thinking; turn around in mind and direction; the repentance verb) as the goal of the desired apparition. The rich man's request for a post-mortem missionary implies that Scripture is insufficient — a direct contradiction of Abraham's position. The irony of the parable is prospective: apo nekrōn (from the dead) anticipates Jesus's own resurrection, which the religious leaders will reject despite being confronted with it.
16.31: ei Mōyseōs kai tōn prophētōn ouk akouousin, oude ean tis ek nekrōn anastē peisthēsontai — if they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone rises from the dead — anastē (rises — aorist active subjunctive of anistēmi = rise, stand up; the resurrection verb) from the dead (ek nekrōn) is the precise phrase for Jesus's resurrection throughout Luke-Acts (24:46; Acts 10:41; 17:3). Peisthēsontai (be convinced, be persuaded — future passive of peithō = persuade) — Abraham's final word predicts the historical outcome: the resurrection of Jesus will not persuade those who have already refused Moses and the Prophets (Luke 20:1-8; Acts 4:1-22).

21.1: anablepas de eiden tous ballontas eis to gazophylakion ta dōra autōn plousious — looking up he saw the rich putting their gifts into the treasury. Anablepas (looking up, raising his eyes) — Jesus is the active, discerning observer; the temple treasury is the focus of his gaze. Gazophylakion (treasury — from Gaza = treasure + phylakē = guard; the treasury court of the women in the Jerusalem temple contained 13 trumpet-shaped bronze chests for different kinds of offerings). The episode immediately follows the denunciation of scribes who devour widows’ houses (20:47); the widow’s offering becomes the visual proof of that spiritual exploitation.
21.2: eiden de kai chēran tina penichran ballousan ekei lepta dyo — he also saw a poor widow put in two very small copper coins. Chēran penichran (a poor widow — penichran is a NT hapax meaning destitute, the poorest of the poor; distinct from the merely ptochos poor) — the widow is doubly identified: by her social status (widow, dependent on charity) and by her economic status (destitute). Lepta dyo (two lepta — the lepton was the smallest coin in circulation, 1/64 of a denarius; Josephus calculates the two as worth about 1/32 of a day’s wage). She gives both; she could have kept one.
21.3: alēthōs legō hymin hoti hē chēra hautē hē ptōchē pleion pantōn ebalen — truly I tell you, this poor widow has put in more than all of them. The evaluative reversal is Jesus’s characteristic hermeneutic: the visible quantity of the gift is overridden by the ratio of gift to total resources. Pleion pantōn (more than all — the comparative) — one widow’s two coins outweigh the combined gifts of all the wealthy donors. The reversal is economic, moral, and theological: the kingdom’s measure of giving is not the amount given but the cost of the gift to the giver.
21.4: pantes gar houtoi ek tou perisseuontos autois ebalon eis ta dōra, hautē de ek tou hystērēmatos autēs holon ton bion hon eichen ebalen — all these people gave their gifts out of their wealth; but she out of her poverty put in all she had to live on. Ek tou perisseuontos (out of the surplus) — the wealthy give from abundance, from what remains after their own needs are met. Holon ton bion hon eichen (all the livelihood she had — bios is life in the sense of the means of life, livelihood, the whole material basis of existence) — the widow’s gift is self-liquidating: she has given herself, not a portion of herself. The gift is a concrete enactment of the total self-giving that discipleship requires.
21.5: kai tinōn legontōn peri tou hierou hoti lithois kalois kai anathemasin kekosmētai — as some of his disciples were remarking about how the temple was adorned with beautiful stones and votive gifts. Lithois kalois (beautiful stones) — Josephus (Ant. 15.391-402) describes the Jerusalem temple’s stones as white stone blocks up to 37.5 x 11.5 x 9 feet; the gleaming white marble was visible from miles away. Anathemata (votive gifts, dedicated offerings — objects hung on or placed in the temple as thank offerings to God) — Herod’s temple was adorned with golden vines, imperial gifts, and war trophies donated by Hellenistic kings. The disciples’ admiration is aesthetically reasonable; Jesus’s response will be theologically devastating.
21.6: tauta ha theōreite eleusontai hēmerai en hais ouk aphethēsetai lithos epi lithō hos ou katalythēsetai — the time will come when not one of these stones you see will be left on another; every one of them will be thrown down. Ouk aphethēsetai lithos epi lithō (not a stone will be left on a stone) — the complete demolition of the entire structure. Josephus (Jewish War 7.1.1) records that after Titus’s destruction in 70 CE, the temple mount was so thoroughly plowed that “there was left nothing to make those who came believe it had ever been inhabited.” Katalythēsetai (will be thrown down, demolished) — the prediction is total: not one stone will survive in architectural relationship to another.
21.7: epērōtēsan de auton legontes: didaskale, pote oun tauta estai kai ti to sēmeion hotan mellē tauta ginesthai — Teacher, they asked, when will these things happen? And what will be the sign that they are about to take place? The disciples’ address Didaskale (Teacher — not Kyrie Lord or Epistata Master) is appropriate to the catechetical context. Their two questions — pote (when) and ti to sēmeion (what the sign) — are standard apocalyptic questions: they want both a timetable and a leading indicator. Luke’s Olivet Discourse (parallel to Mark 13 and Matt 24-25) will answer the sign-question and partially subvert the when-question.
21.8: ho de eipen: blepete mē planēthēte — he replied: Watch out that you are not deceived. The opening command (blepete, watch out; mē planēthēte, do not be led astray) frames the entire discourse as a warning against false eschatological interpretation. Polloi gar eleusontai epi tō onomati mou legontes: egō eimi kai: ho kairos ēngiken — many will come in my name saying I am he and The time has come near. The false claimants combine two claims: messianic identity (egō eimi, I am) and eschatological urgency (ho kairos ēngiken, the time is near). Mē poreutheite opisō autōn (do not follow after them) — the disciples’ protection against deception is the warning itself.
21.9: hotan de akousēte polemous kai akatastas, mē ptoēthēte — when you hear of wars and uprisings, do not be frightened. Akatastas (uprisings, disturbances, revolutions — from a = not + kathistēmi = to establish; the unsettled state of civil conflict) — Luke adds this to Mark’s polemon kai akoai polemōn (wars and rumors of wars). Dei gar tauta genesthai prōton (for these things must happen first) — dei (divine necessity) frames wars and uprisings as part of the providentially ordered sequence; prōton (first) indicates these are not the end itself but precursors. Ouk eutheos to telos (the end will not come immediately) — an explicit anti-eschatological-impatience statement.
21.10: tote elegen autois: egerthēsetai ethnos ep ethnos kai basileia epi basileian — then he said to them: Nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom. The formula ethnos ep ethnos kai basileia epi basileian (nation against nation, kingdom against kingdom) is an OT prophetic pattern (Isa 19:2; 2 Chron 15:6; Hag 2:22) for the collapse of the international order. The pattern echoes the prophetic vision of the world-age before divine intervention; Jesus places it in the category of necessary precursors rather than imminent signs of the final moment. The teaching manages eschatological anxiety: large-scale political violence does not itself signal the imminence of the end.
21.11: seismoi te megaloi kai kata topous limoi kai loimoi esontai — there will be great earthquakes, famines and pestilences in various places. Limoi kai loimoi (famines and plagues/pestilences) — the Greek alliteration (both words begin with loi-/lim-) gives the pairing a memorable quality; both represent the breakdown of the food and health systems that sustain civilization. Phobētra te kai apo ouranou sēmeia megala estai (there will be fearful events and great signs from heaven) — phobētra (terrible/fearful sights) is a NT hapax, a rare word for terrifying spectacles; the sēmeia ap ouranou (heavenly signs) were well-attested in ancient apocalyptic literature (1 Enoch, Sibylline Oracles) and in Josephus’s account of the omens before 70 CE (Jewish War 6.5.3).
21.12: pro de toutōn panton epibalousin eph hymas tas cheiras autōn kai diōxousin — but before all this, they will lay their hands on you and persecute you. Pro de toutōn panton (but before all these things) — Luke’s discourse inserts the persecution of disciples as the first thing to happen, before the cosmic signs. The sequencing is deliberate: the disciples will not be spectators of the end-time drama but participants; their suffering is part of the story, not incidental to it. Paradidomenous eis tas synagōgas kai phylakas (being handed over to synagogues and prisons) — the official-religious and official-civil venues of persecution; apagomenous epi basileis kai hēgemonas (led before kings and governors) = Roman administrative authority.
21.13: apobēsetai de hymin eis martyrion — and so you will bear testimony to me. Apobēsetai eis martyrion (it will turn out for you as testimony/witness) — the persecution that appears to be the disciples’ defeat is reframed as their opportunity: the court appearance before authorities becomes the venue for bearing witness to Jesus. Martyrion (witness, testimony — the root of the word “martyr”) — the testimony before kings and governors is the fulfillment of the mission charge (Acts 1:8: to the ends of the earth). Persecution becomes evangelism; the disciples’ loss of security becomes the world’s gain of gospel proclamation.
21.14: thesthe oun en tais kardiais hymōn mē promeletatē apologēthēnai — but make up your mind not to worry beforehand how you will defend yourselves. Thesthe en tais kardiais hymōn (set it in your hearts — the deliberate decision to hold a conviction) — the command is for a prior, settled commitment that will survive the crisis of arrest. Mē promeletatē (do not pre-practice/pre-meditate — a rare verb; pro- = beforehand + meletaō = practice, rehearse) — the disciples are not to spend mental energy rehearsing their legal defense; what they will need will be provided at the moment. The reason follows immediately in v.15.
21.15: egō gar dōsō hymin stoma kai sophian hē ou dynēsontai antistēnai ē anteipein hapantes hoi antikeimenoi hymin — for I will give you a mouth and wisdom that none of your adversaries will be able to resist or contradict. Stoma kai sophia (a mouth and wisdom — hendiadys for eloquent wisdom or wise speech; the combination draws on the Servant’s “well-trained tongue” and “mouth like a sharp sword” of Isa 49:2 and 50:4). Ou dynēsontai antistēnai ē anteipein (will not be able to resist or contradict) — the double negative is absolute: Jesus promises that the Spirit-given testimony will be irrefutable. This promise is enacted in Acts: Stephen before the Sanhedrin (Acts 6:10), Paul before governors and kings.
21.16: paradothēsesthe de kai hypo goneōn kai adelphōn kai syngenōn kai philōn — you will be betrayed even by parents, brothers and sisters, relatives and friends. The list of betrayers escalates from the intimate to the close: parents, siblings, relatives, friends — all four circles of primary human relationship. Kai thanatoōsousin ex hymōn (and they will put some of you to death) — not all disciples will die; the ex hymōn (of you, some among you) is selective. The promise of v.18 (not a hair shall perish) will therefore be qualified by v.19 (by endurance you will gain your lives) as the comprehensive salvation-promise that transcends physical survival.
21.17: kai esesthe misoumenoi hypo pantōn dia to onoma mou — and you will be hated by everyone because of my name. Misoumenoi hypo pantōn (hated by all/everyone) — the universal hostility prediction is hyperbolic for rhetorical effect: the world in its resistance to Jesus extends that resistance to his disciples. Dia to onoma mou (because of my name) — the disciples are hated not for their own qualities but because they bear Jesus’s name; the hostility is Christological in its origin, transferred through association. The promise that follows (not a hair, v.18) is set in deliberate contrast to the universality of the hatred.
21.18: kai thriks ek tēs kephalēs hymōn ou mē apolētai — but not a hair of your head will perish. The proverbial idiom (also 1 Sam 14:45; 2 Sam 14:11; 1 Kings 1:52; Acts 27:34) expresses absolute divine protection over what matters most. Given v.16’s prediction that some disciples will be killed, the hair-perishing promise is not about physical survival but about the totality of ultimate preservation: the person who is physically killed in testimony is not ultimately lost. Ou mē apolētai (will certainly not perish — the double negative for absolute negation) — the verb apollymi (to perish, to be destroyed) is the antonym of sōzō (to save); the promise is soteriological: no disciple who bears testimony is finally destroyed.
21.19: en tē hypomonē hymōn ktēsasthe tas psychas hymōn — by your endurance you will gain your lives. Hypomonē (endurance, patient steadfastness — from hypo-menō, to remain under; not passive resignation but the active bearing of sustained pressure without collapsing) — Luke’s signature virtue for discipleship under persecution. Ktēsasthe tas psychas hymōn (gain/acquire your lives — ktaomai is to acquire, possess; the future indicative has imperative force) — the paradox of 17:33 (whoever saves their life will lose it) is now inverted: by enduring under persecution rather than abandoning the testimony, the disciple acquires life as a possession. Psychas is the whole person, not merely the physical soul.
21.20: hotan de idēte kyklōumenēn hypo stratopedōn Ierousalēm, tote gnōte hoti ēggiken hē erēmōsis autēs — when you see Jerusalem being surrounded by armies, you will know that its desolation is near. Luke’s version of the abomination/desolation (Mark 13:14; Matt 24:15) replaces the cryptic Danielic reference with the concrete military event: kyklōumenēn hypo stratopedōn (being encircled by military camps) is a precise military-technical description that was fulfilled when Titus’s legions surrounded Jerusalem in 68-70 CE. Erēmōsis (desolation — the LXX term for the Danielic abomination, Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11) — Luke makes explicit what Mark and Matthew leave implicit: the desolation is the military destruction of the city.
21.21: tote hoi en tē Ioudaia pheugetōsan eis ta orē kai hoi en mesō autēs ekchōreitōsan — then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains, let those in the city get out. The flight command is urgent and universal: everyone in Judea, everyone in Jerusalem, even those in the surrounding countryside — all are to flee when the armies arrive. Ekchōreitōsan (let them leave, clear out — ek = out + chōreō = move) is a practical evacuation command. Eusebius (Church History 3.5.3) records that the Jerusalem Christians, warned by an oracle, fled to Pella across the Jordan before the siege; the historicity is disputed but the tradition shows this passage was understood as literal flight-instruction.
21.22: hoti hēmerai ekdikēseōs autai eisin tou plēsthēnai panta ta gegrammena — for this is the time of punishment in fulfillment of all that has been written. Hēmerai ekdikēseōs (days of vengeance/retribution — ekdikēsis is the execution of justice, the satisfaction of a legal claim; the LXX uses it for God’s retributive action against covenant-breakers, Deut 32:35; Hos 9:7; Jer 46:10). Tou plēsthēnai panta ta gegrammena (for all that has been written to be fulfilled/filled up) — the divine necessity of Scripture’s fulfillment frames the destruction: it is not merely a Roman military event but the culmination of the prophetic witness against unfaithful Jerusalem (cf. Isa 63:4; Jer 51:6; Ezek 9:1-6).
21.23: ouai de tais en gastri echousais kai tais thēlazousais en ekeinais tais hēmerais — how dreadful it will be in those days for pregnant women and nursing mothers! The specific concern for the physically vulnerable — those whose physical condition makes flight impossible or dangerous — is the human face of the catastrophe. Estai gar anankē megalē epi tēs gēs kai orgē en tō laō toutō (there will be great distress in the land and wrath upon this people) — anankē (necessity, compulsion, distress) and orgē (wrath) combine physical catastrophe and divine judgment. En tō laō toutō (upon this people) — the particular focus on the people of Israel within the universal eschatological language.
21.24: kai pesountai stomati machairēs kai aichmalōtisthēsontai eis ta ethnē panta — they will fall by the sword and will be taken as prisoners to all the nations. The double fate of the war — death in battle (stomati machairēs, by the mouth/edge of the sword) and captivity (aichmalōtisthēsontai, taken prisoner as war captives) — corresponds to the historical record: Josephus estimates 1.1 million dead in the siege and 97,000 taken captive (Jewish War 6.9.3). Kai Ierousalēm estai patoumenē hypo ethnōn achri hou plērōthōsin kairoi ethnōn (and Jerusalem will be trampled on by the Gentiles until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled) — kairoi ethnōn (the appointed times of the Gentiles) is Luke’s unique phrase for the period between 70 CE and the eschatological consummation; it has a theological limit (achri hou plērōthōsin, until they are fulfilled).
21.25: kai estai sēmeia en hēliō kai selēnē kai astrois — there will be signs in the sun, moon and stars. The cosmic-sign catalogue (sun, moon, stars) draws on OT prophetic imagery of the day of the Lord: Isa 13:10 (sun and moon dark on Babylon’s judgment day); Joel 2:10,30-31 (sun into darkness, moon into blood); Ezek 32:7 (lights darkened over Egypt). The imagery is not necessarily literal astronomical events but the conventional prophetic language for cosmic upheaval accompanying divine judgment. Kai epi tēs gēs synochē ethnōn en aporiai ēchous thalassēs kai salou (on earth anguish of nations in perplexity at the roaring and surging of the sea) — synochē (distress, anxiety, constriction) and aporia (no way out, perplexity) describe the nations’ psychological state.
21.26: apopsychontōn anthrōpōn apo phobou kai prosdokias tōn erchomenōn tē oikoumenē — people fainting from terror, apprehensive of what is coming on the world. Apopsychontōn (fainting, losing consciousness — literally “the breath leaving”; a medical term unique to Luke in the NT) — the visceral, physical terror that the cosmic upheaval will produce. Hai gar dynameis tōn ouranōn saleuthesonai (for the powers of heaven will be shaken) — dynameis (powers — the structural forces that hold the heavenly order in place; in Jewish cosmology, the angels governing the celestial spheres and the laws of nature) will be shaken. The world’s structural stability will be dismantled in preparation for the new order the Son of Man establishes.
21.27: kai tote opsontai ton huion tou anthrōpou erchomenon en nephelē meta dynameōs kai doxēs pollēs — at that time they will see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory. The Parousia prediction directly quotes Dan 7:13 LXX (erchetai meta tōn nephelōn — one like a Son of Man coming with the clouds): the vindicated heavenly figure receives dominion. En nephelē (in a cloud — the singular, not plural, differs from Mark 13:26’s “in clouds with great power and glory”) — the cloud is the vehicle of divine presence (Exod 13:21; 16:10; 1 Kgs 8:10-11). Meta dynameōs kai doxēs pollēs (with power and great glory) — the complete reversal of the passion’s apparent defeat: the condemned Son of Man returns as the vindicated cosmic ruler.
21.28: archomenōn de toutōn ginesthai anakupsate kai eparate tas kephalas hymōn, dioti engizei hē apolytrōsis hymōn — when these things begin to take place, stand up and lift up your heads, because your redemption is drawing near. The command (anakupsate, lift up, straighten up — from bowing in fear) is the disciples’ appropriate posture when the cosmic signs appear: not cowering but confident expectation. Apolytrōsis (redemption, deliverance — the term for ransom-liberation from slavery or captivity, also used in 1:68 of Zechariah’s prophecy about redemption for God’s people) — the cosmic upheaval that terrifies the world’s nations is the approach of the disciples’ redemption; the same events are judgment for the world and liberation for the covenant people.
21.29: kai eipen parabolēn autois: idete tēn sykēn kai panta ta dendra — he told them this parable: Look at the fig tree and all the trees. Luke’s expansion of Mark’s “fig tree” to “fig tree and all the trees” is significant: the lesson is from arboreal seasonality in general, not from the fig tree as a symbolic Israel-indicator (as in some interpretations of Mark). The universalizing (panta ta dendra, all the trees) makes the analogy available to any Gentile reader, not only to those who recognize the fig tree’s Jewish symbolic valence.
21.30: hotan probalosōsin ēdē blepontes aph heautōn ginōskete hoti ēdē engys to theros estin — when they sprout leaves, you can see for yourselves and know that summer is near. Blepontes aph heautōn (seeing for yourselves — the reflexive idiom of empirical self-observation; you do not need an expert to tell you) — the seasonal sign is self-evidently readable by any observer. To theros estin engys (summer is near) — the budding leaves are not summer but the reliable indicator of summer’s approach. The parable’s logic: observable signs reliably indicate approaching realities even before they arrive.
21.31: houtōs kai hymeis, hotan idēte tauta ginomena, ginōskete hoti engys estin hē basileia tou theou — even so, when you see these things happening, you know that the kingdom of God is near. The application is direct: the cosmic signs of vv.25-27 function as the budding leaves — visible indicators that the kingdom of God (hē basileia tou theou) is approaching. The shift from “your redemption” (v.28) to “the kingdom of God” (v.31) connects the disciples’ personal redemption to the broader consummation of God’s reign. Engys estin (is near) — the same construction as the fig tree’s summer-is-near: proximity without a specific moment.
21.32: amēn legō hymin hoti ou mē parēlthē hē genea hautē heōs an panta genētai — truly I tell you, this generation will certainly not pass away until all these things have happened. Hē genea hautē (this generation) is the most contested phrase in the Olivet Discourse. The primary options: (1) the generation of 30 CE whose lifetime saw the fulfillment of vv.12-24 in the events of 66-70 CE; (2) the human race (but genea never means the human race in the NT); (3) the wicked generation (ethical interpretation); (4) the generation alive at the end, whenever that is. Option 1 fits best grammatically and historically: vv.5-24 were substantially fulfilled in 70 CE within a generation of the prophecy, while vv.25-28 await full consummation. Ou mē parēlthē (will certainly not pass away — double negative) reinforces the reliability of the prediction.
21.33: ho ouranos kai hē gē pareleusontai, hoi de logoi mou ou mē pareleusontai — heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away. The contrast between the created cosmos’s transience and Jesus’s words’ permanence is the strongest possible claim of authority: even the structures of creation will end; what Jesus says will not. Pareleusontai (will pass away — the same verb for “pass away” in both clauses, creating a tight antithesis) — the universe’s passing is contingent on God’s decree; Jesus’s words are exempt from that contingency. The claim exceeds OT prophetic authority: the prophets spoke God’s words; Jesus speaks as the one whose own words have divine permanence.
21.34: prosechete de heautois mēpote barythōsin hymōn hai kardiai en kraipale kai methē kai merimnaisi biōtikaisi — be careful, or your hearts will be weighed down with carousing, drunkenness and the anxieties of daily life. The watchfulness call returns from cosmic signs to the inner life of the disciples: the danger is not only external persecution but internal heart-heaviness. Kraipale (carousing, dissipation — the morning sickness after a night of excess drinking; hangover as a metaphor for the dull, heavy-headed incapacity that follows self-indulgence) and methē (drunkenness) are the physical vices; merimnaisi biōtikaisi (worries about daily life, life’s daily cares) is the existential absorption that crowds out eschatological readiness.
21.35: kai epistē eph hymas aiphnidios hē hēmera ekeinē hōs pagis — and that day will close on you suddenly like a trap. Aiphnidios (sudden, unexpected — a NT hapax in Luke; used in 1 Thess 5:3 for the sudden-destruction that comes when people say “peace and security”) — the emphasis on suddenness extends the trap-metaphor: a trap works precisely because its victim does not see it coming. Hōs pagis (like a trap/snare) — the hunting image: the animal absorbed in its ordinary foraging does not see the snare until it is too late. Epeleusetai gar epi pantas tous kathēmenous epi prosōpon pasēs tēs gēs (for it will come upon all who dwell on the face of all the earth) — universal scope: no geographic escape, no exemption by location.
21.36: agrypneite de en panti kairō deomenoi hina kataxiōthēte ekphygein tauta panta ta mellonta ginesthai — be always on the watch, and pray that you may be able to escape all that is about to happen. Agrypneite (stay awake, keep watch — a = not + hypnos = sleep; to be sleepless, vigilant) — the opposite of the carousness and anxiety of v.34; watchfulness is the active alternative to heart-heaviness. Hina kataxiōthēte ekphygein (in order that you may be considered worthy to escape — kataxioō = to be deemed worthy, to be granted) — worthiness to escape is not earned but granted by God in answer to prayer; the prayer is the appropriate posture of dependent readiness. Stathēnai emprosthen tou huiou tou anthrōpou (to stand before the Son of Man) — the eschatological goal of watchfulness: not merely to escape judgment but to stand, upright and accepted, in the presence of the returning Son of Man.
21.37: ēn de tas hēmeras en tō hierō didaskōn, tas de nyktas exerchomenos ēylizeto eis to oros to kaloumenon Elaiōn — each day Jesus was teaching at the temple, and each evening he went out to spend the night on the hill called the Mount of Olives. The summary notation of Jesus’s final days in Jerusalem establishes the pattern: days in the temple teaching (the place of the Jewish teaching institution from which he has been driving out merchants and proclaiming his authority, 19:45-47), nights outside the city on the Mount of Olives. Ēylizeto (lodged, spent the night — from aulē, a courtyard or open space; to camp out in the open) — Jesus has no Jerusalem home; he camps on the hillside.
21.38: kai pas ho laos ōrthrizen pros auton en tō hierō akouein autou — and all the people came early in the morning to hear him at the temple. Ōrthrizen (came early at dawn, rose at daybreak — from orthros, the early morning, the hours just before and after sunrise) — the popular response to Jesus’s temple teaching is eager and early: the people wake at dawn to secure their position to hear him. Pas ho laos (all the people) — Luke’s characteristic notice of the popular enthusiasm that surrounds Jesus’s Jerusalem ministry and which creates the political problem the chief priests must navigate without triggering a riot (22:2). The verse transitions seamlessly into the passion narrative’s opening.
